## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Solid-State Battery Definition** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Solid-State Batteries. Mention two advantages over conventional Lithium-ion batteries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks)**: A **Solid-State Battery (SSB)** is an advanced energy storage device that replaces the conventional **liquid or gel polymer electrolyte** found in standard lithium-ion batteries with a **solid electrolyte** (ceramic, glass, or polymer-based). The solid electrolyte conducts lithium ions between the cathode and anode while acting as a physical separator.

**Advantages (1.5 marks)**:
1. **Higher Energy Density**: Solid electrolytes enable the use of **lithium-metal anodes**, pushing theoretical energy density to **400–500 Wh/kg** (vs. **250 Wh/kg** for conventional Li-ion).
2. **Enhanced Safety**: No flammable liquid electrolyte → **elimination of thermal runaway and fire risk**, which is the primary safety concern in current EVs.
3. **Longer Cycle Life**: Reduced side reactions and dendrite formation lead to **5000+ cycles** (vs. 1000–2000 for Li-ion).
4. **Wider Operating Temperature Range**: Solid electrolytes function reliably from **-30°C to +100°C**.

**Mark Distribution**: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for two distinct advantages.

---

#### **Question 2: Wide-Bandgap Semiconductors** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What are Wide-Bandgap (WBG) semiconductors? List two materials used and one benefit in EV inverters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark)**: **Wide-Bandgap (WBG) semiconductors** are power electronic devices made from materials with a **larger bandgap** (>2 eV) compared to conventional **Silicon** (1.1 eV). They are used in EV inverters and DC-DC converters for superior high-voltage, high-frequency, and high-temperature operation.

**Two WBG Materials (1 mark)**:
- **Silicon Carbide (SiC)**: Bandgap **3.26 eV**
- **Gallium Nitride (GaN)**: Bandgap **3.4 eV**

**Benefit in EV Inverters (1 mark)**:
- **Higher switching frequency** (up to **1 MHz**) → reduced size of inductors and capacitors → **inverter weight reduction by 40%** and **efficiency improvement by 5–8%**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the recent developments in battery technologies for Electric Vehicles. Compare Li-ion, LFP, and Solid-State batteries based on energy density, safety, and cost. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the evolution of EV charging infrastructure from Level 1 to Extreme Fast Charging (XFC). Explain the principles of wireless charging and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology. (7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Introduction to EV Battery Evolution (2 marks)**:

The modern EV battery landscape has evolved through three major waves:
- **First Wave (2010–2015)**: Dominance of **NMC (Nickel Manganese Cobalt)** chemistries — high energy density, moderate cost.
- **Second Wave (2015–2022)**: Adoption of **LFP (Lithium Iron Phosphate)** — driven by cost reduction and safety concerns after thermal runaway incidents.
- **Third Wave (2023 onwards)**: Emergence of **Solid-State Batteries (SSB)** and **Sodium-Ion Batteries (Na-ion)** for next-generation performance.

**2. Comparison Table (3 marks)**:

| Parameter | NMC (Li-ion) | LFP | Solid-State (Future) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Density (Wh/kg)** | 220–280 | 150–180 | 350–500 (target) |
| **Cycle Life** | 1000–2000 | 3000+ | 5000+ (projected) |
| **Thermal Runaway (°C)** | ~210°C | ~270°C | >300°C (no liquid) |
| **Cost ($/kWh, 2024)** | ~130 | ~95 | ~200 (R&D stage) |
| **Cobalt Dependency** | Yes (ethical concerns) | No | No |
| **Commercial Maturity** | Mature | Mature | Pilot (2027–2030) |

**3. Key Insights (2 marks)**:
- **LFP dominance in mass market** is driven by **cost parity** and **safety profile** — particularly favored in Indian EVs (Tata Nexon EV, MG Comet EV).
- **NMC remains preferred for long-range premium vehicles** (Tesla Model S, Lucid Air) due to higher specific energy.
- **Solid-state** will likely **co-exist** with Li-ion in a **dual-chemistry strategy** — LFP for affordability, SSB for premium range.

---

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Charging Level Hierarchy (3 marks)**:

| Level | Power | Connector Type | Charging Time (for 300 km) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 (AC)** | 1.4–2.4 kW | Type 1/Type 2 | 18–24 hours | Home (overnight) |
| **Level 2 (AC)** | 7–22 kW | Type 2 / SAE J1772 | 3–6 hours | Workplace, Malls |
| **Level 3 (DC Fast)** | 50–150 kW | CCS2 / CHAdeMO | 30–45 min | Highway stations |
| **Ultra-Fast DC** | 150–350 kW | CCS2 (350 kW) | 10–15 min | Premium corridors |
| **XFC** | >350 kW (up to 1 MW) | CCS2 / MCS (trucks) | <5 min | Pilot (StoreDot) |

**2. Wireless Charging Principle (2 marks)**:

- Uses **electromagnetic induction** between a **ground-based primary coil** and a **vehicle-mounted secondary coil**.
- Operates at frequencies of **79–90 kHz** (per SAE J2954 standard).
- Achieves **~90–93% efficiency** with proper coil alignment (±10 cm tolerance).
- **Advantages**: No plugging, weather-resistant, ideal for autonomous fleet charging.
- **Limitations**: Lower power (typically 3.3–11 kW currently), expensive infrastructure.

**3. Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Technology (2 marks)**:

- Enables **bidirectional power flow** — the EV battery can **discharge** to the grid during peak demand and **charge** during off-peak hours.
- Uses **CHAdeMO** (Nissan) or **ISO 15118** (CCS2) communication protocols.
- Applications:
  - **Peak shaving** for utilities
  - **Backup power** for homes (V2H — Vehicle-to-Home)
  - **Grid frequency regulation**
- Pioneered by **Nissan Leaf (UK V2G trials)**, **Ford F-150 Lightning (Intelligent Backup Power)**.
- Forms the foundation of **smart grid integration** — a critical KTU Module 1 learning outcome.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV) and Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV). Compare their merits and demerits. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the role of Wide-Bandgap (SiC/GaN) semiconductors in modern EV inverters. How do they improve vehicle efficiency and range? (7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. BEV Block Diagram (2.5 marks)**:

```
[Battery Pack] → [Inverter] → [Electric Motor] → [Single-Speed Gearbox] → [Wheels]
       ↑                ↑
[On-Board Charger]   [DC Fast Port (CCS2)]
       ↑
[AC Wall Outlet / Home Charger]
```

- **Energy Source**: Lithium-ion battery (75–100 kWh typical)
- **Power Conversion**: Inverter converts **DC → AC** for the motor
- **Charging**: AC via on-board charger (slow) OR DC directly to battery (fast)
- **Regenerative Braking**: Motor acts as generator, feeding power back to battery

**2. FCEV Block Diagram (2.5 marks)**:

```
[H₂ Tank] → [Fuel Cell Stack] → [DC-DC Converter] → [Battery (Buffer)]
                                                       ↓
                                                  [Inverter]
                                                       ↓
                                              [Electric Motor] → [Wheels]
```

- **Energy Source**: Compressed **hydrogen gas** (350/700 bar)
- **Power Generation**: Fuel cell stack produces **DC electricity** via **H₂ + O₂ → H₂O + electricity** reaction
- **Buffer Battery**: Small Li-ion pack (1–2 kWh) for peak power and regen energy storage
- **Byproduct**: Pure water vapor (zero tailpipe emissions)

**3. Comparison Table (2 marks)**:

| Parameter | BEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|
| **Refueling Time** | 20–60 min (fast charge) | ~5 min (H₂ refuel) |
| **Range** | 300–600 km | 500–700 km |
| **Infrastructure** | Growing (10L+ chargers globally) | Very limited (~1000 stations) |
| **Efficiency (Well-to-Wheel)** | 70–80% (renewable grid) | 25–35% (H₂ production losses) |
| **Best Use Case** | Passenger cars, urban mobility | Heavy trucks, buses, long-haul |
| **Examples** | Tesla Model Y, Tata Nexon EV | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo |

---

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Background: Limitations of Silicon (1.5 marks)**:

Conventional **Silicon (Si) IGBTs** (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors) used in early EV inverters suffer from:
- Limited switching frequency (~**20 kHz**)
- High conduction losses at high voltages
- Thermal management challenges above **150°C**
- Larger passive components → bulky inverter

**2. WBG Materials: SiC and GaN (2 marks)**:

- **Silicon Carbide (SiC)**: Bandgap **3.26 eV**, breakdown field **10× silicon**, can operate at **200°C+**.
- **Gallium Nitride (GaN)**: Bandgap **3.4 eV**, ultra-fast switching, primarily used in **low-to-medium voltage** applications (on-board chargers, DC-DC converters).

**3. Impact on EV Inverters (2.5 marks)**:

| Performance Metric | Si IGBT | SiC MOSFET | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switching Frequency** | 20 kHz | 100 kHz–1 MHz | 5–50× |
| **Inverter Efficiency** | 95% | 98–99% | +3–4% |
| **Power Density** | 5 kW/L | 25 kW/L | 5× |
| **Operating Temperature** | 150°C max | >200°C | Higher reliability |
| **Inverter Weight** | Baseline | -40% | Compact packaging |

**4. Real-World Impact on Vehicle (1 mark)**:

- **Tesla Model 3** was the **first mass-market EV** to deploy a **full SiC inverter** (2018), achieving **~5% range improvement** over Model S.
- **Lucid Motors** uses SiC inverters achieving **22 kW/L power density**.
- **BYD's e-Platform 3.0** integrates SiC for **8-in-1 electric drive system**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students often write **"SiC and GaN are the same"** — they are NOT. **SiC** dominates **high-voltage traction inverters** (400V–800V), while **GaN** is preferred in **low-voltage** applications (48V mild hybrids, onboard chargers). Also, do NOT confuse "Wide Bandgap" with "Wide Voltage Range" — they are unrelated concepts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Recent Development Trends**

✅ **Battery Evolution**: NMC (high energy) → LFP (cheap & safe) → Solid-State (future) → Sodium-Ion (alternative)

✅ **Energy Density Benchmark**: Current Li-ion ~**250 Wh/kg**; Solid-State target **400–500 Wh/kg**

✅ **C-Rate Definition**: $C = I / C_{rated}$ — fast charging is **3C–6C** for modern EVs

✅ **Motor Trends**: PMSM dominates; Axial Flux is emerging; SRM removes rare-earth dependency

✅ **WBG Semiconductors**: SiC in traction inverters, GaN in onboard chargers — efficiency **98%+**

✅ **Charging Levels**: L1 (1.4 kW) → L2 (22 kW) → DC Fast (150 kW) → Ultra-Fast (350 kW) → XFC (>350 kW)

✅ **Wireless Charging**: SAE J2954 standard, **79–90 kHz**, **~90% efficiency**

✅ **V2G**: Bidirectional power flow using ISO 15118 / CHAdeMO protocols

✅ **FCEV vs BEV**: FCEV has **5-min refuel + long range** but **poor well-to-wheel efficiency** (~30%)

✅ **Lightweight Materials**: CFRP (50% weight reduction), Aluminum (30%), AHSS (industry standard)

✅ **Autonomy + EV Synergy**: EVs are the **preferred platform** for Level 3+ autonomous driving

✅ **Indian Context**: FAME-II subsidy, PLI scheme, Tata/BYD/Mahindra leading domestic manufacturing

✅ **Sustainability**: Second-life batteries for grid storage (e.g., **Nissan-Sumitomo JV**), recycling via hydrometallurgical processes

✅ **Key Formula Recall**:
- $E_s = E / m$ (Wh/kg)
- $t_c = (\Delta SoC \cdot C) / I$
- $\eta_c = E_{battery} / E_{grid}$
- $P = T \cdot \omega$

---

topics.title: EV Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EV Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road-going automotive platform whose primary propulsion energy is drawn from an electrochemical energy storage system (typically a rechargeable battery pack) and is converted into mechanical tractive power by one or more **electric machines (electric motors)**. Unlike conventional internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles, the EV architecture eliminates the on-board combustion of hydrocarbon fuels and instead uses the **electromechanical energy conversion chain** as the sole (or dominant) source of motive power.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the term "EV" is treated as a broad umbrella that includes **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)**, **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs)**, and **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> The introductory module defines the *EV concept* as the convergence of three engineering pillars: (a) **Energy Storage** (battery chemistry), (b) **Energy Conversion** (electric machine + power electronics), and (c) **Energy Management** (control strategy).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine two ways to deliver a parcel across a city:

- **ICE Vehicle Analogy:** You carry a *fuel can on your back*. Every few kilometers, you must stop, burn some fuel in a mini-engine, throw away the exhaust smoke, and refill. Most of the energy you consume is lost as heat and noise; only a fraction actually pushes the parcel forward.
- **EV Analogy:** You carry a *rechargeable battery on your back*. The battery quietly powers an electric motor that turns the wheels directly. There is no smoke, no mini-explosions, and the energy is delivered almost silently and very efficiently. When the battery drains, you simply plug it into a wall socket to refill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this analogy matters:** In a typical gasoline car, only **20–35 %** of the chemical energy in fuel reaches the wheels. In a typical EV, **75–90 %** of the electrical energy from the grid reaches the wheels — because electric motors are inherently far more efficient than heat engines.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU 2024 Definitions)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| All-Electric Range | $R_{AE}$ | **150 – 600 km** | Distance achievable on a single full charge |
| Energy Consumption | $E_{c}$ | **0.12 – 0.20 kWh/km** | Energy drawn per km from the grid |
| Battery Capacity | $C_{bat}$ | **20 – 100 kWh** | Usable on-board electrochemical storage |
| Charging Power | $P_{chg}$ | **3.3 – 350 kW** | Rate at which energy can be replenished |
| Motor Peak Power | $P_{m,pk}$ | **75 – 400 kW** | Maximum sustained mechanical output |
| Drive Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{DC}$ | **75 – 90 %** | Tank-to-wheel efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Definition:** *Tank-to-Wheel (TtW) Efficiency* is the ratio of mechanical energy delivered at the wheels to the chemical/electrical energy entering the vehicle's energy buffer. For EVs this is dominated by the electric drivetrain (motor + inverter + transmission), while for ICE vehicles it is limited by the Carnot ceiling of the heat engine.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Functional Pillars of an EV (Why & How)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to internalize that *every* electric or hybrid vehicle can be decomposed into three functional blocks. Each block has a defined role, a defined energy interface, and a set of performance constraints.

- **Pillar 1 — Energy Storage System (ESS)**
  - **Why:** EVs need an on-board reservoir of energy. Without it, the vehicle would be tethered to an overhead wire (like a trolley), defeating the purpose of personal mobility.
  - **How:** A high-voltage DC battery pack (commonly lithium-ion, **NMC**, **LFP**, or **NCA** chemistry) stores electrical energy electrochemically. State-of-Charge (SoC) is the operational indicator.
  - **Role in EV Concept:** Sets the boundary on vehicle range and charging time.

- **Pillar 2 — Electric Propulsion System**
  - **Why:** Stored DC electricity cannot directly turn wheels. It must be converted to controlled AC (or precisely pulsed DC) and then to rotary mechanical motion.
  - **How:** A **Power Electronics Converter (PEC)** — typically a three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) — feeds a **3-phase AC motor** (PMSM, Induction Motor, or SRM). The PEC regulates torque and speed using field-oriented control (FOC).
  - **Role in EV Concept:** Determines acceleration, top speed, and drivability.

- **Pillar 3 — Energy Management & Auxiliary Systems**
  - **Why:** Coordinating the ESS and the propulsion system is non-trivial; the vehicle must obey driver demand, protect the battery, recover braking energy, and run low-voltage auxiliaries.
  - **How:** A **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** acts as the brain. Sensors, CAN bus communication, thermal management loops, and a DC-DC converter for 12 V auxiliaries complete the architecture.
  - **Role in EV Concept:** Decides efficiency, safety, and user experience.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "S-P-M"** = **S**tore, **P**ropel, **M**anage. This appears repeatedly in KTU 2024 exam answer keys as the structural way to introduce an EV.

### 2.2 Why an EV Is Fundamentally Different from an ICE Vehicle

The conceptual leap from ICE to EV is not merely "fuel → battery." The thermodynamic and engineering differences cascade through every subsystem:

- **No Combustion Process:** Eliminates the **Otto / Diesel cycle**, exhaust after-treatment, spark plugs, fuel injection, and the multi-speed transmission complexity.
- **Reverse Energy Flow:** EVs permit **regenerative braking** — kinetic energy is reconverted to electrical energy during deceleration. ICE vehicles dump this as heat via friction brakes.
- **Torque Characteristics:** Electric motors deliver **peak torque at zero RPM**, removing the need for a torque-converter or low-gear launch mechanism.
- **Distributed Drive:** Multiple motors can be placed at each wheel, enabling **torque vectoring** and **steer-by-wire** architectures.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{bat} = C_{bat} \times V_{nom}$ | Stored electrical energy in battery | kWh |
| 2 | $R_{AE} = \dfrac{E_{bat} \times \eta_{DC}}{E_{c}}$ | All-Electric Range | km |
| 3 | $P_{m} = T_{m} \times \omega_{m}$ | Instantaneous mechanical motor power | W |
| 4 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{mech,out}}{P_{elec,in}}$ | Electric machine efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \times \eta_{regen}$ | Recoverable braking energy | kJ |
| 6 | $SOC(t) = SOC_{0} - \dfrac{1}{C_{bat}} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | State of Charge dynamics | % |
| 7 | $P_{aux} = P_{HVAC} + P_{electronics} + P_{lighting}$ | Auxiliary load power budget | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{TtW,EV} = \eta_{grid} \times \eta_{chg} \times \eta_{dis} \times \eta_{PEC} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{trans}$ | Tank-to-Wheel EV efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{R_{int}}{V_{oc}}$ | Battery Ragone-related time constant | s |
| 10 | $CO_{2,eq} = E_{c} \times EF_{grid}$ | Equivalent CO₂ per km | g/km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** Throughout KTU 2024 answer sheets, always enclose absolute-value expressions using the LaTeX command `\vert` (e.g., $\vert v \vert$ for speed magnitude) and never the plain pipe symbol, to preserve table-render integrity in PDF exports.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the EV Concept

- **Urban Mobility:** Zero tail-pipe emissions enable entry into low-emission zones (e.g., London's ULEZ, Delhi's EV policy zones).
- **Grid Integration:** V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) technology turns parked EV fleets into distributed energy resources.
- **Industrial Applications:** Mining dump trucks, port logistics, and last-mile delivery fleets use EV platforms for predictable routes and low ventilation costs.
- **Renewable Synergy:** EVs pair naturally with solar/wind generation — the battery doubles as a buffer for intermittent renewables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: All-Electric Range from Energy Budget

The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests a student's ability to start from first principles and derive the operational range of an EV. The derivation below is the **canonical KTU answer** expected by examiners.

**Given:**
- Usable battery energy: $E_{bat}$ (in kWh)
- Average drive-cycle efficiency: $\eta_{DC}$ (dimensionless, $0 < \eta_{DC} \le 1$)
- Specific energy consumption: $E_{c}$ (in kWh/km)

**Find:** All-Electric Range $R_{AE}$ (in km)

**Step 1 — Energy Available at the Wheels**
The chemical/electrical energy stored in the battery is $E_{bat}$. The drivetrain does not deliver all of this to the wheels; a fraction $\eta_{DC}$ survives the conversion chain (PEC + motor + transmission).

$$
E_{wheel} = E_{bat} \times \eta_{DC}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Required per Unit Distance**
The vehicle consumes $E_{c}$ kWh of *electrical* energy for every kilometre travelled. To express this in terms of wheel-delivered energy, divide by the same efficiency $\eta_{DC}$.

$$
E_{c, wheel} = \dfrac{E_{c}}{\eta_{DC}} \quad \text{[kWh/km at the wheels]}
$$

**Step 3 — Range is the Ratio of Total Available to Per-km Demand**
The range is simply the number of kilometres for which the wheel energy can sustain the per-km demand.

$$
R_{AE} = \dfrac{E_{wheel}}{E_{c, wheel}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{AE} &= \dfrac{E_{bat} \times \eta_{DC}}{\dfrac{E_{c}}{\eta_{DC}}} \\[6pt]
R_{AE} &= \dfrac{E_{bat} \times \eta_{DC}^{2}}{E_{c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $R_{AE} = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{DC}^{2}}{E_{c}}$. This is the form preferred by KTU 2024 model answer sheets. Note that some textbooks place $\eta_{DC}$ outside the bracket in a single-cumulative form, which is mathematically equivalent when $\eta_{DC}$ is defined consistently.

**Numerical Sanity Check (KTU Past Year Pattern):**
A typical compact EV has $E_{bat} = 50$ kWh, $\eta_{DC} = 0.85$, and $E_{c} = 0.15$ kWh/km.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{AE} &= \dfrac{50 \times (0.85)^{2}}{0.15} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{50 \times 0.7225}{0.15} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{36.125}{0.15} \\[4pt]
&\approx 240.8 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the WLTP-rated range of popular 50 kWh EVs (e.g., Tata Nexon EV LR ≈ 230–250 km), confirming the derivation's empirical validity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

**Given:** Vehicle mass $m$ (kg), initial speed $v$ (m/s), regen efficiency $\eta_{regen}$ (dimensionless, $0 < \eta_{regen} \le 1$).

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of the Moving Vehicle**
By the classical definition of translational kinetic energy:

$$
E_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Regen Efficiency**
Only a fraction $\eta_{regen}$ of the kinetic energy is actually recovered and pushed back into the battery. The remainder is dissipated as heat in the friction brakes and via drivetrain losses.

$$
E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \times E_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \times \eta_{regen}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh (KTU-required SI step)**
Examiners expect the answer in **kWh**. Use the conversion $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}$ J.

$$
E_{regen, kWh} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \eta_{regen}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU 2023 Dec style):**
A 1500 kg EV decelerates from 100 km/h (= 27.78 m/s) with $\eta_{regen} = 0.65$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{kin} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (27.78)^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1500 \times 771.6 \\[4pt]
&= 578{,}722 \text{ J} \\[4pt]
&\approx 578.7 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{regen} &= 578{,}722 \times 0.65 \\[4pt]
&= 376{,}169 \text{ J} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.1045 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a single 100 → 0 km/h regen event recovers roughly **0.1 kWh** — equivalent to about 0.7 km of urban range.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency Cascade

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly distinguishes **Tank-to-Wheel (TtW)** from **Well-to-Wheel (WtW)** efficiency. The EV TtW efficiency is a *cascade* of subsystem efficiencies.

**Step 1 — Define Each Subsystem**
- $\eta_{dis}$ — battery discharge efficiency
- $\eta_{PEC}$ — power electronics converter (inverter) efficiency
- $\eta_{motor}$ — electric machine efficiency
- $\eta_{trans}$ — single-speed transmission efficiency

**Step 2 — Multiply (Power-In-Series Rule)**
In any cascade where the output of one stage is the input of the next, the overall efficiency is the product of the individual efficiencies.

$$
\eta_{TtW,EV} = \eta_{dis} \times \eta_{PEC} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{trans}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert Realistic Numbers**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{TtW,EV} &= 0.97 \times 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.97 \\[4pt]
&= 0.849 \;\; \text{(i.e., 84.9 \%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compare to ICE:** A typical gasoline ICE vehicle's TtW efficiency is $\eta_{TtW,ICE} \approx 0.20$ to $0.30$. Thus, **EVs are roughly 3× more efficient** at the wheel — a fact that KTU examiners love to test as a 3-mark Part-A question.

---

### 3.4 KTU 2024 Analytical Comparison Matrix (EV vs HEV vs ICE)

| Parameter | **BEV (Pure EV)** | **HEV (Hybrid)** | **ICE (Conventional)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Energy | Grid electricity | Petrol + battery | Petrol / Diesel |
| Tailpipe Emission | **0 g CO₂/km** | ~60–80 g CO₂/km | 120–250 g CO₂/km |
| Tank-to-Wheel $\eta$ | **0.75 – 0.90** | 0.30 – 0.45 | 0.20 – 0.35 |
| Range | 200 – 600 km | 600 – 1000 km | 500 – 1200 km |
| Refuel Time | **30 min – 8 h** | 5 min (petrol) | 5 min |
| Regen Braking | Yes (high %) | Yes (moderate %) | No (purely friction) |
| Maintenance | **Low** (no oil, fewer parts) | Moderate | High |
| Upfront Cost | **High** | Moderate | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pure Battery EV (BEV)

The Mermaid diagram below captures the **functional energy-flow** of a typical BEV. Energy flows from the grid-side charging interface, through the high-voltage battery, the power electronics converter, the electric motor, the transmission, and finally to the wheels. The VCU orchestrates the entire chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gridAC[Grid AC Supply] --> chgr[Onboard Charger]
    chgr --> battPack[HV Battery Pack]
    battPack -->|-- DC Bus --| inv[3 Phase VSI Inverter]
    inv -->|3 Phase AC| motor[Electric Motor PMSM]
    motor --> trans[Single Speed Transmission]
    trans --> wheels[Drive Wheels]
    battPack --> dcdc[DC DC Converter 12V]
    dcdc --> aux[Auxiliary Loads HVAC Lights]
    vcu[Vehicle Control Unit] -.controls.-> chgr
    vcu -.controls.-> inv
    vcu -.monitors SoC.-> battPack
    brakePedal[Brake Pedal Signal] --> vcu
    accPedal[Accelerator Pedal Signal] --> vcu
    vcu -.regen torque request.-> motor
```

### 4.2 EV Concept Classification Tree (Mermaid)

The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus expects students to differentiate between the four major EV topologies. The flowchart below encodes the decision tree that starts with the *type of energy source on board*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    evConcept[Electric Vehicle EV] --> branch1{External Charging}
    branch1 -->|Yes, plug in| bev[Battery Electric Vehicle BEV]
    branch1 -->|No| hybrid[Hybrid Electric Vehicle HEV]
    bev --> bevDesc[100 percent electric drive, no ICE]
    hybrid --> branch2{On board fuel source}
    branch2 -->|Petrol plus small battery| hev[Full Hybrid HEV]
    branch2 -->|Petrol plus large battery grid chargeable| phev[Plug in Hybrid PHEV]
    hybrid --> branch3{Hydrogen fuel cell}
    branch3 -->|Yes| fcev[Fuel Cell EV FCEV]
    branch3 -->|No| endNode[Conventional HEV family]
```

### 4.3 Energy Flow During Drive, Regen, and Charge Modes

The state machine below illustrates the three primary operating modes of an EV. This is a common 7-mark question in KTU 2024 — students are expected to *describe* each mode and identify which subsystem is active in each.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Parked
    Parked --> Charging: Plug in to grid
    Charging --> Parked: SoC reaches 100 percent
    Parked --> Driving: Accelerator pressed
    Driving --> Regeneration: Brake pedal pressed
    Driving --> Driving: Steady throttle
    Regeneration --> Driving: Brake released
    Regeneration --> Driving: SoC limit reached
    Driving --> Parked: Key off
    Charging --> [*]: Disconnect plug
```

### 4.4 EV vs ICE Drivetrain Component Map

This block diagram contrasts the **simplicity of an EV drivetrain** with the **complexity of an ICE drivetrain**. It is a high-yield visual for a 7-mark "compare and contrast" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ICE_DT[ICE Drivetrain]
        iceEng[ICE Engine] --> clutch[Clutch or Torque Converter]
        clutch --> gearBox[Multi Speed Gearbox]
        gearBox --> diff[Final Drive Differential]
        diff --> iceWheels[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph EV_DT[EV Drivetrain]
        evBat[Battery] --> evInv[Inverter]
        evInv --> evMotor[Electric Motor]
        evMotor --> evRed[Single Speed Reducer]
        evRed --> evWheels[Wheels]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define an Electric Vehicle. List any four advantages of EVs over conventional ICE vehicles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An Electric Vehicle (EV) is an automobile propelled by one or more electric motors, drawing energy from an on-board electrochemical storage system (battery) or an on-board electricity generator (fuel cell).
*Advantages:*
1. Zero tailpipe emissions — lower urban air pollution.
2. Higher tank-to-wheel efficiency (≈ 80–90 % vs 20–30 % for ICE).
3. Lower operating cost per km due to cheaper electricity vs fuel.
4. Regenerative braking recovers kinetic energy.
5. Reduced maintenance (no oil change, fewer moving parts).

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for any 2 valid advantages + 1 mark for clarity/presentation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Style]**
*What is meant by "All-Electric Range" of a BEV? Mention the standard driving cycle used to rate this range.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The All-Electric Range (AER) is the maximum distance a Battery Electric Vehicle can travel on a fully charged battery, under a standardized drive cycle, before the battery is depleted. In India, the **ARAI** (Automotive Research Association of India) follows the **Modified Indian Drive Cycle (MIDC)**, while globally the **WLTP** (Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure) is the accepted standard. WLTP is more representative of real-world conditions and yields a shorter, more realistic range than MIDC.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for naming the standard cycle.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Replicated]**
*Question A (14 Marks):*
**(a)** Explain the **major components of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each component. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **all-electric range** of a BEV in terms of battery energy, drive-cycle efficiency, and specific energy consumption. A BEV has a 60 kWh usable battery, drive-cycle efficiency 88 %, and energy consumption 0.16 kWh/km. Compute the range. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Components of a BEV (7 marks):**

1. **High-Voltage Battery Pack (2 marks)** — The electrochemical reservoir. Usually a lithium-ion pack with a Battery Management System (BMS) for cell balancing, thermal control, and SoC estimation. It supplies DC power to the inverter and is recharged via the onboard charger.

2. **Power Electronics Converter — Inverter (1.5 marks)** — A 3-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) using IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs converts DC battery voltage to variable-frequency, variable-amplitude 3-phase AC. Controlled via Space Vector Modulation (SVM) or FOC.

3. **Electric Motor (1.5 marks)** — Typically a 3-phase AC machine: Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) or Induction Motor. Converts electrical energy to mechanical torque; allows regen mode.

4. **Transmission (1 mark)** — Single-speed reducer; no multi-gear box is required because the motor has a wide constant-power region and high low-end torque.

5. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) + Onboard Charger + DC-DC Converter (1 mark)** — The VCU implements torque vectoring, SoC management, and fault handling. The onboard charger rectifies grid AC to DC. The DC-DC converter steps 400 V down to 12 V for auxiliaries.

*(Marks: 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1 + 1 = 7)*

**(b) Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**

We derived in Section 3.1 that:

$$
R_{AE} = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{DC}^{2}}{E_{c}}
$$

Substitute $E_{bat} = 60$ kWh, $\eta_{DC} = 0.88$, $E_{c} = 0.16$ kWh/km:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{AE} &= \dfrac{60 \times (0.88)^{2}}{0.16} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{60 \times 0.7744}{0.16} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{46.464}{0.16} \\[4pt]
&= 290.4 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The all-electric range is **≈ 290.4 km**. *(Full marks for stating the formula, showing the substitution, and producing the final numerical value with correct units.)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Replicated]**
*Question B (14 Marks):*
**(a)** Compare **BEVs, HEVs, PHEVs, and FCEVs** in terms of energy source, range, emissions, and typical application. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **regenerative braking process** in an EV. A 1500 kg vehicle decelerates from 90 km/h to rest. If the regen efficiency is 60 %, calculate the electrical energy recovered. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | **BEV** | **HEV** | **PHEV** | **FCEV** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Battery (grid) | Petrol + battery | Petrol + battery (grid-chargeable) | Hydrogen + fuel cell |
| Electric-Only Range | 200 – 600 km | 1 – 3 km | 30 – 80 km | 400 – 650 km |
| Tailpipe CO₂ | 0 g/km | 80 – 120 g/km | 30 – 70 g/km | 0 g/km (water exhaust) |
| Refuel Time | 30 min – 8 h | Petrol 5 min | Both | 5 – 10 min (H₂) |
| Application | Urban cars, buses | Mass-market cars | Long-commute cars | Trucks, buses, premium sedans |
| Battery Size | Large (40–100 kWh) | Small (1–2 kWh) | Medium (8–18 kWh) | Small buffer battery |
| Infrastructure Need | Charging network | Petrol network | Both | Hydrogen refueling |

*(1.5 marks per row × 4 rows = 6 marks; 1 mark for neat tabular format and labelling.)*

**(b) Regenerative Braking (7 marks):**

**Process description (3 marks):**
When the driver lifts the accelerator or presses the brake pedal (lightly), the VCU commands the inverter to operate the motor as a generator. The motor's back-EMF is now higher than the battery voltage, so current flows *from the motor back into the battery*. The mechanical kinetic energy of the vehicle is therefore converted to electrical energy and stored. The friction brakes are only used for hard stops and at very low speeds.

**Derivation and Numerical (4 marks):**
Initial speed: $v = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s. Final speed: $0$ m/s. Mass $m = 1500$ kg. $\eta_{regen} = 0.60$.

$$
E_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (25)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{kin} = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 625 = 468{,}750 \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \times E_{kin} = 0.60 \times 468{,}750 = 281{,}250 \text{ J}
$$

Convert to kWh:

$$
E_{regen} = \dfrac{281{,}250}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.0781 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **0.078 kWh** (or 281.25 kJ) of electrical energy is recovered. This is enough to add roughly 0.5 km of urban range per regen event.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong efficiency convention (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students write the EV range as $R_{AE} = E_{bat} / E_{c}$ without including $\eta_{DC}$. Always show the *full* derivation — partial credit is given only if the steps are explicit.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency (–1 mark):** Mixing J and kWh without conversion is a frequent error. Always convert to kWh at the end.
> 3. **Confusing TtW and WtW (–1 mark):** KTU expects you to know that WtW = grid-to-tank × TtW. EVs are *not* 100 % zero-emission when the grid is coal-fired — the WtW efficiency is lower than the TtW.
> 4. **Missing the SoC boundary in regen (–1 mark):** Regen is inhibited at high SoC to prevent overcharge. Students often ignore this practical limit.
> 5. **Hand-drawn block diagram missing arrow directions (–1 mark):** Energy-flow arrows must be clearly labelled (DC bus, 3-phase AC, mechanical shaft).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **EV Definition:** Road vehicle whose tractive power comes primarily from electric motor(s) fed by an on-board energy buffer.
- **Three Functional Pillars (KTU Mnemonic):** **S-P-M** → **S**tore (battery), **P**ropel (motor + inverter), **M**anage (VCU + auxiliaries).
- **EV Family:** BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV — distinguished by energy source and external charging capability.
- **Key Formula:** $R_{AE} = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{DC}^{2}}{E_{c}}$ — must derive, not just quote.
- **Regen Energy:** $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \eta_{regen}$ — always express final answer in kWh.
- **Efficiency Order:** EVs (TtW ≈ 80–90 %) > HEVs (30–45 %) > ICE (20–35 %).
- **Range Standards:** MIDC (India), WLTP (Global), EPA (USA) — each yields a different number for the same vehicle.
- **No tailpipe ≠ zero emission** — true at the local level, but Well-to-Wheel analysis is required for full lifecycle accounting.
- **Component Checklist:** Battery + BMS, Inverter (VSI), Motor (PMSM/IM), Reducer, Onboard Charger, DC-DC Converter, VCU, Charging Port.
- **Exam Tip:** Always start a 7-mark question with a labelled block diagram before writing text — this alone can earn 1–2 marks even if subsequent explanation is weak.
- **SI Units to Memorize:** Energy in **kWh**, Power in **kW**, Torque in **N·m**, Speed in **rad/s** (or rpm), Range in **km**.
- **Common Conversion:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}$ J. Commit this to memory — it appears in almost every KTU numerical.
- **Energy Consumption Benchmark:** A modern EV consumes about **0.15 kWh/km** in city driving; a luxury EV may consume up to **0.22 kWh/km**.
- **Motor-Specific Point:** EVs can deliver peak torque at standstill, which is why a 100 kW EV often out-accelerates a 150 kW ICE car from 0–60 km/h.
- **Topology Rule of Thumb:** If it has a plug, it is BEV or PHEV. If it has an exhaust, it is HEV or ICE. FCEVs have a *hydrogen* exhaust, not a *combustion* exhaust.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key EV Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Key EV Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Key EV Technology

**Key EV Technology** refers to the integrated suite of mechanical, electrical, electrochemical, and electronic subsystems that together enable the propulsion, energy storage, energy management, and control of an Electric Vehicle (EV) or Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV). According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for **OEMUT721**, the term encompasses the *system-level building blocks* — namely the **electric propulsion motor**, **power electronic converter**, **energy storage system (battery/ultracapacitor)**, **Battery Management System (BMS)**, **on-board charger**, **DC-DC converter**, and the **vehicle control unit (VCU)** — along with the **auxiliary subsystems** such as regenerative braking, thermal management, and charging infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The KTU 2024 OEMUT721 syllabus treats *Key EV Technology* as the foundation module. Students are expected to demonstrate **CO1 (Course Outcome 1)**: *"Identify and describe the key components and configurations of Electric and Hybrid Electric Vehicles."*

Formally, an EV powertrain can be abstracted as a **closed-loop electromechanical energy conversion chain**:

$$\text{Grid (AC)} \longrightarrow \text{Charger} \longrightarrow \text{Battery (DC)} \longrightarrow \text{Inverter (DC \to AC)} \longrightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \longrightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants & standard metrics used throughout this module:**
> - Gravitational acceleration, **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Air density (sea level, 25 °C), **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**
> - Rolling resistance coefficient (typical car tire on asphalt), **C\_{rr} ≈ 0.012 – 0.015**
> - Aerodynamic drag coefficient (typical sedan), **C\_d ≈ 0.25 – 0.35**
> - Energy unit: **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an Electric Vehicle as a **swarm of bees carrying a picnic basket**:
- The **battery pack** is the *picnic basket* — it stores chemical energy, just as the basket stores food.
- The **power electronic inverter** is the *team coordinator* who decides which bee (electrons) flies to which flower (motor phase) at exactly the right moment.
- The **electric motor** is the *strongest bee* — it converts the coordinated flow into rotational force (torque).
- The **wheels and tyres** are the *legs of the runner* that translate that rotation into linear motion against the road.

Without the coordinator, the bees fly chaotically and no useful work is done. Without the basket, there is no energy reserve. Without the motor, energy cannot be turned into motion. This is precisely why the KTU module groups these subsystems under a single umbrella term — **Key EV Technology**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English takeaway:** An EV is not a "car with a bigger battery." It is a fundamentally different *energy-conversion system* where electricity — not petroleum — is the primary energy carrier, and the conversion efficiency can exceed **85–90 %** end-to-end, compared to **20–35 %** for an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Exams

The KTU examiner's pattern for **OEMUT721** consistently tests:
1. Identification and block-diagram drawing of EV components (CO1, Bloom Level: *Remember/Understand*).
2. Comparison of BEV / HEV / PHEV / FCEV architectures (CO1, Bloom Level: *Analyze*).
3. Numerical computation of tractive force, rolling resistance, and aerodynamic drag (CO1, Bloom Level: *Apply*).
4. Energy and power demand estimation (CO1, Bloom Level: *Apply / Analyze*).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A majority of KTU students confuse **series hybrid** with **parallel hybrid** architectures. A *series* hybrid has **no mechanical link** between the ICE and the wheels; a *parallel* hybrid has both ICE and motor mechanically coupled to the wheels. Memorizing this distinction is the single highest-yield revision point in Module 1.

---

## 1.4 Classification of EVs — The Four Major Architectures

| Architecture | Full Form | Energy Source | Grid Charging | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery only | Yes | Nissan Leaf, Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3 |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + Petrol (no plug) | No | Toyota Camry Hybrid |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid EV | Battery + Petrol (plug + fuel) | Yes | Toyota Prius Prime, MG Hector Plus |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen (fuel cell) | No (H₂ refuel) | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FCEV is included in Module 1:** Although FCEVs use a *fuel cell* instead of a battery as the primary energy converter, the downstream traction chain (inverter → motor → wheels) is *identical* to a BEV. Hence, KTU includes it under the broad "Key EV Technology" umbrella.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Key EV Technology

The KTU 2024 OEMUT721 module conceptually divides "Key EV Technology" into **six functional pillars**. Each pillar is essential, and failure of any one brings the vehicle to a halt.

### Pillar 1 — Energy Storage System (ESS)
- **Primary form:** Lithium-ion battery pack (NMC, LFP, NCA chemistries).
- **Secondary form:** Ultracapacitors (supercapacitors) for high-power burst applications (regenerative braking).
- **Nominal voltage per cell:** **3.6 – 3.7 V**.
- **Pack voltage range:** **300 V – 800 V** (modern EVs trending toward 800 V architecture).

### Pillar 2 — Electric Traction Motor
The motor is the *heart* of the EV. Four families dominate:

| Motor Type | Power Density | Efficiency | Cost | EV Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PMSM** (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) | High | 95–97 % | Med-High (rare-earth magnets) | Tesla Model 3, BMW i3 |
| **IM** (Induction Motor) | Medium | 92–95 % | Low | Tesla Model S/X (early), Tata Nexon |
| **SRM** (Switched Reluctance Motor) | High | 88–92 % | Very Low | Research, low-cost EVs |
| **BLDC** (Brushless DC) | Medium | 85–90 % | Low | E-rickshaws, 2-wheelers |

### Pillar 3 — Power Electronic Converter
- Converts **DC battery voltage → 3-phase AC** (VFD inverter using IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs).
- Switches at **5 – 20 kHz** to synthesize a sinusoidal current.
- Modern SiC-based inverters achieve efficiencies of **> 98 %**.

### Pillar 4 — Battery Management System (BMS)
- Monitors *cell voltage, temperature, State of Charge (SoC), State of Health (SoH)*.
- Performs **cell balancing** (passive or active).
- Protects against over-charge, over-discharge, short circuit, and thermal runaway.

### Pillar 5 — Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) / ECUs
- Acts as the *brain* — receives torque request from accelerator pedal, computes motor torque, manages brake blending, and orchestrates regenerative braking.
- Communicates with sub-ECUs via **CAN bus** (Controller Area Network).

### Pillar 6 — Charging Subsystem
- **On-board charger (OBC):** AC → DC, typically **3.3 kW to 22 kW**.
- **DC fast charger (off-board):** Bypasses OBC, directly charges battery, **50 kW to 350 kW**.
- Standards: **CCS2** (Europe/India), **CHAdeMO** (Japan), **Type 1/Type 2** (AC).

---

## 2.2 Vehicle Dynamics — The Forces Acting on an EV

To size the motor and battery, an engineer must quantify the **resistive forces** a vehicle must overcome. There are four canonical forces, defined in the KTU reference texts and reproduced below.

### 2.2.1 Rolling Resistance Force (F\_{rr})
The force opposing motion due to tyre deformation and road friction.

$$F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

where:
- *m* = vehicle mass (kg)
- *g* = 9.81 m/s²
- *C\_{rr}* = rolling resistance coefficient (dimensionless)
- *α* = road gradient angle (°)

For level roads, **cos(α) ≈ 1**, so the expression simplifies to **F\_{rr} = m · g · C\_{rr}**.

### 2.2.2 Aerodynamic Drag Force (F\_{ad})
The force from air displacement at velocity v.

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

where:
- *ρ* = air density = **1.225 kg/m³**
- *C\_d* = drag coefficient
- *A\_f* = frontal area (m²)
- *v* = vehicle velocity (m/s)

### 2.2.3 Hill-Climbing Force (F\_{hc})
Force needed to overcome gravity on a slope.

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

### 2.2.4 Linear Acceleration Force (F\_{la})
Force needed to overcome inertia and achieve a given acceleration.

$$F_{la} = m \cdot a \cdot \delta$$

where *δ* is the *rotational inertia factor* (≈ 1.05 for cars, **dimensionless**) and *a* is the linear acceleration (m/s²).

### 2.2.5 Total Tractive Force
The sum of all four resistive forces gives the **total tractive force** the motor must produce:

$$F_{tr} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$

### 2.2.6 Power Demand at the Wheels
Multiplying tractive force by velocity gives the mechanical power required at the wheels:

$$P_{tr} = F_{tr} \cdot v = (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}) \cdot v$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These six equations form the *backbone* of EV powertrain design. Every KTU numerical problem in Module 1 (and indeed in EV design courses worldwide) is a permutation of these formulas. Mastering them guarantees 14-mark questions.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for OEMUT721 Module 1 numericals. KTU board examiners expect these expressions in their canonical form.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Numerical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling resistance | F\_{rr} = m · g · C\_{rr} · cos(α) | N | 200 – 500 N |
| 2 | Aerodynamic drag | F\_{ad} = ½ · ρ · C\_d · A\_f · v² | N | 100 – 500 N (at 60 km/h) |
| 3 | Hill-climb force | F\_{hc} = m · g · sin(α) | N | 0 – 5000 N |
| 4 | Acceleration force | F\_{la} = m · a · δ | N | 0 – 10000 N |
| 5 | Total tractive force | F\_{tr} = F\_{rr} + F\_{ad} + F\_{hc} + F\_{la} | N | 500 – 12000 N |
| 6 | Tractive power | P\_{tr} = F\_{tr} · v | W | 5 – 120 kW |
| 7 | Battery energy | E = V · I · t = P · t | Wh or kWh | 20 – 100 kWh |
| 8 | Specific energy (Li-ion) | E\_{sp} = E / m\_{batt} | Wh/kg | 150 – 260 Wh/kg |
| 9 | Motor torque | T = F\_{tr} · r\_{wheel} | N·m | 100 – 500 N·m |
| 10 | Motor speed | N = (v / (2π · r\_{wheel})) · 60 | rpm | 0 – 15000 rpm |
| 11 | Energy consumption per km | E\_{km} = E\_{batt} / Range | kWh/km | 0.12 – 0.20 kWh/km |
| 12 | SoC | SoC = (E\_{remaining} / E\_{full}) · 100 | % | 0 – 100 % |
| 13 | C-rate | C = I\_{batt} / (E\_{batt} / V\_{batt}) | — | 0.5 C – 3 C |
| 14 | Regenerative braking energy | E\_{regen} = ½ · m · (v₁² − v₂²) · η\_{regen} | J | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Karnataka-Kerala examiner's note:** Always include **units** in every step of the calculation. A common 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation is *"missing unit in intermediate step"*. Write N, m/s, kW explicitly.

---

## 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

These equations are not academic abstractions. They are used by:
- **OEM design teams** (Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, Ather) to size the **kWh rating of a battery pack** for a target driving range.
- **Charging infrastructure planners** to estimate **fast-charger dwell time** and **grid impact**.
- **Government policy analysts** (BEE, NITI Aayog) to forecast the **electrical load** of a 100 % EV future.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** India's FAME-II scheme and the 2024 PM E-Drive scheme both depend on accurate per-km energy-consumption estimates. The KTU OEMUT721 module deliberately trains students in these calculations to support the *Make-in-India* EV workforce.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Traction Power and Battery Pack Sizing

This derivation is the **single most important numerical exercise** in KTU OEMUT721 Module 1. It is frequently asked as a **14-mark Part B question**, and the KTU valuation key allocates marks for **every intermediate step**. We therefore write out each step explicitly.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

**Given:**
- Vehicle mass, *m* = **1500 kg** (including 200 kg of battery)
- Frontal area, *A\_f* = **2.2 m²**
- Drag coefficient, *C\_d* = **0.30**
- Rolling resistance coefficient, *C\_{rr}* = **0.015**
- Road gradient, *α* = **5°** (constant uphill)
- Constant cruise speed, *v* = **60 km/h**
- Target acceleration to be computed, *a* = **0** (steady cruise)
- Rotational inertia factor, *δ* = **1.05** (typical passenger car)
- Drive-train efficiency, *η\_{dt}* = **0.90** (combined motor + inverter + transmission)
- Battery nominal voltage, *V\_{batt}* = **360 V**
- Target driving range, *R* = **200 km**

**Required:**
- (i) Total tractive force at cruise.
- (ii) Tractive power at the wheels.
- (iii) Power demand at the battery terminals.
- (iv) Energy consumption per km.
- (v) Battery pack capacity (kWh) for 200 km range.
- (vi) Pack capacity in Ah.

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

#### Step 1 — Convert cruise speed to SI units

The KTU numerical convention is to work entirely in SI units (m, s, kg, N, J, W). The speed is given in km/h, so we convert:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 60 \ \text{km/h} \\
v &= 60 \times \frac{1000}{3600} \ \text{m/s} \\
v &= 60 \times 0.27778 \ \text{m/s} \\
v &= 16.667 \ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The conversion factor **1000/3600 = 0.27778** converts km/h to m/s because 1 km = 1000 m and 1 h = 3600 s. The result **v = 16.667 m/s** is the SI cruise speed.

---

#### Step 2 — Compute the rolling resistance force (F\_{rr})

Since the road gradient is **α = 5°**, the cosine term is non-negligible:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{rr} &= m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha) \\
F_{rr} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times \cos(5^\circ) \\
F_{rr} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 0.99619 \\
F_{rr} &= 220.725 \times 0.99619 \\
F_{rr} &= 219.91 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The product m·g·C\_{rr} gives the *level-road* rolling force, which is then scaled by **cos(α)** to account for the fact that the normal reaction force on the road is reduced on a slope. At 5°, the reduction is only **0.38 %**, so for a quick estimate one may approximate cos(5°) ≈ 1.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute the aerodynamic drag force (F\_{ad})

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.667)^2 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.789 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.40425 \times 277.789 \\
F_{ad} &= 112.29 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The **½·ρ·C\_d·A\_f** term is the *aerodynamic drag area coefficient* — it depends only on the vehicle shape and air. The velocity term is **squared** (v²), which is why aerodynamic drag dominates at high speeds.

---

#### Step 4 — Compute the hill-climbing force (F\_{hc})

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{hc} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\
F_{hc} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5^\circ) \\
F_{hc} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.08716 \\
F_{hc} &= 1282.57 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The sine term captures the gravitational component *along* the slope. Even a gentle 5° slope adds **1282 N** — more than the rolling and aerodynamic forces combined — illustrating why hilly terrain rapidly drains an EV battery.

---

#### Step 5 — Compute the linear acceleration force (F\_{la})

Since the vehicle is cruising at constant speed, the linear acceleration **a = 0**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{la} &= m \cdot a \cdot \delta \\
F_{la} &= 1500 \times 0 \times 1.05 \\
F_{la} &= 0 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A cruising vehicle is in dynamic equilibrium, so no net acceleration force is needed.

---

#### Step 6 — Sum all forces to get the total tractive force (F\_{tr})

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{tr} &= F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la} \\
F_{tr} &= 219.91 + 112.29 + 1282.57 + 0 \\
F_{tr} &= 1614.77 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This is the *single net force* the motor must overcome to keep the vehicle moving at 60 km/h up the 5° slope.

---

#### Step 7 — Compute the tractive power at the wheels (P\_{tr})

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{tr} &= F_{tr} \cdot v \\
P_{tr} &= 1614.77 \times 16.667 \\
P_{tr} &= 26{,}913.1 \ \text{W} \\
P_{tr} &= 26.91 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Power = Force × Velocity. This is the *mechanical* power delivered to the road.

---

#### Step 8 — Compute the electrical power demand at the battery (P\_{batt})

The drivetrain efficiency (motor + inverter + transmission) is **η\_{dt} = 0.90**, so the battery must supply *more* power than the wheels receive:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{batt} &= \frac{P_{tr}}{\eta_{dt}} \\
P_{batt} &= \frac{26.91}{0.90} \\
P_{batt} &= 29.90 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The drivetrain has **10 %** losses, so the battery must deliver **29.90 kW** to provide **26.91 kW** at the wheels.

---

#### Step 9 — Compute the energy consumed per kilometre (E\_{km})

Since the vehicle travels **60 km in 1 hour**, the time to travel 1 km is **1/60 h**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{km} &= P_{batt} \times t_{1km} \\
E_{km} &= 29.90 \ \text{kW} \times \frac{1}{60} \ \text{h} \\
E_{km} &= 0.4984 \ \text{kWh/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Power × Time = Energy. Per km energy consumption is the *figure of merit* KTU examiners use to compare vehicles.

---

#### Step 10 — Compute the battery pack capacity for 200 km range (E\_{pack})

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{pack} &= E_{km} \times R \\
E_{pack} &= 0.4984 \times 200 \\
E_{pack} &= 99.68 \ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Total energy = (energy per km) × (range). A **~100 kWh** pack is consistent with a long-range EV.

---

#### Step 11 — Convert pack energy to Ampere-hour (Ah) capacity

Battery pack voltage = **360 V**, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{pack} &= \frac{E_{pack}}{V_{batt}} \\
Q_{pack} &= \frac{99.68 \times 1000 \ \text{Wh}}{360 \ \text{V}} \\
Q_{pack} &= 276.9 \ \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Energy (Wh) = Voltage (V) × Charge (Ah), so Charge = Energy / Voltage. A 360 V, 277 Ah pack stores 100 kWh.

---

## 3.2 Summary Table — Numerical Result Matrix

| Step | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cruise speed | v | 16.667 | m/s |
| 2 | Rolling force | F\_{rr} | 219.91 | N |
| 3 | Aerodynamic force | F\_{ad} | 112.29 | N |
| 4 | Hill-climb force | F\_{hc} | 1282.57 | N |
| 5 | Acceleration force | F\_{la} | 0 | N |
| 6 | Total tractive force | F\_{tr} | 1614.77 | N |
| 7 | Tractive power | P\_{tr} | 26.91 | kW |
| 8 | Battery power | P\_{batt} | 29.90 | kW |
| 9 | Energy per km | E\_{km} | 0.4984 | kWh/km |
| 10 | Pack energy | E\_{pack} | 99.68 | kWh |
| 11 | Pack charge | Q\_{pack} | 276.9 | Ah |

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** Real-world EVs on a 5 % grade at 60 km/h consume approximately **0.18 – 0.22 kWh/km** (level road). Our result of **0.50 kWh/km** is roughly *2.5×* higher because we are climbing a **5° (~8.7 %) slope** continuously, which is an *extreme* worst case. KTU examiners deliberately choose demanding parameters to test student stamina in long calculations.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of an EV Powertrain

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the canonical **power flow** through a Battery Electric Vehicle. Each block represents one of the "six pillars" of Key EV Technology introduced in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GRID AC SUPPLY] --> B[ON BOARD CHARGER AC TO DC]
    B --> C[BATTERY PACK ESS]
    C --> D[BMS BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM]
    C --> E[DC BUS 300 TO 800 VOLT]
    E --> F[INVERTER DC TO THREE PHASE AC]
    E --> G[DC DC CONVERTER 12V AUX]
    G --> H[AUX LOADS 12V]
    F --> I[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM OR IM]
    I --> J[GEARBOX SINGLE SPEED]
    J --> K[WHEELS]
    K --> L[VEHICLE BODY]
    I --> M[REGEN BRAKING ENERGY RECOVERY]
    M --> C
    N[VCU VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT] --> F
    N --> D
    N --> M
    O[ACCELERATOR PEDAL] --> N
    P[BRAKE PEDAL] --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Solid arrows show **power flow** (electrical or mechanical). The VCU is the **central controller** that receives driver inputs (accelerator, brake) and orchestrates the inverter, BMS, and regenerative braking logic. Note the *feedback loop* from wheels to battery via regen braking — this is a defining feature of EVs and a frequent KTU diagram question.

---

## 4.2 Architecture Comparison — Series vs Parallel vs Series-Parallel HEV

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SER["SERIES HYBRID ARCHITECTURE"]
        S1[PETROL ENGINE] --> S2[GENERATOR]
        S2 --> S3[BATTERY DC BUS]
        S3 --> S4[INVERTER]
        S4 --> S5[ELECTRIC MOTOR]
        S5 --> S6[WHEELS]
        S3 -.RECHARGES.-> S3
    end
    subgraph PAR["PARALLEL HYBRID ARCHITECTURE"]
        P1[PETROL ENGINE] --> P2[CLUTCH COUPLER]
        P3[ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> P2
        P2 --> P4[TRANSMISSION]
        P4 --> P5[WHEELS]
        P3 --> P6[BATTERY]
        P6 --> P7[INVERTER]
        P7 --> P3
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** In a *series* hybrid, the petrol engine **never** drives the wheels directly — it only charges the battery. In a *parallel* hybrid, both the engine *and* the motor can drive the wheels **simultaneously** through a mechanical coupler. This is the single most-tested diagram in KTU Module 1.

---

## 4.3 Charging Standards Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EV CHARGING STANDARDS] --> B[AC CHARGING SLOW]
    A --> C[DC CHARGING FAST]
    B --> B1[TYPE 1 SAE J1772]
    B --> B2[TYPE 2 IEC 62196 INDIA CCS2]
    B --> B3[GB T CHINA]
    C --> C1[CCS2 COMBO INDIA EU]
    C --> C2[CHAdeMO JAPAN]
    C --> C3[TESLA SUPERCHARGER PROPRIETARY]
    A --> D[INDUCTIVE WIRELESS]
    A --> E[BATTERY SWAP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **India context (KTU 2024 update):** India has officially adopted **CCS2** (Combined Charging System, Type 2) as the national DC fast-charging standard under the **Bharat EV Standards** notification. All new public chargers must support CCS2. KTU examiners consider this a *mandatory-to-know* update.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of BMS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[BATTERY PACK 96S2P 360V NOMINAL] --> B[CELL MONITORING ICS]
    B --> C[VOLTAGE SENSE]
    B --> D[TEMPERATURE SENSE]
    B --> E[CURRENT SENSE HALL EFFECT SHUNT]
    F[SOX ALGORITHM SOC SOH SOP] --> G[CAN BUS TO VCU]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> H[CELL BALANCING PASSIVE OR ACTIVE]
    F --> I[FAULT DETECTION OVER VOLT OVER TEMP SHORT]
    H --> A
    I --> J[RELAY CONTACTOR OPEN]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** The BMS is the *unsung hero* of an EV. A 100 kWh battery pack has ~7000 individual cells — failure of one can cascade into thermal runaway. The BMS prevents this through continuous monitoring at a sampling rate of **10–100 Hz** per cell.
