The motor must supply additional power to compensate for gearbox, differential, and inverter losses:

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{overall}}$$

### Numerical Continuation:

Using $F_t = 6900$ N and $v = 16.67$ m/s, and $\eta_{overall} = 0.90$:

$$P_{wheel} = 6900 \times 16.67 = 115,023 \text{ W} \approx 115 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{115,023}{0.90} \approx 127.8 \text{ kW} \approx 171 \text{ HP}$$

This matches the typical motor rating of **~125–150 kW** used in mid-size EVs like the Tata Nexon EV Max.

---

## 3.5 Gear Ratio Selection Criterion (Analytical Matrix)

The **optimal fixed gear ratio** is chosen to satisfy **two conflicting constraints**:

**Constraint 1 — Launch Torque Requirement:**
The motor must deliver sufficient wheel torque to overcome the maximum tractive resistance from rest.

$$i_g \geq \dfrac{F_{t,\,max} \cdot r_{wheel}}{T_{motor,\,peak} \cdot \eta_{gear}}$$

**Constraint 2 — Top Speed Requirement:**
The motor's maximum RPM must be high enough to achieve the desired top speed.

$$i_g \leq \dfrac{N_{motor,\,max} \cdot 2\pi \cdot r_{wheel}}{v_{max} \cdot 60}$$

The **optimal ratio** is the geometric mean that satisfies both:

$$\boxed{i_{g,\,opt} = \sqrt{i_{g,\,min} \cdot i_{g,\,max}}}$$

This is a powerful design tool for KTU design problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fixed vs. Variable Gearing

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete powertrain signal and energy flow for both configurations, highlighting where the **TCU (Transmission Control Unit)** intervenes in variable gearing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT["BATTERY PACK"] --> INV["POWER INVERTER"]
    INV --> MTR["ELECTRIC MOTOR PMSM"]
    
    MTR --> GEARBOX{"GEARBOX TYPE"}
    
    GEARBOX -->|Fixed Single-Speed| FIX["FIXED REDUCTION GEAR ig 8 to 12 ratio"]
    GEARBOX -->|Variable Multi-Speed| VAR["MULTI-SPEED TRANSMISSION TCU CONTROLLED"]
    
    FIX --> DIFF["DIFFERENTIAL"]
    VAR --> DIFF
    
    DIFF --> SHAFT["HALF SHAFTS"]
    SHAFT --> WHL["DRIVE WHEELS"]
    
    WHL --> ROAD["ROAD SURFACE"]
    
    VCU["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU"] -.->|Torque Demand Signal| INV
    VCU -.->|Shift Command if Variable| VAR
    
    TCU["TRANSMISSION CONTROL UNIT TCU"] -.->|Shift Logic| VAR
    
    ACC["ACCELERATOR PEDAL"] --> VCU
    BRK["BRAKE PEDAL"] --> VCU
    
    style BAT fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style INV fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style MTR fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style FIX fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style VAR fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style DIFF fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    style WHL fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,color:#000
    style ROAD fill:#a1887f,stroke:#3e2723,color:#fff
    style VCU fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style TCU fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    style ACC fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#212121,color:#000
    style BRK fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#212121,color:#000
```

> **How to read this diagram:**  
> - The **solid arrows** show the **mechanical / electrical power flow** from battery → wheels.  
> - The **dashed arrows** show the **control signal flow** from driver inputs (accelerator, brake) through the VCU to the inverter and gearbox.  
> - Notice that the **TCU only appears in the variable-gearing branch**, because fixed gearing has no shifting decision to make.

---

## 4.2 Gear Reduction Internal Schematic — Fixed Gearing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIN["PINION GEAR Small Z1 20 Teeth"] -->|Mesh| CRN["CROWN GEAR Large Z2 190 Teeth"]
    
    PIN --> SHAFT_M["MOTOR SHAFT"]
    CRN --> SHAFT_W["WHEEL SHAFT"]
    
    SHAFT_W --> WHLD["WHEEL Differential"]
    
    MTR2["MOTOR INPUT 15000 RPM 250 Nm"] --> SHAFT_M
    SHAFT_W --> OUT["WHEEL OUTPUT 1500 RPM 2375 Nm"]
    
    style PIN fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style CRN fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style SHAFT_M fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style SHAFT_W fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style MTR2 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style WHLD fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
```

> **Reading this diagram:** The small pinion (20 teeth) meshes with the large crown (190 teeth). The gear ratio is $i_g = 190/20 = 9.5$. A 15000 RPM motor becomes a 1580 RPM wheel. The torque scales up by the same ratio (minus efficiency losses).

---

## 4.3 Variable Gearing Shift Logic State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEUTRAL
    NEUTRAL --> GEAR1: Accelerator Pressed
    GEAR1 --> GEAR2: Speed > 60 km/h AND Torque Demand Low
    GEAR2 --> GEAR1: Speed < 40 km/h OR Torque Demand High
    GEAR1 --> NEUTRAL: Park Selected
    GEAR2 --> NEUTRAL: Park Selected
    GEAR1 --> GEAR1: Speed Stable
    GEAR2 --> GEAR2: Speed Stable Highway Cruise
    
    note right of GEAR1: Low Ratio i1 ~ 8 to 10. High Torque. Launch Mode.
    note right of GEAR2: High Ratio i2 ~ 4 to 5. Low RPM. Efficiency Mode.
```

> **Engineering takeaway:** The TCU uses **hysteresis** (different shift-up and shift-down speeds) to prevent constant "gear hunting" when the vehicle cruises near the shift point — a critical design consideration for drivability.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Torque-Speed Map (Fixed vs. Variable)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MOTOR["ELECTRIC MOTOR CHARACTERISTIC"]
        CT["CONSTANT TORQUE REGION 0 to N_base ~3000 RPM"]
        CP["CONSTANT POWER REGION N_base to N_max ~15000 RPM"]
    end
    
    subgraph FIXED["FIXED GEAR OPERATION"]
        F1["Vehicle Launch at Low Speed Motor in CT Region High Wheel Torque"]
        F2["Highway Cruise Motor in CP Region Moderate Wheel Torque"]
    end
    
    subgraph VAR["VARIABLE GEAR OPERATION"]
        V1["Gear 1 Launch Motor Stays Near CT Peak"]
        V2["Gear 2 Cruise Motor Stays in High-Efficiency CP Sweet Spot"]
    end
    
    MOTOR --> FIXED
    MOTOR --> VAR
    
    style CT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style CP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style V1 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style V2 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
```

> **The key insight:** In **variable gearing**, the TCU can keep the motor in its **highest-efficiency sweet spot** across a much wider vehicle speed range than a fixed gear can. This is why multi-speed transmissions can extend EV range by **5–8%** under real-world driving cycles.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between fixed gearing and variable gearing configurations in Electric Vehicles. Mention one EV model example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Gearing | Variable Gearing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A single, constant gear ratio between motor and wheels | A transmission that can switch between 2 or more gear ratios |
| **Complexity** | Simple, compact, lightweight | Complex, heavier, requires TCU |
| **Efficiency** | ~95–97% (single mesh) | ~90–93% (multiple meshes) |
| **Example EV** | **Tata Nexon EV** (single-speed reducer) | **Porsche Taycan** (2-speed rear transmission) |
| **Use Case** | Urban commuter EVs | Performance / heavy-duty EVs |

*(Award 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Why is a single-speed transmission sufficient for most modern Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), unlike conventional ICE vehicles?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A single-speed transmission is sufficient for BEVs because of the following inherent characteristics of electric motors:

1. **High Starting Torque:** Electric motors deliver **100% of rated torque from 0 RPM**, eliminating the need for a low "first gear" to launch the vehicle. *(1 mark)*

2. **Wide Constant-Power Region:** Modern PMSM and Induction motors maintain **rated power across a wide RPM range** (e.g., 3000–15,000 RPM), enabling high top speeds without gear changes. *(1 mark)*

3. **No Need for Torque Converter / Clutch:** Unlike ICE engines, electric motors do not stall at low speeds, so no complex clutching mechanism is required. *(1 mark)*

Thus, a simple single-speed reducer suffices, improving efficiency and reducing cost.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Q: An Electric Vehicle uses a single-speed fixed gear reducer to drive the rear wheels.**
- Motor peak torque: $T_m = 320$ Nm
- Motor maximum speed: $N_{max} = 14{,}000$ RPM
- Gear ratio: $i_g = 9.2$
- Gearbox efficiency: $\eta_g = 0.96$
- Wheel radius: $r = 0.32$ m
- Vehicle mass: $m = 1700$ kg
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $f_r = 0.013$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.28$
- Frontal area: $A_f = 2.3$ m²
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**(a)** Derive the relationship between motor torque and wheel torque in a fixed-gear EV. Calculate the maximum tractive force available at the wheels. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the maximum vehicle speed achievable and the total tractive force required to cruise at **80 km/h** on a level road. Comment on whether the motor has sufficient torque for this cruise. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation and Maximum Tractive Force (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The torque at the motor shaft is transmitted through the gear reducer. For a fixed gear ratio $i_g$ and gearbox efficiency $\eta_g$, applying conservation of power:

$$P_{wheel} = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel} = \eta_g \cdot T_m \cdot \omega_{motor}$$

Since $i_g = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{\omega_{wheel}}$, substituting:

$$T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel} = \eta_g \cdot T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \omega_{wheel}$$

Cancelling $\omega_{wheel}$:

$$\boxed{T_{wheel} = \eta_g \cdot T_m \cdot i_g}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
$$T_{wheel} = 0.96 \times 320 \times 9.2 = 2826.24 \text{ Nm}$$

**Maximum Tractive Force (2 marks):**
$$F_{t,max} = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{r} = \dfrac{2826.24}{0.32} = 8832 \text{ N}$$

**Answer:** The maximum tractive force available at the wheels is **8832 N**.

---

**Part (b) — Maximum Speed and Cruise Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Maximum Speed Calculation (3 marks):**
At maximum motor RPM, the corresponding wheel angular velocity is:

$$\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{i_g} = \dfrac{N_{max} \cdot 2\pi}{60 \cdot i_g}$$

$$\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{14000 \times 2\pi}{60 \times 9.2} = \dfrac{87964.6}{552} = 159.36 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to linear vehicle speed:

$$v_{max} = \omega_{wheel} \times r = 159.36 \times 0.32 = 50.99 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{max} = 50.99 \times \dfrac{3600}{1000} = 183.6 \text{ km/h}$$

**Answer:** The maximum vehicle speed is **183.6 km/h**.

**Cruise Tractive Force at 80 km/h (3 marks):**
Convert 80 km/h to m/s: $v = 22.22$ m/s.

Rolling resistance force:
$$F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot f_r = 1700 \times 9.81 \times 0.013 = 216.8 \text{ N}$$

Aerodynamic drag force:
$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times \rho \times C_d \times A_f \times v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times (22.22)^2$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times 493.83 = 194.7 \text{ N}$$

Total tractive force at constant cruise (level road, no acceleration):
$$F_{t,\,cruise} = 216.8 + 194.7 = 411.5 \text{ N}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** This is only **4.7%** of the maximum available tractive force (8832 N), so the motor has ample torque headroom for cruising at 80 km/h. The EV can cruise comfortably.

---

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Compare the architecture of fixed and variable gearing systems in Electric Vehicles. With the help of a block diagram, explain how the Transmission Control Unit (TCU) operates in a 2-speed EV transmission. Discuss the energy efficiency benefits.**

**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a fixed-gear EV powertrain and a variable-gear EV powertrain. Compare them in terms of complexity, efficiency, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operating logic of a 2-speed EV transmission with the TCU. Show how the shift point is determined and discuss the resulting efficiency gains using a numerical example. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Block Diagrams and Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Fixed-Gear EV Powertrain Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[ Battery ] → [ Inverter ] → [ Electric Motor ] → [ Fixed Reduction Gear ig ] → [ Differential ] → [ Half-Shafts ] → [ Wheels ]
```

The fixed-gear EV powertrain consists of:

- **Battery Pack:** Stores DC electrical energy (typically 400V or 800V architecture).
- **Power Inverter:** Converts DC to 3-phase AC for the motor.
- **Electric Motor:** PMSM or Induction motor producing torque across a wide RPM range.
- **Fixed Reduction Gear:** A single-stage helical or planetary reducer with constant ratio (typically 8:1 to 12:1).
- **Differential:** Allows speed difference between left and right wheels during cornering.
- **Half-Shafts and Wheels:** Deliver power to the road surface.

**Variable-Gear EV Powertrain Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[ Battery ] → [ Inverter ] → [ Electric Motor ] → [ 2-Speed Transmission ] → [ Differential ] → [ Wheels ]
                                            ↑
                                      [ TCU Control ]
```

The variable-gear system adds a **2-speed transmission** between the motor and differential, controlled by a **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)** that monitors vehicle speed, accelerator pedal position, and motor torque demand.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Fixed Gearing | Variable Gearing |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | Low | High (TCU + actuators) |
| Drivetrain Efficiency | 95–97% | 90–93% |
| Mass | ~ 30 kg | ~ 70 kg |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Best Application | City commuters, mid-size EVs | Performance EVs, heavy trucks |

---

**Part (b) — 2-Speed TCU Operation and Efficiency Gains (7 Marks):**

**Operating Logic (3 marks):**
A 2-speed EV transmission has two gear ratios:

- **Gear 1 (Low):** $i_1 \approx 8:1$ to $10:1$ — used during launch and low-speed driving. Maximizes torque multiplication at low wheel speeds.
- **Gear 2 (High):** $i_2 \approx 4:1$ to $5:1$ — used during high-speed cruising. Reduces motor RPM, lowering motor losses and improving range.

**Shift Point Determination (2 marks):**
The TCU determines the shift point based on:

- **Vehicle Speed ($v$):** Shift from Gear 1 → Gear 2 when $v > v_{shift}$ (typically 60–80 km/h).
- **Accelerator Pedal Position ($\theta$):** At high pedal positions (aggressive acceleration), the TCU delays upshifts to keep the motor in its high-torque region.
- **Motor Torque Demand:** If high torque is demanded in Gear 2, TCU may downshift back to Gear 1.
- **Hysteresis:** Different upshift and downshift speeds (e.g., upshift at 70 km/h, downshift at 55 km/h) prevent oscillation.

**Numerical Efficiency Example (2 marks):**
Consider an EV cruising at **120 km/h** on a level road:

- **With Fixed Gear** ($i_g = 9.5$): Motor RPM = $\dfrac{120/3.6 \times 60 \times 9.5}{2\pi \times 0.32} = 12{,}100$ RPM. Motor efficiency at this RPM (beyond optimal range) ≈ **92%**.

- **With 2-Speed Gear** (Gear 2, $i_2 = 5$): Motor RPM = $\dfrac{120/3.6 \times 60 \times 5}{2\pi \times 0.32} = 6370$ RPM. Motor efficiency at this RPM (optimal region) ≈ **96%**.

**Efficiency Gain:** $\Delta\eta = 96\% - 92\% = 4\%$ improvement in motor efficiency. Across a full driving cycle, this translates to a **5–8% increase in vehicle range**, demonstrating the engineering value of variable gearing for long-distance EVs.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always convert km/h to m/s before applying $v = \omega \cdot r$. Marks are deducted for unit mismatches.  
> 2. **Missing the Efficiency Factor:** When calculating wheel torque, students often write $T_{wheel} = T_m \cdot i_g$ but forget $\eta_g$. This costs 1–2 marks.  
> 3. **Confusing Gear Ratio Direction:** Remember $i_g = N_{motor}/N_{wheel}$, so motor RPM is **higher** than wheel RPM.  
> 4. **Neglecting Aerodynamic Drag:** At speeds above 80 km/h, $F_{aero}$ dominates. Forgetting it leads to incorrect power calculations.  
> 5. **Ignoring Rolling Resistance on Slopes:** On level ground, $F_{gradient} = 0$. On a slope, both $mg\sin\alpha$ and $mgf_r\cos\alpha$ must be included.  
> 6. **Skipping the Derivation in Part (a):** KTU examiners require the **derivation steps**, not just the final formula. A direct formula without derivation loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

✅ **EV Powertrain Layout:** Battery → Inverter → Motor → Gearbox → Differential → Wheels.

✅ **Fixed Gearing = Single-Speed Reducer:** Used in **90% of modern EVs** (Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Hyundai Kona, Tesla Model 3). Ratio: 8:1 to 12:1.

✅ **Variable Gearing = Multi-Speed TCU-Controlled:** Used in **performance and heavy-duty EVs** (Porsche Taycan 2-speed rear, Tesla Semi). Improves range by 5–8%.

✅ **Core Equations to Memorize:**
   - $T_{wheel} = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$
   - $\omega_m = i_g \cdot v / r_w$
   - $N_m = (i_g \cdot v \cdot 60) / (2\pi \cdot r_w)$
   - $F_t = mgf_r\cos\alpha + mg\sin\alpha + 0.5\rho C_d A_f v^2 + ma$

✅ **Why EVs Don't Need Multi-Speed Boxes (Mostly):** Motors deliver **peak torque at 0 RPM** and have a **wide constant-power region**, eliminating the need for gear changes.

✅ **Trade-off in Gear Ratio Selection:**
   - **Higher $i_g$:** Better launch + hill climb, but lower top speed.
   - **Lower $i_g$:** Higher top speed, but poor low-end torque.
   - **Optimal ratio:** Geometric mean of launch and top-speed requirements.

✅ **Differential is Mandatory** in non-independent-motor EVs to allow left-right wheel speed difference during cornering.

✅ **Typical Gearbox Efficiency:** Single-stage helical = **96–97%**; Multi-stage = **90–93%**.

✅ **Tractive Force Order of Importance at Different Speeds:**
   - **0–40 km/h:** Acceleration resistance dominates.
   - **40–80 km/h:** Rolling resistance dominates.
   - **> 80 km/h:** Aerodynamic drag dominates (quadratic with speed).

✅ **Industry Standards:** Most EV OEMs choose fixed gearing because **simplicity wins** for mass-market cost-effectiveness; variable gearing is reserved for **performance differentiation**.

✅ **Key KTU Exam Keywords to Use:** "Gear reduction," "tractive force," "motor constant-torque region," "TCU shift logic," "wheel slip condition $v = \omega r$."

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: EV System Configuration: Fixed & Variable Gearing*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single & Multiple Motor Drive
In-Wheel Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** In-Wheel Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In-Wheel Drive (IWD)**, also academically referred to as an **In-Wheel Motor (IWM)**, **Hub Motor**, or **Wheel-Hub Drive**, is an advanced electric propulsion architecture in which one or more **electric traction motors are directly integrated inside the wheel hub assembly**, thereby completely eliminating the conventional mechanical drivetrain — including the transmission, propeller shaft, universal joints, and differential.

In this configuration, the electric motor's **rotor** is directly coupled to the wheel rim, and the **stator** is anchored to the steering knuckle or axle stub. Power flow becomes a direct, single-stage electromechanical conversion: **Battery → Power Inverter → Hub Motor → Wheel → Road Surface**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 syllabus**, In-Wheel Drives are studied as a key *distributed-drive* topology that enables true **torque vectoring**, **regenerative braking per-wheel**, and a flat vehicle floor (since no central drivetrain tunnel is required).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **ceiling fan mounted directly on a bicycle wheel** instead of on a ceiling pole. The motor (fan) sits *inside* the wheel itself, so the very thing spinning the blades is the same thing touching the ground. There is no chain, no belt, no gear rod — just the motor turning the wheel directly.

Now replace the ceiling fan with a high-torque electric motor, and you have an **In-Wheel Drive**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world mental hook:** Think of how a **powered wheelchair** works — each large rear wheel has its own motor hidden inside the hub. A car with in-wheel drives simply applies the same idea at higher power and speed levels.

### 1.3 Two Principal Architectural Families

| Family | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct-Drive In-Wheel Motor** | Outer-rotor motor; no reduction gears. Wheel speed = Motor speed. | Low-speed, high-torque EVs (e.g., solar race cars) |
| **Geared (Reduction) In-Wheel Motor** | Inner-rotor PMSM/BLDC with a planetary gear stage (typically ratio **1:4 to 1:10**). | Passenger EVs (Protean, Elaphe, Nidec designs) |

### 1.4 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics for In-Wheel Drive Systems (KTU Board-Relevant):**
> - **Peak Torque per Wheel:** **150 Nm – 700 Nm**
> - **Peak Power per Wheel:** **30 kW – 100 kW**
> - **Nominal Wheel Speed:** **800 rpm – 1500 rpm**
> - **Outer Rotor Diameter:** **300 mm – 420 mm**
> - **Motor Mass (each):** **15 kg – 35 kg**
> - **Efficiency (Geared type):** **92 % – 96 %**
> - **Unsprung Mass Penalty Threshold:** **< 15 % increase over baseline**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The working of an In-Wheel Drive can be decomposed into the following structured chain:

1. **Energy Source Activation:** The high-voltage traction battery (typically **400 V** or **800 V** architecture) supplies DC power.
2. **Power Electronics Stage:** A **3-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** converts DC → variable-frequency AC using IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs.
3. **Electromagnetic Conversion:** The stator winding generates a **rotating magnetic field** whose synchronous speed is given by:
4. **Mechanical Coupling:** The rotor — being either the wheel rim itself (outer-rotor) or coupled to it via a planetary gearbox — drives the tire directly.
5. **Road Loading:** Vertical load $(F_z)$, rolling resistance, and aerodynamic drag oppose motion; torque control at the wheel compensates in real time.
6. **Torque Vectoring Logic:** Each wheel receives an independent torque command $(T_{w,i})$ from the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU).

### 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a VSI?** AC motors need variable frequency to vary speed (since $n_s \propto f$).
- **Why outer-rotor topology for direct-drive?** Larger air-gap radius $\Rightarrow$ higher torque per ampere.
- **Why planetary gears in geared-type?** Electric motors have a narrow **constant-power region**; gear reduction widens the **effective torque band** at the wheel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{p}$ | Synchronous speed of motor (rpm); $f$ = frequency (Hz), $p$ = pole count |
| 2 | $T_{wheel} = T_{motor} \cdot \eta_g \cdot i_g$ | Wheel torque after gear reduction; $i_g$ = gear ratio, $\eta_g$ = gear efficiency |
| 3 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{r_{wheel}}$ | Tractive force at tire contact patch |
| 4 | $P_{wheel} = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ | Mechanical power delivered to road |
| 5 | $a_{max} = \dfrac{F_t - F_{rr} - \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2}{m_{vehicle}}$ | Vehicle longitudinal acceleration |
| 6 | $E_{regen} = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \eta_{regen} \cdot F_{brake} \, dv$ | Recovered braking energy (Joules) |
| 7 | $\omega_{motor} = \omega_{wheel} \cdot i_g$ | Motor speed = wheel speed × gear ratio |
| 8 | $T_{per-wheel,max} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot r_{wheel}}{4}$ | Maximum torque per wheel limited by tire friction (4WD) |
| 9 | $\eta_{system} = \eta_{inverter} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear}$ | Total drivetrain efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.92**) |
| 10 | $J_{eq,unsprung} = J_{rotor} + \dfrac{m_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}^2}{2}$ | Equivalent unsprung inertia seen by suspension |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula **(8)** is the *most important KTU board formula* for in-wheel drives because it determines the upper bound of torque each motor can command before tire slip wastes energy. Examiners frequently test whether students understand that **more motor torque ≠ more acceleration** beyond the friction limit.

## 2.4 Comparative Positioning vs. Conventional Drivetrain

| Parameter | Central Motor + Transmission | In-Wheel Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Drivetrain Components | Many (transmission, propshaft, differential) | **None** |
| Mass Distribution | Concentrated | Distributed per wheel |
| Torque Vectoring | Limited (brake-based) | **Independent per wheel (true TV)** |
| Regenerative Braking | Partial | **100 % available at all wheels** |
| Unsprung Mass | Low | **High (major drawback)** |
| Cabin Packaging | Compromised | **Flat floor possible** |
| Maintenance | High | **Low (sealed units)** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed Relationship

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between motor frequency, number of poles, and rotational speed.

**Starting Point:** A rotating magnetic field completes one mechanical revolution in $p/2$ electrical cycles, where $p$ is the number of magnetic poles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time for 1 electrical cycle} &= \frac{1}{f} \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Number of electrical cycles per revolution} &= \frac{p}{2} \\
\text{Time for 1 mechanical revolution} &= \frac{p}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{f} = \frac{p}{2f} \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Speed } n_s \text{ (rev/sec)} &= \frac{2f}{p} \\
\text{Speed } n_s \text{ (rpm)} &= \frac{2f}{p} \cdot 60 = \frac{120 f}{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
n_s = \frac{120 \, f}{p} \;\; [\text{rpm}]
$$

> **Conversion logic:** We multiplied revolutions/second by 60 to convert to revolutions/minute (rpm), the standard unit used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wheel Torque from Motor Torque via Planetary Gearbox

**Given:** Inner-rotor PMSM with rated torque $T_{motor}$, planetary gear ratio $i_g = 4.5$, gear efficiency $\eta_g = 0.94$.

**Step 1 — Apply gear ratio (torque amplification):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= T_{motor} \cdot i_g \\
T_{out} &= T_{motor} \cdot 4.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply gear efficiency losses:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= T_{out} \cdot \eta_g \\
T_{wheel} &= T_{motor} \cdot 4.5 \cdot 0.94 \\
T_{wheel} &= 4.23 \cdot T_{motor}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to tractive force using tire radius $r = 0.32 \text{ m}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \frac{T_{wheel}}{r} \\
F_t &= \frac{4.23 \cdot T_{motor}}{0.32} \\
F_t &= 13.22 \cdot T_{motor} \;\; [\text{N, when } T_{motor} \text{ in Nm}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Gear reduction trades speed for torque. If $T_{motor} = 80 \text{ Nm}$, then $F_t \approx 1058 \text{ N}$ per wheel — enough to propel a 1500 kg vehicle with controlled acceleration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Acceleration Limit Due to Tire Friction

**Given:** Total vehicle mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$, 4-wheel drive, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.85$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, ignore drag for simplicity.

**Step 1 — Maximum total tractive force:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,max} &= \mu \cdot m \cdot g \\
F_{t,max} &= 0.85 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \\
F_{t,max} &= 12{,}507.75 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Distribute equally to 4 wheels:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,per\,wheel} &= \frac{F_{t,max}}{4} \\
F_{t,per\,wheel} &= \frac{12{,}507.75}{4} = 3126.94 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum acceleration:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{max} &= \frac{F_{t,max}}{m} = \mu \cdot g \\
a_{max} &= 0.85 \cdot 9.81 \\
a_{max} &= 8.34 \text{ m/s}^2 \;\; (\approx 0.85g)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The acceleration ceiling is *independent* of vehicle mass — only tire-road friction and gravity govern it. This is why high-performance EVs cannot exceed ~1g even with 1000 hp.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — In-Wheel Motor Type Selection

| Motor Type | Power Density | Efficiency | Cost | KTU Board Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLDC (Outer Rotor)** | High | 90–94 % | Low | ★★★★ |
| **PMSM (Radial Flux)** | Very High | 94–97 % | High | ★★★★★ |
| **Axial Flux PMSM** | Highest | 95–97 % | Very High | ★★★★ |
| **Switched Reluctance (SRM)** | Medium | 85–90 % | Low | ★★★ |
| **Induction Motor** | Medium | 90–93 % | Medium | ★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)** is the de-facto industry choice (used by Protean Electric, Elaphe, Nidec, Tesla in Plaid prototypes) and the **most frequently examined motor type** in KTU question papers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — In-Wheel Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BAT[Traction Battery 400V DC] --> INV[3-Phase VSI Inverter]
    INV --> CTRL[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    CTRL --> M1[Front Left IWM]
    CTRL --> M2[Front Right IWM]
    CTRL --> M3[Rear Left IWM]
    CTRL --> M4[Rear Right IWM]
    M1 --> W1[Front Left Wheel Tire]
    M2 --> W2[Front Right Wheel Tire]
    M3 --> W3[Rear Left Wheel Tire]
    M4 --> W4[Rear Right Wheel Tire]
    W1 --> RD[Road Surface]
    W2 --> RD
    W3 --> RD
    W4 --> RD
    RD -. Regenerative Energy .-> M1
    RD -. Regenerative Energy .-> M2
    RD -. Regenerative Energy .-> M3
    RD -. Regenerative Energy .-> M4
    M1 -. Inverted Power Flow .-> INV
    M2 -. Inverted Power Flow .-> INV
    M3 -. Inverted Power Flow .-> INV
    M4 -. Inverted Power Flow .-> INV
    INV -. DC Bus .-> BAT
    CTRL <--> SEN[Wheel Speed IMU Steering Sensors]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Geared In-Wheel Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outer Rim Tire Mounting Flange] --> B[Outer Rotor with Permanent Magnets]
    B --> C[Air Gap Electromagnetic Field]
    C --> D[Inner Stator with 3 Phase Windings]
    D --> E[Stator Housing Fixed to Steering Knuckle]
    E --> F[Planetary Gear Reduction Stage]
    F --> G[Output Carrier to Wheel Hub]
    G --> H[Wheel Bearings]
    H --> I[Brake Disc Integrated]
    I --> J[Vehicle Suspension Mounting Points]
```

## 4.3 Control Flow Diagram — Per-Wheel Torque Vectoring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Driver Throttle Pedal Input] --> VCU[VCU Master Controller]
    VCU --> SPLIT[Torque Demand Splitter]
    SPLIT --> TW1[Target Torque Wheel 1]
    SPLIT --> TW2[Target Torque Wheel 2]
    SPLIT --> TW3[Target Torque Wheel 3]
    SPLIT --> TW4[Target Torque Wheel 4]
    TW1 --> FOC1[Field Oriented Controller Wheel 1]
    TW2 --> FOC2[Field Oriented Controller Wheel 2]
    TW3 --> FOC3[Field Oriented Controller Wheel 3]
    TW4 --> FOC4[Field Oriented Controller Wheel 4]
    FOC1 --> PWM1[PWM Inverter Wheel 1]
    FOC2 --> PWM2[PWM Inverter Wheel 2]
    FOC3 --> PWM3[PWM Inverter Wheel 3]
    FOC4 --> PWM4[PWM Inverter Wheel 4]
    PWM1 --> MTR1[In Wheel Motor 1]
    PWM2 --> MTR2[In Wheel Motor 2]
    PWM3 --> MTR3[In Wheel Motor 3]
    PWM4 --> MTR4[In Wheel Motor 4]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Insight:** Notice in the diagrams above how the IWM architecture enables a **closed-loop per-wheel control loop** that is mathematically impossible in conventional drivetrains. Each FOC block runs at ~10 kHz, giving sub-millisecond torque response — this is what enables true yaw control without mechanical differentials.

## 4.4 Power Flow State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> Driving: Throttle Pressed
    Driving --> Regeneration: Brake Pedal
    Regeneration --> Driving: Throttle Released
    Driving --> Standby: Zero Demand
    Regeneration --> Standby: Vehicle Stopped
    Driving --> Fault: Overcurrent or Overtemp
    Regeneration --> Fault: Overcurrent or Overtemp
    Fault --> Standby: Reset Command
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

> *Define In-Wheel Drive. Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **In-Wheel Drive (IWD)** is an electric vehicle propulsion architecture where the traction motor is integrated directly inside the wheel hub, eliminating the transmission, driveshaft, and differential.

**Advantages (any two, 1 mark each):**
1. **Independent per-wheel torque control** enables true torque vectoring and improved handling.
2. **Higher drivetrain efficiency** due to absence of mechanical losses from transmission and differential.
3. **Improved interior packaging** with flat floor and more cabin space.

**Disadvantage (1 mark):**
- Increased **unsprung mass** adversely affects ride comfort and suspension performance.

---

### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> *Compare Geared and Direct-Drive In-Wheel Motors in any four aspects.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

| Aspect | Geared IWM | Direct-Drive IWM |
|---|---|---|
| Gear Stage | Planetary reduction present | No gear, direct coupling |
| Torque Capability | High (geared-up) | Lower per motor mass |
| Efficiency | 92–96 % | 88–92 % (no gear losses but higher copper losses) |
| Noise / NVH | Higher (gear mesh) | Quieter |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only definitions without mentioning **numerical trade-offs, unsprung-mass physics, or regen-braking per-wheel capability**. Marks are deducted when the answer stays purely descriptive. Always include **at least one formula**, **one comparative point**, and **one real-world example** in every 14-mark answer.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of an **In-Wheel Hub Motor** used in electric vehicles. Include a block diagram and discuss the role of the VCU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **maximum tractive force** achievable by a 4-wheel in-wheel-drive EV of mass 1500 kg on dry asphalt ($\mu = 0.9$). If each motor delivers 250 Nm at the wheel and wheel radius is 0.33 m, compute whether the motors are torque-limited or friction-limited. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Outer rotor carries permanent magnets (NdFeB) bonded to the wheel rim.
- Inner stator holds 3-phase distributed windings.
- Stator housing is bolted to the steering knuckle (non-rotating).
- Planetary gear stage couples rotor to wheel hub (in geared variants).
- Integrated brake disc, wheel bearing, and tire mounting flange.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. Battery DC → VSI inverts to 3-phase AC.
2. Stator winding produces rotating magnetic field at synchronous speed $n_s = 120f/p$.
3. Permanent magnets on rotor lock to this field and rotate with it.
4. Torque is transmitted through planetary gears (if present) to the wheel.
5. The VCU controls inverter switching via FOC/SVPWM at 10–20 kHz.

**Role of VCU (1 mark):**
- Computes torque demand per wheel using throttle, brake, steering angle, yaw rate, and wheel-speed sensors.
- Sends torque commands to each motor inverter enabling torque vectoring.

**Diagram (already covered in Section 4.2 above):** Refer to the geared IWM cross-sectional schematic. (Mention if drawn in exam.)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Friction-limited maximum tractive force (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,max} &= \mu \cdot m \cdot g \\
&= 0.9 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \\
&= 13{,}243.5 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Motor-limited tractive force (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,motor} &= \frac{4 \cdot T_{wheel}}{r} \\
&= \frac{4 \cdot 250}{0.33} \\
&= 3030.3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison & Conclusion (2 marks):**
$$
F_{t,motor} = 3030.3 \text{ N} \;\; \ll \;\; F_{t,max} = 13{,}243.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Conclusion:** The vehicle is **motor-torque limited**, not friction-limited. To exploit the available grip, the motor torque must be increased to at least $\frac{13{,}243.5 \cdot 0.33}{4} \approx 1093 \text{ Nm per wheel}$, or alternatively, the tire-road friction must be improved.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **torque vectoring control strategy** for a 4-wheel in-wheel-drive electric vehicle. How does it differ from brake-based vectoring in conventional AWD vehicles? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major challenges** in commercializing in-wheel drives, focusing on unsprung mass, thermal management, and electromagnetic compatibility (EMC). Suggest two engineering solutions for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Torque Vectoring Explanation (4 marks):**
- Each wheel has an independent motor controlled by a dedicated FOC inverter.
- VCU computes target yaw moment $M_z$ from steering angle $\delta$ and measured yaw rate $\dot{\psi}$.
- Torque is **differentially applied**: outer wheels in a turn get more torque, inner wheels less, generating a yaw moment **without any brake intervention**.

**Mathematical Basis (2 marks):**
$$
\Delta T = \frac{M_z \cdot r_{wheel} \cdot J}{0.5 \cdot t \cdot \eta}
$$
where $t$ = track width.

**Difference from Brake-Based Vectoring (1 mark):**
- Conventional AWD uses **brake-based** torque vectoring — it slows the inner wheel using friction brakes, which **wastes energy** as heat and causes brake wear.
- IWD achieves the same yaw moment by **positive differential torque**, which is **energy-efficient** and does not use friction brakes.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 (Control Flow Diagram — Per-Wheel Torque Vectoring).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Challenge 1 — Unsprung Mass (2 marks):**
- Adding 20–35 kg of motor per wheel increases unsprung mass by ~25 %.
- This degrades ride quality, tire grip on bumps, and suspension durability.
- **Solutions (1 mark):**
  - Use **axial-flux PMSM** with very thin disc geometry to reduce mass.
  - Incorporate **active suspension** to compensate dynamically.

**Challenge 2 — Thermal Management (2 marks):**
- Motors in confined wheel wells face limited airflow and are exposed to water splash.
- **Solutions (1 mark):**
  - Use **water-glycol jacket cooling** integrated into the stator housing.
  - Apply **oil spray cooling** to rotor magnets and stator end-windings.

**Challenge 3 — EMC (Electromagnetic Compatibility) (1 mark):**
- High-frequency switching near wheel can interfere with wheel-speed ABS sensors and TPMS.
- **Solutions (1 mark):**
  - Use **Faraday-shielded motor housings**.
  - Use **SiC MOSFET inverters** with controlled dv/dt and common-mode chokes.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember Before Every KTU Board Exam on In-Wheel Drives:**

- [x] **Definition:** In-Wheel Drive = motor integrated inside wheel hub; no transmission/differential.
- [x] **Two Types:** Direct-Drive (outer rotor) vs. Geared (planetary reduction).
- [x] **Preferred Motor:** PMSM (especially radial-flux) for highest efficiency & power density.
- [x] **Key Formula:** $n_s = \dfrac{120f}{p}$ — must be memorized.
- [x] **Key Formula:** $T_{wheel} = T_{motor} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$ — derive stepwise in answers.
- [x] **Key Formula:** $a_{max} = \mu \cdot g$ — independent of vehicle mass.
- [x] **Biggest Advantage:** Independent per-wheel torque → **true torque vectoring**.
- [x] **Biggest Disadvantage:** Increased **unsprung mass** hurting ride comfort.
- [x] **Regenerative Braking:** 100 % available at all wheels, not just driven wheels.
- [x] **Tire-Road Friction Limit:** More motor torque does NOT mean more acceleration once $\mu \cdot m \cdot g$ is reached.
- [x] **Industrial Examples:** Protean Electric (Pd18), Elaphe L1500, Nidec, Lordstown Endurance.
- [x] **Diagram to Always Draw:** Either the wheel cross-section OR the per-wheel torque vectoring control block diagram.
- [x] **Examiner's Bias:** Always quantify answers — use numbers, units, and percentages; avoid pure prose.
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to apply gear efficiency $\eta_g$ — this costs 1 full mark typically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EV Parameters: Weight, Size, Force, Energy & Performance Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EV Parameters: Weight, Size, Force, Energy & Performance Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "EV Parameters"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> EV parameters are the standardized set of **physical, mechanical, electrical, and performance descriptors** that completely characterize an Electric (or Hybrid) Vehicle's design envelope, energy budget, and dynamic capability. They govern vehicle sizing, propulsion system matching, battery sizing, and ultimately the customer's **range, performance, and efficiency**.

In KTU board parlance, an EV design problem is *never* about a single number — it is the **simultaneous satisfaction of multiple parameter constraints**:

1. The vehicle must weigh a certain amount (and carry a payload).
2. It must fit within certain dimensions (so it can park, maneuver, and meet legal road limits).
3. It must overcome a set of resistive + inertial forces (rolling, aero, grade, acceleration).
4. It must store enough electrical energy to do so for a target distance.
5. It must accelerate, climb, and cruise at prescribed performance levels.

These five parameter families are **coupled** — changing one almost always forces changes in the others.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of designing an EV like planning a long-distance bicycle trip with a small electric helper motor:**
>
> - **Weight** = the total mass you (and your backpack) must push. More weight → harder to pedal, more battery needed.
> - **Size** = the bike's wheelbase, frontal area, and how "wind-shaped" it is. A tall, broad bike catches more wind.
> - **Forces** = the four resistances you fight every meter: tire friction (rolling), wind (aero), hill climb (grade), and your own speeding-up (inertia).
> - **Energy** = the size of your battery pack (kWh). It determines how far you can go.
> - **Performance** = how fast you can sprint, climb a flyover, or cruise on the highway without the battery dying too early.

If you double your backpack weight, you don't need the *same* battery — you need a **bigger** battery, a **stronger** motor, and the performance (top speed, 0–60) will **drop** unless you upgrade the powertrain. This coupling is the heart of EV parameter studies.

---

### 1.3 The Five Parameter Families at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight — Classification of EV Parameters:**
>
> | # | Parameter Family | What it Describes | Typical Units |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Weight Parameters** | Mass distribution and load-carrying capacity | kg, N (weight force) |
> | 2 | **Size Parameters** | Geometric envelope of the vehicle | m, m² |
> | 3 | **Force Parameters** | Resistive and tractive forces at the tire–road interface | N |
> | 4 | **Energy Parameters** | Battery storage and specific energy | kWh, Wh/kg, Wh/km |
> | 5 | **Performance Parameters** | Dynamic targets like acceleration, grade, top speed, range | s, %, km/h, km |

Each block below is treated as a sub-system of the EV design problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Weight Parameters

A vehicle does **not** have one weight — it has a *family* of weights that define every loading condition the designer must verify.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Weight Definitions (KTU syllabus wording):**
>
> - **Curb Weight ($m_c$ or $W_c$):** Mass of the vehicle *ready to drive*, including battery, coolant, lubricants, but **no passengers or cargo**. This is essentially the "empty EV" weight.
> - **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW, $m_{gv}$ or $W_{gv}$):** Maximum permissible total weight = curb weight + payload + driver + passengers.
> - **Payload ($m_p$ or $W_p$):** Maximum carried load (passengers × assumed mass + cargo).
> - **Kerb Weight per Axle:** Split of curb weight between front and rear axle — critical for steering, braking, and tire load rating.
> - **Mass Distribution Factor ($\rho$):** Fraction of total mass acting on the driven axle(s). For a rear-wheel-drive EV, typically $\rho \approx 0.45$–$0.55$.
> - **Equivalent Inertial Mass ($m_{eq}$):** Effective mass the motor "sees" during acceleration, $m_{eq} = \delta \cdot m_v$, where $\delta$ is the rotational inertia factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotational Inertia Factor ($\delta$):**
> $$\delta = 1 + \frac{J_w}{m_v R_w^2} + \frac{J_m i_g^2 i_o^2 \eta_t}{m_v R_w^2}$$
> For a typical EV, $\delta \approx 1.05$ to $1.10$ (≈ 5–10 % extra inertial load).

**Why this matters:** Every tractive force and every energy number in Sections 2.3 and 2.4 below uses the **loaded (GVW) mass**, not the curb weight. Students who use $m_c$ instead of $m_{gv}$ in a "fully loaded hill climb" calculation lose easy marks.

---

### 2.2 Size Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Size / Geometric Parameters:**
>
> - **Wheelbase ($L$):** Distance between front and rear axle centers (m). Influences high-speed directional stability and weight transfer.
> - **Track Width ($t$):** Distance between the centers of the two wheels on the same axle (m). Affects cornering and rollover risk.
> - **Overall Length, Width, Height ($L_v$, $W_v$, $H_v$):** For legal road compliance and parking.
> - **Frontal Area ($A_f$):** Projected silhouette area of the vehicle as seen from the front, used in aerodynamic drag. Empirically,
>   $$A_f \approx 0.85 \cdot W_v \cdot H_v$$
> - **Ground Clearance:** Lowest point of the chassis above the road — affects underbody airflow and battery placement.
> - **Center of Gravity Height ($h_g$ or $h_{cg}$):** Height of mass center above the road. For EVs, the heavy battery pack **lowers** $h_g$, improving handling.
> - **Wheel Radius ($R_w$):** Tire outer radius. Directly converts tractive force ↔ motor torque via $F_t = T_w / R_w$.

**Intuition:** A taller, wider vehicle has a bigger $A_f$ → more aerodynamic drag → more battery energy needed to cruise at highway speed. This is why sport-EVs are *low and narrow*.

---

### 2.3 Force Parameters — The Four Resistive Forces

This is the **single most important block** in Module 1 and is worth the majority of marks in any EV design numerical.

> [!NOTE]
> **The tractive-force equation — every EV numerical begins here:**
> $$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$$
> where the **motor must supply** a tractive force $F_t$ at the wheels large enough to overcome the sum of:
>
> 1. **Rolling Resistance Force** ($F_{rr}$)
> 2. **Aerodynamic Drag Force** ($F_{ad}$)
> 3. **Hill-Climb (Grade) Force** ($F_{hc}$)
> 4. **Linear (Acceleration) Inertial Force** ($F_{ia}$)

#### 2.3.1 Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$)

Caused by tire hysteresis as it deforms while rolling.

$$F_{rr} = m_v \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$$

where:
- $m_v$ = vehicle mass (kg) — use $m_{gv}$ for worst case
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ (gravitational acceleration)
- $C_{rr}$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless). Typical: **0.012** for EV low-rolling-resistance tires, 0.015 for conventional radials.
- $\theta$ = road slope angle. For small slopes, $\cos\theta \approx 1$.

**Intuition:** Heavier car → tires deform more per revolution → more energy lost as heat → bigger $F_{rr}$. EVs are heavy (battery!), so this term is **significant**.

#### 2.3.2 Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)

Caused by pushing air out of the way as the vehicle moves.

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

where:
- $\rho_a$ = air density, **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15 °C (ISA standard).
- $C_d$ = coefficient of drag (dimensionless). Typical: 0.22–0.28 for modern EVs (Tesla Model 3 ≈ 0.23, Tata Nexon EV ≈ 0.32).
- $A_f$ = frontal area (m²). Typical: 2.0–2.5 m² for a sedan/SUV.
- $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed conversion (very common KTU trap):**
> $$v\ [\text{m/s}] = \frac{V\ [\text{km/h}]}{3.6}$$
> Forgetting this factor-of-3.6 is a guaranteed mark-loser.

**Intuition:** $F_{ad}$ grows with **the square of speed**. Doubling speed quadruples aerodynamic drag — which is why cruising at 80 km/h is dramatically more efficient than 120 km/h on a long highway trip.

#### 2.3.3 Hill-Climb / Grade Force ($F_{hc}$)

The component of vehicle weight pulling it back down a slope.

$$F_{hc} = m_v \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small $\theta$, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \text{grade in decimal}$. So a "30 % grade" ($\sin\theta \approx 0.262$) means $F_{hc} \approx 0.262\,m_v g$.

#### 2.3.4 Linear Inertial Force ($F_{ia}$)

Newton's second law for translation of the vehicle mass.

$$F_{ia} = m_{eq} \cdot a = \delta \cdot m_v \cdot a$$

where $a$ = desired acceleration in m/s², and $\delta$ accounts for rotating masses (wheels, motor rotor).

#### 2.3.5 Putting it all together

$$F_t = m_v g\, C_{rr}\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + m_v g\sin\theta + \delta m_v a$$

The **required motor torque** at the wheel is:

$$T_w = F_t \cdot R_w \quad\Rightarrow\quad T_m = \frac{F_t R_w}{i_g i_o\,\eta_t}$$

where $i_g$ = gear ratio, $i_o$ = final-drive ratio, $\eta_t$ = transmission efficiency.

---

### 2.4 Energy Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Battery / Energy Metrics (KTU syllabus):**
>
> - **Battery Energy Capacity ($E_b$):** Total energy stored, in **kWh**.
>   $$E_b = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot Q_{\text{nom}} \quad \text{(Wh)} \quad \text{or} \quad V \cdot I \cdot t$$
> - **Specific Energy:** Wh per kg of battery (Li-ion ≈ 150–250 Wh/kg).
> - **Energy Density:** Wh per litre (Li-ion ≈ 250–700 Wh/L).
> - **State of Charge (SOC):** Fraction of usable energy currently in the pack, $0 \le \text{SOC} \le 1$.
> - **Depth of Discharge (DOD):** $1 - \text{SOC}$. Most EVs limit DOD to 0.8 to protect cycle life.
> - **Usable Energy:** $E_{\text{usable}} = \text{SOC}_{\max} - \text{SOC}_{\min}$ times rated capacity.

**Energy consumed per unit distance** (a key design output):

$$E_{\text{per\_km}} = \frac{F_t \cdot v \cdot t}{\text{distance}} = \frac{F_t}{1000\,v}\ \text{Wh/km (when }v\text{ in m/s)}$$

Or equivalently, the *specific energy consumption* $e_c$ (Wh/km) — the EV industry's equivalent of "mileage". A modern EV typically achieves **120–180 Wh/km**.

---

### 2.5 Performance Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Standard EV Performance Targets (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Maximum / Top Speed ($V_{\max}$):** Highest sustainable speed on level road. Set by $P_{\max} = F_t(v_{\max})\cdot v_{\max}$.
> 2. **Acceleration Time ($t_{0-V}$, e.g., 0–60 km/h, 0–100 km/h):** Time to reach a target speed from standstill.
> 3. **Gradeability ($G$):** Maximum slope the vehicle can climb **at a given speed** (typically at crawl speed).
> 4. **Range ($R$):** Total distance on a fully charged battery.
> 5. **Energy Economy / Efficiency:** Wh/km or km/kWh.

**Range equation** (the single most-tested KTU numerical formula):

$$R = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{e_c} = \frac{\text{SOC}_{\max} - \text{SOC}_{\min}}{e_c}\,E_b$$

**Gradeability** (in % grade, vehicle at constant low speed $v_g$, ignoring $F_{ad}$ and $F_{ia}$):

$$G\ \% = \frac{F_t - F_{rr}}{m_v g} \times 100 \quad \text{(at chosen speed)}$$

**Acceleration time** from $v_1$ to $v_2$ (neglecting $F_{rr}$ and $F_{ad}$ for simplicity):

$$t = \frac{\delta m_v}{F_t - F_{rr}}\,(v_2 - v_1)$$

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It is your Module 1 survival sheet.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Curb Weight | Mass of ready-to-drive EV w/o load | $m_c$ | kg |
| 2 | GVW | Curb + Payload + Passengers | $m_{gv}$ | kg |
| 3 | Equivalent Mass | $m_{eq} = \delta m_v$ | $\delta \approx 1.05$–$1.10$ | kg |
| 4 | Frontal Area (approx) | $0.85 W_v H_v$ | $A_f$ | m² |
| 5 | Rolling Resistance | $m_v g C_{rr} \cos\theta$ | $C_{rr} \approx 0.012$ | N |
| 6 | Aero Drag | $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ | $\rho_a = 1.225$ | N |
| 7 | Hill-Climb | $m_v g \sin\theta$ | — | N |
| 8 | Inertial | $\delta m_v a$ | — | N |
| 9 | Tractive Force | $F_{rr}+F_{ad}+F_{hc}+F_{ia}$ | $F_t$ | N |
| 10 | Motor Torque | $T_m = F_t R_w /(i_g i_o \eta_t)$ | $T_m$ | N·m |
| 11 | Battery Energy | $E_b = V_{\text{nom}} Q_{\text{nom}}$ | — | Wh |
| 12 | Specific Consumption | $e_c = F_t v \cdot t / d$ | $e_c$ | Wh/km |
| 13 | Range | $R = E_{\text{usable}} / e_c$ | — | km |
| 14 | Gradeability | $G = (F_t - F_{rr})/(m_v g) \cdot 100$ | — | % |
| 15 | Accel. Time | $t_a = \delta m_v (v_2-v_1)/(F_t-F_{rr})$ | — | s |
| 16 | Top Speed | Set by $P_{\max} = F_t(v_{\max}) v_{\max}$ | $v_{\max}$ | m/s |

**Engineering use:** With this table, any KTU-style numerical — sizing battery, choosing motor, predicting range, finding grade-climb capability, or calculating 0–60 time — can be solved in a structured 4–6 step procedure.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tractive Force Equation from First Principles

**Starting point — Newton's 2nd law on a vehicle moving up a slope:**

A vehicle of mass $m_v$ moves with speed $v$ along a road inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, accelerating at rate $a$. Four external forces act in the direction opposite (or along) its motion.

**Free-body along the road (forward direction positive):**

1. **Tractive force** at the tire contact patches = $F_t$ (forward, supplied by motor).
2. **Rolling resistance** = $F_{rr}$ (backward, due to tire hysteresis).
3. **Aerodynamic drag** = $F_{ad}$ (backward, opposing motion through air).
4. **Component of weight along slope** = $m_v g \sin\theta$ (backward when climbing).
5. **Component of weight normal to slope** = $m_v g \cos\theta$ (does no work in the direction of motion but reduces normal load through $\cos\theta$).

Newton's second law (including rotational inertia factor $\delta$):

$$\delta\, m_v\, a = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - m_v g \sin\theta$$

Solving for the tractive force the motor must deliver:

$$\boxed{F_t = m_v g C_{rr} \cos\theta \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 \;+\; m_v g \sin\theta \;+\; \delta m_v a}$$

This is the canonical EV tractive-force equation. **Every design problem in this module reduces to computing and summing these four terms for a stated driving scenario.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Aerodynamic Drag from Bernoulli / Momentum

Consider a control volume of air of frontal area $A_f$ approaching a stationary vehicle with velocity $v$. The rate of mass flow intercepted by the vehicle is $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a A_f v$.

By momentum theory (Rankine–Fröhlich / actuator-disk approximation), the drag force is:

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, C_d\, \rho_a\, A_f\, v^2$$

The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises from converting kinetic-energy flux into pressure drag; $C_d$ is an empirical shape factor (≈ 0.0 for a flat plate edge-on, ≈ 1.0 for a flat plate face-on, ≈ 0.04 for a teardrop, ≈ 0.23 for a Tesla Model 3).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Acceleration Time $t_{0 \to V}$

**Scenario:** Vehicle accelerates from rest ($v_1 = 0$) to a target speed $v_2 = V$, on level road, neglecting $F_{ad}$ and $F_{hc}$.

Starting from $F_t - F_{rr} = \delta m_v a$:

$$\delta\, m_v\, \frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - F_{rr} = F_{\text{net}}$$

Separating variables:

$$dt = \frac{\delta\, m_v}{F_{\text{net}}}\, dv$$

Integrating from $v = 0$ to $v = V$:

$$\int_0^{t_a} dt = \frac{\delta\, m_v}{F_{\text{net}}}\int_0^V dv$$

$$\boxed{t_a = \frac{\delta\, m_v\, V}{F_t - F_{rr}}}$$

If $F_{ad}$ is included, $F_{\text{net}} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}$ is speed-dependent and the integration must be done numerically or with a closed-form substitution:

$$t_a = \frac{\delta m_v}{\sqrt{\alpha \beta}}\,\tanh^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{\beta}{\alpha}}\,V\right),\quad \alpha = F_t - F_{rr},\ \beta = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Gradeability

**Scenario:** Vehicle climbs a constant slope at constant low crawl speed $v_c$, so $F_{ia}=0$, $F_{ad} \approx 0$ (low speed). Force balance:

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{hc} = m_v g C_{rr}\cos\theta + m_v g \sin\theta$$

Solving for $\sin\theta$:

$$m_v g \sin\theta = F_t - m_v g C_{rr}\cos\theta$$

$$\sin\theta = \frac{F_t}{m_v g} - C_{rr}\cos\theta$$

For small-to-moderate $\theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$:

$$\sin\theta \approx \frac{F_t}{m_v g} - C_{rr}$$

Gradeability in percent (KTU convention):

$$\boxed{G\ \% = \tan\theta \times 100 \approx \sin\theta \times 100 = \left(\frac{F_t}{m_v g} - C_{rr}\right)\times 100}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Range from Energy Balance

**Total energy used over distance $d$ at constant speed $v$:**

$$E_{\text{used}} = F_t \cdot d \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

The usable battery energy must equal $E_{\text{used}}$ (for an ideal, lossless system):

$$E_{\text{usable}} = F_t \cdot R \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{F_t}$$

In practice, expressed in Wh and km with $F_t$ in N and $v$ in m/s:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{e_c}\quad\text{where}\quad e_c = \frac{F_t}{1000\, v}\ \text{Wh/km}}$$

**Refined form** (driving cycle, variable load, accessory load $P_{\text{acc}}$):

$$R = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{\displaystyle\int_0^T \bigl(F_t(t) v(t) + P_{\text{acc}}\bigr)\,dt \,/\, d_{\text{total}}}$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — typical KTU board question:**
>
> *An EV has the following specifications:*
> *Total mass $m_v = 1500$ kg, $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $\delta = 1.08$, wheel radius $R_w = 0.32$ m, gear ratio $i_g i_o = 8.0$, $\eta_t = 0.92$.*
> *The vehicle climbs a 10 % grade at constant 60 km/h. Compute: (a) the tractive force required, (b) the motor torque required, (c) the battery energy used over 50 km at this condition, and (d) the maximum grade it can climb at 30 km/h if motor max torque is 220 N·m.*

**Given:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_v &= 1500\ \text{kg} \\
v &= 60\ \text{km/h} = \tfrac{60}{3.6} = 16.667\ \text{m/s} \\
\theta &= \tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.7106^\circ \\
\sin\theta &= 0.0995,\quad \cos\theta = 0.9950
\end{aligned}$$

**(a) Tractive force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{rr} &= m_v g C_{rr} \cos\theta \\
       &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 0.9950 \\
       &= 175.6\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 \\
       &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.667)^2 \\
       &= 107.2\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{hc} &= m_v g \sin\theta \\
       &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0995 \\
       &= 1464.0\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ia} &= 0 \quad\text{(constant speed)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{F_t = 175.6 + 107.2 + 1464.0 + 0 \approx 1746.8\ \text{N}}$$

**Observation:** On a 10 % grade, the hill-climb term dominates (≈ 84 % of total). The aero drag at 60 km/h is negligible.

**(b) Motor torque:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_w &= F_t R_w = 1746.8 \times 0.32 = 558.98\ \text{N·m (at wheel)} \\
T_m &= \frac{T_w}{i_g i_o\, \eta_t} = \frac{558.98}{8.0 \times 0.92} = 75.95\ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Battery energy for 50 km:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{used}} &= F_t \cdot d = 1746.8 \times 50{,}000 = 8.734 \times 10^7\ \text{J} \\
                &= \tfrac{8.734 \times 10^7}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 24.26\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

**(d) Maximum grade at 30 km/h:**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= 30/3.6 = 8.333\ \text{m/s} \\
F_{ad}' &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (8.333)^2 = 26.8\ \text{N} \\
F_{rr}' &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 1 = 176.6\ \text{N} \\
F_t^{\max} &= \frac{T_m^{\max} \cdot i_g i_o\, \eta_t}{R_w} = \frac{220 \times 8.0 \times 0.92}{0.32} = 5060\ \text{N} \\
F_{hc}^{\max} &= F_t^{\max} - F_{rr}' - F_{ad}' = 5060 - 176.6 - 26.8 = 4856.6\ \text{N} \\
\sin\theta_{\max} &= \frac{F_{hc}^{\max}}{m_v g} = \frac{4856.6}{1500 \times 9.81} = 0.330 \\
\theta_{\max} &= \sin^{-1}(0.330) = 19.27^\circ \\
G_{\max} \% &= \tan(19.27^\circ) \times 100 \approx 34.9\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** Even a small EV can climb a 35 % grade at low speed because the steady-state resisting force at low speed is small; the **motor torque limit**, not battery power, is the binding constraint.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Templates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix as your numerical problem-solving flowchart:**

| Asked Quantity | Required Steps | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Tractive Force | Compute 4 resistive components & sum | $F_t = F_{rr}+F_{ad}+F_{hc}+F_{ia}$ |
| Motor Torque | Convert $F_t$ via wheel radius & gearing | $T_m = F_t R_w /(i_g i_o \eta_t)$ |
| Motor Power | $P_m = F_t \cdot v / \eta_t$ | (kW) |
| Acceleration Time | $t_a = \delta m_v \Delta v /(F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad})$ | (s) |
| Gradeability | Solve $F_t = m_v g(\sin\theta + C_{rr}\cos\theta)$ | (%) |
| Range | $R = E_{\text{usable}}/e_c$ | (km) |
| Battery Sizing | $E_b = e_c \cdot R / (\Delta\text{SOC})$ | (kWh) |
| Specific Consumption | $e_c = F_t v t/d = F_t/(1000v)$ | (Wh/km) |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vehicle Free-Body Diagram — The Four Resistive Forces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROAD[Road Inclined at Angle theta]
        direction LR
        SLOPE[Slope angle theta]
    end

    subgraph VEHICLE[Vehicle Body of mass m_v]
        direction TB
        MASS[m_v g - Weight acting at cg]
        CG[Center of Gravity at height h_g]
    end

    FT[Tractive Force F_t - forward - supplied by motor via wheels]
    FRR[Rolling Resistance F_rr - opposing motion]
    FAD[Aerodynamic Drag F_ad - opposing motion - proportional to v squared]
    FHC[Hill Climb Force F_hc - mg sin theta - opposing climb]
    FIA[Inertial Force F_ia - delta m_v a - during acceleration]

    FT --> VEHICLE
    FRR --> VEHICLE
    FAD --> VEHICLE
    FHC --> VEHICLE
    FIA --> VEHICLE
    MASS --> VEHICLE
    ROAD --> VEHICLE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** All four resistive forces ($F_{rr}, F_{ad}, F_{hc}, F_{ia}$) act opposite to vehicle motion. The motor-supplied $F_t$ is the *only* forward force and must exceed their sum to produce net acceleration.

---

### 4.2 EV Powertrain Parameter-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery Pack - kWh Energy E_b] --> B[DC-DC Converter and Inverter]
    B --> C[Electric Motor - Torque T_m, Speed N_m]
    C --> D[Gearbox - Ratio i_g]
    D --> E[Final Drive - Ratio i_o, Efficiency eta_t]
    E --> F[Wheels - Radius R_w]
    F --> G[Tractive Force F_t at Contact Patch]

    G --> H[F_rr - Rolling Resistance]
    G --> I[F_ad - Aerodynamic Drag]
    G --> J[F_hc - Hill Climb]
    G --> K[F_ia - Inertial Force]

    H --> L[Net Force - F_net]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Vehicle Dynamics - v and a]
    M --> N[Performance: Top Speed, Accel Time, Gradeability]
    A --> O[Range R = E_b / e_c]
    M --> O
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture insight:** The flow has two feedback loops —
> 1. **Performance loop:** Motor torque → tractive force → vehicle acceleration → speed → aero drag → back to required $F_t$.
> 2. **Energy loop:** Battery $E_b$ is depleted over distance to give **Range**. Specific consumption $e_c$ closes the loop.

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Solving KTU EV Numericals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Read the given problem] --> Q1{What is being asked?}
    Q1 -->|Tractive Force| A1[Compute F_rr, F_ad, F_hc, F_ia - Sum them]
    Q1 -->|Motor Torque or Power| A2[Compute F_t - then T_m = F_t R_w / i_g i_o eta_t]
    Q1 -->|Acceleration Time| A3[Compute F_t - then t_a = delta m_v dv / F_net]
    Q1 -->|Gradeability| A4[Solve F_t = m_v g sin theta plus F_rr - find theta]
    Q1 -->|Range| A5[Compute e_c - then R = E_usable / e_c]
    Q1 -->|Battery Sizing| A6[Compute e_c - then E_b = e_c R / delta SOC]

    A1 --> DONE[Write answer with units - cite assumptions]
    A2 --> DONE
    A3 --> DONE
    A4 --> DONE
    A5 --> DONE
    A6 --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this flowchart in exams:** Read the question, identify the *single output asked*, follow the arrow, compute each required sub-term, write the boxed answer with units. Most KTU valuation scripts give 1 mark per sub-step — so **show every step, even simple arithmetic**.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the following terms used in EV parameter analysis, with one-line significance:
(a) Coefficient of rolling resistance $C_{rr}$
(b) Frontal area $A_f$
(c) Rotational inertia factor $\delta$

**Model Answer (3 × 1 mark = 3 marks):**

(a) **$C_{rr}$:** Dimensionless empirical coefficient (typically 0.008–0.015 for radial tires) representing the energy lost per unit distance as a tire deforms while rolling. It directly scales the rolling resistance force: $F_{rr} = m_v g C_{rr} \cos\theta$. Significance: Lower $C_{rr}$ → longer EV range. *(1 mark)*

(b) **$A_f$:** The projected silhouette area of the vehicle as viewed from the front, used in the aerodynamic drag equation. For an近似 rectangular shape, $A_f \approx 0.85 W_v H_v$. Significance: Determines the cross-section of air the vehicle must push aside. Larger $A_f$ → higher drag → reduced range, especially at highway speeds. *(1 mark)*

(c) **$\delta$:** Multiplicative factor (typically 1.05–1.10) that accounts for the additional inertia of rotating components (wheels, motor rotor, driveline) during acceleration. Effective mass = $\delta m_v$. Significance: Without $\delta$, computed acceleration times are optimistic. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the four components of resistive force that an EV must overcome while in motion. Write the equation for each in terms of vehicle parameters.

**Model Answer:**

The four resistive forces are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Rolling Resistance:}\quad & F_{rr} = m_v g\, C_{rr}\, \cos\theta \\
\text{(ii) Aerodynamic Drag:}\quad & F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 \\
\text{(iii) Hill-Climb Force:}\quad & F_{hc} = m_v g \sin\theta \\
\text{(iv) Linear Inertial Force:}\quad & F_{ia} = \delta m_v a
\end{aligned}$$

*(1/2 mark each for naming, 1/4 mark each for the equation, 1/4 mark each for symbol meaning — total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Internal-Choice Convention:** Two alternative questions (A or B) are offered. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full step-by-step model solutions follow.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

A four-seater electric car has the following design data:
- Curb weight = 1400 kg, payload = 300 kg, driver + passengers = 280 kg
- Wheel radius $R_w = 0.31$ m, $C_{rr} = 0.013$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.25$ m²
- $\delta = 1.08$, gear ratio $i_g i_o = 7.8$, $\eta_t = 0.90$
- Battery: 350 V nominal, 200 Ah capacity

**(a)** Compute the tractive force and the motor torque required to maintain **80 km/h on a 6 % up-gradient**, neglecting acceleration.

**(b)** Determine the **range** of the vehicle when it drives a mixed driving cycle with average specific energy consumption $e_c = 160$ Wh/km. Assume usable SOC window is 90 %.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Total mass (1 mark)**

$$m_v = 1400 + 300 + 280 = 1980\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2: Convert speed (1/2 mark)**

$$v = \frac{80}{3.6} = 22.22\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Slope angle (1/2 mark)**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.06) = 3.434^\circ,\quad \sin\theta = 0.0599,\quad \cos\theta = 0.9982$$

**Step 4: Compute each resistive force (3 marks — 1 each + 0.5 for substitution)**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{rr} &= m_v g C_{rr}\cos\theta = 1980 \times 9.81 \times 0.013 \times 0.9982 = 252.0\ \text{N} \\[4pt]
F_{ad} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.25 \times (22.22)^2 = 190.3\ \text{N} \\[4pt]
F_{hc} &= m_v g \sin\theta = 1980 \times 9.81 \times 0.0599 = 1163.4\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Sum and convert to motor torque (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_t &= F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} = 252.0 + 190.3 + 1163.4 = 1605.7\ \text{N} \\
T_w &= F_t R_w = 1605.7 \times 0.31 = 497.8\ \text{N·m (at wheel)} \\
T_m &= \frac{T_w}{i_g i_o\, \eta_t} = \frac{497.8}{7.8 \times 0.90} = 70.93\ \text{N·m (at motor)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{F_t \approx 1606\ \text{N}, \qquad T_m \approx 70.9\ \text{N·m}}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Battery rated energy (2 marks)**

$$E_b = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot Q_{\text{nom}} = 350\ \text{V} \times 200\ \text{Ah} = 70{,}000\ \text{Wh} = 70\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2: Usable energy (2 marks)**

$$E_{\text{usable}} = \Delta\text{SOC} \times E_b = 0.90 \times 70 = 63\ \text{kWh} = 63{,}000\ \text{Wh}$$

**Step 3: Range (3 marks)**

$$R = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{e_c} = \frac{63{,}000}{160} = 393.75\ \text{km}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 394\ \text{km}}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A delivery EV has the following specifications:
- GVW = 2200 kg, $C_{rr} = 0.014$, $C_d = 0.35$, $A_f = 2.8$ m²
- $\delta = 1.10$, $R_w = 0.34$ m, $i_g i_o = 9.5$, $\eta_t = 0.88$
- Maximum motor torque $T_m^{\max} = 280$ N·m
- Maximum motor speed $N_m^{\max} = 9000$ rpm

**(a)** Calculate (i) the maximum grade the vehicle can climb at a steady 25 km/h, and (ii) the maximum speed it can sustain on a level road.

**(b)** Calculate (i) the time to accelerate from 0 to 60 km/h on a level road using maximum motor torque, and (ii) the specific energy consumption in Wh/km during this acceleration phase (assume battery efficiency 0.92).

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum grade at 25 km/h (4 marks):**

Speed: $v = 25/3.6 = 6.944$ m/s. Since the motor max-torque limit binds first, the **maximum tractive force** available at the wheel:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_w^{\max} &= T_m^{\max} \times i_g i_o \times \eta_t = 280 \times 9.5 \times 0.88 = 2340.8\ \text{N·m} \\
F_t^{\max} &= \frac{T_w^{\max}}{R_w} = \frac{2340.8}{0.34} = 6884.7\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Resistive forces at 25 km/h:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.8 \times (6.944)^2 = 72.4\ \text{N} \\
F_{rr} &= 2200 \times 9.81 \times 0.014 \times 1 = 302.1\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Force available for climbing:

$$F_{hc}^{\max} = F_t^{\max} - F_{ad} - F_{rr} = 6884.7 - 72.4 - 302.1 = 6510.2\ \text{N}$$

Grade:

$$\sin\theta_{\max} = \frac{F_{hc}^{\max}}{m_v g} = \frac{6510.2}{2200 \times 9.81} = 0.3017$$

$$\theta_{\max} = 17.57^\circ,\quad G_{\max}\% = \tan(17.57^\circ) \times 100 = 31.7\ \%$$

$$\boxed{G_{\max} \approx 31.7\ \%\ \text{grade at 25 km/h}}$$

**(ii) Maximum speed on level road (3 marks):**

At $v_{\max}$ on level road, motor delivers max **power** (not max torque). Assuming power limit binding:

$$P_{\max} = T_m^{\max} \cdot \omega_m^{\max} = 280 \times \left(\tfrac{2\pi \times 9000}{60}\right) = 280 \times 942.48 = 263{,}894\ \text{W} \approx 264\ \text{kW}$$

At $v_{\max}$ on level road ($F_{hc} = 0$, $F_{ia} = 0$):

$$P_{\max} = (F_{rr} + F_{ad}) v_{\max} = \left(m_v g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v_{\max}^2\right) v_{\max}$$

Let $a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.8 = 0.6003$ N·s²/m²
Let $b = m_v g C_{rr} = 302.1$ N

Equation: $a v^3 + b v - P_{\max} = 0$. Solve iteratively:

Try $v = 75$ m/s (= 270 km/h): $0.6003 \times 421{,}875 + 302.1 \times 75 = 253{,}252 + 22{,}657 = 275{,}909$ W > 264 kW. Slightly high.
Try $v = 73$ m/s (= 263 km/h): $0.6003 \times 389{,}017 + 302.1 \times 73 = 233{,}476 + 22{,}053 = 255{,}529$ W. Slightly low.
Interpolate: $v_{\max} \approx 73.8$ m/s $\approx 265.7$ km/h.

$$\boxed{v_{\max} \approx 265\ \text{km/h on level road}}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**(i) 0–60 km/h acceleration time (4 marks):**

$v_2 = 60/3.6 = 16.667$ m/s. Maximum tractive force = $F_t^{\max} = 6884.7$ N (from part a).

Net force at start (worst case, $v=0$, $F_{ad}=0$):

$$F_{\text{net}} = F_t^{\max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad} \approx 6884.7 - 302.1 - 0 = 6582.6\ \text{N}$$

Using simplified formula (neglect speed-varying $F_{ad}$ for first-order estimate):

$$t_a = \frac{\delta m_v (v_2 - 0)}{F_{\text{net}}} = \frac{1.10 \times 2200 \times 16.667}{6582.6} = \frac{40{,}333}{6582.6} \approx 6.13\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{0 \to 60\ \text{km/h}} \approx 6.1\ \text{s}}$$

**(ii) Specific energy consumption during acceleration (3 marks):**

Energy delivered by motor to wheels (kinetic + rolling loss):

$$E_{\text{wheel}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta m_v v_2^2 + F_{rr} \cdot d$$

where $d = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_2)\cdot t_a = 0.5 \times 16.667 \times 6.13 = 51.08$ m.

$$\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{1}{2}\delta m_v v_2^2 &= 0.5 \times 1.10 \times 2200 \times (16.667)^2 = 335{,}333\ \text{J} \\
F_{rr} \cdot d &= 302.1 \times 51.08 = 15{,}431\ \text{J} \\
E_{\text{wheel}} &= 350{,}764\ \text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

Battery-side energy (accounting for $\eta_{\text{batt}} = 0.92$ and drivetrain $\eta_t = 0.88$):

$$E_{\text{batt}} = \frac{E_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_{\text{batt}}\,\eta_t} = \frac{350{,}764}{0.92 \times 0.88} = 433{,}158\ \text{J} = 120.3\ \text{Wh}$$

Specific consumption:

$$e_c = \frac{E_{\text{batt}}}{d \ (\text{km})} = \frac{120.3}{51.08/1000} = 2354\ \text{Wh/km}$$

$$\boxed{e_c \approx 2350\ \text{Wh/km during 0–60 km/h acceleration}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Speed conversion trap:** Always convert km/h → m/s by dividing by 3.6. Forgetting this gives an answer off by ~13×.
> 2. **Use $m_{gv}$ (loaded), not $m_c$:** "Fully loaded" or "with passengers" means GVW.
> 3. **Don't drop $\delta$:** Accelerations without rotational inertia factor are too optimistic.
> 4. **SOC window vs battery capacity:** Range uses *usable* energy = $\Delta\text{SOC} \times E_b$, not full $E_b$.
> 5. **Show the substitution:** Even when a number is given, write the equation with values plugged in. Marks are awarded per step, not just for the final answer.
> 6. **Cos vs sin confusion:** $F_{rr} \propto \cos\theta$, $F_{hc} \propto \sin\theta$. Mixing them is the single most common error.
> 7. **Units check:** Final answer must carry correct units (N, N·m, km, kWh, s, %). A right number without units loses a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 EV Parameters:**

- **Five parameter families:** Weight, Size, Force, Energy, Performance — all coupled; changing one affects all.
- **Three "mass" definitions:** Curb $m_c$, Payload $m_p$, GVW $m_{gv} = m_c + m_p$. Always confirm which one the question asks for.
- **Rotational inertia factor $\delta$:** $1.05$ to $1.10$ — applied to mass during acceleration only.
- **Frontal area approximation:** $A_f \approx 0.85\, W_v H_v$. Saves time in numericals.
- **Tractive force equation — memorize:**
  $$F_t = m_v g C_{rr}\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + m_v g\sin\theta + \delta m_v a$$
- **Dominant term depends on speed:** At low speed, $F_{rr}$ and $F_{hc}$ dominate; at high speed, $F_{ad}$ dominates; during acceleration, $F_{ia}$ dominates.
- **Air density standard:** $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea-level, ISA).
- **Speed conversion:** $1$ m/s $= 3.6$ km/h.
- **Wheel-to-motor torque:** $T_m = F_t R_w /(i_g i_o\, \eta_t)$.
- **Battery energy:** $E_b$ (Wh) $= V_{\text{nom}}\cdot Q_{\text{nom}}$.
- **Usable energy:** $E_{\text{usable}} = \Delta\text{SOC} \cdot E_b$, where $\Delta\text{SOC} \approx 0.8$–$0.9$ for Li-ion.
- **Range:** $R = E_{\text{usable}} / e_c$. Typical EV $e_c$: 120–180 Wh/km.
- **Gradeability:** Solve $F_t = m_v g \sin\theta + m_v g C_{rr}\cos\theta$. Typical EV max grade: 25–35 %.
- **Acceleration:** $t_a = \delta m_v \Delta v / (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad})$.
- **Top speed:** Bounded by max power: $P_{\max} = F_t(v_{\max})\cdot v_{\max}$.
- **Cos vs Sin:** $F_{rr}$ uses $\cos\theta$; $F_{hc}$ uses $\sin\theta$. Mixing = guaranteed mark loss.
- **Show every step:** KTU valuation rewards the process, not just the boxed answer.
- **Units everywhere:** N, N·m, m/s, kg, kWh, km, s, % — every numerical answer must carry a unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In any EV parameter problem, always begin by listing **Given Data**, then identify which of the four force terms is non-zero for the stated scenario. This 30-second "skeleton step" prevents 80 % of careless mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Hybrid and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Need for Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Need for Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs & EVs):**
> The "need" refers to the **multifaceted engineering, economic, environmental, and geopolitical drivers** that compel the global automotive industry to transition from conventional **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** vehicles, powered predominantly by **petroleum-based fossil fuels**, to **electrified powertrains** — namely **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)**, **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs)**, **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, and **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEMUT721)**, the "need" is broadly classified into **four primary pillars**:
1. **Environmental Necessity** — Reduction of **Greenhouse Gas (GHG)** emissions and urban air pollutants.
2. **Energy Security & Resource Depletion** — Mitigation of finite fossil fuel dependency.
3. **Economic & Industrial Drivers** — Lower operating cost, new manufacturing paradigms, and energy independence.
4. **Performance & Technological Advancement** — Superior torque characteristics, regenerative braking, and quiet operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Bucket with a Hole":**
