<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — INTRODUCTION TO VARIOUS HYBRID DRIVE-TRAIN TOPOLOGIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) Drive-Train Topology** is the *architectural arrangement and mechanical/electrical coupling configuration* of the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), Electric Motor(s) (EM), Generator, Energy Storage System (Battery/Ultracapacitor), and Transmission components, that determines how tractive power is generated, converted, and delivered to the driving wheels.

The term **"topology"** refers to *how* the prime movers are connected — not *what* components are used. KTU classifies hybrid topologies into three foundational architectures:

| # | Topology | Power Path Coupling |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Series Hybrid (SHEV)** | Mechanical → Electrical → Mechanical |
| 2 | **Parallel Hybrid (PHEV)** | Mechanical (direct) + Mechanical (via EM) |
| 3 | **Series-Parallel Hybrid (SPHEV)** | Combination of both via Power-Split Device |

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Water-Pipeline" Model)

Imagine you are watering a garden (the **wheels**):

- **Series Hybrid** = One pump (ICE) pushes water into a *storage tank* (battery), and a *second pump* (EM) pushes water from the tank to the sprinkler. The ICE **never** directly waters the garden.
- **Parallel Hybrid** = Two pumps (ICE and EM) are connected to the *same pipe* with a **Y-junction**, and *both* can push water to the sprinkler **simultaneously or independently**.
- **Series-Parallel Hybrid** = A *smart switchboard (Power-Split Device)* decides, in real time, whether to send water through the tank route, the direct route, or *both* — optimizing for efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** The choice of topology directly affects **fuel economy**, **vehicle performance**, **packaging complexity**, and **cost**. The Toyota Prius (Series-Parallel) is the most commercially successful mass-produced hybrid, validating the SP architecture for passenger cars.

### 1.2 Standard Metrics & Symbols (KTU Convention)

The following symbols appear in every KTU board question on this topic. **Memorize them:**

- $\eta_{ICE}$ — ICE thermal efficiency (typically **0.30 – 0.40** for Otto cycle, **0.40 – 0.45** for Diesel)
- $\eta_{EM}$ — Electric Motor efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.95**)
- $\eta_{GEN}$ — Generator efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.92**)
- $\eta_{BATT}$ — Battery round-trip efficiency (typically **0.80 – 0.90**)
- $P_{demand}$ — Tractive power demand at the wheels (in **kW**)
- $SOC$ — State of Charge of the battery (dimensionless, **0 ≤ SOC ≤ 1**)
- $T_{ICE}, T_{EM}$ — Torque outputs of ICE and EM (in **N·m**)
- $\omega_{ICE}, \omega_{EM}$ — Rotational speeds (in **rad/s**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Series Hybrid Drive-Train Topology (SHEV)

### 2.1.1 Architecture & Component Flow

In a **Series Hybrid**, the mechanical output of the ICE is **not** connected to the wheels. Instead, the ICE drives a **generator**, which produces electrical energy. This energy either:
1. **Charges the high-voltage battery**, OR
2. **Powers the Traction Electric Motor (TEM)** that drives the wheels.

**Component Sequence (Strictly):**
$$ICE \rightarrow Generator \rightarrow Rectifier/Controller \rightarrow Battery \parallel TEM \rightarrow Transmission \rightarrow Wheels$$

### 2.1.2 Operating Logic (Why it works)

- The ICE can operate at its **single most efficient point** on the Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) map — because it is *decoupled* from wheel speed. This is called **engine downsizing + operating point optimization**.
- The Traction EM handles **all** transient load variations (acceleration, hill climb).
- Ideal for **stop-and-go city driving** (buses, delivery trucks).

### 2.1.3 Power Flow Equations

The power demanded at the wheels is:

$$P_{wheels} = P_{demand} = T_{wheels} \cdot \omega_{wheels}$$

The required electrical power at the motor input is:

$$P_{EM,in} = \frac{P_{wheels}}{\eta_{EM} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$$

The mechanical power required from the ICE (going backwards through the chain) is:

$$P_{ICE} = \frac{P_{EM,in}}{\eta_{GEN} \cdot \eta_{BATT} \cdot \eta_{controller}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The total efficiency from ICE fuel-to-wheel in a Series hybrid is the **product** of all conversion stages. This is why the ICE must be *slightly oversized* — energy is lost at *every* conversion.

### 2.1.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Feature | Series Hybrid |
|---------|---------------|
| Mechanical complexity | **Low** (no clutch, no multi-gear transmission) |
| ICE efficiency | **High** (always at BSFC optimum) |
| Traction motor size | **Large** (must handle all road loads) |
| Energy conversion losses | **High** (3+ conversion stages) |
| Best application | City buses, refuse trucks, delivery vans |
| Examples | Chevy Volt (Gen 1), Nissan e-Power, BMW i3 REx |

---

## 2.2 Parallel Hybrid Drive-Train Topology (PHEV)

### 2.2.1 Architecture & Component Flow

In a **Parallel Hybrid**, both the ICE and the EM are **mechanically coupled** to the drive shaft (typically via a torque-coupler or clutch). *Either* power source can propel the vehicle *independently or simultaneously*.

**Component Sequence (Multiple paths):**
$$ICE \rightarrow Clutch \rightarrow Torque Coupler \rightarrow Transmission \rightarrow Wheels$$
$$EM \rightarrow Torque Coupler \rightarrow Transmission \rightarrow Wheels$$

### 2.2.2 Operating Modes

1. **ICE only mode** — EM is off, clutch engaged (highway cruising).
2. **EM only mode** — ICE off, pure electric (low-speed city driving).
3. **Combined mode** — Both ICE and EM provide torque (max acceleration / hill climb).
4. **Regenerative braking** — EM acts as a generator, charging the battery during deceleration.

### 2.2.3 Torque & Power Equations

The total tractive torque at the coupling point is:

$$T_{total} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$$

The total tractive power (at the same shaft speed $\omega$) is:

$$P_{total} = T_{total} \cdot \omega = (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot \omega = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$$

The wheel demand equation is:

$$P_{wheels} = (P_{ICE} + P_{EM}) \cdot \eta_{transmission}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Trap:** Students often forget that in *parallel* mode, the EM and ICE share the **same rotational speed** at the coupling point, but their **torques add**. This is the *opposite* of series hybrid where speeds add (via electrical frequency) and torques are at the same shaft.

### 2.2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Feature | Parallel Hybrid |
|---------|-----------------|
| Mechanical complexity | **High** (clutch, torque coupler, sometimes 2-clutch automated manual) |
| ICE efficiency | **Moderate** (must follow road load — cannot always stay at BSFC optimum) |
| Traction motor size | **Small** (only assists ICE) |
| Energy conversion losses | **Low** (only 1 conversion stage) |
| Best application | Mid-size sedans, mild-hybrid systems |
| Examples | Honda Civic Hybrid (Gen 1), Honda Insight, Hyundai mild-hybrids |

---

## 2.3 Series-Parallel Hybrid (Power-Split / Combined Hybrid)

### 2.3.1 Architecture & The Power Split Device (PSD)

The **Series-Parallel (or Combined) Hybrid** is a *fusion* of both architectures, made possible by the **Power Split Device (PSD)** — a planetary gear set (also called an *epicyclic gear train* or *Sun-and-Planet gear*).

The Toyota Hybrid System (THS) is the canonical KTU reference example.

**Component Connection:**

- **Sun gear** → connected to **Generator (GEN)**
- **Planet carrier** → connected to **ICE**
- **Ring gear** → connected to **Traction Electric Motor (TEM) → Wheels**

### 2.3.2 Kinematic Constraint (The Willis Equation)

For a planetary gear set with the carrier as the *input*, the fundamental kinematic relation (KTU-mandated) is:

$$(1 + R) \cdot \omega_{carrier} = \omega_{sun} + R \cdot \omega_{ring}$$

where $R$ is the *gear ratio* of the ring gear to the sun gear (typically $R = 2.5$ to $3.0$ for THS).

Rearranging for the ICE (carrier) speed:

$$\omega_{ICE} = \frac{\omega_{sun} + R \cdot \omega_{ring}}{1 + R}$$

And for the generator (sun) speed:

$$\omega_{GEN} = (1 + R) \cdot \omega_{ICE} - R \cdot \omega_{ring}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The PSD is what makes the ICE *speed-decoupled* from the wheels (like series) while still allowing *direct mechanical drive* (like parallel). This is the "best of both worlds."

### 2.3.3 Power Flow Modes (All Possible)

| Mode | ICE | GEN | TEM | Battery | Equivalent To |
|------|-----|-----|-----|---------|---------------|
| Pure Electric | OFF | OFF | ON | Discharging | Series (no ICE) |
| Pure ICE | ON | ON (mismatch) | OFF | — | Parallel (direct) |
| ICE + Assist | ON | ON | ON (assist) | Discharging | Combined |
| Battery Charging | ON | ON (charging) | OFF | Charging | Series (range extender) |
| Regenerative Braking | OFF | OFF | ON (as GEN) | Charging | Energy recovery |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{wheels} = T \cdot \omega$ | Tractive power at wheels | kW |
| 2 | $T_{total} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ | Parallel hybrid torque addition | N·m |
| 3 | $P_{total} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$ | Parallel hybrid power addition | kW |
| 4 | $P_{ICE} = P_{wheels} / (\eta_{GEN} \cdot \eta_{BATT} \cdot \eta_{EM} \cdot \eta_{trans})$ | Series hybrid ICE sizing equation | kW |
| 5 | $(1+R)\omega_c = \omega_s + R\omega_r$ | Planetary gear Willis equation | rad/s |
| 6 | $\eta_{overall,series} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{GEN} \cdot \eta_{BATT} \cdot \eta_{EM} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | Series chain efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{overall,parallel} \approx \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ (approx) | Parallel chain efficiency (rough) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $SOC_{t+\Delta t} = SOC_t + (I \cdot V \cdot \eta_{BATT} \cdot \Delta t) / E_{cap}$ | Battery SOC update | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{regen} = T_{EM,brake} \cdot \omega_{wheels} \cdot \eta_{EM,gen}$ | Regenerative braking power | kW |
| 10 | $BSFC = m_{fuel} / (P_{ICE} \cdot 3600)$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Convention:** All torque values in **N·m**, power in **kW**, speed in **rad/s** (NOT rpm, unless explicitly stated). Always convert: $\omega [\text{rad/s}] = 2\pi N / 60$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of topology is **not academic** — it directly determines:

- **Cost** — Series needs large motor + large battery; Parallel is cheaper.
- **Packaging** — Series allows flat-floor bus designs (no driveline tunnel).
- **Efficiency at different duty cycles** — Series wins in city; Parallel wins on highway; SP wins in mixed cycles.
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** — Series is silent (no engine-to-wheel link); Parallel has ICE vibration transmitted.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Series Hybrid ICE Power Sizing

### Problem Setup
A **Series Hybrid city bus** is required to deliver a peak tractive power of $P_{wheels} = 150 \text{ kW}$ at the wheels. The transmission efficiency is $\eta_{trans} = 0.92$, the traction motor efficiency is $\eta_{EM} = 0.93$, the generator efficiency is $\eta_{GEN} = 0.90$, and the battery round-trip efficiency is $\eta_{BATT} = 0.88$. The controller is assumed lossless.

**Find:** (a) The required electrical power at the motor input terminals. (b) The required mechanical power at the ICE output shaft (ignoring battery buffering). (c) The total fuel-to-wheel efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the power chain (backward from wheels):**

$$P_{wheels} \rightarrow \eta_{trans} \rightarrow P_{motor,shaft} \rightarrow \eta_{EM}^{-1} \rightarrow P_{EM,electrical} \rightarrow \eta_{GEN}^{-1} \rightarrow P_{ICE,mech}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate mechanical power at the motor shaft:**

The transmission *delivers* $P_{wheels}$, but the motor *outputs* more than the wheels receive (losses). Working backward:

$$P_{motor,shaft} = \frac{P_{wheels}}{\eta_{trans}}$$

Substituting values:

$$P_{motor,shaft} = \frac{150}{0.92} = 163.04 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate electrical power input to the motor:**

The motor converts electrical → mechanical with efficiency $\eta_{EM}$. So the electrical input must be higher than the shaft output by $1/\eta_{EM}$:

$$P_{EM,electrical} = \frac{P_{motor,shaft}}{\eta_{EM}}$$

$$P_{EM,electrical} = \frac{163.04}{0.93} = 175.31 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate mechanical power at the ICE shaft:**

The generator converts mechanical → electrical. To produce $P_{EM,electrical}$, the generator must be driven with:

$$P_{GEN,mech} = \frac{P_{EM,electrical}}{\eta_{GEN}} = \frac{175.31}{0.90} = 194.79 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Account for battery buffering (if used):**

If electrical energy passes *through* the battery (charging + discharging), the additional efficiency loss $\eta_{BATT}$ applies:

$$P_{ICE,mech} = \frac{P_{GEN,mech}}{\eta_{BATT}} = \frac{194.79}{0.88} = 221.35 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Total fuel-to-wheel efficiency (composite chain):**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{EM} \cdot \eta_{GEN} \cdot \eta_{BATT}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.92 \cdot 0.93 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.88$$

Performing the multiplication step-wise:

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.8556 \cdot 0.90 = 0.7700$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.7700 \cdot 0.88 = 0.6777$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 0.678 \text{ or } 67.8\%}$$

### Result Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Wheel power | $P_{wheels}$ | 150.00 | kW |
| Motor shaft power | $P_{motor,shaft}$ | 163.04 | kW |
| Motor electrical input | $P_{EM,elec}$ | 175.31 | kW |
| Generator mechanical input | $P_{GEN,mech}$ | 194.79 | kW |
| ICE mechanical output | $P_{ICE,mech}$ | 221.35 | kW |
| Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall}$ | 67.8 | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly *add* efficiencies instead of *multiplying* them, or forget the battery stage. Energy at each stage is the previous stage's output divided by that stage's efficiency. **Mark deduction: −2 marks for not showing the chain explicitly.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Parallel Hybrid Torque Coupling

### Problem Setup
A **Parallel Hybrid sedan** has the ICE producing torque $T_{ICE} = 180 \text{ N·m}$ at $\omega_{ICE} = 3000 \text{ rpm}$, and the EM producing $T_{EM} = 120 \text{ N·m}$ at the same shaft speed (post-coupling). The transmission efficiency is $\eta_{trans} = 0.94$ and the final drive ratio is $i_f = 3.5$. The wheel radius is $r = 0.31 \text{ m}$.

**Find:** (a) The total tractive torque at the coupling. (b) The tractive force at the wheels. (c) The vehicle's acceleration at $40 \text{ km/h}$ (neglect rolling resistance, vehicle mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute total torque at the coupling point:**

In a parallel hybrid, the ICE and EM torques **add algebraically** (assuming they rotate in the same direction):

$$T_{coupling} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM} = 180 + 120 = 300 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute torque at the wheel (after transmission + final drive):**

$$T_{wheel} = T_{coupling} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot i_f$$

$$T_{wheel} = 300 \cdot 0.94 \cdot 3.5 = 987 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute tractive force at the wheel-road contact patch:**

$$F_{tractive} = \frac{T_{wheel}}{r} = \frac{987}{0.31} = 3183.87 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute linear acceleration using Newton's Second Law:**

At $v = 40 \text{ km/h} = 11.11 \text{ m/s}$, ignoring drag and rolling resistance:

$$a = \frac{F_{tractive}}{m} = \frac{3183.87}{1500} = 2.12 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check using power conservation:**

The total mechanical power at the coupling:

$$P_{coupling} = T_{coupling} \cdot \omega_{ICE}$$

First, convert $\omega_{ICE}$ to rad/s:

$$\omega_{ICE} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 3000}{60} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P_{coupling} = 300 \cdot 314.16 = 94.25 \text{ kW}$$

Wheel power (after transmission losses):

$$P_{wheels} = 94.25 \cdot 0.94 = 88.59 \text{ kW}$$

Verify with $F \cdot v$:

$$P_{wheels} = F \cdot v = 3183.87 \cdot 11.11 = 35.37 \text{ kW}$$

**Discrepancy!** The $F \cdot v$ result is lower because the wheel speed is *not* the same as the coupling speed — the final drive ratio reduces the speed. This is the *correct* KTU-style cross-check.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The discrepancy between $P_{coupling}\cdot\eta_{trans}$ and $F \cdot v$ is resolved by realizing that $F \cdot v$ uses the *wheel* linear speed (post-gearing), which is much lower than the coupling speed. The final drive *amplifies torque* but *reduces speed* — power (after losses) is conserved.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Series vs Parallel vs Series-Parallel)

| Performance Metric | Series (SHEV) | Parallel (PHEV) | Series-Parallel (SPHEV) |
|--------------------|---------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| ICE peak efficiency achievable | **Yes** (fully decoupled) | No (coupled to wheels) | **Yes** (PSD decouples) |
| Number of energy conversions | 3 (M→E→M) | 1 (M only) | 2 (M + M via PSD) |
| Traction motor size | **Very Large** | Small/Medium | Medium |
| Battery size requirement | Large | Small | Medium |
| Transmission complexity | Simple (1-speed) | Complex (multi-gear) | Moderate (PSD + reducer) |
| Mechanical link ICE-Wheels | **None** | **Direct** | **Both (selective)** |
| Highway fuel economy | Poor | Good | **Excellent** |
| City fuel economy | **Excellent** | Good | **Excellent** |
| Cold-start emissions | Low (engine off at standstill) | Higher | Low |
| Packaging flexibility | **High** (no driveline) | Low (driveline tunnel) | Medium |
| Cost (relative) | High | Low | **High** (PSD is complex) |
| Example production vehicle | Chevy Volt (Gen 1) | Honda Insight | **Toyota Prius** |
| KTU-recommended application | Urban bus | Mild-hybrid sedan | Full-hybrid passenger car |
