# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Series and Parallel hybrid drive-train topologies with suitable block diagrams. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Concise — KTU 3-mark depth):**

| Basis | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|
| Mechanical link ICE–Wheels | **Absent** | **Present** |
| Number of energy conversions | Three (M→E→M) | One (M only) |
| Traction motor size | **Large** | Small |
| ICE always runs at optimum? | **Yes** | No |

> The ICE in Series hybrid is *decoupled* from the wheels, allowing it to operate at the Best BSFC point, while in Parallel hybrid the ICE torque is *coupled* directly to the wheels through a torque coupler.

*(Self-assessment: 3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for concluding statement.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Power Split Device (PSD)? State its role in a Series-Parallel hybrid vehicle. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Power Split Device (PSD)** is an *epicyclic (planetary) gear set* that mechanically couples the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) to two electrical machines (MG1 — generator, and MG2 — traction motor).

**Role in Series-Parallel Hybrid:**
1. It **mechanically splits** the ICE power into two paths: one going to the generator (MG1, like Series mode) and another going directly to the wheels via the ring gear and MG2 (like Parallel mode).
2. It **decouples the ICE speed from the wheel speed** — allowing the ICE to run at its most efficient RPM regardless of vehicle speed.
3. It enables **continuously variable transmission (CVT)-like behavior** without a traditional gearbox.

*(Self-assessment: 3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three roles.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** *Answer ANY ONE FULL QUESTION from each module. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)** drive-train topology. Discuss its **operating modes**, **advantages**, and **limitations** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Series Hybrid Electric Bus has a peak wheel power demand of $200 \text{ kW}$. The following efficiencies are given: $\eta_{trans} = 0.91$, $\eta_{EM} = 0.92$, $\eta_{GEN} = 0.89$, $\eta_{BATT} = 0.85$. Calculate:
  (i) The required mechanical power output of the ICE.
  (ii) The total energy lost in the conversion chain.
  (iii) The overall fuel-to-wheel efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (Verbal description — 2 marks):**
The Series SHEV has the following power flow:
$$ICE \rightarrow Generator \rightarrow Power Electronics \rightarrow \left\{\text{Battery (charging/discharging)}\right\} \rightarrow \text{Traction EM} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

**Operating Modes (3 marks):**
1. **Pure Electric Mode** — ICE OFF, vehicle runs on battery + EM (low speed, idle traffic).
2. **Range Extender Mode** — ICE ON, drives generator, charges battery, also powers EM.
3. **Battery Charging Mode** — ICE ON, generator charges the battery, EM OFF (vehicle stationary or coasting).
4. **Hybrid Boost Mode** — ICE ON, generator provides extra power to EM beyond battery capability (peak load).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- ICE always at BSFC optimum → high city fuel economy.
- Simple transmission (single-speed reducer).
- No mechanical link → flexible packaging (flat-floor bus designs).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Three or more energy conversions → cumulative efficiency loss.
- Traction motor must be sized for *peak* wheel power.
- Poor high-speed fuel economy due to conversion chain losses.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $P_{wheels} = 200 \text{ kW}$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.91$, $\eta_{EM} = 0.92$, $\eta_{GEN} = 0.89$, $\eta_{BATT} = 0.85$.

**(i) Required ICE mechanical power (3 marks):**

Working backward from the wheels:

$$P_{motor,shaft} = \frac{P_{wheels}}{\eta_{trans}} = \frac{200}{0.91} = 219.78 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{EM,elec} = \frac{P_{motor,shaft}}{\eta_{EM}} = \frac{219.78}{0.92} = 238.89 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{GEN,mech} = \frac{P_{EM,elec}}{\eta_{GEN}} = \frac{238.89}{0.89} = 268.42 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{ICE,mech} = \frac{P_{GEN,mech}}{\eta_{BATT}} = \frac{268.42}{0.85} = 315.79 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{ICE,mech} \approx 315.8 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Total energy lost in the conversion chain (2 marks):**

$$P_{lost} = P_{ICE,mech} - P_{wheels} = 315.79 - 200 = 115.79 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{lost} \approx 115.8 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Overall fuel-to-wheel efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{wheels}}{P_{ICE,mech}} = \frac{200}{315.79}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.6333$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 63.3\%}$$

**Cross-check using product of efficiencies:**

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.91 \cdot 0.92 \cdot 0.89 \cdot 0.85 = 0.6324 \approx 63.3\% \checkmark$$

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)** drive-train topology. Discuss its **operating modes** and explain why it is preferred for **highway driving applications**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Parallel Hybrid sedan uses an ICE that delivers $T_{ICE} = 220 \text{ N·m}$ at $\omega_{ICE} = 2500 \text{ rpm}$, and an EM that delivers $T_{EM} = 140 \text{ N·m}$ at the same shaft speed. The combined transmission + final drive efficiency is $\eta_{trans} = 0.93$, the final drive ratio is $i_f = 3.8$, and the wheel radius is $r = 0.32 \text{ m}$. The vehicle mass is $m = 1600 \text{ kg}$. Calculate:
  (i) The total tractive torque at the wheel.
  (ii) The tractive force at the road.
  (iii) The vehicle's acceleration (neglect all resistances). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

$$ICE \rightarrow Clutch \rightarrow \text{Torque Coupler} \leftarrow EM \rightarrow \text{Multi-speed Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Final Drive} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

The ICE and EM are *both mechanically coupled* at the torque coupler. The battery powers the EM via a power inverter.

**Operating Modes (3 marks):**
1. **ICE-Only Mode** — EM OFF, clutch engaged → highway cruising, steady loads.
2. **EM-Only Mode** — ICE OFF, clutch disengaged → pure electric, low-speed city.
3. **Combined (Power Boost) Mode** — Both ON → maximum acceleration.
4. **Regenerative Braking** — EM acts as generator, ICE OFF, clutch disengaged.
5. **Battery Assist Mode** — ICE ON at low load, EM assists → reduces ICE BSFC.

**Why preferred for highway driving (2 marks):**
- Direct mechanical path from ICE to wheels means **only one energy conversion** (mechanical losses only).
- No battery buffering required at steady highway speeds → **no electrochemical losses**.
- ICE operates near its BSFC optimum at steady highway speeds (unlike city driving where it constantly varies).
- The high-efficiency direct-drive path makes the parallel hybrid superior to series for steady-state highway cruising.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $T_{ICE} = 220 \text{ N·m}$, $T_{EM} = 140 \text{ N·m}$, $\omega = 2500 \text{ rpm}$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.93$, $i_f = 3.8$, $r = 0.32 \text{ m}$, $m = 1600 \text{ kg}$.

**(i) Total tractive torque at the wheel (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Torque at the coupling:

$$T_{coupling} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM} = 220 + 140 = 360 \text{ N·m}$$

Step 2 — Torque at the wheel (post transmission × final drive × efficiency):

$$T_{wheel} = T_{coupling} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot i_f$$

$$T_{wheel} = 360 \cdot 0.93 \cdot 3.8$$

Computing stepwise:

$$T_{wheel} = 334.8 \cdot 3.8 = 1272.24 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{wheel} \approx 1272.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(ii) Tractive force at the road (2 marks):**

$$F_{tractive} = \frac{T_{wheel}}{r} = \frac{1272.24}{0.32} = 3975.75 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{tractive} \approx 3975.8 \text{ N}}$$

**(iii) Vehicle acceleration (2 marks):**

Using Newton's Second Law, neglecting all resistances:

$$a = \frac{F_{tractive}}{m} = \frac{3975.75}{1600} = 2.485 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{a \approx 2.48 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Verification using $0$ to $100 \text{ km/h}$ time (optional):**
$$t_{0-100} = \frac{v_{100}}{a} = \frac{27.78}{2.48} \approx 11.2 \text{ s}$$

This is consistent with a typical mid-size parallel hybrid sedan.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 2 Pitfalls:**
> 1. **−2 marks** if you do *not* draw the block diagram in part (a) of either Q-A or Q-B. A diagram is **mandatory**.
> 2. **−2 marks** if the power chain in a Series hybrid question is written in the *forward* direction (from ICE to wheels) without the $\eta$-divided steps. Always work **backward from $P_{wheels}$**.
> 3. **−1 mark** for unit mismatch (kW vs W, rpm vs rad/s). Always include units in the final boxed answer.
> 4. **−1 mark** for confusing *torque addition* (parallel) with *speed addition* (electrical frequency in series). Read the question carefully!
> 5. **−1 mark** if the planetary gear Willis equation is written without defining $R$ explicitly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Bullets (Print this and stick it on your wall before ESE):**

- A **Series Hybrid** has **no mechanical link** between the ICE and the wheels. The ICE → Generator → Battery/Motor → Wheels.
- A **Parallel Hybrid** has a **direct mechanical link** via a torque coupler. ICE and EM torques **add at the coupling**.
- A **Series-Parallel Hybrid** uses a **Planetary Gear Set (Power Split Device)** to combine both modes. The Toyota Prius is the KTU benchmark example.
- In Series: ICE always runs at **Best BSFC point** (advantage for city).
- In Parallel: ICE size can be **smaller** because EM assists during peaks.
- In Series-Parallel: PSD has **Sun Gear (MG1/Generator)**, **Planet Carrier (ICE)**, **Ring Gear (MG2/Traction)**.
- The **Willis equation** for the PSD: $(1+R)\omega_c = \omega_s + R\omega_r$.
- Series hybrid requires a **large traction motor** (sized for peak wheel power).
- Parallel hybrid requires a **smaller EM** (only assists).
- **Energy conversion chain** in Series: $P_{ICE,mech} = P_{wheels} / (\eta_{GEN} \cdot \eta_{BATT} \cdot \eta_{EM} \cdot \eta_{trans})$.
- **Torque addition in Parallel**: $T_{total} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ at the coupling.
- **Tractive force**: $F = T_{wheel} / r$, where $T_{wheel} = T_{coupling} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot i_f$.
- **Acceleration**: $a = F_{tractive} / m$ (ignoring resistances).
- **Regenerative braking** is possible in *all* three topologies.
- The **Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1)** is a Series hybrid. The **Honda Insight (Gen 1)** is a Parallel hybrid. The **Toyota Prius** is a Series-Parallel hybrid.
- KTU expects students to know the **speed conversion** $\omega [\text{rad/s}] = 2\pi N / 60$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies** is the supervisory and instantaneous management of mechanical and electrical energy streams between the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), one or more Electric Machines (M/G — Motor/Generators), and the Energy Storage System (ESS — typically a Lithium-ion traction battery) to satisfy the driver demanded power $P_{req}$ at the wheels while simultaneously optimizing fuel consumption, emissions, and State-of-Charge (SOC) of the battery.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the controller is referred to as the **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** or **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)**. It acts upon three primary drive-train topologies:

1. **Series Hybrid (SHEV)** — ICE is mechanically decoupled from wheels; it drives a generator only.
2. **Parallel Hybrid (PHEV)** — ICE and EM are mechanically coupled to wheels via a torque-additive device (clutch/gears).
3. **Series–Parallel (or Power-Split) Hybrid (SPHEV)** — uses an **epicyclic (planetary) gear set** to continuously vary the engine operating point, exemplified by the Toyota Hybrid System (THS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** The power-split device (Epicyclic/Planetary gear set) is the *heart* of the SPHEV topology. The control problem reduces to governing the speed of two rotating elements: the **Sun gear (driven by MG1)** and the **Ring gear (driven by MG2)**, while the **Planet Carrier is locked to the ICE (Engine)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Teller's Cash Drawer" Intuition

Imagine a bank branch receiving varying customer demand for cash withdrawals. The branch manager has three sources of cash:
- A slow **vault** (the **Engine** — reliable but takes time to access),
- A fast **ATM machine** (the **Battery** — instant but limited in stock),
- A **cash recycler/counter** (the **Motor/Generator** — can give or take cash).

The **Power Flow Controller is the Bank Manager**. At any instant, the manager must decide *how much cash to pull from the vault vs. the ATM* to satisfy customer demand, while ensuring the ATM never runs out of money (SOC constraints) and the vault isn't opened for tiny withdrawals (engine efficiency threshold). The manager also deposits surplus cash back into the ATM (Regenerative Braking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Energy Conservation Law applied to Hybrids:** 
> $$P_{wheels} = P_{engine} \cdot \eta_{eng} + P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{mot} - P_{batt\_disch} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{batt}} \pm P_{losses}$$
> This single equation, in its various topological forms, governs *all* power flow control problems in KTU ESE.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **State of Charge (SOC):** typically maintained between **30 % and 80 %** for Li-ion traction packs.
- **Gear ratio of epicyclic set (R):** $R = N_r / N_s$ (ring teeth / sun teeth), commonly $R = 2.6$ in Toyota THS-II.
- **Battery Nominal Voltage:** **200 V – 400 V DC** for passenger HEVs.
- **Standard Drive Cycles:** NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75 — used for benchmarking the EMS.
- **Engine BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption):** minimized typically at **220 – 250 g/kWh**.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often reward students who explicitly mention *which* drive cycle and *which* SOC window their control strategy targets. Always cite these in long-answer derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Modes of a Hybrid Drive-Train (The Five Canonical Modes)

The HCU selects one (or a blend of) the following modes based on driver demand, SOC, and vehicle speed:

1. **Pure Electric Mode (EV Mode):** $P_{engine} = 0$. The traction motor (MG2) alone drives the wheels. Used during low-speed launch, parking maneuvers, and zero-emission zones. Constraint: $SOC \geq SOC_{min}$.
2. **Engine-Only Mode:** $P_{motor} = 0$ (mechanically decoupled or idling). The ICE directly powers the wheels. Used during steady-state highway cruising where ICE BSFC is at its sweet spot.
3. **Hybrid (Power-Assist) Mode:** $P_{req} > P_{engine\_max\_eff}$, so MG2 supplements ICE. The HCU splits power to keep ICE at its **Optimal Operating Line (OOL)**.
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** $P_{req} < 0$. The motor operates as a generator, charging the battery. Constraint: brake pedal not pressed, or friction brakes blended in.
5. **Charge-Sustaining / Load-Leveling Mode:** ICE produces more power than $P_{req}$; surplus charges the battery. This is the *steady-state equilibrium mode* of the EMS on long trips.

### 2.2 Why Power Flow Control is Non-Trivial — The "Why" Behind the Equations

The naive solution is to always run the ICE at its most efficient point. However:
- ICE efficiency drops catastrophically at **low loads (< 20 % of rated torque)**.
- ICE transient response is slow (≈ 1 – 3 s), but driver demand changes in **< 0.5 s**.
- Battery has **round-trip efficiency ≈ 80 – 90 %** — so the EMS cannot blindly shuffle energy.
- Power electronics (inverters) have thermal limits — typically **50 kW continuous, 100 kW peak** for 30 s.

The control law must therefore be a **constrained real-time optimization** running at 10 – 100 Hz in the HCU.

### 2.3 The Toyota Hybrid System (THS) — Epicyclic Power Split

The THS uses a single planetary gear set with:
- **Sun Gear (S)** — coupled to **MG1** (operates as generator or motor).
- **Planet Carrier (C)** — coupled to **ICE** (the engine is *always* mechanically tied to the carrier).
- **Ring Gear (R)** — coupled to **MG2** (traction motor) and the final drive (wheels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The carrier being tied to the engine means: **Engine speed is *not* directly equal to vehicle speed**. The HCU controls $\omega_{MG1}$ to *set* the engine speed independently of vehicle speed — this is the **"electronic continuously variable transmission (e-CVT)"** behavior.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Notation:** $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s), $T$ = torque (N·m), $P$ = power (W), $R$ = planetary gear ratio ($N_r/N_s$), subscripts $e, m, g, s, r, c$ refer to engine, motor, generator, sun, ring, carrier respectively. Subscript $v$ refers to vehicle/wheels.

| # | Formula | Description / Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{req}(t) = M \dot{v} v + M g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3 + \mu M g \cos\theta \cdot v$ | Driver demanded tractive power (longitudinal dynamics) | W |
| 2 | $P_{wheels} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{ICE} + P_{M/G} \cdot \eta_{M/G} - P_{batt}$ | Universal power balance at wheels | W |
| 3 | $\omega_s \cdot N_s = \omega_r \cdot N_r$ | Relative speed of epicyclic gears (sun vs. ring); carrier grounded | rad/s |
| 4 | $(\omega_r - \omega_c) N_r = -(\omega_s - \omega_c) N_s$ | General Willis/Kinematic equation of planetary set | rad/s |
| 5 | $(1 + R)\omega_c = R\,\omega_s + \omega_r$ where $R = N_r/N_s$ | Speed relation (carrier = engine in THS) | rad/s |
| 6 | $T_s / T_r = -N_s / N_r = -1/R$ | Torque ratio in planetary set (opposing signs) | N·m |
| 7 | $T_c = T_s + T_r$ (sum of torques on carrier) | Torque equilibrium at the planet carrier | N·m |
| 8 | $T_{ICE} = T_c$ and $\omega_{ICE} = \omega_c$ | Engine is locked to carrier in THS | N·m, rad/s |
| 9 | $P_{MG1} = T_{MG1} \cdot \omega_{MG1}$ (with sign convention: $P_{MG1} < 0$ means generating) | MG1 power — controlled variable for engine speed | W |
| 10 | $P_{ICE} = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE}$ | Engine output power (mechanical) | W |
| 11 | $P_{batt} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{batt} - I_{batt}^2 R_{int}$ | Battery terminal power (after internal loss) | W |
| 12 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{Q_{nom}} \int_0^t I_{batt}(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Coulomb-counting SOC update | unitless |
| 13 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{P_{ICE}}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \eta_{ICE}(\omega_e, T_e)}$ | Instantaneous fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 14 | $J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \dot{m}_{fuel}(t) + \beta \left( SOC(t) - SOC^* \right)^2 \right] dt$ | EMS cost functional (Equivalent Consumption Minimization) | kg |
| 15 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{eng} \cdot \eta_{mg} \cdot \eta_{transmission} \cdot \eta_{batt}$ | Overall drivetrain efficiency (cascaded) | unitless |
| 16 | $P_{regen,max} = \tfrac{1}{2} M v^2 \cdot \eta_{regen} / \Delta t$ | Maximum regenerative brake power capture | W |
| 17 | $\omega_{MG1} = (1 + R)\omega_{ICE} - R\,\omega_{MG2}$ | THS engine-speed control law derived from (5) | rad/s |
| 18 | $T_{load} = \frac{M \dot{v} \cdot r_{wheel}}{G_{fd}}$ | Required torque at MG2 output (post final drive) | N·m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Substitution Note for KTU Answer Books:** In KTU valuation, when you write absolute values, always write $\vert x \vert$ (with the LaTeX `\vert` command) and **never** the bare pipe `$\vert x \vert$` *inside* a table cell of a markdown preview tool. For hand-written exams, simply use the standard notation $\vert x \vert$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Toyota Prius (1997–present), Ford Escape Hybrid, Lexus Hybrid Drive, and Hyundai TMED all use variants of the formulae above. The 2024 KTU syllabus reflects this dominance: understanding the **planetary gear-based power split** is essential for *any* industrial role in HEV design at Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, Ather Energy, or Ola Electric.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Kinematic Relation of the Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Set used in THS

We derive the canonical speed relation of the epicyclic gear set with the planet carrier treated as the reference.

**Step 1 — Identify the rotating elements:** Sun (S), Planet (P, multiple), Ring (R), and Carrier (C). We are concerned only with S, R, and C since planets are idlers.

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental relative velocity relation for meshed gears.** When two external gears (or one internal-external pair) mesh, the linear velocities at the pitch point are equal:

$$\omega_s \cdot r_s = -\omega_c \cdot r_s + \omega_c \cdot r_p \text{ (for sun-planet mesh, accounting for carrier motion)}$$

A cleaner starting point is the **Willis Equation** for an epicyclic train:

$$\frac{\omega_s - \omega_c}{\omega_r - \omega_c} = -\frac{N_r}{N_s} = -R$$

This says: *the relative speed of the sun with respect to the carrier, divided by the relative speed of the ring with respect to the carrier, equals the negative of the gear ratio R.*

**Step 3 — Expand the Willis equation to isolate the carrier speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\omega_s - \omega_c) &= -R(\omega_r - \omega_c) \\
\omega_s - \omega_c &= -R\omega_r + R\omega_c \\
\omega_s + R\omega_r &= (1 + R)\omega_c \\
\therefore\ \omega_c &= \frac{\omega_s + R\,\omega_r}{1 + R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the THS coupling:** In Toyota Hybrid System, $\omega_c = \omega_{ICE}$ (engine locked to carrier), $\omega_s = \omega_{MG1}$ (MG1 locked to sun), $\omega_r = \omega_{MG2}$ (MG2 locked to ring). Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{ICE} &= \frac{\omega_{MG1} + R\,\omega_{MG2}}{1 + R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Re-arrange to give the HCU control law for engine speed.** Since the HCU must control $\omega_{ICE}$ to track the Optimal Operating Line, it solves for $\omega_{MG1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{ICE}(1 + R) &= \omega_{MG1} + R\,\omega_{MG2} \\
\therefore\ \omega_{MG1} &= (1 + R)\,\omega_{ICE} - R\,\omega_{MG2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** When the vehicle accelerates, $\omega_{MG2}$ rises. To keep $\omega_{ICE}$ at its most efficient point, the HCU commands $\omega_{MG1}$ to *also* rise (MG1 spins faster, acting as a generator) — the engine power is partly transmitted mechanically to the ring (wheels) and partly converted to electricity via MG1, which can then feed MG2 electrically. This dual path is the **"power split"**.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Power Balance Across the Planetary Gear Set (Energy Conservation)

We derive the energy balance that the HCU must satisfy at every instant.

**Step 1 — Write the power input/output at each port of the gear set.** Let $P_s, P_r, P_c$ be the mechanical powers at the sun, ring, and carrier ports:

$$P_s = T_s \omega_s,\quad P_r = T_r \omega_r,\quad P_c = T_c \omega_c$$

**Step 2 — Apply torque equilibrium at the carrier.** Since the carrier is a rigid body in rotational equilibrium, the algebraic sum of torques acting on it from the three branches equals zero (assuming no external torque on the carrier shaft except the engine):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c + T_s + T_r &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow\ T_c &= -(T_s + T_r)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the gear ratio relation.** For meshed gears, torque scales inversely with teeth (or radii). With $R = N_r/N_s$:

$$T_s = -R\,T_r \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad T_s / T_r = -N_s/N_r = -1/R$$

**Step 4 — Compute the power balance.** Sum the three port powers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s + P_r + P_c &= T_s \omega_s + T_r \omega_r + T_c \omega_c \\
&= T_s \omega_s + T_r \omega_r - (T_s + T_r)\omega_c \\
&= T_s(\omega_s - \omega_c) + T_r(\omega_r - \omega_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Willis relation.** Using $\omega_s - \omega_c = -R(\omega_r - \omega_c)$ from Section 3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s + P_r + P_c &= T_s\bigl[-R(\omega_r - \omega_c)\bigr] + T_r(\omega_r - \omega_c) \\
&= (\omega_r - \omega_c)(-R\,T_s + T_r) \\
&= (\omega_r - \omega_c)\bigl[-R(-R\,T_r) + T_r\bigr] \quad [\text{since } T_s = -R T_r] \\
&= (\omega_r - \omega_c)(R^2 T_r + T_r) \\
&= T_r(\omega_r - \omega_c)(1 + R^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the energy conservation principle.** For an *ideal* (lossless) gear set, the algebraic sum of input powers must equal zero (no energy is stored, no friction):

$$P_s + P_r + P_c = 0 \quad\text{(ideal gear set)}$$

This forces the constraint:

$$T_r(\omega_r - \omega_c)(1 + R^2) = 0$$

Since $T_r \neq 0$ in general (traction torque) and $R \neq 0$ (finite ratio), we must have:

$$\omega_r = \omega_c \quad\text{or}\quad R = 0 \quad \text{(trivial)}$$

The non-trivial solution reveals that for the gear set to be lossless, the *output torque* and *output speed* must be *co-located* — physically, the ring speed equals the carrier speed when the sun is braked. In the real (lossy) case, the residual is dissipated as gear-mesh and windage loss, modelled as $\eta_{gear} \approx 0.97 - 0.99$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is exactly what KTU examiners test in the 14-mark questions.** You are expected to show all 6 steps, mark the energy conservation point, and explain why $R = 0$ is rejected.

### 3.3 Derivation III: Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS) — Cost Function

The ECMS is the most popular real-time implementable EMS. We derive the optimal instantaneous power split.

**Step 1 — Define the equivalent fuel rate.** When the battery delivers $P_{batt} > 0$ (discharging), this energy must *eventually* be replenished by the engine later. The "equivalent fuel" of that electrical energy is:

$$\dot{m}_{eq,batt} = s(t) \cdot \frac{P_{batt}}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \bar{\eta}_{disch} \cdot \bar{\eta}_{chg}}$$

where $s(t)$ is the **equivalence factor** (a time-varying penalty), $Q_{LHV}$ is the lower heating value of fuel, and $\bar{\eta}$ are battery charge/discharge efficiencies.

**Step 2 — Define the total instantaneous equivalent fuel consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{total}(t) &= \dot{m}_{fuel,ICE}(t) + \dot{m}_{eq,batt}(t) \\
&= \frac{P_{ICE}(\omega_e, T_e)}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \eta_{ICE}(\omega_e, T_e)} + s(t) \cdot \frac{P_{batt}}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \bar{\eta}_{batt}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the power balance constraint:**

$$P_{ICE}\cdot\eta_{ICE} + P_{batt}\cdot\eta_{batt} = P_{req}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian and differentiate with respect to the control variable $P_{batt}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= \dot{m}_{total} + \lambda \left( P_{req} - P_{ICE}\eta_{ICE} - P_{batt}\eta_{batt} \right) \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_{batt}} &= \frac{s(t)}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \bar{\eta}_{batt}} - \lambda \eta_{batt} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the optimal battery power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{batt}^* &= \frac{s(t) - \lambda Q_{LHV} \bar{\eta}_{batt}^2}{\eta_{batt}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.** The HCU at every timestep evaluates $P_{batt}^*$ from the formula above. If $P_{batt}^* > 0$ → discharge (motor assists engine). If $P_{batt}^* < 0$ → charge (engine loads the generator). The factor $s(t)$ is adaptively tuned using a PI controller to keep SOC on track — this is the **adaptive ECMS** used in production Toyota/Lexus HEVs.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 14-mark questions on EMS, you will be awarded full marks only if you show the Lagrangian setup *and* explain the role of $s(t)$ in SOC maintenance. Examiners specifically look for the phrase "the equivalence factor penalises future fuel use to keep SOC charge-sustaining."

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode-by-Mode Power Flow Equations

| Operating Mode | $P_{ICE}$ | $P_{MG1}$ | $P_{MG2}$ | $P_{batt}$ | Sign Convention & Notes |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| Pure EV (launch) | $0$ | $0$ | $=P_{req}/\eta_{MG2}$ | $=-P_{MG2}/\eta_{batt}$ | $P_{batt}<0$ (discharging) |
| Engine Only (cruise) | $=P_{req}/\eta_{ICE}$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | MG1/MG2 idle or freewheel |
| Hybrid Assist (accel) | $>0$ (at OOL) | $0$ (or small neg) | $>0$ | $<0$ | MG2 supplements; MG1 may be inactive |
| Regen Braking | $0$ | $0$ | $<0$ (gen) | $>0$ (charging) | Sign reversal; friction brake blended |
| Charge Sustaining | $>P_{req}/\eta_{ICE}$ | $<0$ (gen) | $=P_{req}$ (or partly) | $>0$ | Engine sized larger; excess → battery |
| Series Mode (range-extender EV) | $0$ at wheels | $= P_{ICE}\eta_{ICE}\eta_{MG1}$ | $=P_{req}/\eta_{MG2}$ | $\approx 0$ in steady state | No mechanical link ICE↔wheels |
| Parallel Mode (both propel) | $>0$ | $0$ | $>0$ | $<0$ | Torque-additive via clutch |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of THS Power Flow Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Input Pedal Position and Brake Pedal] --> B[HCU Hybrid Control Unit]
    C[Battery Management System SOC and Voltage] --> B
    D[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> B
    E[Engine ECU Torque and Speed] --> B
    F[Coolant Temperature Sensor] --> B
    B --> G{Operating Mode Selector}
    G --> H1[Pure EV Mode]
    G --> H2[Engine Only Mode]
    G --> H3[Hybrid Assist Mode]
    G --> H4[Regen Braking Mode]
    G --> H5[Charge Sustaining Mode]
    H1 --> I1[MG2 Inverter Command]
    H2 --> I2[Throttle Actuator Command]
    H3 --> I3[MG1 and MG2 Inverter Commands]
    H3 --> I2
    H4 --> I4[MG2 Generator Mode Command]
    H5 --> I3
    H5 --> I2
    I1 --> J[Planetary Gear Set Power Split]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K[Final Drive and Wheels]
    B --> L[Driver Display SOC and Mode]
```

### 4.2 State Diagram of HCU Mode Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PureEV: SOC high and Pedal low
    PureEV --> HybridAssist: P_req above EV limit
    PureEV --> ChargeSustaining: SOC below SOC_min
    HybridAssist --> EngineOnly: Steady cruise and OOL matched
    EngineOnly --> HybridAssist: P_req rises suddenly
    EngineOnly --> ChargeSustaining: SOC below threshold
    HybridAssist --> RegenBraking: Brake pedal pressed
    EngineOnly --> RegenBraking: Brake pedal pressed
    ChargeSustaining --> EngineOnly: SOC restored
    ChargeSustaining --> HybridAssist: P_req spike
    RegenBraking --> PureEV: SOC restored to high
    RegenBraking --> EngineOnly: Brake released and cruise speed
```

### 4.3 Power Split Device — Schematic Representation of THS Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ICE[ICE Engine] -->|Carrier Shaft| PL[Planetary Gear Set]
    MG1[MG1 Generator Motor] -->|Sun Shaft| PL
    PL -->|Ring Shaft| MG2[MG2 Traction Motor]
    MG2 --> INV[Inverter DC AC]
    INV --> BATT[HV Battery Pack]
    MG1 --> INV
    MG2 --> FD[Final Drive Differential]
    FD --> WHL[Drive Wheels]
    BME[Battery Management ECU] -.SOC feedback.-> HCU2[HCU Controller]
    HCU2 -.Torque command.-> MG1
    HCU2 -.Torque command.-> MG2
    HCU2 -.Throttle command.-> ICE
```

### 4.4 Operating Point Visual — ICE Optimal Operating Line (OOL) Tracking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Demands Torque T_dem] --> B{HCU Reads SOC and Engine Map}
    B --> C[Compute P_req and Current Vehicle Speed]
    C --> D[Lookup Engine OOL from BSFC Map]
    D --> E{T_ICE_at_OOL sufficient?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Set T_ICE at OOL, Fill Gap with MG2]
    E -->|No| G[Set T_ICE at Max Torque, MG2 at Max Torque]
    F --> H[Check Engine Speed via MG1 Control]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Command MG1 Speed omega_MG1]
    I --> J[Plant Executes Commands]
    J --> K[Sensors Feedback]
    K --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?** KTU 2024 expects students to demonstrate **functional understanding** of the controller's information flow. The block-level architecture above conveys *exactly* what a physical schematic would: the *causal* interaction between driver inputs, the HCU, the energy domain converters, and the mechanical drivetrain. This is the approach recommended in the KTU OEMUT721 model answer key for Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **State of Charge (SOC)** of a hybrid vehicle battery and explain why the HCU must keep it within a narrow band (typically 30 % – 80 %).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SOC is the ratio of the battery's *currently available* charge $Q(t)$ to its *nominal* full charge $Q_{nom}$:

$$SOC(t) = \frac{Q(t)}{Q_{nom}} \times 100\ \%$$

A typical Li-ion cell has a usable SOC window of about **20 % – 90 %** of its true capacity to avoid:
- **Over-discharge (< 20 %):** irreversible lithium plating, capacity fade, safety risk.
- **Over-charge (> 90 %):** thermal runaway, electrolyte decomposition, fire hazard.
- **Mid-band operation (30 % – 80 %):** maximises cycle life (a 2024 KTU-acceptable figure: ≈ 3000 – 5000 cycles vs. ≈ 500 cycles if cycled 0 % – 100 %).

The HCU enforces this band because the **Charge-Sustaining** strategy of the EMS requires battery headroom to absorb both *regenerative braking energy* and *engine load-leveling surplus*. Without headroom, the EMS has no degrees of freedom for optimisation. *(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for band justification.)*

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Power-Split** and **Torque-Add** hybrid drive-train topologies with one example vehicle for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Power-Split (SPHEV) | Torque-Add (Parallel) |
|---|---|---|
| Coupling Device | Epicyclic gear set | Clutch + parallel gear train |
| Variable ratio | Continuous (e-CVT) | Discrete (manual/automated gears) |
| Engine speed control | Independent of wheel speed | Locked to wheel speed (gear ratio) |
| Example | **Toyota Prius (THS-II)** | **Honda Insight (1st gen, IMA)** / Hyundai Sonata Hybrid |
| Complexity | High (planetary + 2 M/Gs) | Lower (1 M/G + clutch) |
| Efficiency band | Wide — engine can stay at OOL always | Narrow — engine speed follows wheel speed |

*(1 mark each for the distinction table; 1 mark for the example pair.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation-Focused

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / OEMUT721 Module 2]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **power flow control** in a **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) hybrid drive-train** using a planetary gear set. Identify the components connected to the **sun, ring, and carrier** of the planetary set. (7 marks)

**(b)** Starting from the **Willis equation** of an epicyclic gear set, derive the **speed relationship** between the engine, MG1, and MG2 in the Toyota Hybrid System. Show clearly how the HCU controls engine speed independently of vehicle speed. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw (or describe) a planetary gear set with three ports: Sun → MG1, Carrier → Engine, Ring → MG2 + Final Drive. MG1 and MG2 both connect via an inverter to the HV battery. (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3.)

2. **Five power flow modes (3 marks):**
   - **EV mode:** MG2 alone drives the ring; MG1 and engine idle.
   - **Engine-only mode:** Engine drives the carrier; sun is braked (MG1 acts as a generator, dumping electrical energy to MG2 to maintain zero net battery current).
   - **Hybrid mode:** Engine + MG2 both contribute torque; MG1 may be inactive or generate.
   - **Regen:** MG2 acts as a generator, charging battery.
   - **Charge-sustaining:** Engine produces surplus, MG1 generates, battery charges.

3. **Identification of components (2 marks):**
   - **Sun Gear (S):** Meshed with the planets; smallest radius, highest speed, lowest torque. Connected to **MG1 (Generator/Motor 1)**.
   - **Planet Carrier (C):** Holds the planet gears on shafts. Connected to **ICE Crankshaft**.
   - **Ring Gear (R):** Internal-tooth gear, largest radius, lowest speed, highest torque. Connected to **MG2 (Traction Motor)** and the **Final Drive (chain/belt to differential)**.

##### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

1. **Willis equation statement (1 mark):**
   $$\frac{\omega_s - \omega_c}{\omega_r - \omega_c} = -\frac{N_r}{N_s} = -R$$

2. **Cross-multiplication (1 mark):**
   $$\omega_s - \omega_c = -R(\omega_r - \omega_c)$$
   $$\omega_s + R\omega_r = (1 + R)\omega_c$$

3. **Substitute THS couplings (1 mark):** $\omega_s = \omega_{MG1}$, $\omega_r = \omega_{MG2}$, $\omega_c = \omega_{ICE}$:
   $$\omega_{ICE} = \frac{\omega_{MG1} + R\,\omega_{MG2}}{1 + R}$$

4. **Solve for $\omega_{MG1}$ (1 mark):**
   $$\omega_{MG1} = (1 + R)\,\omega_{ICE} - R\,\omega_{MG2}$$

5. **Physical interpretation (2 marks):**
   - At any instant, the HCU *measures* $\omega_{MG2}$ (≈ vehicle speed) and *decides* the desired $\omega_{ICE}$ (from the OOL map).
   - The HCU then *commands* $\omega_{MG1}$ to make the equation hold.
   - **Crucially:** since $\omega_{MG1}$ is an *independent* controlled variable (MG1 is a motor with its own inverter), the engine speed is decoupled from the vehicle speed — this is the "e-CVT" property of the THS.
   - Numerical example: For $R = 2.6$, $\omega_{ICE} = 2000$ rpm, $\omega_{MG2} = 1500$ rpm → $\omega_{MG1} = (3.6)(2000) - 2.6(1500) = 7200 - 3900 = 3300$ rpm (MG1 generating).

6. **Concluding statement (1 mark):** Hence the HCU tracks the ICE's optimal BSFC line by manipulating $\omega_{MG1}$ in real time — this is the essence of *power flow control* in a power-split hybrid.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Common mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Writing the Willis equation with *wrong signs*. The convention is $(\omega_s - \omega_c)/(\omega_r - \omega_c) = -N_r/N_s$. Reversed sign → entire derivation collapses.
> - **Common mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Failing to substitute the THS coupling identities ($\omega_c = \omega_{ICE}$ etc.). Examiners explicitly look for the substitution step.
> - **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Not stating that $R = N_r/N_s$ is the **gear ratio**, not a generic symbol. Always define symbols at the start.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Application-Focused

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / OEMUT721 Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** for a hybrid electric vehicle using the **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)**. Derive the optimal battery power command $P_{batt}^*$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hybrid vehicle has the following data: Vehicle mass $M = 1500$ kg, rolling resistance coefficient $\mu = 0.012$, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m², air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, wheel radius $r = 0.32$ m, final drive ratio $G_{fd} = 4.0$. The vehicle is cruising at constant $v = 90$ km/h on a flat road ($\theta = 0$). Compute the **wheel power** $P_{wheels}$ and the **required motor torque at the wheels** $T_{wheels}$. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

1. **EMS concept (1 mark):** The EMS is the supervisory algorithm in the HCU that decides the instantaneous split of $P_{req}$ between the ICE and the electric machines, subject to SOC sustainability.

2. **ECMS idea (1 mark):** The ECMS treats battery electricity as "virtual fuel" by converting $P_{batt}$ to an *equivalent fuel mass flow rate* $\dot{m}_{eq,batt}$ using an *equivalence factor* $s(t)$.

3. **Equivalent fuel expression (1 mark):**
   $$\dot{m}_{eq,batt} = s(t) \cdot \frac{P_{batt}}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \bar{\eta}_{disch} \cdot \bar{\eta}_{chg}}$$

4. **Total instantaneous cost (1 mark):**
   $$\dot{m}_{total} = \frac{P_{ICE}}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \eta_{ICE}} + s(t) \cdot \frac{P_{batt}}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \bar{\eta}_{batt}}$$

5. **Power balance constraint + Lagrangian setup (1 mark):**
   $$\mathcal{L} = \dot{m}_{total} + \lambda \left( P_{req} - P_{ICE}\eta_{ICE} - P_{batt}\eta_{batt} \right)$$

6. **Differentiation + solution (1 mark):**
   $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_{batt}} = 0 \Rightarrow P_{batt}^* = \frac{s(t) - \lambda Q_{LHV} \bar{\eta}_{batt}^2}{\eta_{batt}}$$

7. **Role of $s(t)$ and adaptive tuning (1 mark):** The factor $s(t)$ is adapted by a PI controller on SOC error $(SOC - SOC^*)$: $s(t+1) = s(t) + K_p (SOC - SOC^*) + K_i \int (SOC - SOC^*) dt$. This makes ECMS a *real-time implementable* EMS used in production HEVs.

##### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Given data (extracted):**
- $M = 1500$ kg
- $\mu = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2$ m²
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $r = 0.32$ m, $G_{fd} = 4.0$
- $v = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, $\theta = 0$, $\dot{v} = 0$

**Step 1 — Resistive forces at constant speed (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{roll} &= \mu M g \cos\theta = 0.012 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.0 = 176.58 \text{ N} \\
F_{drag} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 25^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 625 \\
F_{drag} &= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.66 \cdot 625 = 252.79 \text{ N} \\
F_{grade} &= M g \sin\theta = 0 \text{ N (flat road)} \\
F_{accel} &= M\dot{v} = 0 \text{ N (constant speed)} \\
F_{total} &= 176.58 + 252.79 + 0 + 0 = 429.37 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Wheel power (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheels} &= F_{total} \cdot v = 429.37 \cdot 25 = 10{,}734.25 \text{ W} \approx 10.73 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Wheel torque and motor torque (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheels} &= F_{total} \cdot r_{wheel} = 429.37 \cdot 0.32 = 137.40 \text{ N·m} \\
T_{motor} &= \frac{T_{wheels}}{G_{fd} \cdot \eta_{fd}} = \frac{137.40}{4.0 \cdot 0.95} = 36.16 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Assuming final drive efficiency $\eta_{fd} = 0.95$; if assumed 100 %, $T_{motor} = 34.35$ N·m.)

**Step 4 — Motor speed and motor power verification (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{wheel} &= \frac{v}{r} = \frac{25}{0.32} = 78.125 \text{ rad/s} = 746.0 \text{ rpm} \\
\omega_{motor} &= \omega_{wheel} \cdot G_{fd} = 78.125 \cdot 4 = 312.5 \text{ rad/s} = 2984.4 \text{ rpm} \\
P_{motor,elec} &= T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor} / \eta_{mot} = 36.16 \cdot 312.5 / 0.90 \approx 12{,}556 \text{ W} \approx 12.56 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Account for motor efficiency $\eta_{mot} = 0.90$. The slight increase over $P_{wheels}$ reflects drivetrain losses.)

**Step 5 — Final summarised answer (0.5 mark):**

$$\boxed{P_{wheels} \approx 10.73 \text{ kW},\quad T_{wheels} \approx 137.4 \text{ N·m},\quad T_{motor} \approx 36.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> - **Unit conversion trap (–1 mark):** Speed must be converted to **m/s** (90 km/h = 25 m/s), not used as 90 directly. Most students lose 1 mark here.
> - **Drag formula error (–1 mark):** The factor is **½** $\rho C_d A v^2$, not $\rho C_d A v^2$. Recheck.
> - **Efficiency assumption (–1 mark if omitted):** The problem does not specify $\eta_{fd}, \eta_{mot}$. State your assumption explicitly ("Assuming $\eta_{fd} = 0.95$…"). Examiners will not award marks for a "magical" extra 20 % of power appearing from nowhere.
> - **Grade/accel terms (–0.5 mark if left unevaluated):** Always write $F_{grade} = 0$ and $F_{accel} = 0$ explicitly; don't omit them. The *act of writing* them shows understanding of the longitudinal dynamics equation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Power Flow Control** = real-time split of $P_{req}$ among ICE, M/Gs, and Battery to minimize fuel + emissions while keeping SOC charge-sustaining.
- [x] **Three canonical topologies:** Series (range-extender), Parallel (torque-additive), Series-Parallel/Power-Split (planetary gear based — Toyota THS).
- [x] **THS planetary coupling:** Sun ↔ MG1, Carrier ↔ Engine, Ring ↔ MG2 + Wheels. Engine is *always* locked to the carrier.
- [x] **Willis equation:** $(\omega_s - \omega_c)/(\omega_r - \omega_c) = -N_r/N_s = -R$. **Sign convention is critical.**
- [x] **Engine speed control law:** $\omega_{MG1} = (1 + R)\omega_{ICE} - R\,\omega_{MG2}$. This is the "e-CVT" property.
- [x] **Power balance at wheels:** $P_{wheels} = P_{ICE}\eta_{ICE} + P_{M/G}\eta_{M/G} - P_{batt}/\eta_{batt}$.
- [x] **SOC window:** typically **30 % – 80 %** for Li-ion; managed by adaptive ECMS with equivalence factor $s(t)$.
- [x] **Five operating modes:** Pure EV, Engine Only, Hybrid Assist, Regen Braking, Charge Sustaining.
- [x] **EMS strategies:** Rule-based (deterministic state machine) vs. **Optimization-based (ECMS, DP, Pontryagin's Minimum Principle)**.
- [x] **Key gear ratio in Toyota THS-II:** $R = 2.6$ (commonly tested numerical).
- [x] **Regen braking limit:** $P_{regen,max}$ set by battery charge acceptance (C-rate), motor thermal limit, and SOC ceiling.
- [x] **Energy conversion chain in THS:** Engine → Carrier (mechanical) → split into Sun (MG1 generates electricity) + Ring (MG2 consumes electricity or vice versa).
- [x] **Examiner always wants:** (i) labelled diagram, (ii) explicit Willis derivation, (iii) substitution of THS couplings, (iv) physical interpretation, (v) numerical example with proper units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question says "explain power flow control in a hybrid vehicle," *never* answer in generalities. **Name the topology** (Series / Parallel / Power-Split), **draw the diagram**, **state the governing equation**, and **show the numerical example**. This 4-step structure is the KTU Board Examiner's gold standard for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Machines and Their Controllers: 'Brushed' DC Electric Motor - DC Regulation and Voltage Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electric Machines and Their Controllers: 'Brushed' DC Electric Motor - DC Regulation and Voltage Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 'Brushed' DC Electric Motor — Formal KTU Definition

A **Brushed DC Electric Motor (BDC Motor)** is a rotating electrical machine that converts direct-current (DC) electrical energy into mechanical energy through the interaction of magnetic fields produced by current-carrying conductors, using a **mechanical commutator** and **carbon/graphite brushes** to periodically reverse the current direction in the armature windings, thereby producing unidirectional torque.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMUT721 – Module 3):**
> *An electromechanical energy converter that relies on a stationary field system (stator) and a rotating armature (rotor), with commutation achieved via a segmented copper commutator sliding under spring-loaded carbon brushes. In EV/HEV applications, BDC motors are primarily used in auxiliary subsystems (wipers, window lifters, HVAC blowers, mirror adjusters, seat adjusters) rather than traction drive, due to limitations in power density and brush wear.*

### Constructional Anatomy — Six Indispensable Parts

| S.No | Component | Function | Material |
|------|-----------|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Yoke / Frame** | Mechanical support, magnetic flux return path | Cast iron / Cast steel |
| 2 | **Field Poles & Pole Shoes** | Produce the main magnetic flux (Φ) | Laminated silicon steel |
| 3 | **Field Windings** | Carry exciting current to magnetize poles | Copper wire (enamelled) |
| 4 | **Armature Core** | Houses armature conductors, reduces eddy losses | Laminated silicon steel |
| 5 | **Armature Winding** | Carries armature current (Iₐ); generates EMF | Insulated copper conductors |
| 6 | **Commutator & Brushes** | Mechanical rectifier — converts induced AC to DC at output | Copper segments + Carbon brushes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Commutator + Brush Assembly** is the *defining feature* of a "brushed" machine. It performs two crucial functions:
> (a) It acts as a **mechanical rectifier** — converting the internally induced AC EMF (in the rotating armature) into a unidirectional DC voltage at the output terminals.
> (b) It **reverses armature current** every half-turn to maintain **unidirectional torque** per Faraday's law.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚗

> [!TIP]
> **The Bicycle Pedal Analogy:**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you always push *forward* (in the direction of motion), the swing oscillates and never completes a full rotation. The child's "weight" (load) at the bottom is the **torque requirement**. If you continuously push the swing in the same direction, the brush-and-commutator system is like **your hands** — they always push *tangentially* in the same direction even though the child (armature) keeps rotating. Without the commutator, the force would reverse every 180° and the swing would never gain angular momentum. This is exactly why a DC motor needs the brush-commutator pair to produce **continuous unidirectional torque**.

**In an EV Context:**
Think of a car's power window. A small brushed DC motor with a permanent magnet stator, controlled by a simple 12 V DC supply through a relay/H-bridge, rolls the window up or down. The brushes naturally wear out over years of use, just like brake pads — this is why brushed motors are relegated to **auxiliary loads** in modern EVs, while traction motors are **brushless** (BLDC, PMSM, or induction).

### Governing Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Faraday's Law Constant:** Induced EMF $E = N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$
- **Lorentz Force on a conductor:** $F = BIL$ (Newtons)
- **Mechanical Power Conversion:** $P_m = T \cdot \omega$ (Watts)
- **Electrical Power Input:** $P_e = V \cdot I_a$ (Watts)
- Standard back-EMF constant for a 12 V EV auxiliary brushed motor: typically **60 – 120 V/krpm**
- Commutator peripheral speed limit: **~30 m/s** (above which arcing destroys brushes)

---

## 1.3 Working Principle — Lorentz Force & Fleming's Left-Hand Rule

When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a force given by:

$$\vec{F} = I_a \, \vec{L} \times \vec{B}$$

In a DC motor:
- The **field winding** (or permanent magnets) produces a radial magnetic field **B** between the poles.
- The **armature conductors**, carrying current **Iₐ**, lie in this field.
- By **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**, each conductor experiences a tangential force **F = BIL**.
- The collective force on all conductors produces a **torque T = F × r** that rotates the armature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Commutation Process — Why Brushes Are Needed:**
> For continuous unidirectional rotation, the current in each armature coil must reverse at the moment the coil passes the neutral plane. The **commutator segments** rotate with the armature while the **brushes** remain stationary. As a segment slides under a brush, the connection to the external circuit flips, automatically reversing the current in the coil. This is the heart of "brushed" DC operation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 EMF Equation — The Heart of the DC Motor

Let:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\phi$ = Flux per pole (in Webers)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature in rpm
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature winding

**For a Wave Wound Armature:** $A = 2$
**For a Lap Wound Armature:** $A = P$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution $= \phi P$ (since there are P poles, each pole contributes $\phi$ Webers of flux).
2. Number of revolutions per second $= N/60$.
3. Therefore, flux cut per second by one conductor $= \phi P \cdot N/60$.
4. By Faraday's law, EMF induced in **one conductor** $= \dfrac{\phi P N}{60}$ Volts.
5. EMF induced in the **entire armature** (Z conductors in A parallel paths, each path having $Z/A$ conductors in series):

$$E_b = \dfrac{\phi \, P \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} \;\; \text{(Volts)}$$

This is called the **Back-EMF** (or Counter-EMF) because it opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's Law).

---

## 2.2 Torque Equation

The mechanical torque developed in the armature:

$$T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot E_b \cdot I_a \cdot \dfrac{60}{N} = \dfrac{E_b \, I_a}{\omega}$$

Substituting the EMF equation, the **armature torque** simplifies to:

$$T_a = 0.1591 \; \phi \, P \, Z \, I_a \cdot \dfrac{1}{A} \;\; (\text{N·m})$$

Or equivalently:

$$T_a = K_t \, \phi \, I_a \quad \text{where} \quad K_t = \dfrac{P \, Z}{2\pi A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for EV Engineers:** Torque is *linearly proportional* to armature current. This is why brushed DC motors are popular in actuators — a simple linear current controller gives direct torque control without complex field-oriented algorithms.

---

## 2.3 Speed Equation — Voltage vs. Speed

The voltage equation of a DC motor (KVL applied to the armature circuit):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Substituting $E_b = \dfrac{\phi P Z N}{60 A}$:

$$V = \dfrac{\phi P Z N}{60 A} + I_a R_a$$

Solving for **N (speed in rpm)**:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \phi}} \quad \text{where} \quad K = \dfrac{P Z}{60 A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No-Load Speed (N₀):** When $I_a \approx 0$, $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{K\phi}$ — directly proportional to applied voltage. **This is the basis of DC voltage regulation for speed control in EV auxiliaries.**

---

## 2.4 Power Flow in a Brushed DC Motor

$$P_{input} = V \cdot I_a$$

$$P_{cu\,loss} = I_a^2 \, R_a \quad (\text{armature copper loss})$$

$$P_{developed} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{output}}{P_{input}} = \dfrac{E_b \, I_a}{V \, I_a} = \dfrac{E_b}{V}$$

---

## 2.5 ⚡ DC Voltage Regulation & Voltage Conversion (DC-DC Converters)

In an EV/HEV, the high-voltage traction battery (typically **300–800 V DC**) and the 12 V / 48 V auxiliary bus must be interfaced. The auxiliary brushed DC motors often require **variable DC voltages** for speed control. This is achieved through **DC-DC converters (choppers)**.

### 2.5.1 The DC Chopper — Principle of Operation

A **chopper** is a high-speed semiconductor switch (IGBT/MOSFET) that connects/disconnects the DC source to the load at a high frequency ($f_s$ typically 1–20 kHz). The average output voltage is controlled by varying the **duty cycle** $\alpha$:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = T_{on} \cdot f_s}$$

### 2.5.2 The Four Fundamental Chopper Topologies

| Topology | Function | Average Output Voltage $V_o$ | EV Application |
|----------|----------|------------------------------|----------------|
| **Buck (Step-Down)** | $V_o < V_s$ | $V_o = \alpha \, V_s$ | 48 V → 12 V auxiliary bus |
| **Boost (Step-Up)** | $V_o > V_s$ | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}$ | 12 V → 48 V mild-hybrid boost |
| **Buck-Boost** | $V_o$ can be $\lessgtr V_s$ | $V_o = \dfrac{-\alpha \, V_s}{1 - \alpha}$ | Regenerative braking interface |
| **Cuk / SEPIC** | Non-inverting buck-boost | $V_o = \dfrac{\alpha \, V_s}{1 - \alpha}$ | Bi-directional auxiliaries |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Board Exam Trap:** In the **Buck-Boost** converter, the output voltage is *inverted* (negative polarity with respect to common ground). If the question asks for a non-inverting topology, the answer is **Cuk** or **SEPIC**, not classical Buck-Boost.

---

## 2.6 🎯 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Must Memorize

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Valid For | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{\phi P Z N}{60 A}$ | Back-EMF | All BDC motors | Volts |
| 2 | $T_a = 0.1591 \, \phi P Z I_a / A$ | Armature torque | All BDC motors | N·m |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \phi}$ | Speed | Motoring mode | rpm |
| 4 | $\eta = E_b / V$ | Efficiency | Ideal (no friction) | pu |
| 5 | $V_o = \alpha V_s$ | Buck output | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | Volts |
| 6 | $V_o = V_s / (1 - \alpha)$ | Boost output | $0 \le \alpha < 1$ | Volts |
| 7 | $V_o = -\alpha V_s / (1 - \alpha)$ | Buck-Boost output | $0 \le \alpha < 1$ | Volts |
| 8 | $V_o = \alpha V_s / (1 - \alpha)$ | Cuk/SEPIC output | $0 \le \alpha < 1$ | Volts |
| 9 | $P = T \omega$ | Mechanical power | All rotating machines | Watts |
| 10 | $I_a = (V - E_b) / R_a$ | Armature current | Motoring mode | Amperes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these equations matter in EV Industry:**
> - Equations 1–4: Used by **Bosch, Continental, Denso** while designing EV auxiliary brushed motor controllers (HVAC blower, coolant pump).
> - Equations 5–8: Used by **Tesla, BYD, Tata** in their DC-DC converter design for the auxiliary 12 V / 48 V bus from the high-voltage traction battery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation $E_b = \dfrac{\phi P Z N}{60 A}$

**Given:** A DC machine with $P$ poles, $Z$ total armature conductors, $A$ parallel paths, rotating at $N$ rpm, with useful flux per pole $\phi$ Webers.

**Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Pole:**
The flux linking each pole = $\phi$ Wb. The armature rotates through this flux once per pole-pair per revolution.

**Step 2 — Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Revolution:**
Each conductor cuts flux under all P poles in one revolution:

$$\text{Flux per conductor per rev} = P \cdot \phi \;\; \text{(Webers)}$$

**Step 3 — Time Per Revolution:**
If the armature rotates at $N$ rpm:

$$\text{Time per rev} = \dfrac{60}{N} \;\; \text{(seconds)}$$

**Step 4 — Rate of Flux Cutting (dΦ/dt) Per Conductor:**

$$\dfrac{d\phi_{conductor}}{dt} = \dfrac{P \phi}{60 / N} = \dfrac{P \phi N}{60}$$

**Step 5 — EMF Induced in One Conductor (Faraday's Law):**

$$e_{cond} = \dfrac{P \phi N}{60} \;\; \text{(Volts)}$$

**Step 6 — Number of Conductors Per Parallel Path:**
The $Z$ conductors are distributed over $A$ parallel paths:

$$\text{Conductors per path} = \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

These $Z/A$ conductors are connected *in series* within each parallel path, so their EMFs add.

**Step 7 — Total Back-EMF (Sum of all path EMFs):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_b &= e_{cond} \times \dfrac{Z}{A} \\
E_b &= \dfrac{P \phi N}{60} \times \dfrac{Z}{A} \\
\therefore \;\; E_b &= \dfrac{\phi \, P \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} \;\; \text{(Volts)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot E_b \cdot I_a \cdot \dfrac{60}{N}$

**Step 1 — Mechanical Work Done Per Revolution:**
If the armature develops torque $T_a$ and rotates through one full revolution, the work done:

$$W = T_a \times 2\pi \;\; \text{(Joules per revolution)}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical Energy Converted Per Revolution:**
The power developed electromagnetically is $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a$ Watts.
Energy converted in one revolution:

$$W = E_b \cdot I_a \cdot \dfrac{60}{N} \;\; \text{(Joules)}$$

**Step 3 — Equating Mechanical and Electrical Work (Energy Conservation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_a \times 2\pi &= E_b \cdot I_a \cdot \dfrac{60}{N} \\
T_a &= \dfrac{60}{2\pi N} \cdot E_b \, I_a \\
T_a &= \dfrac{E_b \, I_a}{\omega} \quad \text{where} \;\; \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \;\; \text{rad/s} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Torque in Terms of Flux and Current:**
Substituting $E_b = \dfrac{\phi P Z N}{60 A}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_a &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\phi P Z N}{60 A} \cdot I_a \cdot \dfrac{60}{N} \\
T_a &= \dfrac{\phi \, P \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A} \\
T_a &= 0.1591 \, \phi \, P \, Z \, I_a \,/\, A \;\; \text{(N·m)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Buck Converter Output Voltage

**Circuit:** A switch S (MOSFET) in series with inductor L, diode D, and output capacitor C, fed from DC source $V_s$.

**Step 1 — Mode 1: Switch ON (0 < t < αT):**
- Diode D is reverse-biased (OFF).
- Inductor voltage: $V_L = V_s - V_o$
- Inductor current rises linearly:

$$\Delta I_L^{on} = \dfrac{(V_s - V_o) \, \alpha T}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Mode 2: Switch OFF (αT < t < T):**
- Diode D conducts (freewheeling).
- Inductor voltage: $V_L = -V_o$
- Inductor current falls linearly:

$$\Delta I_L^{off} = \dfrac{V_o \, (1 - \alpha) T}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Volt-Second Balance (Inductor Steady-State):**
For a current-continuous, periodic steady state, the net volt-seconds across L must be zero over one cycle (otherwise the inductor would saturate or de-magnetize):

$$\begin{aligned}
(V_s - V_o) \cdot \alpha T + (-V_o)(1 - \alpha) T &= 0 \\
\alpha V_s - \alpha V_o - V_o + \alpha V_o &= 0 \\
\alpha V_s &= V_o \\
\therefore \;\; V_o &= \alpha \, V_s \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Boost Converter Output Voltage

**Step 1 — Mode 1: Switch ON (0 < t < αT):**
- Diode reverse-biased.
- Inductor charges from source: $V_L = V_s$
- Inductor current rises: $\Delta I_L^{on} = \dfrac{V_s \, \alpha T}{L}$

**Step 2 — Mode 2: Switch OFF (αT < t < T):**
- Diode forward-biased.
- Inductor discharges into load: $V_L = V_s - V_o$ (negative, since $V_o > V_s$)
- Inductor current falls: $\Delta I_L^{off} = \dfrac{(V_s - V_o)(1 - \alpha) T}{L}$

**Step 3 — Volt-Second Balance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s \cdot \alpha T + (V_s - V_o)(1 - \alpha) T &= 0 \\
\alpha V_s + V_s - V_o - \alpha V_s + \alpha V_o &= 0 \\
V_s - V_o + \alpha V_o &= 0 \\
V_s &= V_o (1 - \alpha) \\
\therefore \;\; V_o &= \dfrac{V_s}{1 - \alpha} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Sanity Check:** If $\alpha = 0.5$ and $V_s = 12$ V (12 V EV auxiliary), then $V_o = 12 / (1 - 0.5) = 24$ V. If $\alpha \to 1$, $V_o \to \infty$ — the converter theoretically approaches infinite gain, but practical limits include switch transition losses and inductor saturation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Speed Control Methods

Brushed DC motors in EV auxiliaries (pumps, blowers) need speed control. Three classical methods:

| S.No | Method | Equation Modified | Efficiency | EV Use Case |
|------|--------|-------------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Armature Voltage Control** | $N \propto V$ | High (90–95%) | HVAC blower speed |
| 2 | **Flux Control (Field Weakening)** | $N \propto 1/\phi$ | Moderate (loses torque) | Above base speed (rare in auxiliaries) |
| 3 | **Armature Resistance Control** | $N = (V - I_a R_a)/K\phi$ | Low (I²R loss) | Not used in modern EVs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern EV Practice:** Speed control of brushed DC auxiliaries is **exclusively** done using **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** through a chopper, which is essentially a Buck converter with variable $\alpha$. Field weakening and rheostatic control are *obsolete* in modern EV auxiliaries due to poor efficiency.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Brushed DC Motor + Chopper Speed Control

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 220 V, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.03 Wb. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω. The motor is fed through a Buck converter with input voltage $V_s = 220$ V and duty cycle $\alpha = 0.6$. Find:
(a) The speed at full-load torque of 50 N·m.
(b) The back-EMF at this operating point.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Back-EMF Constant K:**

$$K = \dfrac{P Z}{60 A} = \dfrac{4 \times 800}{60 \times 4} = \dfrac{3200}{240} = 13.333$$

(since lap-wound, A = P = 4)

**Step 2 — Compute Torque Constant:**
From $T_a = \dfrac{\phi P Z I_a}{2\pi A}$:

$$50 = \dfrac{0.03 \times 4 \times 800 \times I_a}{2\pi \times 4} = \dfrac{96 \, I_a}{8\pi} = \dfrac{12 I_a}{\pi}$$

$$I_a = \dfrac{50 \pi}{12} = 13.09 \;\; \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Output Voltage of Buck Converter:**

$$V_o = \alpha V_s = 0.6 \times 220 = 132 \;\; \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Back-EMF Using Voltage Equation:**

$$E_b = V_o - I_a R_a = 132 - (13.09 \times 0.2) = 132 - 2.618 = 129.38 \;\; \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Speed:**

$$N = \dfrac{E_b}{K \phi} = \dfrac{129.38}{13.333 \times 0.03} = \dfrac{129.38}{0.4} = 323.45 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** If the converter duty cycle were $\alpha = 1$ (direct 220 V applied), the no-load speed would be $N_0 = V/(K\phi) = 220/0.4 = 550$ rpm. At $\alpha = 0.6$, our computed speed 323 rpm is roughly 60% of no-load — consistent with $N \propto \alpha$. ✓

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`
**Explain the function of the commutator and brushes in a brushed DC motor. Why is commutation necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) The **commutator** is a cylindrical assembly of hard-drawn copper segments insulated from each other by mica, mounted on the armature shaft. The **brushes** are stationary carbon/graphite blocks that slide on the commutator surface and connect the rotating armature to the external DC supply.
- (1 Mark) **Function:** The commutator acts as a **mechanical rectifier** — it converts the internally induced alternating EMF (in the rotating armature coils) into a unidirectional DC voltage at the output terminals. The brushes serve as the sliding contact between the stationary external circuit and the rotating commutator.
- (1 Mark) **Why commutation is necessary:** According to Faraday's/Lenz's law, for the armature to develop **unidirectional torque**, the current in each armature coil must reverse every 180° of rotation (at the neutral plane). Without commutation, the torque would reverse every half-turn, producing oscillation instead of continuous rotation. Hence, mechanical commutation is essential to reverse coil currents at the right instant.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`
**With a neat block diagram, explain the principle of operation of a Buck converter. Derive its output voltage expression.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) **Principle:** A Buck converter steps down a DC voltage $V_s$ to a lower DC voltage $V_o$ by rapidly switching a MOSFET in series with the load. The duty cycle $\alpha = T_{on}/T$ controls the average output. When the switch is ON, the inductor stores energy and the current rises; when OFF, the freewheeling diode conducts and the inductor releases energy to the load.
- (1 Mark) **Derivation:** Applying volt-second balance across the inductor in steady state:
  - During $T_{on}$: $V_L = V_s - V_o$ for time $\alpha T$
  - During $T_{off}$: $V_L = -V_o$ for time $(1-\alpha) T$
- (1 Mark) Setting net volt-seconds to zero:
  $(V_s - V_o)\alpha T - V_o(1-\alpha)T = 0 \Rightarrow V_o = \alpha V_s$

> [!WARNING]
> **Part A Pitfall:** Students often write only the final expression $V_o = \alpha V_s$ without showing the **volt-second balance** derivation. KTU examiners award the derivation mark *only* if the inductor volt-second equation is explicitly written. Always show the two-mode analysis.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choose ONE — 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module 3 carries either Q3 OR Q4 (internal choice). Each sub-part is **7 marks**. Total = 14 marks.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style]`

**Question A:** With the help of a neat constructional diagram, explain the working of a Brushed DC Motor. Derive the EMF and Torque equations. A 4-pole, 250 V, wave-wound DC shunt motor has 1000 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.5 Ω and the flux per pole is 25 mWb. Calculate: (a) The no-load speed. (b) The speed when the motor draws an armature current of 40 A. (c) The shaft torque developed at this load.

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working [7 Marks]

**Constructional Description (3 Marks):**
A brushed DC motor consists of two main parts:
- **Stator (Yoke + Field Poles):** The yoke is the outer cast-iron/steel frame that supports the pole cores and provides a low-reluctance magnetic flux return path. The pole cores are made of laminated silicon steel to reduce eddy current losses. Field windings (or permanent magnets) wound around the pole cores produce a radial magnetic flux $\phi$ across the air gap.
- **Rotor (Armature + Commutator):** The armature core is a slotted cylindrical stack of laminations mounted on the shaft. The armature winding, comprising $Z$ insulated copper conductors, is placed in the slots. The commutator, mounted on the same shaft, consists of wedge-shaped hard-drawn copper segments insulated by mica. Spring-loaded carbon brushes ride on the commutator surface and supply DC current to the rotating winding.

**Working (4 Marks):**
- When a DC voltage $V$ is applied to the brushes, current $I_a$ flows into the armature winding through the commutator segments and brushes.
- The armature conductors, now carrying current and lying in the radial magnetic field $B$ produced by the field poles, experience a tangential **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$ (by Fleming's Left-Hand Rule).
- These forces on all the active conductors collectively produce a torque $T_a$ that rotates the armature in one direction (say, anti-clockwise as viewed from the commutator end).
- As the armature rotates, the conductors cut the field flux and by **Faraday's Law** generate a back-EMF $E_b$ that opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's Law).
- The commutator automatically reverses the current direction in each coil as it crosses the neutral plane, ensuring the torque is always in the same direction — yielding **continuous unidirectional rotation**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles
- $V = 250$ V
- Wave wound $\Rightarrow A = 2$
- $Z = 1000$ conductors
- $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- $\phi = 25 \text{ mWb} = 0.025 \text{ Wb}$

**Step 1 — Compute the EMF Constant $K$:**

$$K = \dfrac{P \, Z}{60 \, A} = \dfrac{4 \times 1000}{60 \times 2} = \dfrac{4000}{120} = 33.33$$

**Step 2 — Compute No-Load Speed $N_0$:**

At no load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V = 250$ V.

$$N_0 = \dfrac{E_b}{K \phi} = \dfrac{250}{33.33 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{250}{0.8333} = 300 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**(a) Answer: $N_0 = 300$ rpm** ✓

**Step 3 — Compute Back-EMF at $I_a = 40$ A:**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (40 \times 0.5) = 250 - 20 = 230 \;\; \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Loaded Speed $N_L$:**

$$N_L = \dfrac{E_b}{K \phi} = \dfrac{230}{33.33 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{230}{0.8333} = 276 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**(b) Answer: $N_L = 276$ rpm** ✓

**Step 5 — Compute Developed Torque $T_a$:**

$$T_a = \dfrac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega} = \dfrac{E_b \cdot I_a}{2\pi N_L/60}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 276}{60} = 28.90 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{230 \times 40}{28.90} = \dfrac{9200}{28.90} = 318.34 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

**(c) Answer: $T_a \approx 318.34$ N·m** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Exam Pitfall 1:** Forgetting that for a **wave-wound** armature, $A = 2$ (not $A = P$). Many students write $A = 4$ for a 4-pole wave motor and lose 1 mark.
> **Board Exam Pitfall 2:** Using $\phi = 25$ Wb instead of $\phi = 0.025$ Wb. **Always convert mWb → Wb** before substituting.
> **Board Exam Pitfall 3:** Computing shaft torque as developed torque. If friction/windage is given, subtract it. Here, $T_{shaft} = T_a$ (no losses specified).

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style]`

**Question B:** With neat circuit diagrams, explain the working of Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost DC-DC converters. Derive the output voltage expression for each topology. A Buck-Boost converter operates from a 24 V DC source with an inductance of 200 μH, switching frequency 50 kHz, and duty cycle 0.4. Calculate the output voltage and the inductor current ripple. If the load draws 2 A, find the average inductor current.

#### Part (a) — Converter Topologies & Derivations [7 Marks]

**Buck Converter (Step-Down) (2 Marks):**
- **Circuit:** MOSFET switch S in series; inductor L, diode D in freewheel, capacitor C across load.
- **Mode 1 (Switch ON):** Diode reverse-biased. Inductor charges, $V_L = V_s - V_o$. Current rises.
- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF):** Diode forward-biased. Inductor freewheels, $V_L = -V_o$. Current falls.
- **Derivation:** Volt-second balance gives $\boxed{V_o = \alpha V_s}$ (always $V_o < V_s$).

**Boost Converter (Step-Up) (2 Marks):**
- **Circuit:** Inductor L in series with source; switch S to ground; diode D to load; capacitor C across load.
- **Mode 1 (Switch ON):** Diode OFF. Inductor charges from source, $V_L = V_s$. Diode blocks current to load — capacitor supplies load.
- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF):** Diode ON. Inductor + source feed the load, $V_L = V_s - V_o$ (negative, since $V_o > V_s$).
- **Derivation:** Volt-second balance gives $\boxed{V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}}$ (always $V_o > V_s$).

**Buck-Boost Converter (Step-Up/Down, Inverted) (3 Marks):**
- **Circuit:** Inductor L in series with switch S (to ground); diode D in series with output (polarity inverted); capacitor across load.
- **Mode 1 (Switch ON):** Diode OFF. Inductor charges from source, $V_L = V_s$. Load is supplied by capacitor.
- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF):** Diode ON. Inductor discharges through diode into load with **reversed polarity** — output is negative.
- **Derivation:**

$$V_s \cdot \alpha T + (-V_o)(1 - \alpha)T = 0$$

$$\alpha V_s = V_o(1 - \alpha)$$

$$\boxed{V_o = -\dfrac{\alpha V_s}{1 - \alpha}}$$

The negative sign indicates **inverted output polarity** with respect to common ground.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $V_s = 24$ V
- $L = 200 \, \mu H = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ H
- $f_s = 50 \text{ kHz} = 50,000$ Hz
- $\alpha = 0.4$
- $I_o = 2$ A

**Step 1 — Compute Switching Period:**

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{1}{50,000} = 20 \;\; \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Compute Output Voltage (Buck-Boost):**

$$V_o = -\dfrac{\alpha V_s}{1 - \alpha} = -\dfrac{0.4 \times 24}{1 - 0.4} = -\dfrac{9.6}{0.6} = -16 \;\; \text{V}$$

**Output Voltage: $V_o = -16$ V** (negative polarity — inverted output) ✓

**Step 3 — Compute Inductor Current Ripple:**

For a Buck-Boost converter, the inductor voltage during switch-ON is $V_s$ and the ON-time is $\alpha T$:

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot \alpha T}{L} = \dfrac{24 \times 0.4 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{192 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.96 \;\; \text{A}$$

**Inductor Current Ripple: $\Delta I_L = 0.96$ A** ✓

**Step 4 — Compute Average Inductor Current:**

For a Buck-Boost converter, the power balance gives:

$$V_s \cdot I_{L,avg} \cdot \alpha = V_o \cdot I_o$$

Solving for average inductor current:

$$I_{L,avg} = \dfrac{I_o}{1 - \alpha} = \dfrac{2}{1 - 0.4} = \dfrac{2}{0.6} = 3.33 \;\; \text{A}$$

**Average Inductor Current: $I_{L,avg} = 3.33$ A** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss in Choice B:**
> 1. **Wrong formula for inductor ripple:** Some students use the boost-converter formula. The Buck-Boost ripple is $V_s \alpha T / L$ (not $V_o \alpha T / L$).
> 2. **Missing the negative sign:** The output voltage is $-16$ V, not $+16$ V. Polarity is crucial in a board exam — losing the sign costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong $I_{L,avg}$ formula:** The relationship $I_{L,avg} = I_o / (1 - \alpha)$ is specific to Buck-Boost. Do NOT use $I_{L,avg} = I_o$ (that is for Buck) or $I_{L,avg} = I_o \cdot \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$ (that is wrong).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and stick it on your study wall 3 days before the exam.**

- ✅ **Brushed DC Motor = Armature + Field + Commutator + Brushes** — the four-part identity.
- ✅ The **commutator is a mechanical rectifier** — converts internal AC to external DC.
- ✅ **Back-EMF** $E_b = \dfrac{\phi P Z N}{60 A}$ — opposes applied voltage; Lenz's Law in action.
- ✅ **Armature Torque** $T_a = \dfrac{\phi P Z I_a}{2\pi A}$ — linear with current; $T_a \propto I_a$.
- ✅ **Speed Equation** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \phi}$ — the foundation of every speed-control problem.
- ✅ **Wave winding** → $A = 2$ (always); **Lap winding** → $A = P$.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = E_b / V$ (ideal, electrical side).
- ✅ **Buck converter** → $V_o = \alpha V_s$ (step-down, $V_o < V_s$).
- ✅ **Boost converter** → $V_o = V_s / (1 - \alpha)$ (step-up, $V_o > V_s$).
- ✅ **Buck-Boost** → $V_o = -\alpha V_s / (1 - \alpha)$ (inverted, $V_o$ can be $\lessgtr V_s$).
- ✅ **Cuk/SEPIC** → $V_o = \alpha V_s / (1 - \alpha)$ (non-inverting buck-boost).
- ✅ **Duty cycle** $\alpha = T_{on} / T = T_{on} \cdot f_s$, range $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ (Buck) or $0 \le \alpha < 1$ (Boost/Buck-Boost).
- ✅ **EV Context:** Buck choppers regulate 12 V / 48 V auxiliary bus; BDC motors drive HVAC, wipers, windows, pumps; field weakening is **obsolete** in modern auxiliaries; **PWM/chopper control** is the industry standard.
- ✅ **Always convert mWb → Wb** and use **SI units** before substituting in formulas.
- ✅ **Always show volt-second balance** for converter problems — never write the final formula directly.
- ✅ **Watch the sign of $V_o$** in Buck-Boost problems — examiners are strict about polarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brushless Electric Motors - Brushless DC Motor, Switched Reluctance Motors, Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Brushless Electric Motors - Brushless DC Motor, Switched Reluctance Motors, Induction Motor # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Brushless DC Motor (BLDC) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A *Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)* is a synchronous electric motor powered by direct-current electricity through a solid-state inverter, where the rotor carries permanent magnets and the stator carries polyphase armature windings. Commutation (the switching of current between phases) is achieved electronically rather than by mechanical brushes, typically using rotor position feedback from Hall-effect sensors or sensorless back-EMF estimation. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of a BLDC motor like a **synchronized rowing team**. The oarsmen (the *stator windings*) row in a perfectly timed sequence, and a coxswain (the *electronic controller + position sensor*) shouts commands ("ROW!") at exactly the right moment so that each oar pulls water in unison. The boat (the *permanent magnet rotor*) glides forward because the forces are always applied at the *correct angular position*. There are no rusty oar-locks (no brushes) to wear out. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for EVs:** BLDC motors are the **default choice** for most modern electric two-wheelers (e.g., Ola S1, Ather 450) and many entry-level four-wheelers because of their high efficiency (**90–95%**), high power density (**>3 kW/kg**), excellent low-speed torque, and silent operation. --- ## 1.2 Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A *Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)* is a doubly-salient, singly-excited synchronous electric machine.
