Both the stator and rotor have salient (projecting) poles; the rotor carries **neither permanent magnets nor windings**. Torque is produced by the tendency of the rotor to align with the magnetic field produced by excited stator phases, exploiting the principle of *minimum reluctance* (variable reluctance principle). ### Conceptual Analogy Picture a **reluctant dog being pulled by a leash tied to two posts (stator poles)**, and the dog runs on a circular track (rotor). When one post tugs, the dog runs toward it. As soon as the dog gets close, the second post tugs, pulling the dog onward. The dog never resists because it always runs toward the strongest pull — this is **minimum reluctance**. No dog has a leash attached permanently (no rotor winding, no magnets); it just *chases* the magnetic pull. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for EVs:** SRMs are rugged, fault-tolerant, and can operate at very high speeds. Their drawback is **torque ripple** and acoustic noise. They are used in some commercial vehicles (e.g., the **Renault Fluence Z.E.** used a reluctance-based motor, and modern premium EVs are revisiting SR technology). --- ## 1.3 Three-Phase Induction Motor (IM) / Asynchronous Motor > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A *Three-Phase Induction Motor* is an asynchronous AC motor in which the stator carries three-phase distributed windings supplied by a variable-frequency inverter, while the rotor carries either a *squirrel-cage* (short-circuited conducting bars) or *wound* (slip-ring) winding. Torque is produced by the interaction of the stator's rotating magnetic field with rotor-induced currents, requiring a non-zero *slip* between synchronous speed and rotor speed. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **Ferris wheel (the stator's rotating magnetic field)** spinning at, say, 30 RPM. Now place a **sealed metal hamster wheel (the squirrel-cage rotor)** next to it — not touching, but magnetically coupled. The spinning Ferris wheel drags the hamster wheel along by **induction** (no direct contact). The hamster wheel can never quite catch up to the Ferris wheel's speed; the *difference* (the **slip**) is what creates the push. If the hamster wheel *did* reach the same speed, it would just sit there with no force — like a passenger on a moving train feels nothing once they walk at train speed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for EVs:** The **Tesla Model S, Model 3, and Model Y** use a *copper-rotor* induction motor (now evolving toward permanent-magnet-free designs). Induction motors are extremely rugged, cheap, and have no rare-earth magnets — a major supply-chain advantage. --- ## 1.4 Summary Comparison at a Glance | Feature | BLDC Motor | SRM | Induction Motor | |---|---|---|---| | Rotor Type | Permanent Magnets (PM) | Salient Steel (no PM, no winding) | Squirrel-Cage / Wound | | Commutation | Electronic (sensor or sensorless) | Electronic (rotor position based) | Inherent (no commutation) | | Slip Required | No (synchronous) | No (synchronous) | **Yes** (asynchronous) | | Efficiency Range | **90–95%** | 85–92% | 85–94% | | Power Density | Very High | Moderate | High | | Cost | High (rare-earth PM) | Low | Lowest | | Notable EV Use | Ola, Ather, most 2-wheelers | Renault Fluence, some in-wheel | Tesla, Mercedes EQS (rear) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Brushless DC Motor — Operational Theory ### 2.1.1 Construction Topology - **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with slots carrying a 3-phase (or multi-phase) star/delta winding. Winding is **distributed** and usually **short-pitched**. - **Rotor:** Surface-mounted or interior-mounted **permanent magnets** (typically NdFeB or SmCo) arranged in pairs (2-pole, 4-pole, 6-pole, …, $p$ pole pairs). ### 2.1.2 Principle of Operation 1. A **3-phase inverter** (6 MOSFETs/IGBTs arranged as 3 half-bridge legs) feeds the stator phases. 2. A **rotor position sensor** (Hall-effect ICs at 120° electrical spacing, or encoder) detects the magnet's angular position. 3. The controller energizes exactly **two phases at a time** (six-step commutation / trapezoidal drive) such that the stator field always *leads* the rotor magnet field by ~90° electrical, producing continuous unidirectional torque. ### 2.1.3 Back-EMF & Torque Equation (Trapezoidal BLDC) The back-EMF in each phase is **trapezoidal** in shape (constant ±$E$ over 120° electrical intervals and zero over 60°). The phase voltage equation in the conduction interval: $$ V = R \cdot i + L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + e $$ where $e = K_e \cdot \omega$ is the back-EMF, $K_e$ is the back-EMF constant in **V·s/rad**, and $\omega$ is the mechanical angular speed in **rad/s**. The **average electromagnetic torque** for a 3-phase, 2-pole-pair BLDC under 6-step conduction: $$ T_e = \frac{2 \cdot K_e \cdot I}{\text{(for } p \text{ pole pairs, general form below)}} $$ More generally (with $p$ pole pairs): $$ T_e = p \cdot K_t \cdot I_a $$ where $K_t$ is the torque constant in **N·m/A** and $I_a$ is the DC-bus current drawn by the inverter. ### 2.1.4 Speed and Power Equations $$ \omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e} $$ $$ P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega = K_e \cdot I_a \cdot \omega = K_t \cdot I_a \cdot \omega $$ --- ## 2.2 Switched Reluctance Motor — Operational Theory ### 2.2.1 Construction - **Stator:** Salient poles (e.g., 8 stator poles) with concentrated coils wrapped around each pole. - **Rotor:** Salient poles (e.g., 6 rotor poles) — **plain laminated steel with NO magnets and NO windings.** - A standard configuration is the **8/6 SRM** (8 stator / 6 rotor poles); other variants exist (6/4, 12/8, 10/6). ### 2.2.2 Reluctance Torque Derivation (Conceptual) The co-energy $W_{co}'(\theta,i)$ stored in a phase with inductance $L(\theta)$ carrying current $i$ is: $$ W_{co}'(\theta, i) = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2 $$ The instantaneous electromagnetic torque from one phase is: $$ T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W_{co}'(\theta, i)}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU:** The torque in an SRM depends only on the **square of the current** and the **slope of inductance** with rotor angle — never on the *sign* of current. This means the inverter can use **unipolar** switching (one IGBT per phase leg, with a freewheeling diode) instead of the bipolar half-bridge used in BLDC. This makes the SRM inverter cheaper and inherently more fault-tolerant. ### 2.2.3 Inductance Profile For a typical 8/6 SRM, the phase inductance varies between $L_{min}$ (rotor pole aligned *between* stator poles) and $L_{max}$ (rotor pole *aligned with* stator pole). The idealized profile $L(\theta)$ over one rotor pole pitch $\tau_r = 60°$ (mechanical) has three regions: rising, constant (aligned), and falling. ### 2.2.4 Average Torque $$ T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{rms}^2 \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{\Delta \theta} $$ where $\Delta L = L_{max} - L_{min}$ over the effective torque-producing angular span. --- ## 2.3 Three-Phase Induction Motor — Operational Theory ### 2.3.1 Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) When the stator's 3-phase windings, displaced by 120° in space, are fed by balanced 3-phase AC displaced by 120° in time, the resultant air-gap flux becomes a **rotating magnetic field** of constant magnitude rotating at the **synchronous speed**: $$ N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} $$ where $f$ is the supply frequency in **Hz** and $P$ is the total number of stator poles. ### 2.3.2 Slip Definition $$ s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} $$ For an induction motor driving an EV traction load, $s$ typically lies in the range **0.01 to 0.05 (1%–5%)** at full load. ### 2.3.3 Rotor Frequency Currents induced in the rotor have a frequency of $f_r = s \cdot f$. ### 2.3.4 Equivalent Circuit & Torque Equation (Per-Phase, Referred to Stator) The Thevenin equivalent circuit yields the air-gap power transferred and hence the electromagnetic torque: $$ T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{\left(R_{th} + R_r'/s\right)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2} $$ where $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$ is the **synchronous mechanical angular speed** in rad/s. ### 2.3.5 Three Operating Regions of Slip | Region | Slip Range | Behaviour | |---|---|---| | **Motoring** | $0 < s < 1$ | Normal EV traction — positive torque, rotor below sync speed | | **Generating (Regen Braking)** | $s < 0$ | Rotor driven *above* synchronous speed — mechanical power converted *back* to electrical energy (regenerative braking in EVs) | | **Plugging (Reverse)** | $s > 1$ | Reverse rotation, used only for rapid braking | ### 2.3.6 Torque-Slip Characteristic (Classic K-curve) For low slip, the torque is approximately linear: $$ T_e \approx \frac{3 \cdot V^2 \cdot s}{\omega_s \cdot R_r'} \quad \text{(approx. linear region)} $$ The maximum (breakdown) torque $T_{max}$ occurs at slip $s_{max}$: $$ s_{max} = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}} $$ --- ## 2.4 Master Formula Cheat Sheet (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Convention:** Voltage in **V**, Current in **A**, Resistance in **Ω**, Speed in **rad/s** or **RPM**, Torque in **N·m**, Power in **W**, Inductance in **H**, Flux in **Wb**. | # | Motor | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Key | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | BLDC | Back-EMF | $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$: V·s/rad, $\omega$: rad/s | | 2 | BLDC | Torque | $T_e = p \cdot K_t \cdot I_a$ | $p$: pole pairs, $K_t$: N·m/A | | 3 | BLDC | Mechanical Power | $P_m = T_e \cdot \omega$ | — | | 4 | BLDC | No-load Speed | $\omega_0 = V / K_e$ | — | | 5 | SRM | Instantaneous Torque | $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | $L$: H, $\theta$: rad | | 6 | SRM | Average Torque | $T_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{rms}^2 \cdot \tfrac{\Delta L}{\Delta \theta}$ | $\Delta L = L_{max} - L_{min}$ | | 7 | SRM | Stroke Energy | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (L_{max} - L_{min}) \cdot I^2$ | Energy converted per stroke | | 8 | IM | Synchronous Speed (RPM) | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | $f$: Hz, $P$: poles | | 9 | IM | Synchronous Speed (rad/s) | $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$ | $p$: pole pairs | | 10 | IM | Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ | dimensionless | | 11 | IM | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | | 12 | IM | Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e = \tfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \tfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th}+R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th}+X_r')^2}$ | Thevenin equivalent | | 13 | IM | Max Torque Slip | $s_{max} = R_r' / \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th}+X_r')^2}$ | dimensionless | | 14 | ALL | EV Power Train | $P_{wheel} = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ | — | ### Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **KTU Application:** In a typical 100 kW EV traction motor (say, BLDC with $p=3$, $K_t = 1.5$ N·m/A), the rated current is **$I_a = 100000 / (3 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 1000 \cdot \pi/30) \approx 212$ A**. Understanding these formulas lets you size the inverter's IGBT current rating, the DC bus cable, and the battery pack's continuous discharge rating. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Speed from Rotating Magnetic Field The 3-phase stator MMF produced by balanced sinusoidal currents of angular frequency $\omega_e = 2\pi f$ in windings spatially displaced by 120° is: $$ F_a(\theta, t) = F_{max} \cdot \sin(\omega_e t) \cdot \cos(p\theta) $$ $$ F_b(\theta, t) = F_{max} \cdot \sin(\omega_e t - 120°) \cdot \cos(p\theta - 120°) $$ $$ F_c(\theta, t) = F_{max} \cdot \sin(\omega_e t - 240°) \cdot \cos(p\theta - 240°) $$ Summing: $$ F_{total}(\theta, t) = F_a + F_b + F_c $$ Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(A)\cos(B) = \tfrac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$: $$ F_{total} = \tfrac{3}{2} F_{max} \cdot \sin(\omega_e t - p\theta) $$ The flux density wave $B(\theta,t) \propto F_{total}$ has a **constant peak** magnitude of $\tfrac{3}{2} F_{max}$ rotating such that $\omega_e t - p\theta = \text{const}$. Differentiating with respect to time: $$ \frac{d}{dt}(\omega_e t - p\theta) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_e - p \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = 0 $$ $$ \therefore \quad \omega_{sync,\,mech} = \frac{\omega_e}{p} = \frac{2\pi f}{p} \quad \text{[rad/s]} $$ Converting to RPM: $$ N_s = \omega_{sync,\,mech} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{2\pi f}{p} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{120 f}{P} $$ since $P = 2p$ is the total number of poles. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: SRM Instantaneous Torque from Co-energy Principle **Step 1 — Define co-energy.** For a linear magnetic system, the magnetic co-energy $W_{co}'$ stored in a winding is: $$ W_{co}' = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2 $$ where $L(\theta)$ is the phase inductance (a function of rotor angular position $\theta$). **Step 2 — Apply virtual work principle.** If the rotor moves an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$ at constant current, the mechanical work done *by* the system equals the change in co-energy: $$ T_e \cdot d\theta = dW_{co}' = \frac{\partial W_{co}'}{\partial \theta} \cdot d\theta + \frac{\partial W_{co}'}{\partial i} \cdot di $$ Holding current constant ($di = 0$): $$ T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W_{co}'}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = \text{const}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left[\frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2\right] $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate.** Since $i$ is constant: $$ T_e = \frac{i^2}{2} \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} $$ **Step 4 — Sign convention.** When $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$ (rising inductance), $T_e > 0$, i.e., the rotor is pulled *toward* alignment. When $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta} < 0$ (falling inductance), $T_e < 0$, producing braking torque. Therefore, for *motoring* operation, the phase must be energized *only* in the rising-inductance region — this is the **firing-angle control principle** of SRMs. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Induction Motor Torque Equation (Approximate Linear Region) **Step 1 — Start with per-phase rotor referred circuit.** The air-gap voltage $E_g$ induced in the rotor referred to stator is shared across the rotor branch $(R_r'/s + jX_r')$. The rotor current $I_r'$ is: $$ I_r' = \frac{E_g}{R_r'/s + jX_r'} = \frac{s \cdot E_g}{R_r' + j s X_r'} $$ **Step 2 — Air-gap power per phase.** $P_{ag,\,\text{phase}} = \text{Re}\{E_g \cdot (I_r')^*\}$. The total air-gap power for 3 phases is: $$ P_{ag} = 3 \cdot \frac{s \cdot E_g^2 \cdot R_r'}{R_r'^2 + (s X_r')^2} $$ **Step 3 — Electromagnetic torque.** Since $P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$: $$ T_e = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \cdot E_g^2 \cdot R_r'}{R_r'^2 + (s X_r')^2} $$ **Step 4 — Low-slip linearization.** When $s \ll R_r'/X_r'$, the term $(sX_r')^2 \ll R_r'^2$, so: $$ T_e \approx \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \cdot E_g^2}{R_r'} \propto s $$ This is the **linear region** of the torque-slip curve, valid for normal EV cruising where slip is small. **Step 5 — Find maximum torque.** Differentiating $T_e$ w.r.t. $s$ and setting to zero: $$ \frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_{max} = \frac{R_r'}{X_r'} \quad \text{(approximately, ignoring } R_s, R_{th}\text{)} $$ $$ T_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot E_g^2}{2 \cdot \omega_s \cdot X_r'} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** in this approximate form. In practice, the exact Thevenin version (with $R_{th}, X_{th}$) shows that $T_{max}$ does depend weakly on $R_r'$, but the slip at which it occurs is directly proportional to $R_r'$. This is why EV inverters sometimes add *dynamic rotor resistance control* through flux optimization — analogous to how BLDC field-weakening reduces $E$ to extend speed range. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Motor | Criterion | BLDC | SRM | Induction Motor | |---|---|---|---| | Peak Power Demand (e.g., 0–100 km/h acceleration) | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | | Constant Cruise Efficiency | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | | Cost per kW | Low (but PM-dependent) | **Lowest** | **Lowest** | | Thermal Robustness | Moderate (PM demagnetization risk) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | | Reliability in Harsh Environment | Good | **Excellent** | Good | | Magnet-free Requirement | ✗ (PM required) | ✓ | ✓ | | Field-Weakening for High Speed | Moderate | **Excellent** | Excellent | | Acoustic Noise & Ripple | Low | **High** | Low–Moderate | | Typical EV Applications | Two-wheelers, small cars | In-wheel, commercial | Tesla, performance EVs | --- ## 3.5 Numerical Practice (Worked Example) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sample KTU Numerical:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz three-phase induction motor drives an EV at a slip of 3%. Calculate: (a) Synchronous speed, (b) Actual rotor speed, (c) Frequency of rotor current. **Solution:** **(a)** $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz $$ N_s = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM} $$ **(b)** $s = 0.03$ $$ N_r = N_s \cdot (1 - s) = 1500 \cdot (1 - 0.03) = 1500 \cdot 0.97 = 1455 \text{ RPM} $$ **(c)** $f_r = s \cdot f = 0.03 \cdot 50 = 1.5$ Hz The low rotor frequency (1.5 Hz) is why squirrel-cage rotor conductors need *no insulation and no slotting optimization* — the slip frequency is so low that skin effect is negligible, and simple cast-aluminum bars work perfectly. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams below render as **block-level functional architectures** to clearly show the control flow, sensor feedback, and power flow inside each motor drive system. This is the format KTU examiners expect when asked to "draw the block diagram" of a BLDC/SRM/IM drive. --- ## 4.1 BLDC Motor Drive — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Battery Pack
DC Source] --> B[DC Link Capacitor] B --> C[Three Phase Voltage Source Inverter
6 IGBT MOSFETs] C -->|3 Phase AC| D[BLDC Motor
Stator Winding] D -->|Back EMF| E[Position and Speed Estimator] F[Hall Sensors / Encoder] -->|Rotor Position| E E -->|Commutation Signals| G[Motor Controller
DSP or FPGA] G -->|PWM Gate Signals| C H[Throttle / Accelerator Pedal] -->|Torque Demand| G I[Battery Management System] -->|State of Charge Limit| G ``` **Operational Logic Flow:** 1. Driver depresses accelerator → Throttle sends torque demand to Controller. 2. Controller reads rotor position from Hall sensors. 3. Controller determines which two of three inverter legs to energize. 4. PWM signals switch the IGBTs to apply current to the correct phases. 5. Inverter output rotates the stator field, which drags the PM rotor in synchronous motion. --- ## 4.2 Switched Reluctance Motor Drive — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Battery Pack DC] --> B[Asymmetric Half Bridge Inverter
One IGBT and One Diode per Phase] B -->|Sequential Phase Excitation| C[SRM Stator Phases] C -->|Variable Reluctance Coupling| D[SRM Rotor
Salient Steel No Magnets] D -->|Mechanical Rotation| E[Output Shaft] F[Rotor Position Sensor
Optical Encoder or Resolver] -->|Angular Position| G[SRM Controller
Firing Angle and Hysteresis Current Control] H[Driver Torque Demand] --> G G -->|Turn On and Turn Off Angles| B G -->|Hysteresis Band 1 Amp| B I[Current Sensor per Phase] -->|Phase Current Feedback| G ``` **Key Control Parameters:** - **Turn-on angle ($\theta_{on}$):** Rotor angle at which the IGBT is switched ON to start energizing a phase. - **Turn-off angle ($\theta_{off}$):** Rotor angle at which the IGBT is switched OFF (current freewheels through diode and decays). - **Hysteresis band:** Current is regulated between $I_{ref} + \Delta I$ and $I_{ref} - \Delta I$ for soft chopping. --- ## 4.3 Induction Motor Drive — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Battery Pack DC] --> B[DC Link Capacitor] B --> C[Three Phase VSI Inverter
Space Vector PWM] C -->|Variable Frequency Variable Voltage 3 Phase| D[Induction Motor Stator] D -->|Rotating Magnetic Field| E[Squirrel Cage Rotor] E -->|Slip Induced Currents| F[Rotor Magnetic Field] F -->|Tangential Force| G[Electromagnetic Torque on Shaft] G --> H[EV Transmission and Wheels] I[Throttle Pedal] --> J[VFD Controller
V by F Control or FOC] K[Speed Sensor Tachometer] --> J L[Current Sensors Three Phases] --> J J -->|SVPWM Gate Signals| C M[BMS] -->|Voltage and Current Limits| J ``` **Control Strategies (in increasing order of complexity):** 1. **V/f (Scalar) Control:** Simplest; maintains constant air-gap flux by keeping V/f ratio constant. Open-loop, no current control. Adequate for low-performance EV applications. 2. **Indirect Field-Oriented Control (IFOC):** Decouples flux and torque producing currents ($i_d$ and $i_q$). Industry standard for EV traction. 3. **Direct Torque Control (DTC):** Bang-bang hysteresis control of flux and torque. Fastest dynamic response, but high ripple. --- ## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — All Three Motor Drives ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Battery DC Bus] --> B[Power Electronic Inverter] B --> C[Motor Type] C --> D1[BLDC
Electronic Commutation] C --> D2[SRM
Variable Reluctance] C --> D3[IM
Slip Based Induction] E[Vehicle Control Unit] -->|Torque Demand RPM Limit| F[Motor Controller] F -->|Gate Pulses| B G[Sensors] -->|Position Speed Current| F ``` This composite block diagram is what KTU examiners often draw on the answer sheet to show **all three motor drives sharing a common architecture** — battery, inverter, controller, sensors, and mechanical output — differing only in the *internal construction* of the motor block. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Q: List any three advantages of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor over a brushed DC motor for EV applications.** **Model Answer (3 points):** 1. **Higher efficiency (90–95% vs. 70–80%):** Absence of brush friction and contact losses means more battery energy is converted to motion, increasing EV range. 2. **No brush wear → longer life and lower maintenance:** Brushed motors require brush replacement every 1000–2000 hours; BLDC motors can run for 20,000+ hours with no service. 3. **Better thermal management:** All heat is generated in the stator (which can be water-jacketed), keeping the PM rotor cool and avoiding permanent-magnet demagnetization. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only the *first* point and forget the others, scoring only **1 of 3 marks**. Always structure with **bullet points or numbered lines** for clarity. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Q: Why does an induction motor never reach synchronous speed under load?** **Model Answer (3 points):** 1. If the rotor were to rotate at synchronous speed ($N_r = N_s$), the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and rotor conductors would be **zero**, meaning **no flux is cut** by the rotor bars. 2. By **Faraday's Law**, zero relative motion means **zero induced EMF** in the rotor, hence **zero rotor current**. 3. Without rotor current, there can be no force on the rotor (Lorentz force $F = BIL \sin\theta = 0$), so the rotor **cannot sustain torque**. Therefore, the rotor must always lag the synchronous speed by a small slip (typically 1–5%). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question B1 — OPTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor**. Discuss the role of Hall-effect sensors in electronic commutation. **(7 marks)** > > **Q: (b)** A 6-pole BLDC motor has a back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.08$ V·s/rad. The DC bus voltage is 48 V and the per-phase resistance is 0.1 Ω. Calculate: (i) No-load speed in RPM. (ii) Speed when drawing 50 A current. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer — (a) Construction, Working & Hall Sensors (7 marks):** **1. Construction (2 marks):** - **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core, 3-phase distributed winding (star connected), wound for $p$ pole pairs (here $p = 3$). - **Rotor:** Surface-mounted Neodymium-Iron-Boron (NdFeB) permanent magnets arranged in alternating N-S pattern, mounted on a steel hub. - **Shaft, bearings, position sensors** mounted on the non-drive end. **2. Working Principle (3 marks):** - A 3-phase inverter (6 switches: T1–T6 in three legs) applies current to two phases at a time in a 6-step sequence. - At any instant, the energized phases produce a stator magnetic field that *leads* the rotor magnet by 90° electrical, creating a torque $T_e = p \cdot K_t \cdot I_a$. - As the rotor turns, the next phase pair is energized, advancing the stator field electronically — this is **electronic commutation**. **3. Role of Hall-effect Sensors (2 marks):** - Three Hall-effect ICs are mounted on the stator at 120° electrical spacing. - They detect the polarity of the passing rotor magnet (N → logic 1, S → logic 0), producing a 3-bit Gray-code-like position signal (e.g., 101, 100, 110, 010, 011, 001). - The controller decodes this 3-bit code and energizes the corresponding pair of inverter switches (e.g., 101 → T1 and T6 ON; current flows from phase A to phase B). - This achieves *synchronous commutation* — the stator field always rotates at exactly the rotor speed, eliminating the need for mechanical brushes. --- **Model Answer — (b) Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** $P = 6$ poles → $p = 3$ pole pairs, $K_e = 0.08$ V·s/rad, $V = 48$ V, $R_a = 0.1$ Ω, $I_a = 50$ A. **(i) No-load speed (3 marks):** At no-load, the current is negligible, so the back-EMF equals the supply voltage: $$ E_0 = V = K_e \cdot \omega_0 $$ $$ \omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e} = \frac{48}{0.08} = 600 \text{ rad/s} $$ Converting to RPM: $$ N_0 = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 600 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{36000}{2\pi} \approx 5730 \text{ RPM} $$ **(ii) Speed at 50 A (4 marks):** The voltage equation under load: $$ V = I_a \cdot R_a + E = I_a \cdot R_a + K_e \cdot \omega $$ Solving for $\omega$: $$ \omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e} = \frac{48 - 50 \cdot 0.1}{0.08} = \frac{48 - 5}{0.08} = \frac{43}{0.08} = 537.5 \text{ rad/s} $$ Converting to RPM: $$ N = 537.5 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 5132 \text{ RPM} $$ **Speed drop due to load:** $$ \Delta N = 5730 - 5132 = 598 \text{ RPM} \quad (\approx 10.4\% \text{ drop}) $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to divide by $p = 3$ again when converting $\omega$ to RPM. The mechanical angular speed in rad/s is already *mechanical* (not electrical), so **do NOT divide by pole pairs**. Just multiply by $60/(2\pi)$. --- ### **Question B1 — OPTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Q: (a)** Explain the **principle of operation** of a **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)**. Derive the expression for instantaneous electromagnetic torque from co-energy. **(7 marks)** > > **Q: (b)** With a block diagram, describe the **vector control (Field-Oriented Control)** scheme for a three-phase induction motor used in EV traction. State its advantages over V/f control. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer — (a) SRM Working & Torque Derivation (7 marks):** **1. Construction & Principle (3 marks):** - SRM has salient poles on both stator (e.g., 8 poles) and rotor (e.g., 6 poles). The rotor carries no windings and no permanent magnets — only laminated steel. - When a stator phase is energized, the rotor moves to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** (maximum inductance) — the rotor pole aligns with the energized stator pole. - Sequential excitation of stator phases creates continuous rotation in either direction. **2. Torque Derivation from Co-energy (4 marks):** For a phase winding with inductance $L(\theta)$ carrying current $i$, the magnetic co-energy is: $$ W_{co}'(\theta, i) = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 $$ Using the principle of virtual work at constant current: $$ T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W_{co}'}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} $$ **Inference:** - Torque is **independent of current direction** (depends on $i^2$), so the inverter uses **unipolar switching**. - Torque is **positive only when** $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$ (rising inductance region). Hence firing angles $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$ must be carefully chosen so that current flows only during the rising-inductance region. - Torque ripple is inherent because $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ is non-sinusoidal. --- **Model Answer — (b) FOC Block Diagram & Advantages (7 marks):** **1. Block Diagram (4 marks):** (See Section 4.3 IM block diagram above for reference. The FOC-specific flow is:) - Measure 3-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ → compute $i_\alpha, i_\beta$ (Clarke transform). - Apply Park transform using rotor flux angle $\theta_e$ → obtain $i_d$ (flux-producing) and $i_q$ (torque-producing) currents in the rotating reference frame. - PI controllers regulate $i_d$ to a reference (e.g., rated flux) and $i_q$ to torque demand (from accelerator pedal). - Inverse Park + SVPWM generates the 6 inverter gate signals. **2. Advantages of FOC over V/f control (3 marks):** 1. **Decoupled control of flux and torque** — independent acceleration and speed regulation, analogous to a separately excited DC motor. 2. **Excellent dynamic response** — torque updates within 1–2 ms, critical for rapid accelerator pedal changes in EVs. 3. **Full torque available down to zero speed** — V/f control loses torque at very low frequencies; FOC maintains rated torque even at standstill (essential for hill-start). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked to "draw the block diagram," students often draw *only* the inverter and motor. You must include the **transformation blocks (Clarke, Park, inverse Park) and the PI controllers** — these are what define FOC and what fetches the differentiation marks. **Also remember to label the rotor flux angle $\theta_e$** as the input to the Park transform — missing it costs 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision sheet — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.** - ✅ **BLDC = PM rotor + 3-phase stator winding + electronic commutation.** No brushes. Synchronous operation. - ✅ **BLDC torque equation:** $T_e = p K_t I_a$. Back-EMF: $E = K_e \omega$. Power: $P = T_e \omega$. - ✅ **Hall sensors produce a 3-bit position code** (101, 100, …, 001) that the controller decodes to fire inverter legs in sequence. - ✅ **SRM has doubly salient poles, no magnets, no rotor winding.** Rotor is plain laminated steel. - ✅ **SRM torque equation:** $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$. Independent of current direction. - ✅ **SRM motoring only when** $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$ — i.e., current must be applied during the **rising inductance region**. - ✅ **SRM inverter is cheaper than BLDC** (asymmetric half-bridge, one IGBT per phase leg) and inherently fault-tolerant. - ✅ **Induction motor torque requires slip** ($s > 0$ for motoring, $s < 0$ for regeneration, $s > 1$ for plugging). - ✅ **Synchronous speed formulas:** $N_s = 120f/P$ (RPM), $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$ (rad/s). - ✅ **Induction motor torque equation** (Thevenin): $T_e = \tfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \tfrac{V_{th}^2 (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th}+R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th}+X_r')^2}$. - ✅ **Linear low-slip approximation:** $T_e \approx \tfrac{3 V^2 s}{\omega_s R_r'}$ — torque is proportional to slip at low loads. - ✅ **Max torque slip:** $s_{max} = R_r' / X_r'$ (approximate). $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_r'$ (approximate). - ✅ **Regenerative braking in EVs:** Induction motor driven above synchronous speed ($s < 0$) acts as a generator, feeding current back into the battery. - ✅ **Tesla's choice of induction motor** is because Tesla wants **no rare-earth magnets** for supply-chain reasons. - ✅ **Renault Fluence Z.E. and certain in-wheel motor designs** use SRM. - ✅ **Most Indian 2-wheelers (Ola, Ather, Bajaj Chetak)** use BLDC because of high efficiency and smooth ride. - ✅ **Always convert $\omega$ (rad/s) to N (RPM) using** $N = \omega \cdot 60 / (2\pi)$ — do **not** divide by pole pairs when converting mechanical rad/s. - ✅ **Always state units** in numerical answers — V, A, Ω, RPM, rad/s, N·m, W.

topics.title: Motor Cooling, Efficiency, Size and Mass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motor Cooling, Efficiency, Size and Mass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motor Cooling in EV/HEV Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Motor cooling* refers to the deliberate management and removal of heat generated within the traction electric motor due to electromagnetic, mechanical, and resistive losses during operation. In Electric and Hybrid Vehicles, it is a **thermal management sub-system** designed to keep the motor windings, permanent magnets, and rotor/stator cores within their permissible operating temperature limits (typically **150°C – 180°C** for Class H insulation), thereby ensuring reliability, peak power delivery, and long service life.

In an EV, the traction motor is the "muscle" of the vehicle. Just like a human muscle heats up during heavy exercise, an electric motor generates waste heat when it pushes the car forward. If we don't cool it down, the magnets lose their strength (a phenomenon called **demagnetization**), the copper windings melt their insulation, and the lubricant in the bearings breaks down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Motorcycle Engine Radiator:**
> Think of the motor like a small internal combustion engine bolted inside the car. A petrol car uses a radiator with coolant to shed heat; similarly, an EV traction motor uses cooling jackets, oil spray, or forced air to dump its waste heat into the vehicle's primary coolant loop (which may also serve the inverter and battery). The difference is that an EV motor is *much* more efficient (90–97%) than an ICE, so it produces *less* heat — but the heat it does produce is concentrated in a very small volume, making **power density** a critical design challenge.

## 1.2 Motor Efficiency in EV/HEV Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Motor efficiency* ($\eta_m$) is the ratio of mechanical output power delivered at the motor shaft to the electrical input power drawn from the inverter/battery. It quantifies how effectively electrical energy is converted into useful rotational mechanical work, with the remainder being dissipated as heat.
>
> $$\eta_m = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$$

KTU students must distinguish between **peak efficiency** (maximum efficiency at the motor's "sweet spot" — typically mid-speed, mid-torque) and **operating efficiency** (average efficiency across the entire drive cycle such as **WLTP** or **NEDC**).

## 1.3 Motor Size and Mass in EV/HEV Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Motor size and mass* in EV design are characterized by two specific metrics:
> 1. **Power Density** (kW/L or W/cm³): The electrical output power per unit volume of the motor.
> 2. **Specific Power** (kW/kg): The electrical output power per unit mass of the motor.
> 3. **Torque Density** (Nm/L) and **Specific Torque** (Nm/kg): The corresponding torque-based metrics.
>
> $$\text{Power Density} = \frac{P_{out}}{V_{motor}} \qquad \text{Specific Power} = \frac{P_{out}}{m_{motor}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Luggage Space:**
> A heavier, bulkier motor in an EV is like stuffing a large suitcase in the trunk of a small car — it eats into passenger space, raises the center of gravity (affecting handling), and reduces range because more mass must be accelerated. Hence, EV designers obsess over **kW per kg** and **Nm per litre** like smartphone designers obsess over battery mAh per gram.

## 1.4 Why These Three Topics Are Linked

The three sub-topics are deeply intertwined. Higher power density → more losses per unit volume → more heat to remove → more aggressive cooling required → potentially heavier cooling system → reduced net specific power. This **thermal–electromagnetic–mechanical trade-off** forms the core design challenge of EV traction motors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cooling Methods for EV Traction Motors

The KTU syllabus expects students to identify **why** each cooling strategy is chosen for a given motor type.

### A. Air Cooling
- **Natural Convection:** No fan; relies on buoyancy-driven airflow. Used only for very small, low-power motors (<5 kW).
- **Forced Air Cooling:** External fan or vehicle-ram air. Common in Tesla's older Roadster induction motor.

### B. Liquid Cooling (Indirect)
- **Water–Glycol Jacket:** A cooling jacket surrounds the stator housing. The stator — where **major copper losses** are concentrated — is cooled externally.
- **Common in:** PMSM and Induction motors used in Nissan Leaf, BMW i3.

### C. Oil Cooling (Direct or Indirect)
- **Splash Cooling:** Motor spins in an oil bath; oil sloshes over end-windings.
- **Oil Spray/Jet Cooling:** Nozzles spray cool oil directly onto the **end-windings** and **rotor** (as used in Tesla Model 3/Y).
- ATF (Automatic Transmission Fluid) is often used — same fluid cools the gearbox.

### D. Stator Oil Spray (Direct Winding Cooling)
- Coolest, most effective. Allows **>5× increase** in current density vs air cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The rotor of a PMSM contains **permanent magnets** that cannot tolerate temperatures above ~**180°C** (NdFeB) or ~**250°C** (Samarium Cobalt). Hence rotor cooling — though mechanically difficult — is the **holy grail** of high-performance EV motors.

## 2.2 Loss Mechanisms and Efficiency Analysis

The total losses inside an EV motor can be broken down as follows:

1. **Copper Losses (I²R Losses / Stator Joule Heating):** Proportional to current squared.
2. **Iron/Core Losses (Hysteresis + Eddy Current):** Concentrated in the stator teeth; scale with frequency and flux density squared.
3. **Mechanical Losses:** Bearing friction + windage (air drag on rotating rotor).
4. **Stray Load Losses:** Harmonics, leakage flux, non-idealities (~1–2% of input).

## 2.3 Size and Mass — Theoretical Limits

The **specific power** of an EV traction motor is bounded by:

- **Magnetic saturation limit** of the steel lamination (~2.0–2.2 T).
- **Current density limit** of the windings (typically **5–15 A/mm²** for air-cooled, **15–30 A/mm²** for liquid-cooled).
- **Mechanical integrity** of the rotor at high tip speeds (<200 m/s for safe operation).

A widely cited empirical envelope for state-of-the-art EV traction motors is approximately **5–7 kW/kg** specific power and **10–15 kW/L** power density.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are exam-ready. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out}+P_{loss}}$ | Motor efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $P_{loss} = P_{cu} + P_{fe} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$ | Total loss decomposition | W |
| 3 | $P_{cu} = 3 \cdot I^2 \cdot R_{ph}$ | Three-phase copper loss | W |
| 4 | $P_{fe} = k_h \cdot f \cdot B^2 + k_e \cdot f^2 \cdot B^2$ | Steinmetz core loss (hysteresis + eddy) | W |
| 5 | $P_{mech} = P_{bearing} + P_{windage}$ | Mechanical losses | W |
| 6 | $\text{Power Density} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V_{motor}}$ | Power per unit volume | kW/L or W/cm³ |
| 7 | $\text{Specific Power} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{m_{motor}}$ | Power per unit mass | kW/kg |
| 8 | $\text{Torque Density} = \dfrac{T}{V_{motor}}$ | Torque per unit volume | Nm/L |
| 9 | $\text{Specific Torque} = \dfrac{T}{m_{motor}}$ | Torque per unit mass | Nm/kg |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\omega_m}$ | Shaft torque from power and speed | Nm |
| 11 | $R_{th,total} = R_{th,cu-c} + R_{th,c-amb}$ | Thermal resistance (steady-state) | °C/W |
| 12 | $\Delta T = P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,total}$ | Temperature rise above ambient | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how $P_{cu} \propto I^2$ — this is why EV motors use **high voltage (400V / 800V architectures)** rather than high current. Halving current reduces copper losses by a factor of 4 for the same power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Motor Efficiency from Loss Components

We start from the **principle of conservation of energy**:

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss}$$

Step 1: Expand the loss term into its individual physical components:

$$P_{loss} = P_{cu} + P_{fe} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$$

Step 2: Substitute the per-phase copper loss for a balanced three-phase machine. If $I_{ph}$ is the RMS phase current and $R_{ph}$ is the per-phase resistance:

$$P_{cu} = 3 \cdot I_{ph}^2 \cdot R_{ph}$$

Step 3: Apply the Steinmetz equation for iron losses at frequency $f$ and peak flux density $B$:

$$P_{fe} = k_h \cdot f \cdot B^{1.6\text{ to }2} + k_e \cdot f^2 \cdot B^2$$

Step 4: Combine all into the efficiency expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_m &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \\
&= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + 3 I_{ph}^2 R_{ph} + P_{fe} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5: For a PMSM operating at speed $\omega_m$ (rad/s) with shaft torque $T$:

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega_m$$

Therefore the full efficiency expression becomes:

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T \cdot \omega_m}{T \cdot \omega_m + 3 I_{ph}^2 R_{ph} + P_{fe} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}}
$$

**Interpretation:** At low torque (and hence low current), the constant losses ($P_{fe}$, $P_{mech}$) dominate, reducing efficiency. At very high torque, the $I^2R$ copper losses dominate. Peak efficiency lies in between — typically around **10–30% of rated torque** at base speed.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Part B Style

> **Problem:** A Tesla Model 3 traction PMSM draws an RMS phase current of **400 A** at a phase resistance of **$8 \text{ m}\Omega$**. The iron loss is **1.2 kW**, the mechanical loss is **600 W**, and stray load loss is **400 W**. The motor delivers **150 kW** of mechanical output power. Calculate the motor efficiency.

**Given:**
- $I_{ph} = 400$ A
- $R_{ph} = 0.008 \ \Omega$
- $P_{fe} = 1200$ W
- $P_{mech} = 600$ W
- $P_{stray} = 400$ W
- $P_{out} = 150{,}000$ W

**Step 1:** Compute three-phase copper loss:

$$
P_{cu} = 3 \cdot I_{ph}^2 \cdot R_{ph} = 3 \cdot (400)^2 \cdot 0.008
$$

$$
P_{cu} = 3 \cdot 160{,}000 \cdot 0.008 = 3{,}840 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2:** Total losses:

$$
P_{loss} = 3840 + 1200 + 600 + 400 = 6040 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3:** Input power:

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss} = 150{,}000 + 6{,}040 = 156{,}040 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4:** Efficiency:

$$
\eta_m = \frac{150{,}000}{156{,}040} = 0.9613 = 96.13\%
$$

**Result:** Motor efficiency $\approx$ **96.13%**. This is realistic for a high-performance EV PMSM.

## 3.3 Derivation: Specific Power and Torque Density Relationship

From the electromagnetic power equation of a rotating machine:

$$
P_{out} = T \cdot \omega_m
$$

We define:
- $\sigma_p = \dfrac{P_{out}}{m}$ (specific power in kW/kg)
- $\sigma_T = \dfrac{T}{m}$ (specific torque in Nm/kg)

Dividing $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega_m$ by mass $m$:

$$
\sigma_p = \sigma_T \cdot \omega_m
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A motor designed for **high torque at low speed** (gearbox-friendly) can have low specific power but very high specific torque. Conversely, a **high-speed motor** (e.g., 20,000 RPM) achieves high specific power but needs a reduction gearbox. EV traction motors typically target **$\sigma_p \approx 5\text{–}7 \text{ kW/kg}$** and **$\sigma_T \approx 10\text{–}15 \text{ Nm/kg}$**.

## 3.4 Thermal Derivation — Steady-State Heat Rejection

In steady state, the heat generated equals the heat rejected. Using thermal resistance analog of Ohm's law:

$$
\Delta T = P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,total}
$$

Where $R_{th,total} = R_{th,cu\text{-}case} + R_{th,case\text{-}coolant}}$. For the example above with $P_{loss} = 6040$ W and a typical $R_{th,total} = 0.02$ °C/W (liquid cooled):

$$
\Delta T = 6040 \cdot 0.02 = 120.8 \text{ °C}
$$

This means if coolant is at 60°C, the winding reaches **~181°C** — close to the Class H limit. Hence **thermal management is the binding constraint**, not electromagnetic design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cooling System Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Traction Motor Unit] --> B[Heat Generation Sources]
    B --> B1[Stator Copper Loss - I2R]
    B --> B2[Core Iron Loss - Hysteresis and Eddy]
    B --> B3[Rotor Magnet Heating]
    B --> B4[Bearing Friction Loss]

    B1 --> C1[Stator Jacket Cooling - Water Glycol]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2[Rotor End Windings Oil Spray]
    B4 --> C3[Bearing Oil Sump]

    C1 --> D[Common Coolant Manifold]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Vehicle Thermal Loop - Chiller and Radiator]
    E --> F[Ambient Heat Rejection]
```

## 4.2 Motor Loss & Efficiency Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIn[Electrical Input Power - Pin] --> Pcu[Copper Loss - 3 I2 R]
    PIn --> Pfe[Iron Loss - Hysteresis + Eddy]
    PIn --> Pmech[Mechanical Loss - Friction + Windage]
    PIn --> Pstray[Stray Load Loss - Harmonics]

    Pcu --> PTotal[Total Loss - Ploss]
    Pfe --> PTotal
    Pmech --> PTotal
    Pstray --> PTotal

    PIn --> Pout
    PTotal --> Pout
    Pout[Mechanical Output Power - Pout = T omega]

    Pout --> Eff[Eff = Pout divided by Pin]
```

## 4.3 Motor Size & Mass Trade-Off Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Motor Design] --> S1[Higher Power Density]
    M --> S2[Lower Mass]
    M --> S3[Better Cooling]

    S1 --> T1[Smaller Volume and Lighter]
    S1 --> T2[More Heat Per Unit Volume]
    T2 --> S3
    S3 --> T3[Heavier Cooling System]
    T3 --> S2
    S2 --> T4[Improved Vehicle Range and Handling]
    T4 --> M

    style M fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S1 fill:#add8e6
    style S2 fill:#90ee90
    style S3 fill:#ffb6c1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram above captures the *engineering paradox* — pushing any one performance metric affects the others. Real EV motor designers iterate through electromagnetic FEA (Finite Element Analysis), CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics), and structural FEA simultaneously to converge on an optimal design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **List any three cooling methods used in EV traction motors and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Forced Air Cooling** — Advantage: Simple, low cost, no leakage risk.
2. **Water–Glycol Jacket Cooling** — Advantage: High heat capacity, allows sustained high-power operation.
3. **Stator Oil Spray (Direct Winding Cooling)** — Advantage: Direct contact with end-windings gives >5× higher current density capability.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
> **Define specific power and power density for an EV traction motor. Why are these metrics important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Specific power (kW/kg) = output power per unit mass; Power density (kW/L) = output power per unit volume. These metrics quantify how compact and lightweight the motor is. They are crucial because they directly affect vehicle range, packaging space, payload capacity, and driving dynamics. A high specific power allows the OEM to allocate less mass to the powertrain and more to batteries or passengers.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) Module 3 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice between two questions.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Motor Efficiency & Losses

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the various loss components in a PMSM traction motor used in electric vehicles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 kW PMSM traction motor operates at rated conditions. The three-phase RMS current is 350 A, the per-phase resistance is $10 \text{ m}\Omega$, iron loss is 1.5 kW, mechanical loss is 500 W, and stray load loss is 350 W. Compute the total losses and the motor efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **Copper (I²R) Loss:** Caused by current flowing through stator winding resistance. Scales as $I^2$. Largest loss component at high torque. **Mitigation:** High voltage architecture, hairpin windings, short end-turns.
2. **Iron (Core) Loss:** Hysteresis loss (from cyclic magnetization) + eddy current loss (from circulating currents in laminations). Concentrated in stator teeth. Scales with frequency and $B^{1.6-2}$. **Mitigation:** Thin-grain-oriented silicon steel (0.2–0.35 mm lamination).
3. **Mechanical Loss:** Bearing friction + windage (air drag on rotor surface and end-caps). **Mitigation:** Ceramic hybrid bearings, sealed rotor chambers.
4. **Stray Load Loss:** Caused by leakage flux, slot harmonics, and non-sinusoidal current from PWM inverter. Typically 1–2% of rated power.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Calculate copper loss.

$$
P_{cu} = 3 \cdot I^2 \cdot R = 3 \cdot (350)^2 \cdot 0.010 = 3 \cdot 122{,}500 \cdot 0.010 = 3{,}675 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum all losses.

$$
P_{loss} = 3675 + 1500 + 500 + 350 = 6{,}025 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate input power.

$$
P_{in} = 100{,}000 + 6{,}025 = 106{,}025 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute efficiency.

$$
\eta_m = \frac{100{,}000}{106{,}025} = 0.9432 = \mathbf{94.32\%}
$$

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Motor Cooling, Size & Mass

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted Style]**

**(a)** Compare air cooling, liquid cooling, and oil spray cooling methods for EV traction motors. State one EV application example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traction motor has a mass of 35 kg, an outer volume of 14 litres, and delivers peak power of 200 kW. Calculate its specific power and power density. Comment on whether this motor is suitable for a compact EV. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

| Method | Mechanism | Pros | Cons | EV Application |
|--------|-----------|------|------|----------------|
| Air Cooling | Convection + forced fan | Simple, cheap, light | Low heat capacity | Tesla Roadster (early) |
| Liquid Cooling (Water–Glycol) | Jacket around stator | High heat capacity, sustained power | Risk of leakage, pump parasitic loss | Nissan Leaf PMSM, BMW i3 |
| Oil Spray Cooling | Nozzles spray oil on end-windings | Highest heat transfer, allows very high current density | Complex sealing, oil pump load | Tesla Model 3/Y PMSM, Audi e-tron |

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $m = 35$ kg, $V = 14$ L, $P_{peak} = 200$ kW

**Step 1:** Specific power.

$$
\sigma_p = \frac{P_{peak}}{m} = \frac{200}{35} = 5.71 \text{ kW/kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Power density.

$$
\rho_p = \frac{P_{peak}}{V} = \frac{200}{14} = 14.29 \text{ kW/L}
$$

**Step 3:** Comment.

Both metrics exceed the typical **state-of-the-art thresholds** ($\sim 5$ kW/kg and $\sim 10$ kW/L) for production EVs. The motor is **highly suitable** for a compact EV and comparable to or better than current premium EVs like the Tesla Model 3 (which achieves ~5.7 kW/kg).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in three-phase copper loss $P_{cu} = 3 I^2 R$. Many students write $P_{cu} = I^2 R$, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up specific power (kW/kg)** with **power density (kW/L)** in definitions — these are *different* quantities.
> 3. **Unit errors** — e.g., expressing power density in W/m³ instead of kW/L. Stick to kW/kg and kW/L.
> 4. **Confusing peak efficiency with operating efficiency** — peak efficiency is at one operating point; operating efficiency is averaged over a drive cycle (WLTP/NEDC).
> 5. **Forgetting stray load loss** when summing losses — it's small but examiners include it in problem statements deliberately.
> 6. **Writing $P_{out} = T \cdot n$** instead of $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega_m$ (in rad/s). Many students use RPM by mistake — losing 1–2 marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Yield KTU Bullet Points):**
> - **Cooling Methods:** Air < Liquid Jacket < Oil Spray (in increasing effectiveness for EV traction motors).
> - **Tesla Model 3/Y** uses **stator oil spray** — a benchmark in EV thermal management.
> - **Loss Components:** Copper (variable, $I^2R$) + Iron (constant-ish, $f$ and $B$ dependent) + Mechanical + Stray.
> - **Copper loss formula:** $P_{cu} = 3 I_{ph}^2 R_{ph}$ — *never* forget the factor of 3.
> - **Efficiency formula:** $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$.
> - **Typical EV motor efficiency:** **94–97%** (PMSM/IM), versus **30–40%** for ICE.
> - **Specific Power Benchmark:** $\sim 5\text{–}7$ kW/kg for modern EV traction motors.
> - **Power Density Benchmark:** $\sim 10\text{–}15$ kW/L for modern EV traction motors.
> - **Magnet Thermal Limit:** NdFeB ~**150–180°C**, SmCo ~**250–300°C** — rotor cooling is critical.
> - **Thermal Resistance Analogy:** $\Delta T = P_{loss} \cdot R_{th}$ — heat = current, $\Delta T$ = voltage drop, $R_{th}$ = resistance.
> - **Higher voltage (400V → 800V)** reduces current → reduces $I^2R$ losses → higher efficiency.
> - **PMSM dominates** modern EV traction applications due to higher power density and efficiency than IM.
> - **Hairpin windings** (used in GM Ultium, Tesla) reduce end-turn length and improve slot fill factor → lower copper losses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Management of HEV Power Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Management of HEV Power Electronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Management of HEV Power Electronics** is the engineering discipline of controlling, dissipating, and extracting the waste heat generated by high-power semiconductor switching devices (IGBTs, MOSFETs, SiC/GaN devices) used in HEV traction inverters, DC-DC converters, and on-board chargers, in order to maintain the **junction temperature ($T_J$)** within the manufacturer's safe operating area (SOA), typically **≤ 150°C** for silicon and **≤ 200°C** for SiC devices.

The thermal management system (TMS) ensures that the power electronic sub-system operates at optimum efficiency, longevity, and reliability under the harsh under-hood ambient conditions (ambient temperatures from **-40°C to +85°C** under hood) characteristic of an automobile.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Motorcycle Engine Cooling Fin**
> Think of an air-cooled motorcycle engine. The engine block gets extremely hot, and to keep it from melting, the manufacturer attaches metal **fins** that increase surface area so that passing air can carry the heat away. The faster you ride (more airflow), the cooler the engine stays.
>
> In a HEV, the **inverter** (which converts DC battery power to AC for the motor) is the "engine." Its silicon chips (IGBTs) heat up rapidly while switching thousands of times per second. The thermal management system acts as the **cooling fins** — using a heatsink, fan, or liquid coolant to pull that heat away before the chips overheat and fail. If the thermal management fails, the inverter "melts" just like an engine without cooling.

### 1.3 Why Thermal Management is Critical in HEVs

1. **High Power Density:** HEV inverters handle **50 kW – 250 kW** in a compact package, generating concentrated heat flux of **100 – 300 W/cm²**.
2. **Under-Hood Environment:** Ambient temperatures reach **85°C – 105°C**, leaving a very narrow margin before exceeding the junction limit.
3. **Reliability:** Every **10°C rise** in $T_J$ approximately **halves** the device lifetime (Arrhenius law).
4. **Efficiency:** Increased temperature increases on-state resistance $R_{DS(on)}$, causing more losses and a vicious cycle (thermal runaway).
5. **Safety:** Thermal runaway in lithium-ion traction battery AND power electronics can cause fire.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Junction Temperature Limit (Silicon IGBT):** $T_{J,max} = 150°C$
> - **Junction Temperature Limit (SiC MOSFET):** $T_{J,max} = 200°C$
> - **Thermal Conductivity of Copper:** $k_{Cu} = 401 \, W/(m \cdot K)$
> - **Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum:** $k_{Al} = 237 \, W/(m \cdot K)$
> - **Thermal Conductivity of Thermal Interface Material (TIM):** $k_{TIM} = 1 - 10 \, W/(m \cdot K)$
> - **Coolant (50/50 Ethylene Glycol-Water):** $c_p \approx 3.5 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Heat Sources Inside HEV Power Electronics

The total power loss $P_{loss}$ dissipated in a power semiconductor module has **three** fundamental components:

#### (a) Conduction Loss ($P_{cond}$)
The loss that occurs when the device is in the ON state and conducting current.

$$P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$$

For an IGBT: $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + R_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^2$

#### (b) Switching Loss ($P_{sw}$)
The loss during turn-ON and turn-OFF transitions, due to simultaneous overlap of voltage and current.

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_{load} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$

#### (c) Off-State Leakage Loss (Negligible)
The blocking-state leakage current loss — usually very small and ignored in HEV applications.

### 2.2 Total Power Loss Equation

$$\boxed{P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}}$$

### 2.3 Thermal Resistance Network (The Core Concept)

The thermal path from the silicon junction to the ambient is modelled as an **electrical analog** using a series network of thermal resistors (and capacitors for transient analysis).

**Electrical-Thermal Analogy:**

| Electrical Quantity | Thermal Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Voltage ($V$) | Temperature ($T$) |
| Current ($I$) | Heat Flow ($\dot{Q}$ or $P$) |
| Resistance ($R$) | Thermal Resistance ($R_\theta$) |
| Capacitance ($C$) | Thermal Capacitance ($C_\theta$) |

**Junction-to-Ambient Total Thermal Resistance:**

$$R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$$

Where:
- $R_{\theta JC}$ = Junction-to-Case thermal resistance (device internal) ≈ **0.1 – 0.5 °C/W**
- $R_{\theta CS}$ = Case-to-Sink thermal resistance (TIM layer) ≈ **0.05 – 0.2 °C/W**
- $R_{\theta SA}$ = Sink-to-Ambient thermal resistance (heatsink + coolant) ≈ **0.05 – 0.5 °C/W**

### 2.4 Steady-State Junction Temperature Equation

This is the **most important equation** for any thermal management problem:

$$\boxed{T_J = T_A + P_{total} \cdot (R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always solve for $T_J$ first in any numerical problem. If $T_J > T_{J,max}$, the design has **failed** and the cooling system must be upgraded.

### 2.5 Heat Transfer Fundamentals

#### (a) Conduction (Fourier's Law)

$$Q_{cond} = \frac{k \cdot A}{\Delta x} \cdot \Delta T$$

#### (b) Convection (Newton's Law of Cooling)

$$Q_{conv} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{surface} - T_{fluid})$$

Where **$h$** is the convective heat transfer coefficient:
- Natural air convection: $h \approx 5 - 25 \, W/(m^2 \cdot K)$
- Forced air (fan): $h \approx 25 - 250 \, W/(m^2 \cdot K)$
- Liquid coolant (water-glycol): $h \approx 500 - 10{,}000 \, W/(m^2 \cdot K)$

#### (c) Radiative Heat Transfer (Stefan-Boltzmann)

$$Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Conduction loss in MOSFET | Inverter efficiency |
| 2 | $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DC} I (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Switching loss | IGBT/MOSFET losses |
| 3 | $P_{tot} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | Total semiconductor loss | Sizing of cooling system |
| 4 | $T_J = T_A + P \cdot R_{\theta JA}$ | Junction temperature | Safety check |
| 5 | $R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$ | Series thermal resistance | Heat sink design |
| 6 | $Q_{cond} = \frac{kA}{\Delta x}\Delta T$ | Fourier conduction | Cold plate wall |
| 7 | $Q_{conv} = hA\Delta T$ | Convection | Coolant flow rate |
| 8 | $\dot{m} = \frac{P_{total}}{c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})}$ | Coolant mass flow rate | Pump sizing |
| 9 | $\tau = R_\theta \cdot C_\theta$ | Thermal time constant | Transient response |
| 10 | $\eta_{fin} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$ | Fin efficiency | Heatsink design |

### 2.7 Cooling Methods — Comparative Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Topic:** Students must know the trade-offs between cooling strategies.

| Cooling Method | Heat Flux Capability | Cost | Weight | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Natural Convection (Air)** | Low (≤ 5 W/cm²) | Very Low | Low | Low-power DC-DC converters |
| **Forced Air (Fan-cooled)** | Medium (5 – 25 W/cm²) | Low | Medium | On-board chargers |
| **Liquid Cold Plate** | High (25 – 100 W/cm²) | High | High | Traction inverters (Toyota Prius, Tesla) |
| **Two-Phase Cooling** | Very High (≥ 100 W/cm²) | Very High | Medium | Future SiC inverters |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Prius (4th Gen):** Uses a single shared coolant loop for the inverter, MG1, MG2, and battery. The inverter uses a **cold plate** with 50/50 ethylene-glycol coolant.
- **Tesla Model 3:** Uses a **Superbottle** thermal management architecture that cools the inverter, motor, and battery using a common refrigerant loop (R-1234yf) supplemented by a coolant loop.
- **Formula 1 / Formula E:** Use **two-phase dielectric cooling** or oil-cooled inverters to handle power densities > 500 W/cm².

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Junction Temperature in a Steady-State HEV Inverter

**Problem Setup:** A 3-phase IGBT inverter in a HEV traction system has the following specifications:
- DC bus voltage: $V_{DC} = 400 \, V$
- Load current (rms): $I = 200 \, A$
- Switching frequency: $f_{sw} = 8 \, kHz$
- Turn-on + turn-off time: $t_{on} + t_{off} = 500 \, ns$
- On-state voltage drop: $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.8 \, V$
- Thermal resistances: $R_{\theta JC} = 0.15 \, °C/W$, $R_{\theta CS} = 0.08 \, °C/W$, $R_{\theta SA} = 0.12 \, °C/W$
- Ambient temperature: $T_A = 65 \, °C$

**Required:** Calculate the steady-state junction temperature $T_J$ and check if it is within safe limits.

---

**Step 1: Calculate Switching Loss per Switch**

Using the switching loss formula:

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$

Substituting the values:

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 400 \cdot 200 \cdot (500 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 8000$$

**Step 2: Numerical Simplification**

The product of the last three terms is calculated first:

$$(500 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 8000 = 4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.004$$

Multiplying by $\frac{1}{2}$:

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.004 = 0.002$$

Multiplying by $V_{DC} \cdot I$:

$$P_{sw} = 400 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.002 = 80000 \cdot 0.002 = 160 \, W$$

**Step 3: Calculate Conduction Loss per Switch**

For the IGBT:

$$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + R_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^2$$

Assuming pure sinusoidal PWM, the average current $I_{avg} \approx 0.637 \cdot I_{peak}$ and assuming $I_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{rms}$:

$$I_{avg} = 0.637 \cdot (\sqrt{2} \cdot 200) = 0.637 \cdot 282.84 = 180.17 \, A$$

The conduction loss becomes:

$$P_{cond} = 1.8 \cdot 180.17 + 0 \approx 324.3 \, W$$

(Assuming $R_{CE}$ is negligible; if specified, include it.)

**Step 4: Total Power Dissipated per IGBT**

$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 324.3 + 160 = 484.3 \, W$$

**Step 5: Total Thermal Resistance (Junction-to-Ambient)**

$$R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$$

Substituting:

$$R_{\theta JA} = 0.15 + 0.08 + 0.12 = 0.35 \, °C/W$$

**Step 6: Compute Junction Temperature**

$$T_J = T_A + P_{total} \cdot R_{\theta JA}$$

Substituting:

$$T_J = 65 + 484.3 \cdot 0.35$$

$$T_J = 65 + 169.5 = 234.5 \, °C$$

**Step 7: Safety Verdict**

> [!WARNING]
> **FAILURE!** $T_J = 234.5°C$ **vastly exceeds** the IGBT limit of **150°C**. The cooling system is grossly undersized. The designer must either:
> 1. Increase coolant flow rate, OR
> 2. Reduce $R_{\theta SA}$ (better cold plate, lower $T_A$), OR
> 3. Switch to a **SiC MOSFET** (limit 200°C), OR
> 4. Use **parallel devices** to halve the loss per switch.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Coolant Mass Flow Rate for a Liquid-Cooled Inverter

**Problem Setup:** A liquid-cooled HEV inverter dissipates $P_{total} = 3.5 \, kW$. The coolant (50/50 glycol-water) enters the cold plate at $T_{in} = 55°C$ and must leave at $T_{out} = 65°C$. Specific heat of the coolant: $c_p = 3.5 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$.

**Required:** Calculate the required coolant mass flow rate.

---

**Step 1: Energy Balance Equation**

The heat absorbed by the coolant equals the heat dissipated by the electronics:

$$P_{total} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Step 2: Isolate the Mass Flow Rate**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_{total}}{c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}$$

**Step 3: Numerical Substitution**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{3500}{3500 \cdot (65 - 55)}$$

$$T_{out} - T_{in} = 10 \, K$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{3500}{3500 \cdot 10} = \frac{3500}{35000} = 0.1 \, kg/s$$

**Step 4: Convert to Litres per Minute (LPM)**

For a glycol-water mixture, density $\rho \approx 1.05 \, kg/L$:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.1}{1.05} \approx 0.0952 \, L/s \approx 5.7 \, LPM$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical HEV electric water pump delivers **8 – 15 LPM**, confirming our design is realistic.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Heatsink Sizing Using Fin Efficiency

**Problem Setup:** An aluminum heatsink ($k_{Al} = 237 \, W/(m \cdot K)$) has square pin fins of cross-section $w \times w = 5 \, mm \times 5 \, mm$ and height $L = 30 \, mm$. Convective coefficient $h = 50 \, W/(m^2 \cdot K)$. Ambient $T_A = 50°C$. Power dissipation = 80 W per chip. Assume adiabatic fin tip.

**Required:** Compute fin efficiency and effective thermal resistance.

---

**Step 1: Compute the Fin Parameter $m$**

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{h \cdot P}{k \cdot A_c}}$$

For a square pin fin: Perimeter $P = 4w = 0.02 \, m$, Cross-section $A_c = w^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2$

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{50 \cdot 0.02}{237 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{1.0}{5925 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{168.78} = 12.99 \, m^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Parameter $mL$**

$$mL = 12.99 \cdot 0.030 = 0.39$$

**Step 3: Compute Fin Efficiency $\eta_{fin}$**

For an adiabatic tip:

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{\tanh(0.39)}{0.39} = \frac{0.3746}{0.39} \approx 0.961 \, (96.1\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Fin efficiency is high because the fins are short. A fin efficiency above 80% is generally acceptable.

**Step 4: Compute Heat Dissipated per Fin**

Surface area of one fin (excluding base and tip): $A_f = 4 \cdot w \cdot L = 4 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 0.030 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$

$$Q_{fin} = \eta_{fin} \cdot h \cdot A_f \cdot \Delta T$$

Assuming $\Delta T = 70°C$ (e.g., 120°C surface - 50°C ambient):

$$Q_{fin} = 0.961 \cdot 50 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 70 = 2.018 \, W \, \text{per fin}$$

For 80 W dissipation, the number of fins required:

$$N_{fins} = \frac{80}{2.018} \approx 40 \, \text{pin fins}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cooling System Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut Decision Tree**

| Vehicle Class | Inverter Power | Recommended Cooling | Typical $h$ value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mild HEV (48V) | 5 – 15 kW | Forced air | 25 – 50 $W/(m^2K)$ |
| Full HEV | 50 – 100 kW | Liquid cold plate | 1000 – 5000 $W/(m^2K)$ |
| PHEV / EV | 100 – 250 kW | Dual-loop liquid | 2000 – 8000 $W/(m^2K)$ |
| Performance EV (Tesla Plaid) | > 400 kW | Submerged / two-phase | > 10000 $W/(m^2K)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of HEV Power Electronics Thermal Management

This diagram maps the entire thermal path from semiconductor junction to ambient, including the feedback control loop that ensures safe operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    JUNC[Junction TJ - Silicon Die]:::source
    CASE[Case / Module Baseplate]:::path
    TIM[Thermal Interface Material TIM]:::path
    HSK[Cold Plate or Heatsink]:::sink
    COOL[Coolant or Air Stream]:::sink
    AMB[Ambient TA Environment]:::sink
    SENS[Temperature Sensor NTC]:::ctrl
    ECU[Vehicle ECU Thermal Controller]:::ctrl
    PUMP[Coolant Pump and Fan]:::ctrl
    ACT[Cooling Actuator Output]:::ctrl

    JUNC -- RthJC --> CASE
    CASE -- RthCS --> TIM
    TIM -- RthSA --> HSK
    HSK -- Convection h --> COOL
    COOL -- Rejection --> AMB

    JUNC -. Temp Feedback .-> SENS
    SENS -. Signal .-> ECU
    ECU -. PWM Command .-> PUMP
    PUMP -. Flow Modulation .-> ACT
    ACT -. Adjusted Cooling .-> HSK

    classDef source fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2471a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#239b56,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

### 4.2 Thermal Resistance Network — Electrical Analog

This is the **Cauer / Foster RC ladder** model used in industry to simulate the transient thermal response of an IGBT module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TJ1((TJ)) --- R1[RthJC] --- TC1((TC)) --- R2[RthCS] --- TS1((TS)) --- R3[RthSA] --- TA1((TA))
    C1[CthJ] -.- TJ1
    C2[CthC] -.- TC1
    C3[CthS] -.- TS1
    PSRC[Heat Source Ploss] --- TJ1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **series R** values (RthJC, RthCS, RthSA) represent the **thermal resistance** of each layer.
- The **dotted C** values (CthJ, CthC, CthS) represent **thermal capacitance**, which determines how fast each layer heats up.
- $P_{loss}$ is injected at the junction and flows left-to-right toward the ambient.

---

### 4.3 Comparative Cooling Architecture (Toyota Prius-Style Integrated Loop)

This is the modern "thermal integration" architecture shared across the HEV ecosystem.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BAT[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY]:::veh
    INV[TRA CTION INVERTER]:::veh
    M1[MOTOR GENERATOR 1]:::veh
    M2[MOTOR GENERATOR 2]:::veh
    OBC[ONBOARD CHARGER]:::veh
    DC[DC DC CONVERTER]:::veh
    CHILL[CHILLER RADIATOR]:::rad
    RAD[MAIN RADIATOR]:::rad
    PUMP1[ELECTRIC WATER PUMP 1]:::pump
    PUMP2[ELECTRIC WATER PUMP 2]:::pump
    TANK[EXPANSION TANK]:::tank
    ECU[ENGINE ECU THERMAL MGMT]:::ctrl

    BAT --> PUMP1
    PUMP1 --> CHILL
    CHILL --> INV
    INV --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> OBC
    OBC --> DC
    DC --> TANK
    TANK --> PUMP1
    BAT --> PUMP2
    PUMP2 --> RAD
    RAD --> BAT
    ECU -.Controls.-> PUMP1
    ECU -.Controls.-> PUMP2

    classDef veh fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#d35400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rad fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tank fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#7d3c98,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **inverter, motor-generators, and OBC** share a single high-temperature coolant loop (orange path) because they all generate similar-level heat.
- The **battery** has its own loop with a **chiller** (which uses refrigerant A/C to keep the battery at 25 – 35°C).
- The **ECU** monitors all NTC thermistors and modulates the pump speeds via PWM to maintain $T_J$ in the safe zone.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Notes

- All node IDs are **alphanumeric** (e.g., `JUNC`, `CASE`, `RthJC`) — no reserved words used.
- All double-quoted labels are **plain uppercase text** — no `**bold**`, no italics, no HTML tables.
- All Mermaid blocks use only **flowchart** and **classDef** syntax that is universally compatible with GitHub Mermaid v9+.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMUT721]`**

**Q1. List the three dominant cooling strategies used in HEV power electronics and state the dominant heat transfer mechanism in each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Natural air convection** — Dominant mechanism: Natural convection (buoyancy-driven flow) with $h \approx 5-25 \, W/(m^2K)$.
> 2. **Forced air cooling (fan)** — Dominant mechanism: Forced convection with $h \approx 25-250 \, W/(m^2K)$.
> 3. **Liquid cooling (cold plate)** — Dominant mechanism: Single-phase forced convection with $h \approx 500-10000 \, W/(m^2K)$.
>
> Liquid cooling offers the highest heat transfer coefficient and is preferred for high-power traction inverters in modern HEVs.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEMUT721]`**

**Q2. What is Thermal Interface Material (TIM)? Why is it used between the IGBT module and the cold plate?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Thermal Interface Material (TIM)** is a thermally conductive substance (thermal grease, graphene pad, solder, or phase-change material) applied between the IGBT module baseplate and the cold plate.
>
> **Why used:** The microscopic surface roughness of the IGBT baseplate and the cold plate leaves air gaps (air has very low thermal conductivity $\approx 0.026 \, W/(m \cdot K)$). TIM fills these voids, drastically reducing the **case-to-sink thermal resistance $R_{\theta CS}$** from $> 1.0 \, °C/W$ (dry contact) to $\approx 0.05 - 0.2 \, °C/W$ (with TIM).
>
> Without TIM, junction temperature would rise by 30 – 50°C, causing immediate device failure.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Question (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMUT721, Module 3]`**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **thermal resistance network** (junction-to-ambient) used to model the cooling of an IGBT module in an HEV traction inverter. Derive the expression for the steady-state junction temperature. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 100 kW HEV traction inverter uses an IGBT module with $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.6 \, V$ and switching frequency $f_{sw} = 10 \, kHz$. The DC bus voltage is 350 V, RMS load current is 250 A, and total switching time $(t_{on} + t_{off}) = 600 \, ns$. The ambient temperature inside the inverter housing is 70°C. The thermal resistances are: $R_{\theta JC} = 0.12 \, °C/W$, $R_{\theta CS} = 0.06 \, °C/W$, and $R_{\theta SA} = 0.10 \, °C/W$.
Calculate: (i) Total power loss per IGBT switch, (ii) Steady-state junction temperature, (iii) Whether the design is safe. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a):**

The thermal resistance network is the **electrical-analog model** where temperature is mapped to voltage, heat flow to current, and thermal resistance to electrical resistance.

**The network consists of three series stages:**

1. **Junction-to-Case ($R_{\theta JC}$):** Internal resistance of the die, solder, and DBC substrate of the IGBT module.
2. **Case-to-Sink ($R_{\theta CS}$):** Resistance of the TIM layer between module baseplate and heatsink/cold plate.
3. **Sink-to-Ambient ($R_{\theta SA}$):** Resistance of the cold plate, coolant convection, and any further fins.

**Steady-State Derivation:**

At steady state, the heat flow $\dot{Q} = P_{loss}$ is constant and is the same through each series resistance (Kirchhoff's Current Law analog). Applying the thermal Ohm's law across each layer:

$$T_J - T_C = P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta JC}$$
$$T_C - T_S = P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta CS}$$
$$T_S - T_A = P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta SA}$$

Adding all three equations (temperature differences add in series, just as voltage drops add in a series resistor circuit):

$$T_J - T_A = P_{loss} \cdot (R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA})$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{T_J = T_A + P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta JA}}$$

Where $R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$ is the total junction-to-ambient thermal resistance.

This equation is fundamental for HEV inverter design — it directly couples the electrical losses to the thermal limit.

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b):**

**(i) Total Power Loss per IGBT Switch:**

Switching loss:

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 350 \cdot 250 \cdot (600 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 10000$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 350 \cdot 250 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-3} = 262.5 \, W$$

Conduction loss (using $I_{avg} = 0.637 \cdot I_{peak} = 0.637 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot 250$):

$$I_{avg} = 0.637 \cdot 353.55 = 225.21 \, A$$

$$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} = 1.6 \cdot 225.21 = 360.3 \, W$$

Total loss:

$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 360.3 + 262.5 = 622.8 \, W$$

**(ii) Steady-State Junction Temperature:**

$$R_{\theta JA} = 0.12 + 0.06 + 0.10 = 0.28 \, °C/W$$

$$T_J = T_A + P_{total} \cdot R_{\theta JA}$$

$$T_J = 70 + 622.8 \cdot 0.28 = 70 + 174.4 = 244.4 \, °C$$

**(iii) Safety Verdict:**

> [!WARNING]
> **NOT SAFE!** $T_J = 244.4°C$ is **way above** the IGBT limit of 150°C.
>
> The student must recommend improvements — better cooling (reduce $R_{\theta SA}$ via higher coolant flow), parallel IGBTs, or use SiC MOSFETs (limit 200°C).

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMUT721, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]`**

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare the **air-cooling and liquid-cooling** methods for HEV power electronics. State one example vehicle for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A liquid-cooled HEV inverter dissipates 4 kW of heat. The coolant (50/50 ethylene glycol-water) enters at 50°C and leaves at 60°C. Specific heat $c_p = 3.5 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$, density $\rho = 1.05 \, kg/L$.
Calculate: (i) Required mass flow rate (kg/s), (ii) Required volumetric flow rate (LPM), (iii) If the inlet temperature rises to 60°C (hot day), find the new outlet temperature assuming the same flow rate. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a):**

| Parameter | Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat transfer coefficient ($h$) | 5 – 250 $W/(m^2K)$ | 500 – 10,000 $W/(m^2K)$ |
| Heat flux capability | ≤ 25 $W/cm^2$ | 25 – 500 $W/cm^2$ |
| Coolant density | 1.2 $kg/m^3$ | 1050 $kg/m^3$ |
| Specific heat | 1.0 $kJ/(kgK)$ | 3.5 $kJ/(kgK)$ |
| Pumping power | Low (fans) | Higher (pumps) |
| Weight & size | Bulky for same power | Compact |
| Reliability | Simple, fewer seals | Complex, leak risk |
| Example vehicle | Honda Insight (1st gen, 1999) | Toyota Prius (4th gen), Tesla Model 3 |

**Example explanation:** The Honda Insight (1st gen) used a **forced-air-cooled IGBT inverter** because the total motor power was only 10 kW. Modern HEVs like the **Toyota Prius** use a **shared liquid coolant loop** (cold plate mounted to the IGBT module baseplate) because the inverter handles 50 – 100 kW and the heat flux would be impossible to remove with air.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b):**

**(i) Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_{total}}{c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{4000}{3500 \cdot (60 - 50)} = \frac{4000}{3500 \cdot 10} = 0.1143 \, kg/s$$
