**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.1143}{1.05} = 0.1089 \, L/s$$

Converting to LPM:

$$\dot{V} = 0.1089 \cdot 60 = 6.53 \, LPM$$

**(iii) New outlet temperature (inlet = 60°C):**

Using the same $\dot{m}$ and $\dot{V}$:

$$T_{out} = T_{in} + \frac{P_{total}}{\dot{m} \cdot c_p}$$

$$T_{out} = 60 + \frac{4000}{0.1143 \cdot 3500}$$

$$T_{out} = 60 + \frac{4000}{400.0} = 60 + 10 = 70 \, °C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Insight:** On a hot day, the coolant enters hotter, so the outlet also rises by the same 10°C. Since the **inlet temperature has a direct linear effect on $T_J$**, designers must size the radiator (or chiller) large enough to keep the inlet ≤ 55°C even at peak ambient conditions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $t_{on} + t_{off}$** — must be in **seconds** when using SI. Common error: writing it as 600 ns directly into a 350 V × 250 A formula without converting to seconds (loses 1 mark minimum).
> 2. **Confusing $I_{rms}$ and $I_{avg}$** — for sinusoidal PWM, the **average current is $0.637 \cdot I_{peak}$**, not $I_{rms}$. Many students write $P_{cond} = V_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}$ which is dimensionally wrong for the *average* power computation.
> 3. **Not summing all three thermal resistances** — many students use only $R_{\theta JC}$ and forget $R_{\theta CS}$ and $R_{\theta SA}$, producing an unrealistically low $T_J$ and an "unsafe" conclusion.
> 4. **Ignoring the verdict** — examiners **deduct marks** if the final answer does not include a clear SAFE / UNSAFE statement and a brief recommendation.
> 5. **Mixing up $c_p$ and $\rho$** — for coolant flow rate, students frequently divide by $\rho$ when they should divide by $c_p$. Always write the energy balance first to avoid sign errors.
> 6. **Not drawing the thermal resistance ladder** — a question worth 7 marks requires a **labelled sketch**. A purely text-based answer loses 2 – 3 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - [x] **Total power loss** = Conduction loss + Switching loss.
> - [x] **Switching loss formula:** $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DC} I (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ — *always convert ns to s*.
> - [x] **Conduction loss for IGBT:** $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + R_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^2$.
> - [x] **Conduction loss for MOSFET:** $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.
> - [x] **Thermal resistance series:** $R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$.
> - [x] **Junction temperature equation:** $T_J = T_A + P_{total} \cdot R_{\theta JA}$ — *this is the central design equation*.
> - [x] **IGBT limit:** $T_{J,max} = 150°C$; **SiC MOSFET limit:** $T_{J,max} = 200°C$.
> - [x] **Coolant mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})}$.
> - [x] **Liquid cooling is preferred** for traction inverters > 50 kW.
> - [x] **TIM** reduces $R_{\theta CS}$ from > 1 °C/W (dry) to ~ 0.05 – 0.2 °C/W.
> - [x] **Every 10°C rise in $T_J$ halves device lifetime** (Arrhenius degradation).
> - [x] **Fin efficiency:** $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$, where $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$.
> - [x] **Thermal time constant:** $\tau = R_\theta \cdot C_\theta$ — controls how fast the junction heats up during a sudden load step.
> - [x] **Modern HEVs** use shared coolant loops for the inverter + motor-generators + battery, controlled by an ECU-based thermal management algorithm.
> - [x] **Two-phase / immersion cooling** is the future for SiC-based ultra-high-power-density inverters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing the Drive System: Matching the Electric Machine and the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sizing the Drive System: Matching the Electric Machine and the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drive System Sizing** in a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) is the systematic engineering process of determining the **rated power, continuous power, peak torque, and rated speed** of both the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and the **Electric Machine (EM)** such that their combined output satisfies the vehicle's dynamic and regulatory performance targets (gradeability, top speed, 0–100 km/h acceleration, fuel economy, and emissions) while minimizing mass, cost, and packaging volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Matching the EM and ICE** refers to the coordinated sizing and operating-point selection in which the ICE operates predominantly in its high-efficiency region (sweet spot) and the EM supplies the transient torque/power deficit (boosting) and absorbs the regenerative braking energy (recuperation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **two-person rowing boat crossing a river**:
- The **ICE** is the *strong, steady senior rower* — he pulls the oar with consistent, efficient strokes but takes time to change pace.
- The **Electric Machine (EM)** is the *agile, quick junior rower* — he can instantly provide an extra burst of power or instantly stop pulling when needed.
- The **river current (driving demands)** keeps changing — sometimes calm (cruising), sometimes a rapid (acceleration), sometimes against a stiff wind (climbing a hill).

**Sizing** is choosing how strong each rower must be:
- If the senior is too small → boat is sluggish on the open river.
- If the junior is too small → the boat cannot accelerate quickly when a rapid appears, and cannot harvest energy when flowing downstream (regenerative braking).
- If both are oversized → the boat is heavy, wastes energy, and is costly to build.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Targets in Sizing (used in KTU numerical problems):**
> - Maximum cruise speed: **120–180 km/h**
> - 0–100 km/h acceleration time: **6–12 s**
> - Maximum gradability: **30 % at low speed**
> - Specific power target: **8–12 kW/tonne (mass)**

### 1.3 Why Sizing Matters — KTU 2024 Significance

In a parallel or power-split HEV, the **ICE and EM share a common torque-speed map** at the transmission input. If their individual ratings are not matched, the vehicle will:
1. Have an oversized battery and EM (over-cost, over-weight).
2. Run the ICE outside its BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) island (poor FE).
3. Fail to meet regulatory drive-cycle emissions (e.g., **World Harmonised Light Vehicle Test Procedure — WLTP**) and Corporate Average Fuel Economy (**CAFE**) norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU takeaway:** The drive system is **not** sized by simply adding ICE + EM rated power. It is sized by **time-sharing** the load between the two sources based on the drive cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework of Sizing

**Step 1 — Compute Total Power Demand (P_demand)**
Sum of all tractive forces × vehicle velocity at each point in the drive cycle.

**Step 2 — Decompose into Steady-State and Transient Demand**
- Steady state: cruising, climbing at constant speed.
- Transient: 0–100 km/h, overtaking.

**Step 3 — Partition Demand between ICE and EM**
- **ICE** → average demand (efficient steady operation).
- **EM** → peak / transient demand + regenerative energy recovery.

**Step 4 — Apply Sizing Constraints**
- **Maximum power rule:** P_ICE + P_EM ≥ P_demand(max).
- **Continuous power rule:** P_ICE ≥ P_cruise(continuous).
- **Gradeability rule:** Both machines together must deliver grade-climbing torque.
- **Battery rule:** Energy stored ≥ energy needed for all-EV mode window (e.g., 2–5 km start-off in urban WLTP).

**Step 5 — Verify with Drive-Cycle Simulation**
Typical KTU reference cycles: **NEDC, WLTP Class 3, Indian MIDC (Modified Indian Driving Cycle).**

**Step 6 — Down-Select the Final Rating**
Apply cost, weight, and packaging constraints.

### 2.2 Sizing Criteria — Critical Engineering Rules

| # | Criterion | Engineering Rationale |
|---|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | P_ICE (continuous) ≥ P_cruise at 100 km/h | ICE alone can sustain highway cruise without battery drain. |
| 2 | (P_ICE + P_EM)_peak ≥ P_acceleration peak | Combined power must deliver 0–100 km/h time. |
| 3 | T_EM(peak) ≥ T_max demanded at launch (low speed) | EM provides launch torque (ICE has poor low-end torque). |
| 4 | Battery E ≥ E_all-EV_window | Pure-electric range in city. |
| 5 | (η_EM + η_battery + η_motor-controller) ≥ 0.75 | Recovers ≥25 % braking energy. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule-of-Thumb (KTU exam favourite):**
> In a typical mid-size HEV sedan of mass 1500 kg, the ICE is sized to **~50–60 %** of the maximum demand power, and the EM is sized to **~30–40 %**. The overlap allows power blending and ensures the ICE can be decoupled at low loads (cylinder deactivation / stop-start).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit | Boundary / Typical Value |
|--------|----------|---------------------|------|--------------------------|
| F_t | Total tractive force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$ | N | — |
| F_rr | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\alpha$ | N | C_rr ≈ **0.012** (car), **0.005** (truck) |
| F_ad | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,V^2$ | N | ρ = **1.225 kg/m³**, C_d A ≈ **0.6–0.8 m²** |
| F_hc | Hill-climbing force | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$ | N | α = grade angle |
| F_ia | Inertial (acceleration) force | $F_{ia} = m \cdot a$ | N | a in m/s² |
| P_t | Tractive power at wheels | $P_t = F_t \cdot V$ | W | — |
| P_demand | Power demand at wheels | $P_{demand} = \dfrac{V}{\eta_t}\,(F_{rr}+F_{ad}+F_{hc}+F_{ia})$ | W | η_t = **0.92–0.95** (transmission) |
| P_ICE | ICE rated power | $P_{ICE} = k_1 \cdot P_{demand,max}$ | W | k_1 ≈ **0.5–0.6** |
| P_EM | EM rated power | $P_{EM} = k_2 \cdot P_{demand,max}$ | W | k_2 ≈ **0.3–0.4** |
| E_batt | Battery energy | $E_{batt} = \dfrac{V \cdot F_{t,EV} \cdot d_{EV}}{\eta_{dis}}$ | Wh | d_EV = **2–5 km** city range |
| T_EM(peak) | EM peak torque | $T_{EM,peak} = \dfrac{(P_{ICE}+P_{EM})}{\omega_{max}} \cdot s$ | N·m | s = **1.5–2** overload factor |
| BSFC | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $g/kWh$ | g/kWh | Best ≈ **220 g/kWh** (diesel), **250 g/kWh** (gasoline) |
| η_total | Drive system efficiency | $\eta = \eta_{ICE}\cdot\eta_{t}\cdot\eta_{EM}\cdot\eta_{batt}$ | — | **0.30–0.38** typical HEV |
| ω_max | Max angular speed of EM | $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{r_{wheel}}$ | rad/s | r_wheel ≈ **0.30–0.35 m** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Toyota Hybrid System (THS-II)** in the Prius: ~1.8 L Atkinson ICE (72 kW) + EM1 (53 kW) + EM2 (23 kW) — total 100 kW combined. ICE sized below peak demand; EM1 handles starting + low-speed EV; EM2 acts as generator and traction motor on rear axle.
2. **Honda i-MMD** uses a smaller ICE (≈ 1.5 L, 77 kW) and a larger traction EM (≈ 135 kW) — series-mode dominant, with ICE direct-clutch lock for high-speed cruise.
3. **Hyundai TMED (Transmission-Mounted Electric Device)** in Ioniq Hybrid: 1.6 L GDi ICE (77 kW) + 32 kW EM + 8.9 kWh Li-ion (PHEV variant) — sized for parallel-mode cruising.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Demand for Steady Cruise (V = constant, a = 0, α = 0)

At constant speed on a flat road, the only opposing forces are rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag.

$$
F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad}
$$

Substituting the standard forms:

$$
F_t = m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2
$$

The tractive power at the wheels is force times velocity:

$$
P_t = F_t \cdot V = (m g C_{rr})V + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^3
$$

To find the power at the **engine/motor shaft** (input to the transmission), divide by the transmission and drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{demand} = \dfrac{V}{\eta_t}\left(m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2\right)\,}
$$

**Step-by-step numerical example (KTU standard):**
- m = **1500 kg**, g = 9.81 m/s², C_rr = 0.012
- C_d A = 0.7 m², ρ = 1.225 kg/m³
- V = 100 km/h = 27.78 m/s, η_t = 0.93

**Row 1 — Rolling resistance term:**

$$
F_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

**Row 2 — Aerodynamic drag term:**

$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (27.78)^2 = 331.21 \text{ N}
$$

**Row 3 — Total tractive force:**

$$
F_t = 176.58 + 331.21 = 507.79 \text{ N}
$$

**Row 4 — Tractive power at wheels:**

$$
P_t = 507.79 \times 27.78 = 14\,108 \text{ W} = 14.11 \text{ kW}
$$

**Row 5 — Demand at shaft:**

$$
P_{demand} = 14\,108 / 0.93 = 15\,170 \text{ W} \approx 15.2 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 1500 kg car cruising at 100 km/h requires only **~15 kW** at the shaft. This sets the **continuous ICE rating** in a parallel HEV.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Demand for Maximum Acceleration (0 → 100 km/h in time t_a)

For maximum acceleration, include the inertial term $F_{ia} = m a$, where $a$ is the average acceleration.

$$
a = \dfrac{V_{max}}{t_a}
$$

Substituting $V_{max} = 27.78$ m/s and $t_a = 10$ s (typical target):

$$
a = 27.78 / 10 = 2.778 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

Total tractive force during acceleration:

$$
F_t = m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2 + m a
$$

**Row 1 — Inertial force:**

$$
F_{ia} = 1500 \times 2.778 = 4\,167 \text{ N}
$$

**Row 2 — Aerodynamic drag at V_max:**

$$
F_{ad} = 331.21 \text{ N} \text{ (as before)}
$$

**Row 3 — Rolling resistance:**

$$
F_{rr} = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

**Row 4 — Total tractive force (peak):**

$$
F_t = 176.58 + 331.21 + 4\,167 = 4\,674.79 \text{ N}
$$

**Row 5 — Tractive power at wheels at V_max:**

$$
P_t = 4\,674.79 \times 27.78 = 129\,860 \text{ W} \approx 129.9 \text{ kW}
$$

**Row 6 — Demand at shaft (with η_t):**

$$
P_{demand} = 129\,860 / 0.93 = 139\,635 \text{ W} \approx 139.6 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Maximum acceleration demands ~**140 kW** — about **9.2×** the cruise demand. This sets the **combined peak** of ICE + EM.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sizing Partition (Matching Rule)

Apply sizing coefficients k_1 and k_2 to P_demand(max):

$$
P_{ICE} = k_1 \cdot P_{demand,max}
$$
$$
P_{EM} = k_2 \cdot P_{demand,max}
$$

With the engineering constraint that they must cover the peak together:

$$
k_1 + k_2 \geq 1.0
$$

Typical design choice for a parallel HEV: $k_1 = 0.6$, $k_2 = 0.4$:

**Row 1 — ICE rated power:**

$$
P_{ICE} = 0.6 \times 139.6 = 83.76 \text{ kW}
$$

**Row 2 — EM rated power:**

$$
P_{EM} = 0.4 \times 139.6 = 55.84 \text{ kW}
$$

**Row 3 — Combined peak:**

$$
P_{peak} = 83.76 + 55.84 = 139.60 \text{ kW} \;\;(\text{matches demand})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification step:** ICE alone (83.76 kW) must exceed cruise demand (15.2 kW) by a factor of ~5.5, which is safe and allows headroom for the BSFC sweet-spot operation. ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Battery Energy Sizing for Pure-EV Mode

For a target all-electric range $d_{EV}$ in city driving (low V, no climb):

$$
E_{batt} = \dfrac{F_{t,EV} \cdot d_{EV}}{\eta_{dis}}
$$

with $F_{t,EV} \approx m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V_{city}^2$ at city speed $V_{city} = 50$ km/h = 13.89 m/s.

**Row 1 — City cruise demand at 50 km/h:**

$$
F_{t,EV} = 176.58 + 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (13.89)^2
$$
$$
F_{t,EV} = 176.58 + 82.79 = 259.37 \text{ N}
$$

**Row 2 — Energy for d_EV = 5 km = 5000 m, η_dis = 0.90:**

$$
E_{batt} = \dfrac{259.37 \times 5000}{0.90} = 1\,441\,000 \text{ J} = 0.40 \text{ kWh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A small ~**0.4 kWh** pack suffices for 5 km city EV mode (this is roughly the size of the Toyota Yaris hybrid battery). For 50 km EV (PHEV), scale by 10× → ~**4–8 kWh**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Torque-Speed Matching Between ICE and EM

The traction motor must deliver peak torque at low speed (launch), while the ICE delivers peak power around mid-speed.

EM torque characteristic (constant-torque region up to base speed $\omega_b$):

$$
T_{EM} = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{\omega} \;\;\text{for}\;\; \omega \leq \omega_b
$$

Constant-power region (above $\omega_b$):

$$
P_{EM} = T_{EM} \cdot \omega = \text{const.}
$$

For the same vehicle at launch ($\omega \approx 50$ rad/s, V = 5 m/s):

$$
T_{EM,launch} = \dfrac{55\,840}{50} = 1116.8 \text{ N·m (electrical side)}
$$

After transmission ratio $G = 8$ and tyre radius r = 0.32 m:

$$
T_{wheel} = 1116.8 \times 8 \times 0.32 / 0.32 = 8\,934 \text{ N·m at the wheel}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matching insight:** The EM must be **sized for torque (not power)** at launch, but **sized for power (not torque)** at high speed. The ICE complements this by being **sized for sustained mid-speed power** with good BSFC.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Operating Regime | Dominant Source | Sizing Driver |
|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Launch (V → 0) | EM | Peak torque of EM |
| Low-speed city (V < 40 km/h) | EM (pure EV) | Battery energy + EM continuous power |
| Mid-speed cruise (40–80 km/h) | ICE alone | ICE BSFC sweet spot (~2000–3000 rpm) |
| High-speed cruise (V > 100 km/h) | ICE + EM blend | ICE rated power + EM assist |
| Hard acceleration | ICE + EM (peak sum) | Combined peak power |
| Climbing (grade 30 %) | ICE + EM (max torque) | Combined peak torque |
| Braking / deceleration | EM (as generator) | EM peak regen power + battery charge rate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sizing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Vehicle Performance Targets] --> A1[Top Speed]
    A0 --> A2[0 to 100 km per h Time]
    A0 --> A3[Gradeability Percent]
    A0 --> A4[Drive Cycle MIDC WLTP]
    B0[Sizing Master Equation] --> B1[Ft equals m g Crr plus 0.5 rho Cd A V squared plus m g sin alpha plus m a]
    B0 --> B2[Pt equals Ft times V divided by eta t]
    C1[Power Demand Map vs V] --> D1{Max Demand P demand max}
    C2[Energy Demand Map vs d] --> D2{Total Energy E cycle}
    D1 --> E1[Apply Sizing Coefficients]
    E1 --> E1a[k1 equals 0.6 for ICE]
    E1 --> E1b[k2 equals 0.4 for EM]
    E1a --> F1[ICE Rated Power P ICE]
    E1b --> F2[EM Rated Power P EM]
    D2 --> G1[Battery Energy E batt]
    F1 --> H1[Validate BSFC sweet spot]
    F2 --> H2[Validate peak torque at launch]
    G1 --> H3[Validate all EV range d EV]
    H1 --> I1[Final Sizing Output]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    I1 --> I2[ICE plus EM Parallel Drive System]
```

### 4.2 Operating-Point Matching Diagram (ICE vs EM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Low_Speed[Low Speed Region]
        L1[EM dominates: Launch torque]
        L2[ICE: Idle stop start]
    end
    subgraph Mid_Speed[Mid Speed Region]
        M1[ICE alone: BSFC sweet spot]
        M2[EM: Off or charging]
    end
    subgraph High_Speed[High Speed Region]
        H1[ICE plus EM: Combined peak]
        H2[EM boost for overtaking]
    end
    subgraph Braking[Braking Region]
        R1[EM as generator: Regen]
        R2[Friction brake assist]
    end
    Low_Speed --> Mid_Speed
    Mid_Speed --> High_Speed
    High_Speed --> Braking
    Braking --> Low_Speed
```

### 4.3 Torque-Speed Envelope Matching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Torque Demand Envelope] --> B[ICE Torque Speed Map]
    A --> C[EM Torque Speed Map]
    B --> D[ICE Max Torque at 2000 rpm]
    C --> E[EM Max Torque at 0 to 1000 rpm]
    D --> F[Sum Envelope at Each Speed]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Vehicle Demand Met?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Sizing Acceptable]
    G -->|No| I[Resize: Increase EM peak or ICE displacement]
    I --> B
    I --> C
```

### 4.4 Power-Stream Partitioning Flow (Energy Management View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Total Power Demand P demand] --> S1{Operating Point}
    S1 -->|Low speed EV| P1[Pure EM Mode]
    S1 -->|Mid speed cruise| P2[Pure ICE Mode]
    S1 -->|Hard accel| P3[ICE plus EM Peak Mode]
    S1 -->|Brake| P4[Regen Mode]
    P1 --> B0[Battery Discharge]
    P2 --> F0[ICE Fuel Burn]
    P3 --> F0
    P3 --> B0
    P4 --> B1[Battery Charge]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*State the three primary engineering criteria used for sizing the electric machine and the ICE in a parallel hybrid electric vehicle. Justify each with one line.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **(a) Power criterion — Peak demand:** $P_{ICE} + P_{EM} \geq P_{demand,max}$ → ensures the combined output meets the 0–100 km/h acceleration target. **(1 mark)**
2. **(b) Continuous power criterion:** $P_{ICE} \geq P_{cruise,100\,km/h}$ → ICE alone sustains highway cruise without battery drain. **(1 mark)**
3. **(c) Torque criterion at launch:** $T_{EM,peak} \geq T_{launch} / (\eta_t G)$ → EM delivers low-end torque that ICE cannot. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Why is the ICE never sized to deliver the full peak power of a hybrid vehicle? Give two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Reason 1 (Efficiency):** The ICE's best BSFC region is at **~25–35 %** of its wide-open-throttle (WOT) power. Sizing it to peak demand would force the engine to operate at **< 20 %** load during most city driving, increasing specific fuel consumption by **30–50 %**. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Reason 2 (Emissions & Weight):** A smaller ICE running near its sweet spot has lower NOx/PM emissions, better cold-start behaviour, and **15–25 %** less mass — directly improving FE and reducing 0–100 km/h time. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

A mid-size parallel hybrid sedan has the following specifications:
- Curb mass m = 1500 kg
- Rolling resistance coefficient C_rr = 0.012
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient times frontal area C_d A = 0.7 m²
- Air density ρ = 1.225 kg/m³
- Transmission efficiency η_t = 0.93
- Wheel radius r = 0.32 m
- Target 0–100 km/h time t_a = 10 s
- Target maximum grade α = 30 % at 30 km/h
- Sizing coefficients: k_1 = 0.6 (ICE), k_2 = 0.4 (EM)

### Part (a) — Calculate the maximum power demand of the vehicle. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert 0–100 km/h target to average acceleration.** (1 mark)

$$
a = \dfrac{100 \times 1000 / 3600}{10} = \dfrac{27.78}{10} = 2.778 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the four tractive force components at V_max = 27.78 m/s.** (3 marks)

$$
F_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (27.78)^2 = 331.21 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{hc} = 0 \text{ N (flat road at max accel)}
$$

$$
F_{ia} = 1500 \times 2.778 = 4\,167 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive force and tractive power.** (2 marks)

$$
F_t = 176.58 + 331.21 + 4\,167 = 4\,674.79 \text{ N}
$$

$$
P_t = F_t \times V = 4\,674.79 \times 27.78 = 129\,860 \text{ W} \approx 129.9 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Demand at shaft.** (1 mark)

$$
P_{demand,max} = \dfrac{P_t}{\eta_t} = \dfrac{129\,860}{0.93} \approx 139.6 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Final Answer (Part a):** $P_{demand,max} \approx 139.6 \text{ kW}$

---

### Part (b) — Size the ICE and EM. Verify the gradeability at 30 km/h on a 30 % grade. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply sizing coefficients.** (1 mark)

$$
P_{ICE} = 0.6 \times 139.6 = 83.76 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{EM} = 0.4 \times 139.6 = 55.84 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify gradeability at 30 km/h = 8.33 m/s, α = arctan(0.30) = 16.7°.** (4 marks)

Forces at 30 km/h on 30 % grade (steady, a = 0):

$$
F_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times \cos(16.7°) = 169.4 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (8.33)^2 = 29.76 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{hc} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(16.7°) = 4\,229 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_t = 169.4 + 29.76 + 4\,229 = 4\,428.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Power required at shaft.** (1 mark)

$$
P_{demand,grade} = \dfrac{4\,428.2 \times 8.33}{0.93} = 39\,650 \text{ W} \approx 39.65 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification.** (1 mark)

Since $P_{ICE} = 83.76$ kW $> P_{demand,grade} = 39.65$ kW, the ICE alone (with battery off) can climb the 30 % grade at 30 km/h with **~44 %** of its rated power. ✓

> **Final Answer (Part b):** $P_{ICE} = 83.76$ kW, $P_{EM} = 55.84$ kW. Gradeability is satisfied with ~44 % ICE loading.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A mild-hybrid city car uses a downsized 1.0 L turbocharged ICE. Justify the sizing decisions:

### Part (a) — Explain why the ICE in an HEV is typically sized to 50–60 % of the maximum demand power, while the EM is sized to 30–40 %. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **ICE sizing (k_1 = 0.5–0.6):** (3 marks)
   - The ICE's optimal BSFC map occupies **25–35 %** of WOT power. Sizing ICE at 100 % demand would mean it operates at 5–15 % load 90 % of the time → FE penalty **30–50 %**, emissions increase, and weight penalty.
   - The engine is also downsized for **thermal efficiency at part-load** (Atkinson cycle), and the EM "fills the gaps" during transients. Examples: Toyota 1.8 L (72 kW) in 100 kW Prius, Honda 1.5 L (77 kW) in 135 kW Insight.

2. **EM sizing (k_2 = 0.3–0.4):** (3 marks)
   - The EM must (i) deliver launch torque (T_peak = 1.5–2× continuous rating), (ii) absorb full regenerative braking energy (limited by battery C-rate, **2–5 C**), and (iii) maintain ~2–5 km all-EV city range.
   - The EM should not exceed **~40 %** of demand because (a) the battery and inverter cost scales roughly linearly with EM power, (b) EM mass = 2–3 kg/kW (PMSM) limits further increase, and (c) it must fit between the engine and transmission (TMED layout).

3. **Overlapping rating rule:** (1 mark)
   - $k_1 + k_2 \geq 1$ ensures peak power is met; the overlap (here 0% if $k_1+k_2 = 1$, otherwise reserved) supports power-blend modes and is sized using WLTP topographical cycle.

---

### Part (b) — A target all-EV city range of 5 km is required at 50 km/h average speed. Compute the minimum battery energy. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Forces at V_city = 50 km/h = 13.89 m/s, a = 0, α = 0.** (2 marks)

$$
F_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (13.89)^2 = 82.79 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{t,EV} = 176.58 + 82.79 = 259.37 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Power at wheels and shaft.** (1 mark)

$$
P_{wheel,EV} = 259.37 \times 13.89 = 3\,603 \text{ W} = 3.60 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{shaft,EV} = 3.60 / 0.93 = 3.87 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to cover 5 km at 50 km/h.** (1 mark)

$$
t = 5 \text{ km} / 50 \text{ km/h} = 0.1 \text{ h} = 360 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy stored in battery (accounting for discharge efficiency η_dis = 0.90).** (3 marks)

$$
E_{batt} = \dfrac{P_{shaft,EV} \times t}{\eta_{dis}} = \dfrac{3.87 \times 0.1}{0.90} = 0.43 \text{ kWh}
$$

> **Final Answer (Part b):** $E_{batt,min} \approx 0.43 \text{ kWh}$ (≈ 1.55 MJ)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by η_t** — students often compute P_t at the wheels and stop, losing 1–2 marks per sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing "V" units** — using km/h directly in $F_{ad} = 0.5 \rho C_d A V^2$ gives a 10⁶ error. Always convert to m/s.
> 3. **Adding P_ICE and P_EM in series-mode** — in a series HEV, ICE powers only the generator (not the wheels directly). Combined peak ≠ sum of ICE + EM at the wheels.
> 4. **Ignoring rolling resistance on grades** — using $\sin\alpha$ alone; the full expression is $mg(C_{rr}\cos\alpha + \sin\alpha)$.
> 5. **Not validating gradeability** — many students size the components but skip the **verification** step (5-mark item in part b).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module-3):**

- **Sizing is a time-sharing problem, not an addition problem.** ICE and EM are rated such that their **time-averaged contributions** match the drive-cycle demand.
- The ICE covers **average power**; the EM covers **peaks and valleys**.
- The four tractive forces are: **rolling resistance (V), aerodynamic drag (V³), hill climbing (constant), and inertial (transient).**
- **Aerodynamic drag dominates at high speed** (V³) — the third-power dependency means a doubling of speed **octuples** the drag power.
- **Inertial force dominates during acceleration** — the EM must supply this peak instantaneously.
- **Power matching rule:** $P_{ICE} + P_{EM} \geq P_{demand,max}$ **and** $P_{ICE} \geq P_{cruise}$.
- **Torque matching rule:** $T_{EM,peak} \geq T_{launch}$ (because ICE has poor low-end torque and EM has constant-torque region up to base speed).
- **Battery sizing rule:** $E_{batt} = F_{t,EV} \cdot d_{EV} / \eta_{dis}$ for the all-EV range target.
- **Sizing coefficients** for a parallel HEV: $k_1 \approx 0.5–0.6$ (ICE), $k_2 \approx 0.3–0.4$ (EM).
- **Verification steps** are mandatory in KTU numericals: (i) BSFC sweet-spot check, (ii) gradeability check, (iii) EV range check, (iv) max-speed check.
- **Real-world examples to memorise:** Toyota THS-II (72 kW ICE + 53 kW EM1 + 23 kW EM2), Honda i-MMD (77 kW ICE + 135 kW EM), Hyundai TMED (77 kW ICE + 32 kW EM).
- **Units discipline:** m/s for V, N for F, W for P, J or kWh for E. Mixing km/h with m/s is the #1 mark-loss reason in KTU numerics.
- **Standard values to remember:** C_rr = **0.012**, C_d A = **0.7 m²**, ρ = **1.225 kg/m³**, η_t = **0.93**, g = **9.81 m/s²**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Energy Storage Requirements in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Energy Storage Requirements in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Storage Requirements in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs)** refer to the quantified set of performance, durability, safety, and operational specifications that an energy storage system (ESS) must satisfy to deliver tractive power, store regenerative braking energy, and provide sufficient driving range under real-world duty cycles.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the energy storage system is the *single most critical sub-assembly* of any electrified powertrain because it simultaneously dictates:

1. The all-electric driving **range** (energy-limited performance).
2. The **acceleration and gradeability** capability (power-limited performance).
3. The **lifetime cost of ownership** (cycle-life and calendar-life limited).
4. The **thermal management** and **safety architecture** of the vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4, Topic 1):** Students must be able to *list, justify, and quantify* the design trade-offs between **specific energy (Wh/kg)**, **specific power (W/kg)**, **energy density (Wh/L)**, **power density (W/L)**, **cycle life**, **calendar life**, **round-trip efficiency**, and **cost per kWh**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower" of an EV

Imagine an electric vehicle as a city, and the energy storage system as a **water tower** supplying every house (the motor).

- **How big is the tank?** → Determines how far the city can run before the water runs out (driving range = energy capacity).
- **How fast can water flow out of the pipe?** → Determines how many houses can be served at once (peak power = acceleration / hill climbing).
- **How quickly can the tank be refilled?** → Determines charging time (regenerative braking acceptance & grid charging rate).
- **How many fill–empty cycles can the tower survive?** → Cycle life (years of service).
- **How much water leaks or evaporates per day even when idle?** → Self-discharge rate (calendar aging).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** An EV's energy storage is not just "a big battery." It is a *carefully engineered trade-off* between **how much energy** it can hold, **how fast** it can release or absorb that energy, **how long** it lasts, and **how cheaply and safely** it can be manufactured.

---

## 1.3 The Six Pillars of Energy Storage Requirements

The KTU board expects every student to articulate these six foundational requirements:

| # | Requirement | Symbol / Unit | Why It Matters |
|---|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Specific Energy** | Wh/kg | Determines vehicle weight & range |
| 2 | **Energy Density** | Wh/L | Determines pack volume & packaging |
| 3 | **Specific Power** | W/kg | Determines acceleration & regen acceptance |
| 4 | **Power Density** | W/L | Determines peak power in small volume |
| 5 | **Cycle Life** | Cycles | Determines years of service (warranty) |
| 6 | **Calendar Life** | Years | Determines shelf-life and storage degradation |

Additional secondary requirements include **round-trip efficiency (%)**, **operating temperature window (°C)**, **internal resistance / impedance (mΩ)**, **state-of-charge (SOC) window**, and **cost ($/kWh)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box — Ragone Plot:** A Ragone plot is a log–log chart of **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the X-axis vs. **specific power (W/kg)** on the Y-axis. It is the single most important visualization used to compare different ESS technologies. Devices high on the plot deliver more power per kg; devices far on the right deliver more energy per kg. The *ideal* ESS for a BEV sits in the **upper-right corner**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — Step-by-Step Logic

The energy storage requirements of a vehicle are derived from the **vehicle-level performance targets**. The systematic methodology used by automotive OEMs is:

### **Step 1 — Establish the Energy Demand (Range-Limited Requirement)**

The required usable energy is computed from the target driving range, vehicle mass, rolling resistance coefficient, drag coefficient, frontal area, and assumed drive cycle (e.g., WLTP, FTP-75, NEDC).

### **Step 2 — Establish the Power Demand (Performance-Limited Requirement)**

The required peak power is computed from the maximum grade, maximum acceleration (0–100 km/h time), and top speed. This requirement dictates the *minimum* power capability of the ESS, independent of energy content.

### **Step 3 — Apply Drive-Cycle Realism**

A real drive cycle alternates between propulsion and regeneration. The ESS must therefore support a high **charge acceptance rate (C-rate in)** during braking without exceeding thermal or voltage limits.

### **Step 4 — Account for Auxiliary Loads**

HVAC, lighting, infotainment, and battery thermal management draw continuous parasitic power — typically **1–3 kW**. The ESS must size for *propulsion + auxiliary* loads.

### **Step 5 — Apply Degradation Margins**

End-of-life (EOL) energy retention is typically **80% of beginning-of-life (BOL)**. To meet range targets at EOL, the pack must be over-sized by ~25% at BOL.

### **Step 6 — Apply Safety and Cost Constraints**

After the technical sizing, **safety margins (cell-to-pack thermal runaway propagation)** and **target cost ($/kWh)** are applied as boundary conditions. The final ESS design is the optimum that simultaneously satisfies all six steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind Step 5:** A lithium-ion cell that begins life with 100% capacity will, after ~2000 cycles, retain only ~80%. If the vehicle is marketed with a 400 km range based on BOL energy, the customer will experience only ~320 km at end-of-warranty unless the pack is over-sized. This is why modern BEVs often advertise a *larger* pack than the bare-minimum range calculation suggests.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the column headings in the table below. KTU 2024 Part A questions frequently ask for the *unit* and *physical meaning* of each metric.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Specific Energy** | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}}$ | Wh/kg | Energy per unit mass |
| 2 | **Energy Density** | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{V_{pack}}$ | Wh/L | Energy per unit volume |
| 3 | **Specific Power** | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{m_{pack}}$ | W/kg | Peak power per unit mass |
| 4 | **Power Density** | $P_{vol} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{pack}}$ | W/L | Peak power per unit volume |
| 5 | **C-rate (charge)** | $C_{in} = \dfrac{I_{charge}}{Q_{Ah}}$ | 1/h | Charge current normalized to capacity |
| 6 | **C-rate (discharge)** | $C_{out} = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{Ah}}$ | 1/h | Discharge current normalized to capacity |
| 7 | **Round-trip efficiency** | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Energy recovered vs. energy stored |
| 8 | **Usable SOC window** | $\Delta SOC = SOC_{max} - SOC_{min}$ | % | Fraction of capacity actually cycled |
| 9 | **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** | $DoD = 1 - SOC$ | % | Energy removed per cycle |
| 10 | **Cycle life** | Empirical, function of DoD, C-rate, T | cycles | Number of full cycles before 80% retention |
| 11 | **Self-discharge rate** | $\dfrac{dSOC}{dt}$ at rest | %/month | Energy lost during storage |
| 12 | **Energy for range** | $E_{req} = \dfrac{R \cdot (mg C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2})}{\eta_{drivetrain}}$ | Wh | Pack energy to cover range R |
| 13 | **Power for grade** | $P_{grade} = v \cdot (mg \sin\theta + mg C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2})$ | W | Power to climb angle θ at speed v |
| 14 | **Power for acceleration** | $P_{acc} = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_{f}^{2} - v_{i}^{2}) / t_{acc} + F_{rr} v + F_{ad} v$ | W | Power to reach v_f in time t_acc |
| 15 | **Peukert's Law** | $I^{n} \cdot t = C_{nominal}$ | — | Capacity loss at high discharge rates |
| 16 | **Ragone normalized metric** | $\dfrac{E_{sp}}{E_{sp,ref}} \cdot \dfrac{P_{sp}}{P_{sp,ref}}$ | — | Combined energy-power figure of merit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Equation (12) is used in *every* OEM feasibility study to size the initial pack. Equation (13) is used to verify that the chosen cell can deliver the *gradeability* target. Equation (14) is used to size the *peak* power electronics and motor.

---

## 2.3 The Power–Energy Trade-off (The Fundamental Conflict)

No single energy storage technology simultaneously maximizes both energy and power. This is the **Ragone trade-off**:

- **Batteries (Li-ion)** → high energy, moderate power. Dominant for range.
- **Ultracapacitors (EDLCs)** → very high power, low energy. Dominant for transients.
- **Fuel cells (H₂)** → extremely high energy, low power. Need a battery buffer.
- **Flywheels** → high power, moderate energy. Mostly retired in automotive.
- **Hybrid ESS (Battery + Ultracapacitor)** → combines the strengths; the *most likely* near-term optimum for heavy-duty HEVs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Required to Cover a Target Range

We derive the energy stored in the pack required to propel a vehicle of mass **m** over a distance **R** on level ground at constant velocity **v**, neglecting auxiliary loads.

### Step 1: Identify the resistive forces at constant speed on a level road

At constant velocity on flat ground, the tractive force must overcome **rolling resistance** and **aerodynamic drag**:

$$F_{trac} = F_{rr} + F_{ad}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the standard expressions

The rolling resistance force is $F_{rr} = m g C_{rr}$ and the aerodynamic drag is $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$. Therefore:

$$F_{trac} = m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$$

### Step 3: Compute the mechanical work done over distance R

The work done against the resistive forces over distance R is $W = F_{trac} \cdot R$:

$$W = R \cdot \left( m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2} \right)$$

### Step 4: Convert mechanical work to electrical energy at the battery terminals

Dividing by the drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{d}$ (which combines motor + inverter + transmission losses) gives the energy that must leave the battery pack:

$$E_{req} = \dfrac{R}{\eta_{d}} \cdot \left( m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2} \right)$$

### Step 5: Numerical check (typical compact BEV)

Take $m = 1500$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_{d} = 0.29$, $A = 2.2$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $v = 27.78$ m/s (100 km/h), $\eta_{d} = 0.88$, $R = 300$ km $= 3 \times 10^{5}$ m.

Begin with rolling resistance term:

$$F_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

Now the aerodynamic drag term:

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.2 \times (27.78)^{2} = 300.42 \text{ N}$$

Sum to get the total tractive force:

$$F_{trac} = 176.58 + 300.42 = 477.00 \text{ N}$$

Work done over 300 km:

$$W = 477.00 \times 3 \times 10^{5} = 1.431 \times 10^{8} \text{ J}$$

Convert to Wh and divide by efficiency:

$$E_{req} = \dfrac{1.431 \times 10^{8}}{0.88 \times 3600} = 4.517 \times 10^{4} \text{ Wh} \approx 45.2 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 45.2 kWh is the *usable* energy at the wheels. End-of-life over-sizing (Step 5 of §2.1) means the *actual installed* pack capacity will be ~55–60 kWh — exactly what real compact BEVs (e.g., Tata Nexon EV, Hyundai Kona) install for a 400 km marketed range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Peak Power Required to Climb a Grade

### Step 1: Force balance on a slope

On an incline of angle θ, gravity contributes a component $mg \sin\theta$ to the resistive force. The required tractive force is:

$$F_{trac} = m g \sin\theta + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$$

### Step 2: Power = Force × Velocity

For a vehicle climbing at constant speed v:

$$P_{grade} = v \cdot F_{trac} = v \cdot \left( m g \sin\theta + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2} \right)$$

### Step 3: Numerical check (30% grade, 50 km/h, 1500 kg)

Take $\theta = \arctan(0.30) = 16.7°$, $v = 13.89$ m/s (50 km/h), $m = 1500$ kg.

Compute $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$:

$$\sin(16.7°) = 0.2874, \quad \cos(16.7°) = 0.9578$$

Gravity component:

$$m g \sin\theta = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.2874 = 4230.0 \text{ N}$$

Rolling resistance component (on slope):

$$m g C_{rr} \cos\theta = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 0.9578 = 169.13 \text{ N}$$

Aerodynamic drag (recalling v = 13.89 m/s):

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.2 \times (13.89)^{2} = 75.07 \text{ N}$$

Total tractive force:

$$F_{trac} = 4230.0 + 169.13 + 75.07 = 4474.20 \text{ N}$$

Mechanical power:

$$P_{mech} = 13.89 \times 4474.20 = 62{,}146 \text{ W} \approx 62.1 \text{ kW}$$

Electrical power drawn from the pack (η_d = 0.88):

$$P_{elec} = \dfrac{62{,}146}{0.88} = 70{,}620 \text{ W} \approx 70.6 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A 30% grade at 50 km/h demands ~70 kW *continuous*. The ESS must therefore support a sustained C-rate of $\frac{70{,}600 \text{ W}}{E_{pack} \times 3600} = \frac{70{,}600}{45{,}200 \times 3600} \approx 0.43$ C for a 45 kWh pack. A 3C discharge capability is the *industry benchmark* for traction packs to handle grade + acceleration transients.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Delivered During Regenerative Braking

### Step 1: Energy available at the wheels during deceleration

When a vehicle of mass m decelerates from $v_i$ to $v_f$:

$$\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_{i}^{2} - v_{f}^{2})$$

### Step 2: Energy actually recovered to the ESS

The regenerative system cannot recover 100% of the kinetic energy (brake thermal losses, motor/inverter losses, battery charge-acceptance limit). The recovered energy is:

$$E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \eta_{m} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_{i}^{2} - v_{f}^{2})$$

A typical combined efficiency is $\eta_{regen} \cdot \eta_{m} \cdot \eta_{inv} \approx 0.55$ to $0.70$.

### Step 3: Numerical check (1500 kg, 100 → 0 km/h, 60% recovery)

$$v_i = 27.78 \text{ m/s}, \quad v_f = 0$$

$$\Delta KE = 0.5 \times 1500 \times (27.78)^{2} = 5.788 \times 10^{5} \text{ J}$$

$$E_{regen} = 0.65 \times 5.788 \times 10^{5} = 3.762 \times 10^{5} \text{ J} = 104.5 \text{ Wh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** A single 100→0 km/h stop in a 1500 kg car recovers ~104 Wh — enough to power ~10 houses for an hour. The ESS must have a high *charge-acceptance C-rate* (typically ≥ 2C to 4C) to capture this energy in the few seconds of braking without overheating or hitting the upper voltage cut-off.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Drive Cycle / Event | Energy Demand (Wh/km) | Power Demand (kW) | ESS Metric That Dominates |
|--------------------|------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Constant 100 km/h cruise | 130–170 | 15–25 | **Specific Energy** |
| Urban stop-and-go (WLTP) | 150–200 | 5–15 (avg) | **Cycle life, η_RT** |
| 0–100 km/h acceleration (7 s) | negligible | 100–150 (peak) | **Specific / Power density** |
| 30% grade, 50 km/h | 50 (per km climbed) | 60–80 (sustained) | **Continuous C-rate, thermal** |
| Hard regen (100→0 km/h) | 100 Wh per event | up to 70 (peak) | **Charge C-rate, voltage limits** |
| Idle + HVAC | 1000 Wh/h | 1.5–3 | **Calendar life, parasitic loss** |

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the terms (i) Specific Energy and (ii) Specific Power. State their typical SI units and explain why a high value of both is desired in an EV energy storage system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Specific Energy** is the amount of electrical energy stored per unit mass of the energy storage device.

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{stored}}{m_{pack}} \quad [\text{Wh/kg}]$$

A high specific energy means the pack is *light* for a given range, which directly reduces vehicle mass and improves efficiency.

**Specific Power** is the peak electrical power deliverable per unit mass of the device.

$$P_{sp} = \frac{P_{max}}{m_{pack}} \quad [\text{W/kg}]$$

A high specific power means the pack can deliver *large bursts* of current for acceleration and hill climbing, and *accept large bursts* during regenerative braking, without overheating.

**Why both are needed:** Range demands **high specific energy**; acceleration and regen demand **high specific power**. The ideal EV ESS must satisfy both — hence the use of Ragone plots to compare technologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (KTU 2024):** Award full 3 marks only if the student writes the *unit* explicitly and gives one sentence on the *physical significance*. A unit-less answer loses 1 mark.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a Ragone plot, briefly explain why no single energy storage technology is sufficient for a hybrid electric vehicle. What is the typical solution adopted?**

**Model Answer:**

A Ragone plot is a log–log plot of **specific energy (Wh/kg)** versus **specific power (W/kg)** used to compare ESS technologies. The *ideal* ESS would lie at the top-right corner of this plot.

- **Batteries (Li-ion):** High specific energy (150–250 Wh/kg) but moderate specific power (250–700 W/kg) — excellent for range but cannot deliver extreme peak power.
- **Ultracapacitors (EDLCs):** Low specific energy (5–10 Wh/kg) but extremely high specific power (5,000–15,000 W/kg) — perfect for transients but cannot store enough energy for range.
- **Fuel cells:** Very high specific energy but very low specific power — need a buffer.

Since **batteries** and **ultracapacitors** occupy *different* regions of the Ragone plot, no single technology satisfies both energy and power requirements simultaneously.

**Typical solution:** A **hybrid energy storage system (HESS)** combining a high-energy battery with a high-power ultracapacitor, managed by a DC–DC converter and a supervisory energy management strategy. The battery supplies average power; the ultracapacitor handles transient peaks and captures regenerative energy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in this module feature **internal choice**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### ✍️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the total energy required at the battery terminals for a vehicle of mass *m* to travel a distance *R* at constant speed *v* on a level road. Define all symbols used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A compact BEV has the following specifications: $m = 1400$ kg, $C_{rr} = 0.010$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.1$ m², $\eta_{d} = 0.90$. Calculate (i) the energy required to travel 250 km at a constant speed of 90 km/h, and (ii) the power required to cruise at 120 km/h on a level road. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a): Derivation of the Range-Energy Equation**

**Step 1:** At constant velocity on level ground, Newton's first law requires the tractive force to balance the resistive forces:

$$F_{trac} = F_{rr} + F_{ad}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the standard physics expressions:

- Rolling resistance: $F_{rr} = m g C_{rr}$
- Aerodynamic drag: $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$

$$F_{trac} = m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$$

**Step 3:** The mechanical work performed against these forces over distance R is:

$$W_{mech} = F_{trac} \cdot R = R \left( m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2} \right)$$

**Step 4:** To find the *electrical* energy that must be drawn from the battery pack, we divide by the drivetrain efficiency $\eta_d$ (which lumps motor, inverter, and transmission losses):

$$E_{req} = \frac{R}{\eta_{d}} \left( m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2} \right)$$

**Where:**

- $E_{req}$ = electrical energy required at the battery terminals (J)
- $R$ = target range (m)
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $C_{rr}$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless)
- $\rho$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³)
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)
- $A$ = frontal area (m²)
- $v$ = constant cruise speed (m/s)
- $\eta_d$ = drivetrain efficiency (dimensionless, 0 < η ≤ 1)

---

#### **Part (b): Numerical Evaluation**

**Given:** $m = 1400$ kg, $C_{rr} = 0.010$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.1$ m², $\eta_d = 0.90$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $R = 250$ km $= 2.5 \times 10^5$ m.

##### **(i) Energy to travel 250 km at v = 90 km/h = 25 m/s**

Compute rolling resistance force:

$$F_{rr} = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.010 = 137.34 \text{ N}$$

Compute aerodynamic drag at 25 m/s:

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times (25)^{2} = 225.05 \text{ N}$$

Total tractive force:

$$F_{trac} = 137.34 + 225.05 = 362.39 \text{ N}$$

Mechanical work over 250 km:

$$W = 362.39 \times 2.5 \times 10^5 = 9.060 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$

Electrical energy at battery terminals:

$$E_{req} = \frac{9.060 \times 10^7}{0.90} = 1.0067 \times 10^8 \text{ J}$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E_{req} = \frac{1.0067 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 27.96 \text{ kWh}$$

##### **(ii) Power required to cruise at v = 120 km/h = 33.33 m/s on level road**

Compute aerodynamic drag at 33.33 m/s:

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times (33.33)^{2} = 400.05 \text{ N}$$

Rolling resistance (unchanged):

$$F_{rr} = 137.34 \text{ N}$$

Total tractive force:

$$F_{trac} = 137.34 + 400.05 = 537.39 \text{ N}$$

Mechanical power at the wheels:

$$P_{mech} = v \cdot F_{trac} = 33.33 \times 537.39 = 17{,}910 \text{ W} \approx 17.9 \text{ kW}$$

Electrical power at the battery:

$$P_{elec} = \frac{17.91}{0.90} = 19.9 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Set:** (i) $E_{req} \approx 28.0$ kWh; (ii) $P_{elec} \approx 19.9$ kW at 120 km/h cruise.

---

### ✍️ **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a Ragone plot? Draw a labelled schematic and explain how it is used to compare the suitability of batteries, ultracapacitors, and fuel cells for EV applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hybrid ESS combines a 30 kWh Li-ion battery and a 0.5 kWh ultracapacitor pack. The Li-ion pack has a specific energy of 180 Wh/kg and the ultracapacitor pack has 6 Wh/kg. The Li-ion delivers 70 kW peak and the ultracapacitor delivers 80 kW peak. Calculate (i) the total mass of the HESS, (ii) the combined specific energy of the HESS, and (iii) the combined specific power of the HESS. Comment on the suitability of this combination for an urban delivery HEV.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a): Ragone Plot — Concept and Application**

A **Ragone plot** is a log–log graph of **specific power (W/kg)** on the Y-axis versus **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the X-axis. It was introduced by David Ragone (1968) to compare the energy-power capabilities of different energy storage devices in a single chart.

**Typical positions of devices on the Ragone plot:**

| Technology | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Specific Power (W/kg) |
|------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Li-ion battery** | 150–250 | 250–700 |
| **Ultracapacitor (EDLC)** | 5–10 | 5,000–15,000 |
| **Hydrogen fuel cell** | 500–1000 (system) | 100–500 |
| **Lead-acid battery** | 30–50 | 100–200 |
| **NiMH battery** | 60–100 | 200–300 |

**Interpretation for EV applications:**

- The **Li-ion battery** sits in the *mid-right* of the plot — ideal for **primary propulsion energy** (range).
- The **ultracapacitor** sits at the *top-left* — ideal for **transient power** (acceleration and regen).
- The **hydrogen fuel cell** sits at the *far right* (high energy) but *low on Y-axis* (low power) — requires a battery or ultracapacitor buffer.

**Conclusion:** A Ragone plot shows that **no single technology** occupies the top-right corner, justifying the use of **hybrid ESS** (HESS) architectures in modern HEVs and FCEVs.

---

#### **Part (b): Numerical Analysis of the Hybrid ESS**

**Given:**
- Battery: $E_{bat} = 30$ kWh $= 30{,}000$ Wh, $E_{sp,bat} = 180$ Wh/kg, $P_{bat} = 70$ kW $= 70{,}000$ W
- Ultracapacitor: $E_{uc} = 0.5$ kWh $= 500$ Wh, $E_{sp,uc} = 6$ Wh/kg, $P_{uc} = 80$ kW $= 80{,}000$ W

##### **(i) Total Mass of the HESS**

Mass of the Li-ion pack:

$$m_{bat} = \frac{E_{bat}}{E_{sp,bat}} = \frac{30{,}000}{180} = 166.67 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of the ultracapacitor pack:

$$m_{uc} = \frac{E_{uc}}{E_{sp,uc}} = \frac{500}{6} = 83.33 \text{ kg}$$

Total HESS mass:

$$m_{HESS} = m_{bat} + m_{uc} = 166.67 + 83.33 = 250.00 \text{ kg}$$

##### **(ii) Combined Specific Energy of the HESS**

Total stored energy:

$$E_{total} = 30{,}000 + 500 = 30{,}500 \text{ Wh}$$

Combined specific energy:

$$E_{sp,HESS} = \frac{E_{total}}{m_{HESS}} = \frac{30{,}500}{250.00} = 122.0 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

##### **(iii) Combined Specific Power of the HESS**

Total peak power (assuming both can deliver peak simultaneously — worst case):

$$P_{total} = 70{,}000 + 80{,}000 = 150{,}000 \text{ W}$$

Combined specific power:

$$P_{sp,HESS} = \frac{P_{total}}{m_{HESS}} = \frac{150{,}000}{250.00} = 600 \text{ W/kg}$$

##### **Comment on Suitability for an Urban Delivery HEV**

- The combined specific energy of **122 Wh/kg** is lower than a pure Li-ion pack (180 Wh/kg) — a *trade-off* in exchange for higher power.
- The combined specific power of **600 W/kg** is significantly higher than Li-ion alone (~420 W/kg) — enabling strong **regenerative braking capture** during frequent urban stops.
- The ultracapacitor (0.5 kWh) provides a small but powerful *buffer* for stop-and-go traffic, ideal for urban delivery routes.
- The 150 kW combined peak power easily handles short acceleration bursts in dense city traffic.
- **Verdict:** This HESS is **highly suitable** for an urban delivery HEV because such vehicles have **frequent regen events** (the ultracapacitor's strength) and **moderate daily range requirements** (the battery's role), making the trade-off favorable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Set:** (i) $m_{HESS} = 250$ kg; (ii) $E_{sp,HESS} = 122$ Wh/kg; (iii) $P_{sp,HESS} = 600$ W/kg.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $\eta_d$** — Students frequently compute *mechanical* energy and present it as the *battery* energy. Always check whether the question asks for energy at the wheels or at the battery terminals. Loss: **2–3 marks per part**.
> 
> 2. **Mixing units of velocity** — The formula $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ requires $v$ in **m/s**, not km/h. A common error is plugging in $v = 90$ instead of $v = 25$. Loss: **2 marks per occurrence**.
> 
> 3. **Using $mg$ instead of $mgC_{rr}$** — Rolling resistance is *proportional* to $C_{rr}$, not equal to gravity. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 
> 4. **Conflating SOC and DoD** — A student who writes "100% DoD" instead of "0% SOC" will lose **1 mark** in definition questions. Remember: $DoD = 1 - SOC$.
> 
> 5. **Missing the "Comment" sub-part** — In KTU 2024, every numerical sub-part in Part B ends with a *comment/discussion* line worth **1–2 marks**. Skipping it loses marks even if the calculation is perfect.
> 
> 6. **Drawing a Ragone plot without axes labelled in log scale** — A linear-scale plot is *not* a Ragone plot. KTU examiners specifically check for the log–log convention. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ The **six pillars** of EV/HEV energy storage requirements are: **specific energy, energy density, specific power, power density, cycle life, and calendar life**.
- ✅ **Specific energy (Wh/kg)** governs driving *range*; **specific power (W/kg)** governs *acceleration and regen* capability.
- ✅ The **Ragone plot** is a log–log chart of $P_{sp}$ vs. $E_{sp}$ and is the canonical tool for comparing ESS technologies.
- ✅ **Li-ion batteries** dominate BEVs; **ultracapacitors** dominate regenerative transients; **fuel cells** need a buffer.
- ✅ The **range-energy equation** is $E_{req} = \frac{R}{\eta_d} (m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2)$ — must always be divided by $\eta_d$ to get battery-side energy.
- ✅ The **grade-power equation** is $P_{grade} = v (m g \sin\theta + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2)$.
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency** of Li-ion is ~85–95%; of ultracapacitor is ~90–98%; of fuel cell system is ~40–60%.
- ✅ **Cycle life** is heavily dependent on **DoD**, **C-rate**, and **temperature**. A 100% DoD Li-ion cell may last ~1000 cycles; at 50% DoD it can last ~3000 cycles.
- ✅ **End-of-life** (EOL) is conventionally defined as **80% of beginning-of-life (BOL) capacity**.
- ✅ **Hybrid ESS (HESS)** combining battery + ultracapacitor is the optimum architecture for heavy-duty HEVs and urban delivery vehicles.
- ✅ The **C-rate** is the discharge or charge current divided by the rated capacity: $C = I / Q_{Ah}$. A 3C discharge on a 50 Ah cell means 150 A.
- ✅ **State of Charge (SOC)** is the *available* capacity as a fraction of *rated* capacity; **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** is its complement.
- ✅ Always **state units explicitly** in KTU answers — examiners deduct marks silently for unit omissions.
- ✅ Always **define every symbol** in derivation questions — un-defined symbols can lose up to **1 mark** per derivation.
- ✅ In numerical problems, **end with a "comment"** sentence on the engineering significance of the result. This is a KTU 2024 expectation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Based Energy Storage and Its Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Battery Based Energy Storage and Its Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Battery as Energy Storage in Electric & Hybrid Vehicles

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **battery** in an electric/hybrid vehicle context is an electrochemical energy storage device that converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy through controlled redox reactions, and is reversible (rechargeable). In EV powertrains, batteries serve as the primary **Rechargeable Energy Storage System (RESS)** that supplies electrical energy to the traction motor and recaptures regenerative braking energy.

## 1.2 Key Terminology — The Battery Lexicon

| Term | Definition | Unit / Notation |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| **Cell** | The smallest electrochemical unit producing a fixed voltage (e.g., Li-ion ≈ **3.6–3.7 V nominal**). | V |
| **Module** | A group of cells connected in series and/or parallel, mechanically and electrically integrated. | — |
| **Pack** | The complete battery assembly comprising modules, BMS, cooling, and housing. | — |
| **Capacity (Q)** | Total charge a fully charged battery can deliver. | **Ampere-hour (Ah)** |
| **Nominal Voltage (V_n)** | Reference operating voltage of a cell/pack. | **Volt (V)** |
| **Energy (E)** | Total work a battery can perform, E = V × Q. | **Watt-hour (Wh)** |
| **Power (P)** | Instantaneous rate of energy delivery, P = V × I. | **Watt (W)** |
| **Energy Density** | Energy per unit mass/volume. | **Wh/kg** or **Wh/L** |
| **Power Density** | Power per unit mass/volume. | **W/kg** or **W/L** |
| **C-Rate** | Discharge/charge current normalized to rated capacity. | **C** (dimensionless) |
| **SOC** | State of Charge — present capacity as % of rated. | **%** |
| **DOD** | Depth of Discharge — 1 − SOC. | **%** |
| **SOH** | State of Health — current max capacity vs original. | **%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** Examiners consistently test the *difference* between energy and power density, and the *interpretation of C-rate*. Memorize the energy/power density table of Li-ion, NiMH, and Lead-acid.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tank"

Imagine a **water tank** connected to a tap:
- **Tank size (Capacity in Ah)** = How much water the tank can hold.
- **Water pressure (Voltage)** = Force pushing water out.
- **Pipe diameter (C-rate)** = How fast water can flow out.
- **Total useful water (Energy in Wh)** = Pressure × Volume = V × Q.
- **Flow rate at the tap (Power in W)** = Pressure × Flow rate = V × I.

> A **large tank at low pressure** stores lots of energy slowly (high energy density, low power density — e.g., a laptop battery).
> A **small tank at high pressure** delivers energy very fast (high power density, low energy density — e.g., a supercapacitor).
> An **EV battery** must balance both — a moderately sized tank at high pressure, with a *fat pipe* (high C-rate) for acceleration.

## 1.4 Why Batteries Dominate EV Energy Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The selection of a battery for an EV is governed by the simultaneous maximization of: **(1) Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** for range, **(2) Specific Power (W/kg)** for acceleration, **(3) Cycle Life** for longevity, **(4) Calendar Life** for shelf life, and **(5) Safety** under abuse conditions. No single chemistry excels in all five — this is the central engineering trade-off in EV battery design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Battery Operational Principles

A rechargeable battery operates on **reversible electrochemical reactions**. During discharge:
- **Anode (negative electrode):** Oxidation occurs — releases electrons to external circuit.
- **Cathode (positive electrode):** Reduction occurs — accepts electrons.
- **Electrolyte:** Provides ionic conduction between electrodes.
- **Separator:** Physically separates electrodes while allowing ion flow.

During charging, an external power source forces current in the reverse direction, driving the non-spontaneous reverse reaction.

## 2.2 The C-Rate Concept — "Speed of Energy Withdrawal"

The **C-rate** is the most misunderstood yet most-examined parameter:

$$ I = C\text{-rate} \times Q_{\text{rated}} $$

**Worked Examples (KTU-style):**
- A **50 Ah** battery discharged at **1C** delivers **50 A** for **1 hour**.
- The same battery at **2C** delivers **100 A** for **0.5 hour** (30 minutes).
- At **0.5C**, it delivers **25 A** for **2 hours**.
- A **5C discharge** (typical for EV acceleration) yields **250 A** for **12 minutes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher C-rate → Higher internal heat generation (I²R) → Lower usable capacity (Peukert effect for Lead-acid) and accelerated degradation.**

## 2.3 SOC, DOD, and the Linear Approximation

The **State of Charge (SOC)** at any instant is mathematically expressed as:

$$ \text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau $$

For **constant current** discharge, this simplifies to:

$$ \text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}_0 - \frac{I \cdot t}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\% $$

The **Depth of Discharge (DOD)** is the complement:

$$ \text{DOD} = 100\% - \text{SOC} $$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the formula set most frequently tested in KTU ESE Module 4 questions. Master every entry.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Boundary / Limitation |
|---|---------|-------------|------|----------------------|
| 1 | $E = V_{\text{nom}} \times Q$ | Energy stored in a fully charged battery | Wh | Assumes constant V_nom |
| 2 | $P = V \times I$ | Instantaneous electrical power | W | DC or RMS for AC |
| 3 | $E_{\text{sp}} = E / m$ | Specific energy (gravimetric) | Wh/kg | Compare chemistries |
| 4 | $E_{\text{vol}} = E / V_{\text{vol}}$ | Volumetric energy density | Wh/L | Pack-level includes casing |
| 5 | $P_{\text{sp}} = P / m$ | Specific power | W/kg | Pulse rating for EV |
| 6 | $I = C \times Q_{\text{rated}}$ | C-rate definition | A | $C \in [0, \infty)$ |
| 7 | $t_{\text{discharge}} = Q / I = 1/C$ | Discharge time at constant C | h | Ideal case |
| 8 | $\text{SOC} = (Q_{\text{remain}} / Q_{\text{rated}}) \times 100$ | State of charge | % | $0 \leq \text{SOC} \leq 100$ |
| 9 | $\text{SOH} = (Q_{\text{max,current}} / Q_{\text{rated,new}}) \times 100$ | State of health | % | EOL typically at 80% |
| 10 | $V_{\text{term}} = V_{\text{oc}} - I \cdot R_{\text{int}}$ | Terminal voltage under load | V | Linear R_int model |
| 11 | $P_{\text{loss}} = I^2 R_{\text{int}}$ | Ohmic power loss | W | Heat generation |
| 12 | $Q_{\text{gen}} = I^2 R_{\text{int}} \cdot t$ | Total heat generated | J | Adiabatic approximation |
| 13 | $\eta_{\text{coulombic}} = Q_{\text{out}} / Q_{\text{in}}$ | Coulombic (Ampere-hour) efficiency | % | $< 100\%$ for all real batteries |
| 14 | $\eta_{\text{energy}} = E_{\text{out}} / E_{\text{in}}$ | Energy (round-trip) efficiency | % | $80\text{–}95\%$ for Li-ion |
| 15 | $E_{\text{usable}} = Q \cdot V_{\text{nom}} \cdot \text{DOD}_{\text{max}} / 100$ | Usable energy accounting for DOD limits | Wh | EV design uses 80% DOD typically |

## 2.5 Battery Modeling — The Equivalent Circuit Approach

The **Thevenin equivalent circuit** is the most widely used first-order model:

$$ V_{\text{terminal}}(t) = V_{\text{oc}}(\text{SOC}) - I(t) \cdot R_{\text{int}} - V_{\text{RC}}(t) $$

where $V_{\text{RC}}(t)$ captures the **transient polarization** (RC pair) representing diffusion and charge-transfer overpotentials.

For **steady-state analysis** (which is what KTU primarily tests):

$$ V_{\text{terminal}} = V_{\text{oc}} - I R_{\text{int}} $$

This simple model assumes:
- $V_{\text{oc}}$ depends only on SOC (lookup table or linear approximation).
- $R_{\text{int}}$ is constant or SOC/temperature dependent.
- No transient dynamics are considered.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In automotive practice, this analytical framework is used for:
1. **Range estimation:** Driving cycle → Power demand → Energy consumed → SOC trajectory.
2. **Thermal management design:** $I^2 R$ losses dictate cooling capacity.
3. **State-of-Health monitoring:** Tracking capacity fade over cycles.
4. **Cell-to-pack imbalance mitigation:** BMS balancing decisions based on SOC differentials.
5. **Warranty modeling:** Cycle-life vs DOD curves (e.g., Arrhenius + rainflow counting).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stored Energy from Voltage and Capacity

**Statement:** A fully charged EV battery pack has a nominal voltage of $V_{\text{nom}}$ and capacity $Q$ (in Ah). Derive the expression for total stored energy $E$.

### Step 1 — Recall the definition of electrical energy

Electrical energy is the integral of power over time. For DC circuits:

$$ E = \int_0^t P(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_0^t V(\tau) I(\tau)\, d\tau $$

### Step 2 — Assume constant voltage and constant current discharge

For a controlled discharge at nominal voltage:

$$ E = V_{\text{nom}} \int_0^t I(\tau)\, d\tau $$

### Step 3 — Recognize the integral of current is charge

The total charge delivered is:

$$ Q = \int_0^t I(\tau)\, d\tau $$

### Step 4 — Substitute Q into the energy expression

$$ \boxed{E = V_{\text{nom}} \times Q} $$

where $E$ is in **Watt-hours** if $V$ is in Volts and $Q$ is in Ampere-hours. This is the foundational energy equation for any battery analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time to Full Discharge at a Given C-Rate

**Statement:** Show that the time to fully discharge a battery at constant C-rate is $t = 1/C$.

### Step 1 — Express discharge current in terms of C-rate

By the definition of C-rate:

$$ I = C \times Q_{\text{rated}} $$

### Step 2 — Total charge to deliver equals rated capacity

For full discharge, the battery must deliver its entire rated charge $Q_{\text{rated}}$.

### Step 3 — Write the charge balance

$$ Q_{\text{rated}} = I \times t = (C \times Q_{\text{rated}}) \times t $$

### Step 4 — Solve for t

$$ t = \frac{Q_{\text{rated}}}{C \times Q_{\text{rated}}} = \frac{1}{C} $$

$$ \boxed{t_{\text{discharge}} = \frac{1}{C} \text{ [hours]}} $$

**Verification:** At $C = 1$, $t = 1$ hour ✓. At $C = 0.5$, $t = 2$ hours ✓. At $C = 2$, $t = 0.5$ hour (30 min) ✓.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SOC Evolution Under Constant Current

**Statement:** A 100 Ah Li-ion battery starts at 90% SOC. A constant discharge current of 50 A is drawn. Derive the SOC as a function of time.

### Step 1 — Initial condition

$$ \text{SOC}(0) = 90\% $$

### Step 2 — Apply the SOC definition

$$ \text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(0) - \frac{1}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \int_0^t I\, d\tau $$

### Step 3 — Substitute constant I

$$ \text{SOC}(t) = 90 - \frac{I \cdot t}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\% $$

### Step 4 — Plug in numerical values

$$ \text{SOC}(t) = 90 - \frac{50 \times t}{100} \times 100 = 90 - 50t $$

where $t$ is in **hours**.

### Step 5 — Interpret

The SOC drops at **50% per hour** (this is **0.5C discharge**, as expected since $I = 50$ A and $Q = 100$ Ah, so $C = 0.5$).

The battery reaches 0% SOC at $t = 1.8$ hours (108 minutes).

$$ \boxed{\text{SOC}(t) = 90 - 50t \;\;[\%], \quad t \in [0,\,1.8]\text{ h}} $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Heat Generation During a Discharge Cycle

**Statement:** Derive the total heat generated in a battery delivering constant current $I$ for time $t$, with internal resistance $R_{\text{int}}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the heat source

The dominant heat source in a battery is **Joule heating** from the internal resistance (ohmic loss).

### Step 2 — Instantaneous power dissipated as heat

$$ P_{\text{loss}}(t) = I^2(t) \cdot R_{\text{int}} $$

### Step 3 — Integrate over discharge time

$$ Q_{\text{gen}} = \int_0^t P_{\text{loss}}(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_0^t I^2(\tau) R_{\text{int}}\, d\tau $$

### Step 4 — Assume constant current

For a controlled constant-current discharge:

$$ Q_{\text{gen}} = I^2 R_{\text{int}} \int_0^t d\tau = I^2 R_{\text{int}} \cdot t $$

$$ \boxed{Q_{\text{gen}} = I^2 R_{\text{int}} \cdot t \;\;[\text{Joules}]} $$

### Step 5 — Consequence for EV design

At **$C = 1$** (e.g., 50 A from a 50 Ah cell), heat generation is **$2.5 \times 10^3 R_{\text{int}}$ watts**. For a typical Li-ion cell with $R_{\text{int}} = 20$ mΩ, this yields **50 W per cell**. A 100-series pack generates **5 kW of heat** continuously at 1C — this is *why* EV battery packs require active liquid cooling.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Coulombic vs Energy Efficiency

**Statement:** A battery is charged with 100 Ah and delivers 96 Ah on discharge at an average voltage of 3.5 V during discharge and 3.7 V during charge. Compute both efficiencies.

### Step 1 — Coulombic (Ampere-hour) efficiency

$$ \eta_{\text{coulombic}} = \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{96}{100} = 96\% $$

### Step 2 — Energy in vs energy out

Energy supplied to the battery during charge:

$$ E_{\text{in}} = V_{\text{ch}} \times Q_{\text{in}} = 3.7 \times 100 = 370 \text{ Wh} $$

Energy delivered during discharge:

$$ E_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{dis}} \times Q_{\text{out}} = 3.5 \times 96 = 336 \text{ Wh} $$

### Step 3 — Energy (round-trip) efficiency

$$ \eta_{\text{energy}} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} = \frac{336}{370} \approx 90.8\% $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{coulombic}} = 96\%, \quad \eta_{\text{energy}} \approx 90.8\%} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Energy efficiency is *always lower* than coulombic efficiency whenever $V_{\text{ch}} > V_{\text{dis}}$ (which is universal). The product $\eta_{\text{energy}} = \eta_{\text{coulombic}} \times (\bar{V}_{\text{dis}} / \bar{V}_{\text{ch}})$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Analysis Task | Governing Equations | KTU-Relevant Output |
|---------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Determine discharge time | $t = 1/C$ | Hours / minutes |
| Compute pack energy | $E = V_{\text{pack}} \times Q_{\text{pack}}$ | Wh or kWh |
| Specific energy check | $E_{\text{sp}} = E/m$ | Wh/kg |
| SOC trajectory | $\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}_0 - (I t / Q) \times 100$ | % |
| Terminal voltage under load | $V_t = V_{\text{oc}} - IR_{\text{int}}$ | V |
| Heat generation | $Q_{\text{gen}} = I^2 R t$ | J |
| Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_E = E_{\text{out}}/E_{\text{in}}$ | % |
| Cycle-life wear | $L = L_0 \times (DOD/DOD_0)^{-k}$ (Peukert-like) | cycles |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Battery Pack Hierarchical Architecture (Cell → Module → Pack → Vehicle)

This block diagram illustrates the **aggregation hierarchy** from a single electrochemical cell to a vehicle-level energy storage system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cellA["CELL LFP 3.2V 100Ah"]
    cellB["CELL LFP 3.2V 100Ah"]
    cellC["CELL LFP 3.2V 100Ah"]
    cellD["CELL LFP 3.2V 100Ah"]
    cellE["CELL LFP 3.2V 100Ah"]
    cellF["CELL LFP 3.2V 100Ah"]

    s1["SERIES STRING 14S = 44.8V Nominal"]
    s2["PARALLEL GROUP 2P = 200Ah"]

    module1["BATTERY MODULE 14S2P 44.8V 200Ah 8.96 kWh"]

    bms1["MODULE LEVEL BMS CELL MONITORING"]
    bms2["PACK LEVEL BMS MASTER CONTROL"]
    bms3["THERMAL MANAGEMENT COOLING PLATE"]
    bms4["HIGH VOLTAGE CONTACTOR AND FUSE"]

    pack1["BATTERY PACK 400V 200Ah 80 kWh"]

    vcu1["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU"]
    inv1["POWER INVERTER"]
    mtr1["TRACTION MOTOR"]

    cellA --> s1
    cellB --> s1
    cellC --> s1
    cellD --> s1
    cellE --> s2
    cellF --> s2
    s1 --> module1
    s2 --> module1
    module1 --> bms1
    bms1 --> bms2
    bms2 --> bms3
    bms2 --> bms4
    module1 --> pack1
    bms2 --> pack1
    bms3 --> pack1
    bms4 --> pack1
    pack1 --> vcu1
    vcu1 --> inv1
    inv1 --> mtr1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** A single 3.2 V / 100 Ah LFP cell is the fundamental unit. Connecting **14 cells in series (14S)** yields a 44.8 V / 100 Ah module string. Connecting **2 such strings in parallel (2P)** doubles the capacity to 200 Ah while keeping voltage at 44.8 V. Multiple such modules (typically 8–10 in series for a 400 V pack) form the final traction battery.

## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) — Thevenin First-Order

The simplified **R-int model** used in steady-state battery analysis:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    voc["OPEN CIRCUIT VOLTAGE V_OC FUNCTION OF SOC"]
    rint["INTERNAL RESISTANCE R_INT OHMIC PLUS CHARGE TRANSFER"]
    vterm["TERMINAL VOLTAGE V_T MEASURED AT EXTERNAL TERMINALS"]
    load["EXTERNAL LOAD MOTOR OR CHARGER"]

    voc --> rint
    rint --> vterm
    vterm --> load
    load -.feeds back SOC.-> voc
```

**Governing equation** (steady state):

$$ V_t = V_{\text{oc}}(\text{SOC}) - I \cdot R_{\text{int}} $$

> During **discharge**, $I > 0$ and $V_t < V_{\text{oc}}$.
> During **charge**, $I < 0$ (sign convention) and $V_t > V_{\text{oc}}$.
> $V_{\text{oc}}$ rises monotonically with SOC for Li-ion chemistries.

## 4.3 Ragone Plot — Energy vs Power Trade-off (Conceptual Block)

The **Ragone plot** is the canonical visualization for comparing energy storage technologies. The block below shows the conceptual map.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axisP["HORIZONTAL AXIS SPECIFIC POWER W/kg LOG SCALE"]
    axisE["VERTICAL AXIS SPECIFIC ENERGY Wh/kg LOG SCALE"]

    zone1["HIGH POWER LOW ENERGY ZONE SUPERCAPACITORS 5 to 10 Wh/kg 10000 W/kg"]
    zone2["BALANCED ZONE LITHIUM ION NMC 200 to 250 Wh/kg 500 to 3000 W/kg"]
    zone3["HIGH ENERGY LOW POWER ZONE LITHIUM METAL OR SOLID STATE 400 to 500 Wh/kg 200 W/kg"]
    zone4["LEGACY ZONE LEAD ACID 30 to 40 Wh/kg 100 to 200 W/kg"]
    zone5["INTERMEDIATE ZONE NIMH 60 to 80 Wh/kg 200 to 1000 W/kg"]

    axisP --> zone1
    axisP --> zone2
    axisP --> zone3
    axisP --> zone4
    axisP --> zone5
    axisE --> zone1
    axisE --> zone2
    axisE --> zone3
    axisE --> zone4
    axisE --> zone5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ragone plot interpretation for KTU:** "Diagonal-up-right" is the ideal direction (both high energy and high power). EV batteries target the **balanced zone** (NMC/LFP). A supercapacitor alone cannot give range; a fuel cell cannot give peak power. Hence **hybrid energy storage** (battery + supercapacitor) is an active research direction.

## 4.4 Battery Management System Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensors["CELL SENSORS VOLTAGE CURRENT TEMPERATURE"]
    adc["SIGNAL CONDITIONING AND ADC"]
    micro["MICROCONTROLLER SOC SOH ESTIMATION"]
    bal["CELL BALANCING ACTIVE OR PASSIVE"]
    therm["THERMAL CONTROL COOLING FAN OR PUMP"]
    comm["CAN COMMUNICATION TO VCU"]
    protect["PROTECTION RELAYS CONTACTORS FUSES"]

    sensors --> adc
    adc --> micro
    micro --> bal
    micro --> therm
    micro --> comm
    micro --> protect
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Define C-rate of a battery and explain its significance in EV applications with one example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **C-rate** is defined as the current at which a battery is charged or discharged, normalized to its rated capacity, given by:

$$ I = C \times Q_{\text{rated}} $$

It is a dimensionless quantity that expresses the **speed** at which a battery is (dis)charged relative to its 1-hour rating.
