**Significance in EVs (2 marks):**
- During **acceleration/regenerative braking**, the battery operates at high C-rates (3C–5C).
- During **cruising**, low C-rates (0.2C–0.5C) are used for efficiency.
- High C-rates cause increased **I²R heat** and **capacity derating**, affecting thermal design and range.

**Example (1 mark):** A 50 Ah battery discharged at 2C delivers 100 A for 30 minutes, enabling a peak power output of $P = V \times I = 3.6 \times 100 = 360$ W per cell.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Distinguish between energy density and power density. Why are both important in EV battery selection?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Energy Density (Wh/kg) | Power Density (W/kg) |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Energy stored per unit mass | Power deliverable per unit mass |
| Determines | **Vehicle range** | **Acceleration & regen capability** |
| Optimized by | Cathode chemistry (NMC, LFP) | Cell architecture (thin electrodes, large surface area) |
| Typical Li-ion | **150–250 Wh/kg** | **500–3000 W/kg** |

**Why both matter (1 mark):** An EV needs high energy density for **long range** *and* high power density for **acceleration and regenerative braking**. A supercapacitor has high power density but low energy density (short range). A fuel cell has high energy density but low power density (slow response). Hence, EV batteries must lie in the **balanced zone** of the Ragone plot.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`
**"With a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical architecture of a battery pack for an electric vehicle. Compute the total pack energy, nominal voltage, and specific energy for a pack configured as 96S2P using 3.6 V / 50 Ah NMC cells, with a total pack mass of 350 kg."**

**OR**

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**"Explain the Thevenin equivalent circuit model of a battery. A 3.6 V / 100 Ah Li-ion cell has an open-circuit voltage of 3.7 V at 50% SOC and an internal resistance of 25 mΩ. Compute (i) terminal voltage at 1C discharge, (ii) power loss, and (iii) total heat generated over a 1-hour discharge at this rate. Comment on the thermal management implications."**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B-A (14 marks)

**(a) Hierarchical Architecture (7 marks):**

The battery pack of an EV is structured in a **three-level hierarchy**:

1. **Cell (Level 1):** The fundamental electrochemical unit, producing a fixed nominal voltage (3.6 V for NMC) and a fixed capacity (50 Ah).
2. **Module (Level 2):** A mechanical and electrical assembly of multiple cells, typically in a series-parallel configuration (e.g., 12S2P = 12 cells in series, 2 strings in parallel).
3. **Pack (Level 3):** The complete battery system comprising multiple modules, a **Battery Management System (BMS)**, **thermal management hardware** (cooling plates, pumps, refrigerant), **high-voltage contactors**, **fuses**, and a **housing**.

**Auxiliary systems in the pack:**
- **BMS Master Controller:** Monitors voltage, current, temperature; estimates SOC and SOH; executes cell balancing.
- **Cooling System:** Liquid cold plate or air-forced convection; maintains cells within 15–35 °C optimal range.
- **Safety Disconnects:** HV contactors, pyro-fuses, and MSD (Manual Service Disconnect) for emergency isolation.

**(b) Pack Energy, Voltage, Specific Energy Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: 96S2P, $V_{\text{cell}} = 3.6$ V, $Q_{\text{cell}} = 50$ Ah, $m_{\text{pack}} = 350$ kg.

**Step 1 — Pack nominal voltage (series connection adds voltage):**

$$ V_{\text{pack}} = N_s \times V_{\text{cell}} = 96 \times 3.6 = 345.6 \text{ V} $$

**Step 2 — Pack capacity (parallel connection adds capacity):**

$$ Q_{\text{pack}} = N_p \times Q_{\text{cell}} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Ah} $$

**Step 3 — Total pack energy:**

$$ E_{\text{pack}} = V_{\text{pack}} \times Q_{\text{pack}} = 345.6 \times 100 = 34{,}560 \text{ Wh} = 34.56 \text{ kWh} $$

**Step 4 — Specific energy (gravimetric):**

$$ E_{\text{sp}} = \frac{E_{\text{pack}}}{m_{\text{pack}}} = \frac{34{,}560}{350} \approx 98.7 \text{ Wh/kg} $$

$$ \boxed{V_{\text{pack}} = 345.6 \text{ V}, \quad Q_{\text{pack}} = 100 \text{ Ah}, \quad E_{\text{pack}} = 34.56 \text{ kWh}, \quad E_{\text{sp}} \approx 98.7 \text{ Wh/kg}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The specific energy (~99 Wh/kg) is **lower than cell-level** (~200 Wh/kg) because the pack mass includes modules, BMS, cooling, contactors, and housing — typically a **40–50% mass penalty**.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B-B (14 marks)

**(a) Thevenin Equivalent Circuit Model (7 marks):**

The **Thevenin model** represents a battery as a **nonlinear voltage source** $V_{\text{oc}}(\text{SOC})$ in series with an **internal resistance** $R_{\text{int}}$ and an optional **RC parallel network** capturing transient polarization.

**Components and physical meaning:**

| Element | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---------|--------|------------------|
| Voltage source | $V_{\text{oc}}(\text{SOC})$ | Open-circuit voltage; thermodynamic equilibrium potential of the cell, depends on SOC and temperature. |
| Series resistance | $R_{\text{int}}$ | Ohmic resistance: sum of electrolyte, contact, and electrode resistances; current-dependent. |
| RC pair (optional) | $R_{\text{ct}} \Vert C_{\text{dl}}$ | Charge-transfer resistance $R_{\text{ct}}$ and double-layer capacitance $C_{\text{dl}}$; captures transient diffusion and activation polarization. |

**Steady-state equation** (with no transient polarization):

$$ V_t = V_{\text{oc}}(\text{SOC}) - I \cdot R_{\text{int}} $$

**During discharge** ($I > 0$): $V_t < V_{\text{oc}}$ — terminal voltage sags under load.
**During charge** ($I < 0$): $V_t > V_{\text{oc}}$ — terminal voltage rises during charging.
**At open circuit** ($I = 0$): $V_t = V_{\text{oc}}$ — true EMF of the cell.

This model is used by the **BMS** to estimate SOC and predict runtime, and by **vehicle energy management systems** to predict range.

**(b) Numerical Computations (7 marks):**

Given: $V_{\text{oc}} = 3.7$ V at 50% SOC, $R_{\text{int}} = 25$ mΩ = 0.025 Ω, $Q = 100$ Ah.

**Step 1 — Compute discharge current at 1C:**

$$ I = 1C \times Q = 1 \times 100 = 100 \text{ A} $$

**Step 2 — Terminal voltage during discharge:**

$$ V_t = V_{\text{oc}} - I \cdot R_{\text{int}} = 3.7 - 100 \times 0.025 = 3.7 - 2.5 = 1.2 \text{ V} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** This terminal voltage of **1.2 V is unrealistically low** for a Li-ion cell at 50% SOC. The 25 mΩ internal resistance is unrealistically high (typical is 1–5 mΩ for automotive Li-ion). Students must check if their $R_{\text{int}}$ value is realistic; otherwise the problem statement likely intended $R_{\text{int}} = 2.5$ mΩ, giving $V_t = 3.7 - 0.25 = 3.45$ V, which is realistic. **Always validate numbers against physical intuition.**

**Step 3 — Power loss:**

$$ P_{\text{loss}} = I^2 R_{\text{int}} = (100)^2 \times 0.025 = 250 \text{ W} $$

(If $R_{\text{int}} = 2.5$ mΩ: $P_{\text{loss}} = 100^2 \times 0.0025 = 25$ W per cell.)

**Step 4 — Total heat over 1 hour at constant current:**

$$ Q_{\text{gen}} = P_{\text{loss}} \times t = 250 \times 3600 = 900{,}000 \text{ J} = 900 \text{ kJ} $$

(If $R_{\text{int}} = 2.5$ mΩ: $Q_{\text{gen}} = 25 \times 3600 = 90{,}000$ J = 90 kJ.)

**Step 5 — Thermal management implications:**

For a pack of 96 such cells in series (current through all cells is the same), total heat per hour at 1C is:

$$ Q_{\text{gen,pack}} = 96 \times 900{,}000 = 86.4 \text{ MJ} \approx 24 \text{ kWh}_{\text{thermal}} $$

This **continuous thermal load** is comparable to the **electrical power output** of a small space heater! It cannot be dissipated by natural convection — **active liquid cooling is mandatory** for EV battery packs to maintain cells within the safe 15–35 °C window and prevent thermal runaway.

$$ \boxed{V_t = 1.2 \text{ V (as given)}, \quad P_{\text{loss}} = 250 \text{ W}, \quad Q_{\text{gen}} = 900 \text{ kJ per cell per hour}} $$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
> 1. **Confusing Energy (Wh) and Capacity (Ah).** They are *not* the same. Multiply by voltage to convert.
> 2. **Forgetting the parallel multiplier in pack energy.** In a 96S2P pack, only $N_p$ affects capacity, only $N_s$ affects voltage.
> 3. **Using cell-level specific energy as pack-level.** Pack mass includes housing, BMS, cooling — typically 40–50% mass overhead.
> 4. **Sign error in $V_t$ equation during charging** — students forget the sign convention on $I$.
> 5. **Neglecting heat generation in design calculations.** $I^2 R$ losses are non-trivial; for 96 cells at 1C, thermal management is a *primary*, not secondary, design constraint.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Battery Based Energy Storage and Its Analysis**

- [x] **Battery hierarchy:** Cell → Module → Pack → System. Series raises voltage; parallel raises capacity.
- [x] **Energy formula:** $E = V \times Q$ in Watt-hours. Multiply by 1000 for kWh.
- [x] **C-rate definition:** $I = C \times Q_{\text{rated}}$; discharge time $t = 1/C$ hours.
- [x] **SOC definition:** $\text{SOC} = (Q_{\text{remain}}/Q_{\text{rated}}) \times 100\%$. DOD = 100 − SOC.
- [x] **SOH definition:** $\text{SOH} = (Q_{\text{max,current}}/Q_{\text{rated,new}}) \times 100\%$. End-of-life typically at 80% SOH.
- [x] **Terminal voltage equation:** $V_t = V_{\text{oc}} - IR_{\text{int}}$. Valid for steady state, constant current.
- [x] **Heat generation:** $Q_{\text{gen}} = I^2 R_{\text{int}} t$. Drives the *need* for active cooling.
- [x] **Energy density vs Power density:** Energy density → range; Power density → acceleration. Both required for EVs.
- [x] **Specific energy order (typical):** Lead-acid (~35) < NiMH (~70) < LFP (~120) < NMC (~200) < Solid-state (projected 400+) Wh/kg.
- [x] **Round-trip efficiency:** Coulombic ≈ 99%, Energy ≈ 85–95% for Li-ion. Energy efficiency is *always* less than coulombic.
- [x] **Usable energy:** $E_{\text{usable}} = Q \times V \times \text{DOD}_{\text{max}}$. EVs typically limit DOD to 80% for cycle life.
- [x] **Equivalent circuit model:** Thevenin: $V_{\text{oc}}$ in series with $R_{\text{int}}$; RC pair for transients.
- [x] **BMS functions:** Monitoring, SOC/SOH estimation, cell balancing, thermal control, communication, protection.
- [x] **Pack sizing calculation:** Always compute (i) $V_{\text{pack}} = N_s V_{\text{cell}}$, (ii) $Q_{\text{pack}} = N_p Q_{\text{cell}}$, (iii) $E_{\text{pack}} = V_{\text{pack}} Q_{\text{pack}}$, (iv) $E_{\text{sp}} = E_{\text{pack}}/m_{\text{pack}}$.
- [x] **Real-world EV pack examples:** Nissan Leaf (~24 kWh, LFP), Tesla Model 3 LR (~75 kWh, NCA), Tata Nexon EV (~30 kWh, LFP).
- [x] **Key trade-off:** No single chemistry optimizes energy, power, life, safety, and cost simultaneously. LFP prioritizes safety and life; NMC/NCA prioritize energy density.
- [x] **Thermal management is non-negotiable** in EV packs — $I^2 R$ heat at 1C continuous is a *kW-scale* load per module.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super Capacitor Based Energy Storage and Its Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Super Capacitor Based Energy Storage and Its Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Supercapacitor** (also known as an **Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC)** or **Ultracapacitor**) is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores electrical energy through *electrostatic charge separation* at the interface between an electrode and an electrolyte. Unlike conventional capacitors, supercapacitors utilize a high-surface-area carbon electrode (activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, or graphene) and an extremely thin electric double-layer (typically **0.3–0.8 nm**) to achieve capacitances ranging from **1 Farad to over 10,000 Farads** in a single cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> A supercapacitor is a high-capacity electrochemical capacitor with capacitance values much higher than other capacitors, but with lower voltage limits. It bridges the gap between electrolytic capacitors and rechargeable batteries in terms of energy density and power density.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **supercapacitor like a high-speed water bucket brigade**:

- A **conventional capacitor** is like a *small cup* filled with water — quick to fill, quick to empty, but holds very little.
- A **battery** is like a *large underground water tank* — holds enormous amounts, but pouring water in and out is slow (chemical reactions).
- A **supercapacitor** is like a *massive, shallow swimming pool* — holds a *huge amount of water* (energy) but can be filled or drained in *seconds* (high power).

In an **Electric Vehicle (EV)**, this means supercapacitors can deliver the *instantaneous bursts of power* required during hard acceleration or regenerative braking, while batteries provide the long-range steady-state energy.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value (Supercapacitor) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Voltage (single cell) | **2.5 – 2.85** | V |
| Capacitance (single cell) | **100 – 3,400** | F |
| Specific Energy | **5 – 10** | Wh/kg |
| Specific Power | **1,000 – 10,000** | W/kg |
| Cycle Life | **500,000 – 1,000,000+** | cycles |
| Operating Temperature | **−40 to +85** | °C |
| Charge Time | **0.3 – 30** | seconds |
| Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR) | **0.1 – 10** | mΩ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Supercapacitors excel in the **power density** domain, while batteries dominate the **energy density** domain. This complementary relationship is the foundation of *Hybrid Energy Storage Systems (HESS)* in modern EVs.

## 1.4 Historical Context & Evolution

The concept originated from **Helmholtz's 1853 double-layer theory**. The first practical supercapacitor was patented by **Standard Oil Company, Sohio (1966)** using activated carbon. **Maxwell Technologies** commercialized it in the 1990s. Today, supercapacitors are deployed in:
- Formula 1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery Systems)
- Toyota Yaris Hybrid (12V start-stop system)
- Bus regenerative braking systems (e.g., MAN, Solaris)
- Wind turbine pitch control backup
- IoT and grid frequency regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — The Electric Double Layer

When a voltage is applied across two carbon electrodes immersed in an electrolyte, **two distinct charge layers form** at each electrode-electrolyte interface. This creates what is known as the **Helmholtz Double Layer**.

### Layer Structure (Three-Region Model):

1. **Inner Helmholtz Plane (IHP):** Specifically adsorbed ions in direct contact with the electrode.
2. **Outer Helmholtz Plane (OHP):** Solvated (hydrated) ions closest to the electrode.
3. **Diffuse Layer (Gouy-Chapman Layer):** Continuously distributed ions whose concentration decays exponentially into the bulk electrolyte.

The total interfacial capacitance is a **series combination** of the Helmholtz layer capacitance (C_H) and the diffuse layer capacitance (C_D):

$$\frac{1}{C_{total}} = \frac{1}{C_H} + \frac{1}{C_D}$$

## 2.2 Why Carbon? — The Material Science Perspective

Supercapacitor electrodes require:
- **Very high specific surface area** (activated carbon: **1,500 – 3,000 m²/g**)
- **Controlled pore size distribution** (micropores <2 nm, mesopores 2–50 nm)
- **High electrical conductivity**
- **Electrochemical stability** in the operating voltage window

This is why **activated carbon, carbon aerogels, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and graphene** are the preferred electrode materials.

## 2.3 Classification of Supercapacitors

| Type | Charge Storage Mechanism | Electrode Material | Energy Density |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EDLC (Symmetric)** | Electrostatic (Double Layer) | Activated Carbon / Both | Medium |
| **Pseudocapacitor** | Faradaic (Redox) | Metal Oxides / Conducting Polymers | High |
| **Hybrid (Asymmetric)** | Both EDLC + Pseudocapacitive | Carbon + Metal Oxide | Very High |
| **Battery-Type Hybrid** | EDLC + Battery-like | Li-ion + Capacitor | Highest |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, when asked to "classify supercapacitors," always mention the symmetric (EDLC), asymmetric (hybrid), and pseudocapacitor distinction.

## 2.4 Equivalent Circuit Models

### Model 1: Ideal Simple Model
A supercapacitor is modeled as a **single capacitance (C)** in series with a **small resistor (ESR)** and in parallel with a **large leakage resistor (R_leak)**.

### Model 2: Two-Branch Model (Most Common in KTU Texts)
Captures the **short-term** (immediate response) and **long-term** (delayed redistribution) charging behavior:

- **Immediate branch:** C_immediate (fast response)
- **Delayed branch:** R_delay + C_delayed (slow redistribution over minutes)

### Model 3: Three-Branch Model (High-Fidelity)
Adds a third branch representing **self-discharge** (longest time constant, hours).

### Model 4: Transmission Line (Porous Electrode) Model
Used for **detailed physics-based** simulation, modeling the distributed RC network along the pore length.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel-plate (used for double-layer) | F |
| 2 | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in a capacitor | J (or Wh) |
| 3 | $P = \frac{V^2}{4R_{ESR}}$ | Maximum deliverable power | W |
| 4 | $\tau = R_{ESR} \times C$ | RC time constant | seconds |
| 5 | $SOC = \frac{V_{cap} - V_{min}}{V_{max} - V_{min}} \times 100\%$ | State of Charge (voltage-based) | % |
| 6 | $E_{specific} = \frac{E}{m}$ | Gravimetric Energy Density | Wh/kg |
| 7 | $P_{specific} = \frac{P}{m}$ | Gravimetric Power Density | W/kg |
| 8 | $E_{volumetric} = \frac{E}{\mathcal{V}}$ | Volumetric Energy Density | Wh/L |
| 9 | $\eta_C = \frac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic Efficiency | % |
| 10 | $I = C \frac{dV}{dt}$ | Instantaneous current during constant-power delivery | A |
| 11 | $P(t) = V(t) \times I(t)$ | Instantaneous power at terminals | W |
| 12 | $R_{ESR,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{ESR,i}$ | Total ESR of series-stacked cells | Ω |
| 13 | $C_{total,series} = \frac{C_{cell}}{N}$ | Total capacitance of N series cells | F |
| 14 | $V_{stack} = N \times V_{cell}$ | Stack voltage for N series cells | V |
| 15 | $V_{balance} = V_{cell} \times \left(1 + \frac{R_{ESR,bal}}{R_{ESR,cell}}\right)$ | Voltage balancing equation | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** When supercapacitor cells are placed in **series**, the total capacitance **decreases** (C_total = C/N), and the **ESR increases** (R_total = N × R_cell). This is a common exam trap.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Regenerative Braking:** During deceleration, supercapacitors can absorb 100% of available braking energy (vs. ~30–40% for batteries alone) due to high charge acceptance.
2. **Load Leveling:** Pairing a battery with a supercapacitor extends battery life by 2–3× by smoothing peak current demands.
3. **Cold-Cranking in HEVs:** Providing 12V/48V start-stop power reliably over hundreds of thousands of cycles.
4. **Voltage Stabilization:** Maintaining DC bus voltage during transient load changes in EV powertrains.
5. **Emergency Power Backup:** Used in EV charging stations for blackout ride-through.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capacitance of the Electric Double Layer

Starting from Gauss's Law applied to a parallel-plate-like arrangement of the Helmholtz layer:

**Step 1 — Surface Charge Density:**
From Gauss's Law, the surface charge density at the electrode is:

$$\sigma = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 E$$

where $E$ is the electric field across the double layer of thickness $d$.

**Step 2 — Voltage Across the Layer:**

$$V = E \times d$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Capacitance (per unit area):**

$$C_A = \frac{\sigma}{V} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 E}{E \times d} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Total Capacitance (using area A of porous electrode):**

$$C = C_A \times A = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting Realistic Values for Supercapacitor:**

Let $\varepsilon_r = 10$ (for organic electrolyte), $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $A = 2{,}000$ m²/g (activated carbon), and $d = 0.5$ nm $= 5 \times 10^{-10}$ m.

For 1 gram of activated carbon:

$$C = \frac{10 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2{,}000}{5 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{C = \frac{1.7708 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-10}} = 354.16 \text{ F/g}}$$

This explains why supercapacitors achieve hundreds of Farads per gram, leading to **Farad-level single-cell devices**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Stored and Energy Density

For a supercapacitor charged to voltage $V$, the energy stored is:

**Step 1 — Differential Energy:**

$$dE = V \cdot dQ = V \cdot C \cdot dV$$

**Step 2 — Integration from 0 to V:**

$$E = \int_0^V V \cdot C \, dV = C \int_0^V V \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Solving the Integral:**

$$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Density (per unit mass m):**

$$E_{mass} = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{C V^2}{2m}$$

**Step 5 — For a Maxwell BCAP3000 cell (C = 3000 F, V = 2.7 V, m = 0.51 kg):**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \times 3000 \times (2.7)^2 = 10{,}935 \text{ J} = 3.04 \text{ Wh}$$

$$E_{mass} = \frac{3.04}{0.51} \approx 5.96 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Deliverable Power

The maximum power is delivered when the load resistance equals the internal ESR (impedance matching).

**Step 1 — Circuit Model:** A charged capacitor (voltage V) in series with ESR (R) driving a load R_L.

**Step 2 — Voltage Divider Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out} = V \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_{ESR} + R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Output Current:**

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{ESR} + R_L}$$

**Step 4 — Power Delivered to Load:**

$$P = I^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{V^2 R_L}{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Power Condition (dP/dR_L = 0):**

Setting $R_L = R_{ESR}$:

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR}}}$$

**Step 6 — Practical Example:** For V = 2.7 V and R_ESR = 0.4 mΩ:

$$P_{max} = \frac{(2.7)^2}{4 \times 0.0004} = \frac{7.29}{0.0016} = 4{,}556 \text{ W} = 4.56 \text{ kW}$$

This is the **power available from a single 3000 F cell** during peak discharge.

## 3.4 Derivation: Series and Parallel Cell Combination

For **N series cells** (each with C_cell, R_cell):

**Capacitance (series):**

$$C_{series} = \frac{C_{cell}}{N}$$

**Voltage (series):**

$$V_{series} = N \times V_{cell}$$

**ESR (series):**

$$R_{ESR,series} = N \times R_{ESR,cell}$$

**Energy (series) remains the same as single cell:**

$$E_{series} = \frac{1}{2} C_{series} V_{series}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C_{cell}}{N} \cdot (N \cdot V_{cell})^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_{cell} V_{cell}^2$$

For **M parallel cells**:

$$C_{parallel} = M \cdot C_{cell}$$

$$R_{ESR,parallel} = \frac{R_{ESR,cell}}{M}$$

$$V_{parallel} = V_{cell}$$

$$E_{parallel} = M \times \frac{1}{2} C_{cell} V_{cell}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_{parallel} V_{cell}^2$$

## 3.5 Derivation: State of Charge (SOC) Estimation

Supercapacitor SOC is **not** estimated via coulomb counting (like batteries) because the voltage varies linearly and continuously. Instead, the **terminal voltage** is used directly.

**Step 1 — Voltage-Based SOC Formula:**

$$SOC(t) = \frac{V_{cap}(t) - V_{min}}{V_{max} - V_{min}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Derivation from Capacitor Equation:**

$$Q(t) = C \cdot V_{cap}(t)$$

$$SOC(t) = \frac{Q_{available}(t)}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{max} - Q_{used}(t)}{Q_{max}} = \frac{C \cdot V_{max} - C \cdot V_{cap}(t)}{C \cdot (V_{max} - V_{min})}$$

Simplifying:

$$SOC(t) = \frac{V_{max} - V_{cap}(t)}{V_{max} - V_{min}} = 1 - \frac{V_{cap}(t) - V_{min}}{V_{max} - V_{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Linear Voltage Profile During Discharge:**

For a constant current discharge, the voltage profile is:

$$V_{cap}(t) = V_{max} - \frac{I \cdot t}{C}$$

Substituting into SOC equation:

$$\boxed{SOC(t) = 1 - \frac{I \cdot t}{C(V_{max} - V_{min})}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Constant-Power Discharge Analysis

For an EV supercapacitor pack undergoing constant-power discharge, the **current is not constant**; it **rises as voltage drops**. Let us derive this:

**Step 1 — Power Equation:**

$$P = V(t) \cdot I(t) = \text{constant}$$

Therefore:

$$I(t) = \frac{P}{V(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitor Current Equation:**

$$I(t) = -C \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Equating Both Expressions:**

$$-C \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{P}{V}$$

**Step 4 — Separating Variables:**

$$-C \cdot V \, dV = P \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Integration from V_max to V(t):**

$$-C \int_{V_{max}}^{V(t)} V \, dV = P \int_0^t dt$$

$$-C \left[\frac{V^2}{2}\right]_{V_{max}}^{V(t)} = P \cdot t$$

**Step 6 — Solving for V(t):**

$$-\frac{C}{2} \left(V(t)^2 - V_{max}^2\right) = P \cdot t$$

$$\frac{C}{2} \left(V_{max}^2 - V(t)^2\right) = P \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{V(t) = \sqrt{V_{max}^2 - \frac{2 P t}{C}}}$$

**Step 7 — End-of-Discharge Time (when V(t) = V_min):**

$$t_{end} = \frac{C(V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)}{2P}$$

This result is widely used in EV range calculations for supercapacitor-only urban vehicles.

## 3.7 Derivative: Discharge Time under Constant Current

For a constant current I discharge:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = -\frac{I}{C}$$

Integrating:

$$V(t) = V_{max} - \frac{I}{C} t$$

End-of-discharge time:

$$t_{end} = \frac{C \cdot (V_{max} - V_{min})}{I}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Supercapacitor-Based Energy Storage Subsystem in an EV

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Bus from Battery Pack] --> B[DC-DC Bidirectional Converter]
    C[Supercapacitor Pack SC1 SC2...SCn] --> B
    B --> D[Inverter]
    D --> E[Three Phase AC Motor]
    E --> F[Wheels Drive Train]
    G[Brake Pedal Signal] --> H[Regenerative Braking Controller]
    H --> C
    I[Accelerator Pedal Signal] --> J[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    J --> B
    J --> D
    K[Supercapacitor Management System SMS] --> C
    K --> L[Temperature Sensors]
    K --> M[Voltage Sensors]
    K --> N[Current Sensors]
```

## 4.2 Diagram: Equivalent Circuit Models Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supercapacitor Equivalent Circuit Models] --> B[Model 1: Simple RC]
    A --> C[Model 2: Two Branch]
    A --> D[Model 3: Three Branch]
    A --> E[Model 4: Transmission Line]
    B --> B1[Capacitor C]
    B --> B2[Series Resistor ESR]
    B --> B3[Parallel Leakage Resistor Rleak]
    C --> C1[Immediate Branch C0]
    C --> C2[Delayed Branch R1 plus C1]
    D --> D1[Short term branch]
    D --> D2[Medium term branch]
    D --> D3[Long term self discharge branch]
    E --> E1[Distributed RC ladder along pore]
    E --> E2[Used for physics based simulation]
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Supercapacitor Discharge Profile under Constant Current

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start t0 Vmax 2.7V] --> P2[Linear Drop Region]
    P2 --> P3[Midpoint tmid Vmid]
    P3 --> P4[End tend Vmin 0.5V]
    P4 --> P5[Cutoff Protection Activated]
    P2 -.- R1[Slope dV/dt = minus I divided by C]
    P3 -.- R2[SOC at midpoint = 50 percent]
    P4 -.- R3[Total energy delivered = 0.5 C Vmax squared minus Vmin squared]
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Ragone Plot — Energy vs Power Density Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ragone Plot Energy vs Power] --> B[Conventional Capacitors]
    A --> C[Supercapacitors]
    A --> D[Hybrid Supercapacitors]
    A --> E[Li-ion Batteries]
    A --> F[Fuel Cells]
    B --> B1[High Power Low Energy region]
    C --> C1[Mid Range Optimal Zone]
    C --> C2[Power 1k to 10k W/kg]
    C --> C3[Energy 5 to 10 Wh/kg]
    D --> D1[Bridging Gap to Batteries]
    E --> E1[High Energy Lower Power]
    F --> F1[High Energy Continuous]
```

## 4.5 Diagram: Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Bus] --> B[Battery Pack]
    A --> C[Supercapacitor Pack]
    B --> D[DC-DC Buck-Boost Converter for Battery]
    C --> E[DC-DC Buck-Boost Converter for Supercapacitor]
    D --> A
    E --> A
    F[Energy Management Strategy EMS] --> D
    F --> E
    G[Load Demand Signal] --> F
    F --> H[Frequency Splitting Logic]
    H --> I[Low Frequency Power to Battery]
    H --> J[High Frequency Power to Supercapacitor]
```

## 4.6 Diagram: Charging and Discharging Modes of a Supercapacitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Idle State] --> B[Charging Mode]
    A --> C[Discharging Mode]
    B --> B1[Electrons Accumulate on Negative Electrode]
    B --> B2[Positive Ions Migrate to Negative Electrode Interface]
    B --> B3[Double Layer Forms at Both Interfaces]
    C --> C1[Load Draws Current]
    C --> C2[Voltage Drops Linearly with Time]
    C --> C3[Energy Released as P = V times I]
    B --> D[State Stored Energy Increases]
    C --> E[State Stored Energy Decreases]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define supercapacitor. List any four advantages of supercapacitors over conventional batteries in EV applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A supercapacitor (EDLC) is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores energy via electrostatic charge separation at the electrode-electrolyte interface, achieving capacitances of hundreds to thousands of Farads.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Higher Power Density:** Deliver 10–100× more power per kg than batteries, enabling instant acceleration and rapid regenerative braking.
2. **Longer Cycle Life:** 500,000 to 1,000,000+ cycles vs. 1,000–3,000 for Li-ion batteries.
3. **Faster Charging:** Fully charged in seconds vs. hours for batteries.
4. **Wider Operating Temperature Range:** −40°C to +85°C without significant performance degradation, unlike batteries.
5. **Higher Efficiency:** 95–98% round-trip efficiency vs. 80–90% for batteries.
6. **No Deep-Discharge Damage:** Can be discharged to 0V without permanent capacity loss.

**Exam Tip:** Mentioning any 4 of the above gives full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is an electric double layer? Sketch the charge distribution across the double layer and explain its significance in supercapacitor operation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **electric double layer** is the structure of charge carriers that forms at the interface between an electrode and an electrolyte when a voltage is applied. It consists of two parallel layers of opposite charge separated by a monolayer of solvent molecules, typically only **0.3–0.8 nm** thick.

**Charge Distribution (Inner to Outer):**
1. **Electrode Surface:** Negative charges (electrons) on the carbon surface.
2. **Inner Helmholtz Plane (IHP):** Specifically adsorbed cations (closest layer of ions).
3. **Outer Helmholtz Plane (OHP):** Solvated cations in the closest approach layer.
4. **Diffuse Layer (Gouy-Chapman):** Continuously decreasing cation concentration into the bulk electrolyte.

**Significance:**
- The extremely small separation distance $d$ (~0.5 nm) and enormous effective surface area $A$ (~2000 m²/g of activated carbon) yield **enormous capacitance** per unit mass.
- The electrostatic (non-faradaic) nature means **no chemical bond breaking**, giving rise to extremely long cycle life.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / ESE Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the energy stored in a supercapacitor. A 3000 F supercapacitor is charged to 2.7 V. Calculate:**
- **(i) The total energy stored in joules and watt-hours.**
- **(ii) The available energy if it is discharged only down to 1.35 V (50% SOC).**
- **(iii) The maximum deliverable power if the ESR is 0.4 mΩ.**
**(7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain with neat diagrams the two-branch equivalent circuit model of a supercapacitor. Discuss the role of each component and how it differs from the simple RC model. Why is the two-branch model preferred for long-duration simulations?**
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Derivation:**

$$E = \int_0^V V \cdot C \, dV = C \int_0^V V \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

**(i) Total energy stored:**

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3000 \times (2.7)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 3000 \times 7.29 = 10{,}935 \text{ J}$$

Converting to watt-hours:

$$E_{total} = \frac{10{,}935}{3600} = 3.038 \text{ Wh}$$

**(ii) Available energy (discharge to 1.35 V):**

The energy equation for discharge from V_max to V:

$$E_{available} = \frac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^2 - V^2)$$

$$E_{available} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3000 \times \left[(2.7)^2 - (1.35)^2\right]$$

$$E_{available} = 1500 \times (7.29 - 1.8225) = 1500 \times 5.4675$$

$$E_{available} = 8{,}201.25 \text{ J} = 2.278 \text{ Wh}$$

**Note:** This shows that **75% of the total energy is delivered in the upper 50% of the voltage range** — a critical insight for supercapacitor sizing.

**(iii) Maximum deliverable power:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR}} = \frac{(2.7)^2}{4 \times 0.0004} = \frac{7.29}{0.0016}$$

$$P_{max} = 4{,}556.25 \text{ W} \approx 4.56 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Two-Branch Equivalent Circuit Diagram:**

The two-branch model consists of:
- **Branch 1 (Immediate Response):** Capacitance C_0 in series with ESR (R_ESR). This branch represents the **instantaneous** charge/discharge behavior, dominating the response in the first few seconds.
- **Branch 2 (Delayed Response):** Resistance R_1 in series with capacitance C_1, representing the **slow redistribution of charge** within porous electrode pores (occurring over minutes).
- Both branches are in parallel with a **leakage resistance R_leak** (very high value, ~10 kΩ), modeling self-discharge.

**Why Two-Branch is Preferred for Long-Duration Simulations:**

1. The simple RC model assumes a fixed capacitance, but real supercapacitors exhibit **voltage-dependent capacitance** (charge redistribution across pores takes time).
2. The C_1 branch captures the **pore diffusion dynamics**, which becomes significant during slow charge/discharge cycles (e.g., overnight charging).
3. The two-branch model accurately predicts the **time-dependent voltage recovery** (also called "voltage relaxation" or "self-distribution") after a current pulse.
4. It improves SOC estimation accuracy from ~85% (simple RC) to >97% over hours-long timescales.
5. It is computationally efficient — much faster than full transmission line models — making it suitable for embedded BMS/SMS processors.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Simple RC | Two-Branch |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy (short time) | Good | Good |
| Accuracy (long time) | Poor | Excellent |
| Voltage Relaxation | Not modeled | Modeled |
| SOC Error | 10–15% | <3% |
| Computational Cost | Very Low | Low |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / ESE Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) combining a battery and a supercapacitor for an EV. Discuss the role of the Energy Management Strategy (EMS) and the advantages of such a hybrid topology.**
**(7 Marks)**

**(b) A supercapacitor module consists of 18 cells connected in series. Each cell has C = 1500 F and ESR = 2.5 mΩ. The rated voltage per cell is 2.7 V.**
- **(i) Calculate the equivalent capacitance, total ESR, and stack voltage of the module.**
- **(ii) If this module supplies a constant current of 200 A during acceleration, calculate the time to discharge from full charge to 0.9 V per cell.**
- **(iii) Compare the energy density and power density of this supercapacitor module with a typical Li-ion battery having 150 Wh/kg and 250 W/kg, given the supercapacitor module weighs 12 kg and stores 14 Wh of usable energy.**
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**HESS Architecture Block Diagram:**

The HESS consists of:
- A **battery pack** (high energy density source)
- A **supercapacitor pack** (high power density source)
- Two **bidirectional DC-DC converters** (one for each storage element) to interface with the common DC bus
- An **inverter** that converts DC to 3-phase AC for the traction motor
- An **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** controller that splits power demand between the two sources

**Power Splitting Logic (Frequency-Based):**
- The EMS uses a **low-pass filter** to split the demanded power into:
  - **Low-frequency component (steady-state power):** Routed to the **battery** (e.g., cruising on highway).
  - **High-frequency component (transient power):** Routed to the **supercapacitor** (e.g., acceleration bursts, regenerative braking spikes).

**EMS Strategies (Common Approaches):**
1. **Rule-based (Deterministic):** Threshold-based logic using SOC of both sources.
2. **Fuzzy Logic:** Rule-based with linguistic variables for SOC, power demand, and temperature.
3. **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS):** Optimizes a cost function in real-time.
4. **Dynamic Programming / Model Predictive Control (MPC):** Optimal but computationally intensive.

**Advantages of HESS:**

1. **Extended Battery Life:** 2–3× cycle life improvement as supercapacitor handles high-current transients.
2. **Higher Regenerative Braking Recovery:** 60–80% vs. 30–40% with battery alone.
3. **Better Cold-Start Performance:** Supercapacitor delivers power at low temperatures without battery damage.
4. **Reduced System Sizing:** Smaller battery can be used since supercapacitor handles peaks.
5. **Improved Vehicle Performance:** Higher peak power available for acceleration.
6. **Enhanced Safety:** Battery operates in narrower SOC window, reducing thermal runaway risk.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** N = 18 cells in series, C_cell = 1500 F, ESR_cell = 2.5 mΩ, V_cell = 2.7 V, I = 200 A, V_final_cell = 0.9 V, m_module = 12 kg.

**(i) Module Parameters:**

**Equivalent Capacitance (series):**

$$C_{module} = \frac{C_{cell}}{N} = \frac{1500}{18} = 83.33 \text{ F}$$

**Total ESR (series):**

$$R_{ESR,total} = N \times R_{ESR,cell} = 18 \times 2.5 \text{ m}\Omega = 45 \text{ m}\Omega = 0.045 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Stack Voltage:**

$$V_{stack} = N \times V_{cell} = 18 \times 2.7 = 48.6 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Time to Discharge:**

Per-cell discharge voltage swing:

$$\Delta V_{cell} = V_{max} - V_{min} = 2.7 - 0.9 = 1.8 \text{ V}$$

For the entire stack:

$$\Delta V_{stack} = 18 \times 1.8 = 32.4 \text{ V}$$

Time of discharge under constant current:

$$t = \frac{C_{module} \times \Delta V_{stack}}{I} = \frac{83.33 \times 32.4}{200} = \frac{2{,}700}{200} = 13.5 \text{ seconds}$$

Alternatively, per cell:

$$t = \frac{C_{cell} \times \Delta V_{cell}}{I} = \frac{1500 \times 1.8}{200} = 13.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**(iii) Comparison:**

**Supercapacitor Module:**
- Energy Density: 14 Wh / 12 kg = **1.167 Wh/kg**
- Power Density: For max power, $P_{max} = V_{stack}^2 / (4 R_{ESR}) = (48.6)^2 / (4 \times 0.045) = 2361.96 / 0.18 = 13{,}122$ W.
- Power Density: 13,122 W / 12 kg = **1,093.5 W/kg**

**Li-ion Battery:** 150 Wh/kg energy, 250 W/kg power.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Supercapacitor Module | Li-ion Battery | Ratio (SC/Batt) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Density | 1.167 Wh/kg | 150 Wh/kg | 0.0078 (battery is **128× higher**) |
| Power Density | 1,093.5 W/kg | 250 W/kg | 4.37 (supercapacitor is **4.37× higher**) |
| Cycle Life | 500,000+ | 1,000–3,000 | 167–500× longer |
| Charge Time | ~15 sec | ~1–4 hours | 240–960× faster |

**Conclusion:** The supercapacitor module has **dramatically lower energy density** but **higher power density** and longer cycle life, confirming its role in **transient power applications** in EVs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Confusing energy and power density** — Students often swap units (Wh/kg vs. W/kg). Remember: Energy is *capacity*, Power is *rate*.
> 2. **Forgetting the quadratic energy relationship** — In supercapacitors, energy is proportional to V², not V. So discharging from 2.7V to 1.35V releases 75% of total energy, not 50% — this catches many students.
> 3. **Series vs. parallel confusion** — In series, capacitance drops, ESR rises. In parallel, capacitance rises, ESR drops.
> 4. **Mixing battery SOC and supercapacitor SOC** — Battery SOC is coulomb-counted; supercapacitor SOC is voltage-based.
> 5. **Drawing equivalent circuits without labeling component values** — Always annotate R_ESR, C, and the time constant.
> 6. **Ignoring voltage balancing** in series-stacked cells — Cells with mismatched capacitance experience unequal voltage, leading to over-voltage damage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:

- **Supercapacitor = EDLC** (Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor). Energy storage is **electrostatic**, not electrochemical.
- **Key Equation:** $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ (quadratic in voltage — extract 75% energy in upper 50% voltage range).
- **Capacitance of double layer:** $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$ — with $d \approx 0.5$ nm, $A \approx 2000$ m²/g.
- **Three layers of double layer:** IHP, OHP, Diffuse (Gouy-Chapman) layer.
- **Three materials used:** Activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene (focus on **high surface area**).
- **Three types of supercapacitors:** EDLC (symmetric), Pseudocapacitor (faradaic), Hybrid (asymmetric).
- **Equivalent Circuit Models:** Simple RC → Two-Branch → Three-Branch → Transmission Line (increasing accuracy, increasing cost).
- **Series Cells:** C_total = C/N, R_total = N × R, V_total = N × V.
- **Parallel Cells:** C_total = N × C, R_total = R/N, V_total = V.
- **SOC Estimation:** Voltage-based, NOT coulomb-counted. $SOC = (V_{cap} - V_{min})/(V_{max} - V_{min})$.
- **Ragone Plot:** Supercapacitors in mid-region; batteries in high-energy region; capacitors in high-power region.
- **HESS Topology:** Battery + Supercapacitor with bidirectional DC-DC converters, controlled by EMS.
- **EMS Power Splitting:** Low-pass filter separates low-frequency (battery) and high-frequency (supercapacitor) power.
- **Max Power Transfer:** Occurs when R_load = R_ESR, giving $P_{max} = V^2 / (4 R_{ESR})$.
- **Time Constant:** $\tau = R_{ESR} \times C$ — defines the time-domain response of the cell.
- **Operating Voltage per cell:** 2.5–2.85 V (organic electrolyte); voltage balancing is critical for series stacks.
- **Key Advantages vs. Batteries:** Power density (10–100×), cycle life (100–1000×), charge time (100–1000×), temperature range (3–4× wider).
- **Key Disadvantage vs. Batteries:** Low energy density (5–10 Wh/kg vs. 100–250 Wh/kg).
- **EV Applications:** Regenerative braking, acceleration boost, 12V/48V start-stop, voltage stabilization, HESS.
- **Coulombic Efficiency:** 95–98% for supercapacitors (vs. 80–90% for batteries).
- **Self-Discharge Mechanism:** Voltage-dependent leakage through R_leak; typically 1–5% per day.
- **Thermal Behavior:** Low internal heating due to low ESR; still requires thermal monitoring above 65°C.
- **Industry Leaders:** Maxwell Technologies, Skeleton Technologies, Eaton, Panasonic, LS Mtron.
- **Formula to Memorize for KTU:**
  - $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$
  - $P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4R_{ESR}}$
  - $\tau = R_{ESR} C$
  - $C_{series} = C_{cell}/N$
  - $V(t) = \sqrt{V_{max}^2 - 2Pt/C}$ (constant power discharge)
- **Famous Use Cases:** Toyota Yaris Hybrid (12V start-stop), Mazda i-ELOOP (regenerative supercapacitor), Lamborghini Sesto Elemento (supercapacitor for peak boost), Formula 1 KERS.
- **Future Direction:** Hybrid Li-ion / supercapacitor cells, graphene-electrode supercapacitors, solid-state supercapacitors for next-gen EVs.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Note:**
> When answering supercapacitor questions, **always**: (1) Define terms precisely using KTU syllabus language, (2) Draw circuit diagrams with labels and component values, (3) Show full mathematical derivation with units at every step, (4) End with a real-world EV application example. This structure consistently scores 90%+ in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultra Capacitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ultra Capacitors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ultracapacitor (Supercapacitor / Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor — EDLC):** An electrochemical energy storage device that stores energy **electrostatically** by means of the separation of charge at the electrode–electrolyte interface, rather than by faradaic (chemical) reactions as in conventional batteries. It bridges the gap between conventional dielectric capacitors and batteries, delivering orders of magnitude higher energy density than capacitors and orders of magnitude higher power density than batteries. The operational foundation is the **Electric Double Layer (EDL)** — a nanoscale layer of oppositely charged ions that forms spontaneously at every electrode/electrolyte interface. Because the separation distance (the Debye length) is on the order of **0.3 – 0.8 nm** (compared to microns in conventional capacitors), the capacitance per unit area becomes extraordinarily high. **Standard Classification (per IEC 62391):** | Class | Capacitance Range | Internal Resistance | Applications | |---|---|---|---| | Class 1 | Up to 100 F | Low | Memory back-up | | Class 2 | 100 F – 4700 F | Medium | EV peak power | | Class 3 | > 4700 F | High | Heavy traction | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for EVs:** During regenerative braking, peak power can reach **100–200 kW** for a few seconds. A lithium-ion battery struggles to absorb such transient pulses without degradation. Ultracapacitors accept and deliver these pulses effortlessly, hence they are typically coupled with the battery in a **hybrid energy storage system (HESS)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an **ultracapacitor as a charged sponge** and a **battery as a fuel tank**: - A **fuel tank (battery)** holds a *huge amount of energy* but releases it slowly through a small pipe — equivalent to high energy density, low power density. - A **charged sponge (ultracapacitor)** holds *much less energy* but releases it almost instantly when squeezed — equivalent to lower energy density, very high power density. **Real-world analogy:** Consider a high-rise building fire. A water reservoir (battery) is the main supply — reliable and long-lasting. But the fire trucks need a sudden burst of water for the initial attack. They use a **fire hydrant directly connected to a high-pressure booster (ultracapacitor)** — it doesn't store as much, but it delivers an explosive surge. That is exactly what an ultracapacitor does during EV acceleration or regenerative braking. ## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics - **Specific Energy (Gravimetric):** typically **5 – 10 Wh/kg** - **Specific Power:** typically **1 000 – 10 000 W/kg** - **Cycle Life:** **> 500 000 – 1 000 000 cycles** - **Operating Temperature:** **−40 °C to +65 °C** - **Nominal Cell Voltage:** **2.5 V – 2.7 V** (aqueous 1 V, organic 2.7 V) - **Charge Time:** seconds to minutes - **Coulombic Efficiency:** ≈ **95 – 98 %** > [!TIP] > The **Ragone plot** (energy density vs. power density, both on log scale) is the universal way to compare energy storage devices. Ultracapacitors sit diagonally opposite to fuel cells, while batteries lie in between — making ultracapacitors the **"power device"** and batteries the **"energy device"**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — The Electric Double Layer When an activated-carbon electrode is immersed in an electrolyte and a voltage is applied: 1. **Ion adsorption:** Ions of opposite polarity in the electrolyte migrate toward the electrode surface. 2. **Formation of EDL:** Two layers of charge form: - **Inner Helmholtz Plane (IHP):** specifically adsorbed (e.g., solvent) molecules. - **Outer Helmholtz Plane (OHP):** non-specifically adsorbed solvated ions. - **Diffuse layer (Gouy–Chapman):** thermally distributed ions beyond OHP. 3. The **Stern model** combines the compact (Helmholtz) and diffuse (Gouy–Chapman) layers into a unified description. This two-layer charge separation acts as a capacitor with an *extremely small* effective plate separation, giving enormous capacitance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Helmholtz capacitance per unit area:** > $$C_{H} = \frac{\varepsilon_{r}\,\varepsilon_{0}}{d}$$ > where **d ≈ Debye length ≈ 0.3 – 0.8 nm** in a concentrated electrolyte. ## 2.2 Construction of an Ultracapacitor Cell | Component | Material | Function | |---|---|---| | Electrodes | Activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, aerogel | Provide enormous surface area (**1 000 – 3 000 m²/g**) | | Electrolyte | Aqueous (H₂SO₄, KOH) **or** organic (TEABF₄ in ACN) | Source of mobile ions | | Separator | Polypropylene / cellulose membrane | Prevents short circuit, allows ion migration | | Current collectors | Aluminium foil | Electrical contact | | Cell housing | Stainless steel / laminated aluminium | Hermetic sealing | ## 2.3 Types of Ultracapacitors 1. **Electric Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC)** — pure electrostatic storage (carbon electrodes). High cycle life, lower energy density. 2. **Pseudocapacitor** — fast faradaic redox reactions at/near the surface (e.g., MnO₂, RuO₂, conducting polymers). Higher energy density but shorter life. 3. **Hybrid Capacitor (asymmetric / Li-ion capacitor)** — one EDL electrode + one battery-type electrode. Combines high energy + high power (e.g., LIC by JM Energy). ## 2.4 Equivalent Circuit Models ### (a) Simple RC Model A single capacitor **C** in series with an **Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR)** and parallel leakage resistance **R_p**. ### (b) Three-Branch / Transmission-Line Model Used for accurate dynamic simulation — represents distributed capacitance and resistance through the porous electrode depth. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in EVs - **Regenerative braking energy recovery** (recovers 30–40 % of braking energy that batteries cannot absorb). - **Peak-power assist during acceleration** (load levelling on the battery, extending its life). - **Cold-start assist** at low temperatures. - **Stabilizing the DC bus voltage** in fuel-cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai, Honda Clarity). - **Start–stop systems** in micro-hybrid (12 V / 48 V) architectures. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **KTU examination essentials** — memorize both the expression and the unit. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Capacitance of a parallel-plate | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,A}{d}$ | F | $A$ = electrode area, $d$ = separation | | 2 | Capacitance of EDLC (cylindrical pore) | $C = \dfrac{2\,\varepsilon_{r}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | F | $a$, $b$ = inner/outer pore radii | | 3 | Energy stored | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C\,V^{2}$ | J (or Wh) | Fundamental energy equation | | 4 | Specific energy | $E_{m} = \dfrac{E}{m} = \dfrac{C V^{2}}{2 m}$ | J/kg or Wh/kg | Mass-based | | 5 | Volumetric energy | $E_{v} = \dfrac{E}{V_{ol}} = \dfrac{C V^{2}}{2 V_{ol}}$ | J/m³ or Wh/L | Volume-based | | 6 | Power delivered | $P = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4 R_{ESR}}$ | W | Matched-load, max power | | 7 | Specific power | $P_{m} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4\,R_{ESR}\,m}$ | W/kg | | | 8 | RC time constant | $\tau = R_{ESR} \cdot C$ | s | Smaller is better for high power | | 9 | Charging time (constant power) | $t_{ch} = \dfrac{C V}{2 I_{av}} = \dfrac{C V^{2}}{2 P}$ | s | Linear approximation | | 10 | Discharge current (constant load R_L) | $i(t) = \dfrac{V_{0}}{R_{L}+R_{ESR}} e^{-t/\tau}$ | A | First-order behaviour | | 11 | Number of cells in series | $N_{s} = \lceil V_{bus}/V_{cell} \rceil$ | — | For HV pack | | 12 | Effective pack capacitance | $C_{pack} = \dfrac{C_{cell}}{N_{s}}$ | F | Series reduces C | | 13 | Effective pack voltage | $V_{pack} = N_{s} V_{cell}$ | V | | | 14 | Cell voltage limits | $V_{min} = \tfrac{1}{2}V_{rated},\ V_{max} = V_{rated}$ | V | Safe operating window | | 15 | State of charge (voltage-based) | $SOC = \dfrac{V - V_{min}}{V_{max} - V_{min}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Linear (vs. battery non-linear) | | 16 | Stored charge | $Q = C \cdot V$ | C (or Ah) | $1\,\text{Ah} = 3\,600\,\text{C}$ | | 17 | Energy per cell in Wh | $E_{Wh} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} / 3\,600$ | Wh | Practical unit | | 18 | Efficiency (round-trip) | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | 90–98 % for EDLC | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical numerical values to remember:** > - Vacuum permittivity: $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m > - Debye length in organic electrolyte: ≈ **0.7 nm** > - Activated-carbon BET surface area: **1 500 – 3 000 m²/g** > - Self-discharge rate: **5 – 10 % per day** (first 24 h), then much slower. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in an Ultracapacitor The energy stored in any capacitor is obtained by integrating the instantaneous power $p = v \cdot i$ over a charging cycle, where $i = C \dfrac{dv}{dt}$. **Step 1 — Express power in terms of voltage:** $$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = v(t) \cdot C\,\dfrac{dv}{dt}$$ **Step 2 — Integrate from $0$ to final voltage $V$ (assuming charge from 0 V):** $$E = \int_{0}^{V} v \cdot C\,dv$$ **Step 3 — Perform the integration:** $$E = C\,\left[\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{V} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C\,V^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to the practical unit Wh (1 Wh = 3 600 J):** $$E_{Wh} = \dfrac{C\,V^{2}}{7\,200}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this is the cornerstone derivation:** Every EV energy-sizing problem (pack sizing, range extension by ultracapacitor, regenerative-braking capture) begins with this equation. The $V^{2}$ dependence is why ultracapacitors are operated up to their maximum rated voltage — doubling the voltage quadruples the stored energy. ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer (Matched-Load) A charged ultracapacitor of capacitance $C$ at voltage $V$ drives an external load $R_L$ through its internal ESR $R_{ESR}$. The total loop resistance is $(R_L + R_{ESR})$. Power dissipated in $R_L$ is: **Step 1 — Current in the loop:** $$I = \dfrac{V}{R_L + R_{ESR}}$$ **Step 2 — Power in the load:** $$P_L = I^{2}\,R_L = \dfrac{V^{2}\,R_L}{(R_L + R_{ESR})^{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Maximize w.r.t. $R_L$ by differentiating and setting to zero:** $$\dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \dfrac{V^{2}\,(R_L + R_{ESR})^{2} - V^{2}\,R_L \cdot 2(R_L + R_{ESR})}{(R_L + R_{ESR})^{4}} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the numerator:** $$(R_L + R_{ESR}) - 2 R_L = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_L = R_{ESR}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $R_L = R_{ESR}$ back into the power equation:** $$P_{max} = \dfrac{V^{2}\,R_{ESR}}{(2R_{ESR})^{2}} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4\,R_{ESR}}$$ **Step 6 — Specific power (mass-normalized):** $$P_{m,\,max} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4\,R_{ESR}\,m}$$ > [!TIP] > **Design implication:** The lower the ESR (better electrolyte wetting, thinner separator, denser electrode), the higher the deliverable peak power. This is why carbon-coating of current collectors and ionic-liquid electrolytes are active research frontiers. ## 3.3 Derivation: Effective Pack Capacitance and Voltage When $N_s$ identical cells of capacitance $C_{cell}$ are connected in **series** to build a high-voltage bus, and $N_p$ cells are connected in **parallel** for additional capacity: **Step 1 — Series: capacitance reduces:** $$\dfrac{1}{C_{branch}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{s}} \dfrac{1}{C_i} = \dfrac{N_{s}}{C_{cell}} \;\Rightarrow\; C_{branch} = \dfrac{C_{cell}}{N_{s}}$$ **Step 2 — Parallel: capacitances add:** $$C_{pack} = N_{p} \cdot C_{branch} = \dfrac{N_{p}\,C_{cell}}{N_{s}}$$ **Step 3 — Total pack voltage:** $$V_{pack} = N_{s} \cdot V_{cell}$$ **Step 4 — Total stored energy of the pack:** $$E_{pack} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C_{pack}\,V_{pack}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{N_{p}\,C_{cell}}{N_{s}} \cdot (N_{s} V_{cell})^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,N_{p}\,N_{s}\,C_{cell}\,V_{cell}^{2}$$ **Step 5 — Total stored energy of the pack in Watt-hours:** $$E_{pack,\,Wh} = \dfrac{N_{p}\,N_{s}\,C_{cell}\,V_{cell}^{2}}{7\,200}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** Adding cells in parallel increases energy linearly with $N_p$, while adding cells in series increases energy linearly with $N_s$ as well (because $V^2$ grows with $N_s^2$ but $C$ falls as $1/N_s$). In a properly sized HESS, parallel strings of capacitors handle peak current, while series strings handle bus voltage. ## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Recovered During Regenerative Braking Consider an EV of mass $m$ decelerating from velocity $v_0$ to $v_f$ with regenerative fraction $\eta_{reg}$. The recoverable kinetic energy is: $$E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_0^{2} - v_{f}^{2})$$ **Step 1 — Effective recapture:** $$E_{cap} = \eta_{reg}\cdot E_{kin}$$ **Step 2 — Required ultracapacitor capacitance to absorb $E_{cap}$ when its voltage rises from $V_{low}$ to $V_{high}$:** $$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C\,(V_{high}^{2} - V_{low}^{2}) = E_{cap}$$ **Step 3 — Solving for C:** $$C = \dfrac{2\,E_{cap}}{V_{high}^{2} - V_{low}^{2}}$$ **Step 4 — In Wh, with $V$ in volts and $E$ in Wh:** $$C = \dfrac{7\,200 \, E_{cap,\,Wh}}{V_{high}^{2} - V_{low}^{2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Voltage swing utilisation is critical.** Allowing the ultracapacitor to swing from $V_{low} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{max}$ to $V_{high} = V_{max}$ uses **75 %** of its full energy capacity. Discharging further below half-voltage would cause an exponentially large capacitance requirement. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A 2 400 F ultracapacitor is charged to 2.7 V and supplies a 0.5 Ω load through an ESR of 0.8 mΩ. Determine (a) the energy stored, (b) the maximum instantaneous power, (c) the time to discharge to 1.35 V through the load, and (d) the average discharge power. **Given:** $C = 2\,400$ F, $V_{0} = 2.7$ V, $R_L = 0.5\ \Omega$, $R_{ESR} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega$, $V_{f} = 1.35$ V. **Step (a) — Energy stored:** $$E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2\,400 \times (2.7)^{2}$$ $$E = 1\,200 \times 7.29 = 8\,748\ \text{J} = 2.43\ \text{Wh}$$ **Step (b) — Maximum power (matched load):** $$P_{max} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4\,R_{ESR}} = \dfrac{(2.7)^{2}}{4 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{7.29}{3.2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2\,278\ \text{W}$$ **Step (c) — Discharge time to $V_{f} = 1.35$ V:** $$v(t) = V_{0}\,e^{-t/(R_{L} C)} \;\Rightarrow\; 1.35 = 2.7 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ $$e^{-t/\tau} = 0.5 \;\Rightarrow\; t = \tau \ln 2$$ $$\tau = R_{L}\,C = 0.5 \times 2\,400 = 1\,200\ \text{s}$$ $$t = 1\,200 \times 0.693 = 831.6\ \text{s} \approx 13.86\ \text{min}$$ **Step (d) — Average power over discharge:** $$E_{delivered} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C\,(V_{0}^{2} - V_{f}^{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2\,400 \times (7.29 - 1.8225) = 6\,561\ \text{J}$$ $$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{delivered}}{t} = \dfrac{6\,561}{831.6} \approx 7.89\ \text{W}$$ > [!TIP] > The **average power is much less than peak power** because the voltage (and hence current) decays exponentially. This is precisely why ultracapacitors are paired with DC-DC converters that maintain a regulated bus voltage in EV applications. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ultracapacitor Construction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Cell["Ultracapacitor Cell Cross Section"] direction LR A1["Aluminium Current Collector Positive"] --> E1["Activated Carbon Electrode Positive
BET area 1500-3000 m2 per g"] E1 --> SP["Porous Separator
Polypropylene membrane
ion-permeable"] SP --> E2["Activated Carbon Electrode Negative
BET area 1500-3000 m2 per g"] E2 --> A2["Aluminium Current Collector Negative"] end EL["Liquid Electrolyte
Organic TEABF4 in Acetonitrile
or Aqueous H2SO4 / KOH"] -. fills pores .-> E1 EL -. fills pores .-> E2 EL -. fills pores .-> SP A1 --> T1["Positive Terminal"] A2 --> T2["Negative Terminal"] style Cell fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px style E1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style E2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style SP fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style EL fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#7e22ce ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — EDL Formation (Charge Storage) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Neg["Negative Electrode"] NE["Carbon Surface
negatively charged"] IONS_N["Cations of Electrolyte
e.g. TEA plus"] end subgraph Pos["Positive Electrode"] PE["Carbon Surface
positively charged"] IONS_P["Anions of Electrolyte
e.g. BF4 minus"] end V["Applied Voltage V"] --- NE V --- PE NE -- "Helmholtz plane d = 0.3-0.8 nm" --- IONS_N PE -- "Helmholtz plane d = 0.3-0.8 nm" --- IONS_P IONS_N ===|"Diffuse Gouy-Chapman layer"| IONS_P style Neg fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c style Pos fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style IONS_N fill:#fde68a style IONS_P fill:#bbf7d0 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — EV Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Storage["Hybrid Energy Storage System"] BAT["Li-ion Battery Pack
400 V nominal
50-100 kWh"] UC["Ultracapacitor Pack
150-300 V nominal
0.5-2 kWh"] end DC1["Bidirectional DC-DC Converter 1"] --- BAT DC2["Bidirectional DC-DC Converter 2"] --- UC DC1 --- BUS["High-Voltage DC Bus
650 V nominal"] DC2 --- BUS BUS --> INV["Traction Inverter
3-phase IGBT/SiC"] INV --> M["EV Traction Motor
200-300 kW peak"] M -- "Regenerative Braking" --> INV INV -- "Brake Energy" --> BUS BUS -- "Charges UC first" --> DC2 BUS -- "Surplus to Battery" --> DC1 REGEN["Peak Power Pulses
100-200 kW for 5-10 s"] -. absorbed by .-> UC CRUISE["Steady Cruise Power
20-40 kW"] -. supplied by .-> BAT style Storage fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#0f172a style UC fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309 style BAT fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Ultracapacitor Equivalent Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR TP["Positive Terminal"] --- R1["ESR
Equivalent Series Resistance

kilo-ohms"] RN --- TN["Negative Terminal"] C1 -. parallel .-> RN style C1 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px style R1 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c style RN fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ## 4.5 Schematic — Ragone Plot (Energy vs Power Density) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Plot["Ragone Plot — log-log Energy Density vs Power Density"] direction TB A["Fuel Cells
E: 300-1000 Wh/kg
P: 100-500 W/kg"] B["Li-ion Battery

E: less than 0.1 Wh/kg
