Complementary .-> D style A fill:#fde68a style B fill:#bbf7d0 style C fill:#bfdbfe style D fill:#fecaca ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Ragone plot:** Devices in the **upper-right** are ideal (high energy + high power) — none exist yet. Ultracapacitors dominate the right side, batteries the top-left. This is why an EV with a **HESS** (battery + ultracapacitor) shifts the operating point towards the upper-right corner. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 pattern]** ### Question 1 (3 marks) **Differentiate between an ultracapacitor and a conventional electrolytic capacitor.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Conventional Capacitor | Ultracapacitor (EDLC) | |---|---|---| | Energy storage | Electrostatic (dielectric) | Electrostatic (electric double layer) | | Capacitance | µF to mF | 1 F to > 5 000 F | | Plate separation | Microns | Nanometres (Debye length) | | Voltage per cell | Up to 1 kV | 1 V (aqueous) – 2.7 V (organic) | | Energy density | < 0.1 Wh/kg | 5 – 10 Wh/kg | | Power density | Very high (> MW/kg) | High (5–15 kW/kg) | | Cycle life | Infinite | > 500 000 | | Charge time | µs | Seconds to minutes | *(2 marks for the table; 1 mark for the concluding sentence on energy-storage mechanism.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 marks) **State any three advantages of using ultracapacitors in an electric vehicle.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Very high power density (5 – 15 kW/kg)** — absorbs regenerative-braking surges and supplies peak acceleration power that would otherwise damage the battery. *(1 mark)* 2. **Long cycle life (> 500 000 cycles)** — practically maintenance-free; the EV battery's life is extended by offloading peak cycles. *(1 mark)* 3. **Wide operating temperature range (−40 °C to +65 °C)** — reliable cold-start and hot-climate operation where battery performance degrades. *(1 mark)* > [!TIP] > Additional advantages examiners reward: high round-trip efficiency (90–98 %), fast charging (seconds), environmentally benign (carbon-based), no risk of thermal runaway. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **confuse the capacitance formula for a series pack ($C_{pack} = C_{cell}/N_s$) with a parallel pack**, and they often **forget the ESR energy-loss term** when computing maximum power. Always read the question twice — "are the cells in series or parallel?" --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 pattern]** **(a)** With a neat labelled schematic, explain the construction and working of an electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC). Discuss the role of activated carbon as the electrode material. *(7 marks)* **(b)** An ultracapacitor module consists of **18 cells in series**, each rated **2.7 V and 3 000 F** with an **ESR of 0.5 mΩ per cell**. Calculate: - (i) the pack voltage and equivalent pack capacitance, - (ii) the energy stored in the fully charged pack (in Wh), - (iii) the maximum peak power deliverable (matched-load) at full charge, - (iv) the time constant of the pack. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part A(a) **Construction (3 marks):** A single EDLC cell is built as a sandwich of two porous carbon electrodes separated by an ion-permeable membrane, both impregnated with electrolyte, and contacted by metallic current collectors. - **Activated-carbon electrodes:** BET surface area 1 500 – 3 000 m²/g, typical thickness 100 – 200 µm, coated onto an aluminium current-collector foil. - **Separator:** porous polypropylene or cellulose, 20 – 50 µm thick, prevents electrical contact but allows ion flow. - **Electrolyte:** organic (1 mol/L TEABF₄ in acetonitrile) for 2.7 V operation, or aqueous (H₂SO₄/KOH) for 1 V operation. - **Housing:** hermetically sealed aluminium-laminate pouch or stainless-steel cylindrical can. **Working (3 marks):** When a voltage $V$ is applied between the two electrodes: - The positive electrode attracts **anions** (e.g. $\text{BF}_4^{-}$); the negative electrode attracts **cations** (e.g. $\text{TEA}^{+}$) from the electrolyte. - A layer of solvated ions is held within ≈ 0.3 – 0.8 nm of each carbon surface — this is the **Helmholtz double layer**. - The combination of the two Helmholtz layers behaves as **two capacitors in series**, giving: $$C_{cell} = \left(\dfrac{1}{C_{EDL,+}} + \dfrac{1}{C_{EDL,-}}\right)^{-1}$$ - For symmetric cells ($C_{EDL,+} = C_{EDL,-} = C_{EDL}$), the cell capacitance is $C_{cell} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{EDL}$. **Why activated carbon? (1 mark):** - Extremely high specific surface area (1 500 – 3 000 m²/g) ⇒ very high $A$ in $C = \varepsilon A / d$. - Pore size distribution (mostly 2 – 5 nm) matches the Debye length, allowing ions to access internal surfaces. - Good electrical conductivity, electrochemical stability across the voltage window, and low cost. --- #### Model Solution — Part A(b) **Given:** $N_s = 18$, $V_{cell} = 2.7$ V, $C_{cell} = 3\,000$ F, $R_{ESR,cell} = 0.5$ mΩ. **(i) Pack voltage and equivalent pack capacitance (2 marks):** $$V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell} = 18 \times 2.7 = 48.6\ \text{V}$$ $$C_{pack} = \dfrac{C_{cell}}{N_s} = \dfrac{3\,000}{18} = 166.67\ \text{F}$$ **Equivalent pack ESR (adds in series for 18 cells):** $$R_{ESR,pack} = N_s \cdot R_{ESR,cell} = 18 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 9 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega = 9\ \text{m}\Omega$$ **(ii) Energy stored at full charge (2 marks):** $$E_{pack} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{pack} V_{pack}^{2}$$ $$E_{pack} = 0.5 \times 166.67 \times (48.6)^{2}$$ $$E_{pack} = 0.5 \times 166.67 \times 2\,361.96 = 196\,830\ \text{J}$$ Converting to Wh: $$E_{pack,\,Wh} = \dfrac{196\,830}{3\,600} = 54.67\ \text{Wh}$$ **(iii) Maximum peak power — matched load (2 marks):** $$P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{pack}^{2}}{4\,R_{ESR,pack}}$$ $$P_{max} = \dfrac{(48.6)^{2}}{4 \times 9 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{2\,361.96}{0.036}$$ $$P_{max} \approx 65\,610\ \text{W} \approx 65.6\ \text{kW}$$ **(iv) Pack time constant (1 mark):** $$\tau = R_{ESR,pack} \cdot C_{pack} = 9 \times 10^{-3} \times 166.67 = 1.5\ \text{s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes in this question:** > - Using $C_{cell} = 3\,000$ F directly in $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ (you **must** use the *pack* capacitance). > - Forgetting to square the pack voltage when computing energy. > - Mixing the per-cell ESR with the pack ESR (multiplied vs. divided by $N_s$). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **Ragone plot**. Discuss where ultracapacitors, lithium-ion batteries, fuel cells and conventional capacitors are positioned on it, and justify the use of a **hybrid energy storage system (HESS)** in modern electric vehicles. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A **1 200 F, 2.5 V** ultracapacitor is used as the peak-power buffer in a micro-hybrid start–stop system. The starter motor draws **300 A peak for 1.5 seconds** during engine cranking, after which the capacitor must recover to **2.0 V** in **30 seconds** from a **14 V alternator** through a buck-boost DC-DC converter. - (i) Calculate the energy delivered by the ultracapacitor during cranking (assume constant current). - (ii) Compute the final voltage of the ultracapacitor after the crank event. - (iii) Determine the average charging current required to refill it to 2.0 V in 30 s. - (iv) Comment on whether this ultracapacitor is adequately sized. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part B(a) **Ragone plot definition (2 marks):** The Ragone plot is a log–log graph of **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the y-axis vs. **specific power (W/kg)** on the x-axis. It is the standard tool to compare energy-storage devices; the further a device sits towards the **upper-right**, the more ideal it is. **Position of devices (3 marks):** | Device | Specific Energy | Specific Power | Region on Plot | |---|---|---|---| | Fuel cell (H₂) | 300 – 1 000 Wh/kg | 100 – 500 W/kg | Top-left | | Li-ion battery | 100 – 250 Wh/kg | 200 – 1 500 W/kg | Middle | | Ultracapacitor (EDLC) | 5 – 10 Wh/kg | 5 000 – 15 000 W/kg | Right side | | Conventional capacitor | < 0.1 Wh/kg | > 10⁷ W/kg | Far right | **Hybrid ESS justification (2 marks):** - No single device occupies the ideal upper-right corner. - A **HESS combines a high-energy device (Li-ion battery)** with a **high-power device (ultracapacitor)** via DC-DC converters, achieving a system-level Ragone point that approaches the upper-right. - In a HESS, the battery handles steady-state cruise power, while the ultracapacitor handles **transient peak loads** — regen-braking, acceleration, cold-start. This: - Extends battery life (less stress, fewer deep cycles). - Recovers braking energy that the battery cannot absorb. - Improves cold-start reliability. - Enables smaller, lighter battery packs. --- #### Model Solution — Part B(b) **Given:** $C = 1\,200$ F, $V_0 = 2.5$ V (initial), $I_{crank} = 300$ A, $t_{crank} = 1.5$ s. **(i) Energy delivered during cranking (2 marks):** Charge removed: $$\Delta Q = I \cdot t = 300 \times 1.5 = 450\ \text{C}$$ Voltage drop across capacitance: $$\Delta V = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C} = \dfrac{450}{1\,200} = 0.375\ \text{V}$$ Final voltage after cranking: $$V_f = V_0 - \Delta V = 2.5 - 0.375 = 2.125\ \text{V}$$ Energy delivered (constant current approximation): $$E_{del} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C\,(V_0^{2} - V_f^{2}) = 0.5 \times 1\,200 \times (6.25 - 4.516)$$ $$E_{del} = 600 \times 1.734 = 1\,040.4\ \text{J} = 0.289\ \text{Wh}$$ **(ii) Final voltage after crank — already computed above (1 mark):** $$V_f = 2.125\ \text{V}$$ **(iii) Average charging current to refill from 2.125 V to 2.0 V in 30 s (2 marks):** Wait — the problem says "recover to 2.0 V". Since $V_f = 2.125\ \text{V} < 2.0\ \text{V}$ is **false** (2.125 > 2.0), the ultracapacitor is already above 2.0 V; no charging is needed for the basic 2.0 V target. Instead, let's interpret it as the desired **fully-recharged** state being 2.5 V (full). Recalculating for charging from 2.125 V back to 2.5 V: Charge to be added: $$\Delta Q_{ch} = C\,(V_{target} - V_f) = 1\,200 \times (2.5 - 2.125) = 450\ \text{C}$$ Average charging current: $$I_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_{ch}}{t_{ch}} = \dfrac{450}{30} = 15\ \text{A}$$ **(iv) Sizing comment (2 marks):** - The voltage drop of only **0.375 V** (15 %) on a 2.5 V capacitor is healthy — the device is **not overstressed**. - The energy delivered per crank (0.289 Wh) is sufficient for a 4-cylinder micro-hybrid crank cycle. - The required recharge current of 15 A is well within the capability of a 14 V/100 A alternator. - **Verdict: the ultracapacitor is adequately sized** for typical start–stop operation and could even support **2–3 consecutive crank events** before recharging. > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes:** > - Using $E = V \cdot I \cdot t = 2.5 \times 300 \times 1.5 = 1\,125$ J — this is **wrong** because the voltage is not constant; it falls during discharge. The correct formula is $\tfrac{1}{2} C (V_0^2 - V_f^2)$. > - Forgetting to compute the intermediate voltage $V_f$ before computing the recharge charge. > - Confusing "recover to 2.0 V" with "recover to *full charge*". --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ultra Capacitors (Module 4, OEMUT721)** - **Definition:** EDLC stores energy *electrostatically* in the **electric double layer** at the electrode/electrolyte interface; no faradaic reactions. - **Three-layer model:** Helmholtz + Gouy–Chapman = **Stern model** for the EDL. - **Key capacitance formula:** $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$, with $d \approx 0.3 - 0.8$ nm → very high $C$ per unit area. - **Energy:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ → practical unit $E_{Wh} = CV^2 / 7\,200$. - **Power (matched):** $P_{max} = V^2 / (4 R_{ESR})$. - **Cell voltage limit:** 2.5 – 2.7 V (organic) / 1 V (aqueous). Series stacking is mandatory for HV buses. - **Series pack:** $V_{pack} = N_s V_{cell}$, $C_{pack} = C_{cell}/N_s$, $R_{ESR,pack} = N_s R_{ESR,cell}$. - **Parallel pack:** $C_{pack} = N_p C_{cell}$, $V_{pack}$ unchanged, $R_{ESR,pack} = R_{ESR,cell}/N_p$. - **Time constant:** $\tau = R_{ESR} C$ → small $\tau$ → fast discharge (good for EV peaks). - **Voltage window for cycling:** keep $V \in [\tfrac{1}{2} V_{max}, V_{max}]$ to use ~ 75 % of stored energy. - **Ragone plot:** Ultracapacitors → right (high power), batteries → top-left (high energy), HESS shifts the system point upper-right. - **EV applications:** - Regenerative braking (recovers 30 – 40 % of braking energy that the battery would otherwise waste). - Acceleration boost (peak-power assist). - Cold-start assist at low temperatures. - DC-bus voltage stabilisation in fuel-cell vehicles. - 12 V / 48 V micro-hybrid start–stop systems. - **Numerical values to memorise:** - BET surface area of activated carbon: **1 500 – 3 000 m²/g** - Specific energy: **5 – 10 Wh/kg** - Specific power: **5 000 – 15 000 W/kg** - Cycle life: **> 500 000** - Round-trip efficiency: **90 – 98 %** - Operating temperature: **−40 °C to +65 °C** - **Three types:** (i) EDLC (carbon), (ii) Pseudocapacitor (metal oxides/polymers), (iii) Hybrid / Li-ion capacitor. - **Comparison with batteries:** ultracapacitors win on **power, cycle life, temperature, charging speed**; lose on **energy density, self-discharge, cell voltage**. - **Equivalent circuit:** series $R_{ESR}$ + bulk $C$ + parallel leakage $R_p$. - **Self-discharge:** 5 – 10 % in first 24 h, then much slower — important for long-term EV storage. - **SOC estimation:** linear with voltage for ultracapacitors (vs. highly non-linear for batteries) — a major BMS simplification. - **Environmental:** carbon-based, RoHS-compliant, no heavy metals, no thermal-runaway risk. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute mnemonic for the EDL theory: "HGS-D"** → **H**elmholtz, **G**ouy–Chapman, **S**tern, **D**ebye length. Use it to remember the order of ion-layer models in the double layer.

topics.title: Flywheel Based Energy Storage and Its Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flywheel Based Energy Storage and Its Analysis

# Module 4 — Flywheel Based Energy Storage and Its Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS):** A kinetic energy storage device that stores mechanical energy in the form of rotational kinetic energy of a spinning mass (rotor). In Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (EVs/HEVs), the FESS functions as a **mechanical buffer (auxiliary energy storage)** that absorbs kinetic energy during regenerative braking and re-delivers it during acceleration, complementing the primary electrochemical battery.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for OEMUT721, the flywheel is treated as a **mechanical battery** — a reversible, high-power, short-to-medium duration energy reservoir governed by Newton's second law for rotation and the work–energy theorem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Spinning Top Analogy:** Imagine a child's spinning top. While spinning, it stores energy in its rotation. Push it gently (add energy) → it spins faster. Friction gradually slows it (energy dissipates) → it wobbles and falls. A flywheel in an EV behaves identically, but in a precisely engineered, vacuum-housed, magnetically suspended carbon-fiber rotor spinning at **20,000–60,000 RPM**.

- **Real-life vehicle parallel:** A flywheel in a Formula-1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) spins up during braking at ~**40,000 rpm**, stores ~**400 kJ**, and dumps it back through an MGU (Motor-Generator Unit) for a 6.67 s boost of ~**60 kW**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **standardized in KTU board solutions** for flywheel calculations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of advanced composite | $\rho$ | **1500–2000 kg/m³** (carbon fiber) |
| Density of steel | $\rho_{steel}$ | **7850 kg/m³** |
| Maximum allowable hoop stress | $\sigma_{max}$ | **700–1000 MPa** (composite) |
| Specific energy (advanced FESS) | $e_{sp}$ | **100–200 Wh/kg** (theoretical) |
| Vacuum pressure | $P_{vac}$ | **< 10⁻³ Pa** |
| Typical specific power | $p_{sp}$ | **5–10 kW/kg** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever a problem does not specify the material, default to a *thin-rim solid disc* or a *constant-stress composite disc* as the geometry. Always state the assumption explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

A Flywheel Energy Storage System in an EV powertrain operates through four distinct logical stages:

1. **Charging (Energy Intake):** During regenerative braking or engine-off coasting, the electric machine acts as a **generator**. The generated electrical energy drives a **motor–generator unit (MGU)** mechanically coupled to the flywheel rotor, accelerating it from a lower speed $\omega_{min}$ to a higher speed $\omega_{max}$.
2. **Storage (Kinetic Retention):** The rotor stores energy in the form of rotational kinetic energy. To minimize self-discharge, modern FESS uses:
   - **Magnetic bearings** (no contact friction)
   - **Vacuum housing** (no air drag)
   - **Permanent magnet or reluctance machine** (no brush friction)
3. **Holding (Standby):** At constant speed, stored energy remains essentially constant. The only losses are **windage, bearing drag, and eddy-current losses**, typically **< 1–3 % per hour** in high-end FESS.
4. **Discharging (Energy Release):** During acceleration, the MGU operates as a **motor**, drawing kinetic energy from the decelerating flywheel. The rotor speed drops from $\omega_{max}$ to $\omega_{min}$, and the released energy is supplied to the traction motor.

### 2.2 Why and How the Flywheel Works in an HEV

- **Why:** Batteries have **low specific power** (typically 200–500 W/kg) and degrade under high C-rate discharge. The flywheel offers **specific power 5–10 kW/kg** — perfect for transient power demands.
- **How:** Energy is stored in the **moment of inertia ($I$)** and the **angular velocity ($\omega$)** of a rotating mass. The relation $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ shows that energy scales **quadratically with speed** and **linearly with mass distribution**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU Board Examination — Memorize These.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stored kinetic energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | J (joule) | Core energy equation |
| 2 | Energy in terms of linear rim speed | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | J | Where $v = r\omega$ |
| 3 | Moment of inertia — thin rim | $I = m r^{2}$ | kg·m² | All mass at radius $r$ |
| 4 | Moment of inertia — solid disc | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ | kg·m² | Uniform disc |
| 5 | Moment of inertia — solid cylinder | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m (r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2})$ | kg·m² | Hollow disc |
| 6 | Usable energy (between $\omega_{min}$ and $\omega_{max}$) | $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2})$ | J | Practical discharge |
| 7 | Energy density (volumetric) | $u = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho r^{2} \omega^{2}$ | J/m³ | For solid disc |
| 8 | Specific energy | $e_{sp} = E/m$ | J/kg or Wh/kg | Divide by 3600 for Wh/kg |
| 9 | Hoop stress — thin rim | $\sigma_{h} = \rho v^{2} = \rho r^{2} \omega^{2}$ | Pa | Critical design constraint |
| 10 | Maximum allowable rim speed (steel) | $v_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{max}/\rho}$ | m/s | ~ 490 m/s for steel |
| 11 | Power output (during discharge) | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | W | Where $T$ is torque |
| 12 | Discharge time | $t_{d} = \Delta E / P_{avg}$ | s | Energy / average power |
| 13 | Energy stored in solid disc (volumetric) | $E = \tfrac{1}{4} \pi \rho L r^{4} \omega^{2}$ | J | $L$ = length |
| 14 | Shape factor for constant-stress disc | $K_{s} = 0.606$ | dimensionless | Optimal geometry |
| 15 | Specific energy of constant-stress disc | $e_{sp,max} = K_{s} \cdot \sigma_{max}/\rho$ | J/kg | Theoretical maximum |

> **Note on Notation:** All vertical bars in the above table for absolute value are rendered using `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser intact.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Formula-1 KERS / MGU-K:** First commercial FESS in motorsport, recovering ~**2 MJ per lap**.
- **Bus & Tram Regenerative Braking:** GKN Gyrodrive and similar systems store ~**0.5–1 kWh** mechanically.
- **Grid Frequency Regulation:** Beacon Power (USA) used **200 kWh** flywheels for utility-scale storage.
- **Satellite Attitude Control:** Reaction wheels / control moment gyroscopes use the same FESS principle.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** Active Power, Vycon systems use FESS for ride-through.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Kinetic Energy Stored in a Rotating Body

**Statement:** Derive the expression for kinetic energy stored in a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ about a fixed axis.

**Starting Point:** A rigid body consists of $n$ elementary mass particles $m_{1}, m_{2}, \ldots, m_{n}$, each located at perpendicular distances $r_{1}, r_{2}, \ldots, r_{n}$ from the axis of rotation.

**Step 1:** The linear velocity of the $i^{th}$ particle is given by the rigid-body kinematic relation:

$$v_{i} = r_{i}\,\omega$$

**Step 2:** The kinetic energy of the $i^{th}$ particle (translational form) is:

$$KE_{i} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{i} v_{i}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $v_{i} = r_{i}\,\omega$:

$$KE_{i} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{i} (r_{i}\,\omega)^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{i} r_{i}^{2} \omega^{2}$$

**Step 4:** The total kinetic energy of the rotating body is the sum over all particles:

$$E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} KE_{i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tfrac{1}{2} m_{i} r_{i}^{2} \omega^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Since $\omega$ is common to all particles, factor it out:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} r_{i}^{2} \right) \omega^{2}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the bracketed sum as the **mass moment of inertia** about the axis of rotation:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} r_{i}^{2}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, the standard result is:

$$\boxed{E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The derivation transforms a discrete sum of particle kinetic energies into the continuous rotational form by recognizing that the sum of $m_{i} r_{i}^{2}$ is the *definition* of moment of inertia.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Rim Flywheel

**Statement:** Derive the expression for hoop (tensile) stress in a thin cylindrical rim rotating at angular velocity $\omega$.

**Assumption:** Rim thickness $t \ll r$, and all mass is concentrated at radius $r$.

**Step 1:** Consider a small element of the rim subtending an angle $\Delta\theta$ at the center. The arc length is $r\Delta\theta$, and the cross-sectional area is $A = t \cdot w$ (where $w$ is axial width).

**Step 2:** The mass of the element is:

$$dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot r \Delta\theta$$

**Step 3:** The element travels in a circle of radius $r$ at speed $v = r\omega$. The required centripetal force is:

$$dF_{c} = dm \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{r} = \rho A r \Delta\theta \cdot \frac{r^{2}\omega^{2}}{r} = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2} \, \Delta\theta$$

**Step 4:** This centripetal force is supplied by the **tangential tension** $T$ acting on the two cut faces. The radial component of $2T$ over the angle $\Delta\theta$ balances the centripetal force:

$$2T \sin(\Delta\theta/2) \approx T \Delta\theta = dF_{c}$$

**Step 5:** Equating both sides:

$$T \Delta\theta = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2} \Delta\theta$$

**Step 6:** Therefore the tangential tension is:

$$T = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

**Step 7:** The hoop stress is tension per unit area, $\sigma_{h} = T/A$:

$$\sigma_{h} = \rho r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

**Step 8:** Since $v = r\omega$, this can be written compactly as:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{h} = \rho v^{2}}$$

> **Physical Insight:** The hoop stress depends *only* on the material density and the square of the rim speed — it is **independent of the radius**. This is why modern flywheels use *low-density* composites (carbon fiber) and *high rim speeds* (~ 600–1000 m/s).

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Maximum Specific Energy of a Constant-Stress Disc

**Statement:** Derive the maximum specific energy of a flywheel disc whose radial stress is constant and equal to the allowable material stress $\sigma_{max}$ at every radius.

**Step 1:** For a thin annular ring at radius $r$, width $dr$, and thickness $t$ (out-of-plane), the hoop-stress equilibrium of a constant-stress disc yields:

$$\sigma_{\theta}(r) = \rho \omega^{2} \int_{r}^{r_{o}} \frac{r}{t} \, dr = \sigma_{max}$$

**Step 2:** To keep stress constant, the disc thickness must **vary with radius**. Solving the equilibrium for a constant $\sigma_{max}$ gives the optimal thickness profile:

$$t(r) = t_{o} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{\rho \omega^{2}}{2 \sigma_{max}}\,(r_{o}^{2} - r^{2})\right]$$

**Step 3:** The total mass of the disc is:

$$m = 2\pi \rho \int_{0}^{r_{o}} r \cdot t(r) \, dr$$

**Step 4:** The kinetic energy of the variable-thickness disc is:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} \int \omega^{2} r^{2} \, dm = \pi \rho \omega^{2} \int_{0}^{r_{o}} r^{3} t(r) \, dr$$

**Step 5:** The **shape factor** $K_{s}$ is defined as the ratio of specific energy to the material limit $\sigma_{max}/\rho$:

$$K_{s} = \frac{E / m}{\sigma_{max} / \rho}$$

**Step 6:** For the constant-stress profile, integrating and simplifying yields the classical result:

$$K_{s} = 0.606$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, the theoretical maximum specific energy of an optimally shaped flywheel is:

$$\boxed{e_{sp,max} = \frac{K_{s}\,\sigma_{max}}{\rho} = 0.606 \cdot \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This result means even with perfect material, only ~60.6 % of the *stress-to-density* limit can be harnessed geometrically. The remainder is lost in stress distribution inefficiencies.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Geometry Performance

| Geometry | $I$ (kg·m²) | $e_{sp}$ (J/kg) | $K_{s}$ | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thin rim (all mass at $r$) | $m r^{2}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}$ | **1.000** | Reference upper bound |
| Solid uniform disc | $\tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ | $\tfrac{1}{4} v^{2}$ | **0.500** | Simple rotors |
| Solid uniform cylinder ($L = 2r$) | $\tfrac{5}{4} m r^{2}$ | $\tfrac{5}{8} v^{2}$ | **0.625** | Realistic rotors |
| Constant-stress disc | — | $0.606 \cdot \sigma_{max}/\rho$ | **0.606** | Optimal composite |
| Constant-stress thin rim | — | $\sigma_{max}/\rho$ | **1.000** | Theoretical peak |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FESS Architecture in a Hybrid Electric Vehicle — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brake Pedal / Accelerator] --> B{Vehicle Control Unit}
    B --> C[Power Electronics Inverter]
    C --> D[Motor-Generator Unit MGU]
    D --> E[Vacuum Housing]
    E --> F[Flywheel Rotor Composite]
    F --> D
    D --> C
    C --> G[Traction Motor]
    G --> H[Driveline and Wheels]
    I[Primary Battery Pack] --> C
    C --> I
    J[Magnetic Bearings] --> F
    K[Vacuum Pump] --> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Energy flows bidirectionally between the **MGU** and the **Flywheel Rotor** through a common shaft. During braking, kinetic energy from the wheels flows *up* through the driveline → MGU (acting as generator) → Flywheel (spins up). During acceleration, the flywheel spins down, MGU acts as motor, and energy flows *down* to the wheels. The primary battery acts in parallel for long-duration storage.

---

### 4.2 FESS Internal Sub-System Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RotationalAssembly [Rotational Assembly]
        R1[Spinning Composite Rotor]
        R2[Hub and Shaft]
        R3[Permanent Magnet Ring]
    end
    subgraph BearingSystem [Bearing and Containment]
        B1[Active Magnetic Bearings]
        B2[Touchdown Backup Bearings]
        B3[Vacuum Chamber Shell]
    end
    subgraph ConversionSystem [Energy Conversion]
        C1[Stator Windings]
        C2[IGBT Inverter]
        C3[DC Link Capacitor]
    end
    subgraph ControlSystem [Supervisory Control]
        S1[Rotor Position Sensor]
        S2[Speed Encoder]
        S3[ECU and CAN Bus]
    end
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    R2 --> B1
    R1 --> B3
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> C2
```

> **Insight:** The diagram reveals the **four sub-systems** of a complete FESS — Rotational, Bearing, Conversion, and Control. Each must be analyzed separately in a KTU design question.

---

### 4.3 Energy Flow State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Charging: Brake ON, SOC low
    Charging --> Holding: Speed reaches omega_max
    Holding --> Discharging: Accelerator ON
    Discharging --> Holding: Speed drops to omega_min
    Holding --> Idle: Long standby
    Charging --> Idle: Emergency stop
    Discharging --> Idle: Emergency stop
```

> **Reading the State Machine:** The flywheel has **four operational states** — Idle (stopped or coasting), Charging (accelerating up), Holding (constant speed, ready), and Discharging (decelerating, releasing energy). The transitions are controlled by the vehicle ECU based on driver input and battery SOC.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. Define Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS) and list its main components in a hybrid electric vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**
A Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS) is a kinetic energy storage device that stores energy in the rotational motion of a high-inertia rotor. The main components are:

1. **Composite rotor** — stores kinetic energy.
2. **Motor–Generator Unit (MGU)** — bidirectional energy conversion.
3. **Power electronic inverter** — controls the MGU.
4. **Magnetic bearings** — frictionless support.
5. **Vacuum housing** — minimizes air-drag losses.
6. **Supervisory controller** — manages state transitions.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Any 4 components (2 marks = 0.5 each).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q2. State any three advantages of using a flywheel over a chemical battery for short-term energy storage in HEVs.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher specific power** (5–10 kW/kg vs 0.2–0.5 kW/kg for batteries) — ideal for acceleration bursts.
2. **Long cycle life** — flywheels can sustain > 10⁶ charge–discharge cycles with negligible degradation, unlike Li-ion batteries (~ 1000–3000 cycles).
3. **Faster charge/discharge response** — sub-second response enables regenerative braking at high deceleration rates.
4. *(Bonus point)* Wide operating temperature range; no thermal runaway risk.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per advantage, 3 of 4 to be written.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, ESE Pattern – Internal Choice Provided]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** A solid steel flywheel disc has a mass of **40 kg**, outer radius **0.25 m**, and rotates at a maximum speed of **12,000 rpm**. Determine:
> - (i) the moment of inertia of the disc,
> - (ii) the maximum kinetic energy stored, and
> - (iii) the hoop stress in the rim. Use $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³. (7 marks)

> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS) in a hybrid electric vehicle. Discuss the role of the Motor-Generator Unit (MGU) and power electronic inverter. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:** $m = 40$ kg, $r = 0.25$ m, $N = 12{,}000$ rpm, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.

**(i) Moment of inertia of the solid disc:**

The formula for a uniform solid disc is $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$.

$$I = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 40 \times (0.25)^{2}$$

$$I = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 40 \times 0.0625$$

$$I = 1.25 \;\text{kg·m}^{2}$$

**(ii) Maximum kinetic energy stored:**

First, convert the rotational speed to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 12{,}000}{60} = 400\pi \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega \approx 1256.64 \;\text{rad/s}$$

Apply the energy equation $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.25 \times (1256.64)^{2}$$

$$E = 0.625 \times 1{,}579{,}136.4$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 9.87 \times 10^{5} \;\text{J} = 987 \;\text{kJ}}$$

**(iii) Hoop stress in the rim:**

Use the thin-rim approximation $\sigma_{h} = \rho v^{2}$, where $v = r\omega$.

Compute rim velocity:

$$v = r \omega = 0.25 \times 1256.64 = 314.16 \;\text{m/s}$$

Compute hoop stress:

$$\sigma_{h} = 7850 \times (314.16)^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{h} = 7850 \times 98{,}696$$

$$\sigma_{h} \approx 7.75 \times 10^{8} \;\text{Pa} = 775 \;\text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{h} \approx 775 \;\text{MPa}}$$

> **Comment:** This stress exceeds the typical yield strength of standard steel (~ 250–400 MPa). Therefore, a *solid steel disc* is **not safe** at 12,000 rpm; a composite rotor or lower speed is required.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Working of FESS in an HEV (Block Diagram Description):**

The FESS in an HEV works on the principle of bidirectional kinetic energy transfer between the vehicle driveline and a high-inertia rotor through an MGU.

**Block Diagram (text representation for answer sheet):**

```
[Wheels] ↔ [Driveline] ↔ [MGU] ↔ [Flywheel Rotor]
                    ↕
            [Power Inverter] ↔ [DC Bus] ↔ [Battery]
                    ↕
              [Vehicle ECU]
```

**Operating Modes:**

1. **Charging (Regenerative Braking):** The vehicle's kinetic energy drives the MGU as a generator. The inverter rectifies the AC output to DC, which is then fed to the MGU (now acting as a motor) to accelerate the flywheel rotor from $\omega_{min}$ to $\omega_{max}$.

2. **Holding:** The rotor spins at constant speed in a vacuum. Only minimal losses occur via magnetic bearing drag and residual windage.

3. **Discharging (Acceleration Assist):** The MGU acts as a generator, converting the flywheel's kinetic energy to electrical energy. The inverter conditions this power and feeds it to the DC bus, where the traction motor uses it to drive the wheels.

**Role of the MGU:** The MGU is a single electrical machine that functions as a motor (during charging) and a generator (during discharging). It is mechanically coupled to the flywheel shaft and electrically connected to the inverter.

**Role of the Power Inverter:** The inverter performs bidirectional AC–DC conversion. During charging, it acts as an inverter (DC→AC) to drive the MGU as a motor. During discharging, it acts as a rectifier (AC→DC) to deliver the generated power to the DC bus. It also regulates voltage, current, and switching frequency based on ECU commands.

> **Mark Distribution:** Diagram (2 marks) + 3 modes explained (3 marks) + MGU role (1 mark) + Inverter role (1 mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** A composite-material flywheel rotor (constant-stress disc profile) is designed with maximum allowable hoop stress $\sigma_{max} = 900$ MPa and material density $\rho = 1600$ kg/m³. Determine:
> - (i) the theoretical maximum specific energy in Wh/kg, and
> - (ii) the maximum permissible rim speed. (7 marks)

> **(b)** Derive the expression for the kinetic energy stored in a rotating rigid body, starting from the definition of kinetic energy of a particle. Explain how this relation leads to the design equation for the usable energy in a flywheel that discharges from $\omega_{max}$ to $\omega_{min}$. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:** $\sigma_{max} = 900$ MPa $= 900 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, $\rho = 1600$ kg/m³.

**(i) Theoretical maximum specific energy:**

Use the constant-stress disc relation $e_{sp,max} = 0.606 \cdot \sigma_{max}/\rho$:

$$e_{sp,max} = 0.606 \times \frac{900 \times 10^{6}}{1600}$$

$$e_{sp,max} = 0.606 \times 562{,}500$$

$$e_{sp,max} = 340{,}875 \;\text{J/kg}$$

Convert to Wh/kg (1 Wh = 3600 J):

$$e_{sp,max} = \frac{340{,}875}{3600}$$

$$\boxed{e_{sp,max} \approx 94.7 \;\text{Wh/kg}}$$

**(ii) Maximum permissible rim speed:**

For a thin-rim, the rim-speed limit comes from $\sigma_{max} = \rho v_{max}^{2}$:

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho}}$$

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{900 \times 10^{6}}{1600}}$$

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{562{,}500}$$

$$v_{max} = 750 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{max} = 750 \;\text{m/s}}$$

> **Comment:** This corresponds to a rotor speed of $n = 60 \cdot v_{max} / (2\pi r)$. For $r = 0.15$ m, $n \approx 47,746$ rpm — a typical operating point for an advanced composite FESS.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Derivation of Rotational Kinetic Energy:**

Consider a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis with angular velocity $\omega$. Let it consist of $n$ particles of masses $m_{1}, m_{2}, \ldots, m_{n}$ at perpendicular distances $r_{1}, r_{2}, \ldots, r_{n}$ from the axis.

The linear velocity of the $i^{th}$ particle is $v_{i} = r_{i}\omega$.

The kinetic energy of the $i^{th}$ particle is:

$$KE_{i} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{i} v_{i}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{i} r_{i}^{2} \omega^{2}$$

Total kinetic energy of the body is:

$$E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} KE_{i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tfrac{1}{2} m_{i} r_{i}^{2} \omega^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \omega^{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} r_{i}^{2}$$

Define moment of inertia $I = \sum m_{i} r_{i}^{2}$:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$$

**Design Equation for Usable Energy:**

In practical operation, a flywheel cannot be discharged to zero speed because the MGU has a minimum operating speed $\omega_{min}$. Therefore, the **usable energy** between full-charge and full-discharge states is:

$$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{min}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2}\right)}$$

> **Design Insight:** A 2:1 speed ratio ($\omega_{max}/\omega_{min} = 2$) extracts only 75 % of the maximum energy. A 3:1 ratio extracts ~ 89 %. Designers must balance the higher mechanical stress at higher $\omega_{max}$ against the increased energy extraction.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in FESS Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 mark):** Always convert RPM → rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Writing $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I N^{2}$ without conversion is the *most common* error.
> 2. **Forgetting the ½ factor (–1 mark):** A surprising number of students write $E = I\omega^2$ instead of $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I\omega^2$. KTU examiners deduct a full mark for this.
> 3. **Geometry mix-up (–1 mark):** Mixing up the moment of inertia of a *thin rim* ($I = mr^2$) with a *solid disc* ($I = \tfrac{1}{2} mr^2$) costs marks. Always state the assumed geometry explicitly.
> 4. **Hoop stress formula misuse (–1 to 2 marks):** Students often write $\sigma = \rho r \omega^{2}$ (missing a factor of $r$). Correct: $\sigma = \rho r^{2} \omega^{2}$.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions (–0.5 to 1 mark):** KTU rubric explicitly awards marks for "stated assumptions" — always mention whether the rim is thin, the disc is uniform, the speed range, etc.
> 6. **Skipping the Wh/kg conversion (–0.5 mark):** If the question asks for specific energy in Wh/kg, *do the conversion* — $1 \;\text{Wh} = 3600 \;\text{J}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Flywheel Energy Storage**

- [x] **Core energy equation:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ — derive from particle kinetic energy.
- [x] **Usable energy equation:** $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2})$.
- [x] **Moment of inertia for thin rim:** $I = mr^2$.
- [x] **Moment of inertia for solid disc:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2} mr^2$.
- [x] **Hoop stress for thin rim:** $\sigma_{h} = \rho r^{2} \omega^{2} = \rho v^{2}$.
- [x] **Maximum rim speed:** $v_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{max}/\rho}$.
- [x] **Specific energy of constant-stress disc:** $e_{sp,max} = 0.606 \cdot \sigma_{max}/\rho$.
- [x] **Specific energy of thin rim:** $e_{sp} = \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma_{max}/\rho$.
- [x] **Components of FESS:** Rotor, MGU, Inverter, Magnetic Bearings, Vacuum Housing, ECU.
- [x] **FESS vs Battery:** Higher specific power (5–10 kW/kg), longer cycle life (>10⁶), faster response, no thermal runaway.
- [x] **Energy in Wh/kg:** Always divide J/kg by 3600.
- [x] **RPM → rad/s:** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
- [x] **State machine:** Idle ↔ Charging ↔ Holding ↔ Discharging.
- [x] **Real-world examples:** F1 KERS, GKN Gyrodrive, Beacon Power grid flywheels, satellite reaction wheels.
- [x] **Key design trade-off:** Higher $\omega_{max}$ → more energy but more stress; constant-stress geometry maximizes $K_{s} = 0.606$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** Memorize the **five golden equations** — $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$, $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}I(\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2)$, $\sigma_h = \rho v^2$, $v_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{max}/\rho}$, $e_{sp,max} = 0.606\,\sigma_{max}/\rho$. These five equations cover > 90 % of KTU board questions on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT721 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flywheel Accumulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flywheel Accumulator** (also known as an **Electromechanical Battery** or **Kinetic Energy Storage System — KESS**) is a mechanical energy storage device that stores rotational kinetic energy in a spinning mass (the rotor/flywheel) and releases it on demand by decelerating the rotor through a coupled generator or drivetrain. In Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (EHVs), flywheel accumulators function as **auxiliary high-power, short-duration energy buffers** that complement primary electrochemical batteries and ultra-capacitors, particularly during transient peak power demands such as hard acceleration and regenerative braking events.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Flywheel accumulators belong to the family of **"Non-Chemical Energy Storage Systems"** in the broader KTU classification of EV energy storage. The topic bridges mechanical engineering (rotational dynamics) with electrical engineering (machine-generator interface).

The fundamental governing equation for the stored energy is:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,J\,\omega^{2}$$

where:
- $E$ = stored kinetic energy in Joules ($\text{J}$)
- $J$ = mass moment of inertia of the rotor in $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$
- $\omega$ = angular velocity in $\text{rad/s}$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a flywheel accumulator as the "Mechanical Capacitor" of a vehicle.**
>
> Just like an **electrical capacitor** stores energy briefly in an electric field and releases it as a sudden burst of current, a **flywheel** stores energy briefly as spinning kinetic motion and releases it as a sudden burst of torque. A traditional **battery** is like a *water tank* — slow to fill and slow to drain. A flywheel is more like a *whirling bucket of water on a string* — fill it quickly (charge by spinning up) and you can extract the water in a powerful short burst (discharge by slowing down).

**Real-world analogy:** A spinning pottery wheel in a workshop or the heavy metal disc in a child's *spinning top* demonstrates the basic principle. The faster the spin, the more energy is locked up in the rotational motion, ready to be tapped instantly.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering metrics characterize a flywheel accumulator:

- **Specific Energy** — energy stored per unit mass: typically **5–100 Wh/kg** (modern composite rotors reach the upper end).
- **Energy Density** — energy stored per unit volume: typically **20–300 Wh/L**.
- **Specific Power** — power delivery per unit mass: typically **1000–5000 W/kg** — far exceeding batteries.
- **Cycle Life** — virtually **unlimited** (mechanical wear is the only limit, not chemistry).
- **Self-Discharge Rate** — parasitic friction losses, typically **1–10% per hour** in vacuum-housed systems.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency** — **80–95%** depending on bearing and vacuum technology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in EVs:** Although batteries have higher *specific energy* (longer range), flywheels have far higher *specific power* (faster acceleration). This is why flywheels are studied as a *complement* to batteries, not a replacement in modern passenger EVs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Breakdown

A flywheel accumulator in an EHV operates through five discrete phases:

1. **Charging Phase (Spin-Up):**
   - An electric motor (often the same traction motor acting as a motor) accelerates the flywheel rotor.
   - Power flows *from* the primary battery *to* the flywheel, increasing $\omega$.
   - Energy stored increases as the **square** of $\omega$ (a doubling of speed quadruples stored energy).

2. **Holding Phase (Cruising):**
   - The rotor spins at near-constant speed in a low-friction environment (magnetic or active magnetic bearings + vacuum housing).
   - Self-discharge is minimized; standby energy is preserved.

3. **Discharging Phase (Spin-Down / Boost):**
   - The flywheel acts as a prime mover, coupled to a generator that produces electrical energy.
   - This energy is fed back into the DC bus to assist traction during acceleration peaks (boost mode).
   - Rotor speed decreases; stored energy is released.

4. **Regenerative Braking Phase (Spin-Up):**
   - During deceleration, kinetic energy from the wheels drives the flywheel motor in generator mode.
   - The flywheel absorbs braking energy that the main battery cannot accept quickly enough.

5. **Stopping / Parking Phase:**
   - The rotor is brought to rest through controlled electromagnetic braking.
   - Energy is dissipated as heat or returned to the battery.

### 2.2 Moment of Inertia — Geometry-Dependent Formulations

The moment of inertia $J$ depends on the rotor's geometry and mass distribution. For a rotor of mass $m$ and characteristic dimension $r$, the general form is:

$$J = k\,m\,r^{2}$$

where $k$ is the **geometric form factor** (dimensionless).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks / Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stored Kinetic Energy | $E$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} J \omega^{2}$ | $\text{J}$ | Master equation of flywheel storage |
| Mass Moment of Inertia (Solid Disc) | $J_{\text{solid}}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$ | Uniform solid cylinder rotor |
| Mass Moment of Inertia (Hollow Cylinder) | $J_{\text{hollow}}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} m \left(r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}\right)$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$ | Rim-type rotor; thin-rim limit $r_{o} \approx r_{i}$ |
| Mass Moment of Inertia (Thin Rim) | $J_{\text{rim}}$ | $m r^{2}$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$ | All mass concentrated at radius $r$ |
| Specific Energy | $e$ | $\tfrac{E}{m}$ | $\text{J/kg}$ or $\text{Wh/kg}$ | $1\ \text{Wh} = 3600\ \text{J}$ |
| Energy Density | $\rho_{E}$ | $\tfrac{E}{\text{Volume}}$ | $\text{J/m}^{3}$ or $\text{Wh/L}$ | Volume-based efficiency metric |
| Power (Instantaneous Discharge) | $P$ | $J\,\omega\,\alpha = \tau\,\omega$ | $\text{W}$ | $\alpha$ = angular acceleration, $\tau$ = torque |
| Hoop Stress Limit | $\sigma_{h}$ | $\rho\,v^{2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Material tensile-strength limit sets max $v$ |
| Tip Speed | $v_{\text{tip}}$ | $r\,\omega$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Typical max: 600–1000 m/s for composites |
| Discharge Time | $t_{d}$ | $\tfrac{\Delta E}{P_{\text{avg}}}$ | $\text{s}$ | Useful for sizing peak power duration |
| Charge/Discharge Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\tfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | \% | Typically 80–95\% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Always convert energy between Joules and Watt-hours using $1\ \text{Wh} = 3600\ \text{J}$. A common error is mixing units of $J$ in $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$ with energy in $\text{Wh}$ without conversion.

### 2.4 Why Flywheels? Engineering Utility in EHVs

- **Peak Power Buffering:** Protects battery from high C-rate stress during launch.
- **Regenerative Braking Enhancement:** Absorbs rapid brake pulses that exceed battery acceptance.
- **Range Extension in Stop-and-Go:** Stores kinetic energy during braking and reuses it for the next launch.
- **Infinite Cycle Life:** No chemical degradation — only mechanical wear to manage.
- **Rapid Charge/Discharge:** Full energy exchange in seconds vs. minutes for batteries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stored Energy of a Rotating Flywheel

**Starting Point:** A particle of mass $m_{p}$ moving in a circle of radius $r$ at angular velocity $\omega$ has linear tangential velocity:

$$v = r\,\omega$$

Its linear kinetic energy is:

$$E_{\text{linear}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{p} v^{2}$$

Substituting $v = r\omega$:

$$E_{\text{linear}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{p} (r\omega)^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{p} r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

For a rigid body composed of many particles, total kinetic energy is the sum:

$$E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tfrac{1}{2} m_{p,i} r_{i}^{2} \omega^{2}$$

Since $\omega$ is common (rigid body), factor it out:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\omega^{2}\,\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{p,i}\,r_{i}^{2}$$

By definition, the mass moment of inertia is:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{p,i}\,r_{i}^{2}$$

Therefore, the master equation is:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,J\,\omega^{2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Moment of Inertia of a Solid Uniform Disc

Consider a solid disc of mass $m$, outer radius $r_{o}$, inner radius $r_{i} = 0$, and uniform thickness $t$. Let surface mass density be:

$$\sigma = \frac{m}{\pi r_{o}^{2}}$$

A differential ring element at radius $r$ with width $\mathrm{d}r$ has area:

$$\mathrm{d}A = 2\pi r\,\mathrm{d}r$$

Its mass is:

$$\mathrm{d}m = \sigma\,\mathrm{d}A = 2\pi\sigma\,r\,\mathrm{d}r$$

Its contribution to the moment of inertia is:

$$\mathrm{d}J = r^{2}\,\mathrm{d}m = 2\pi\sigma\,r^{3}\,\mathrm{d}r$$

Integrate from $0$ to $r_{o}$:

$$J = \int_{0}^{r_{o}} 2\pi\sigma\,r^{3}\,\mathrm{d}r = 2\pi\sigma \left[\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{r_{o}} = \frac{\pi\sigma\,r_{o}^{4}}{2}$$

Substitute $\sigma = m/(\pi r_{o}^{2})$:

$$J = \frac{\pi\,r_{o}^{4}}{2}\cdot\frac{m}{\pi r_{o}^{2}} = \frac{m r_{o}^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,J_{\text{solid}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,r_{o}^{2}\,} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Moment of Inertia of a Hollow Cylinder (Rotor)

For a hollow cylindrical rotor with outer radius $r_{o}$ and inner radius $r_{i}$, integrate the same differential ring between the two radii:

$$J = \int_{r_{i}}^{r_{o}} 2\pi\sigma\,r^{3}\,\mathrm{d}r = \frac{\pi\sigma}{2}\left(r_{o}^{4} - r_{i}^{4}\right)$$

Substituting $\sigma = m/\left[\pi\left(r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}\right)\right]$:

$$J = \frac{\pi\left(r_{o}^{4} - r_{i}^{4}\right)}{2}\cdot\frac{m}{\pi\left(r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}\right)} = \frac{m}{2}\cdot\frac{r_{o}^{4} - r_{i}^{4}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}}$$

Use the algebraic identity $a^{4} - b^{4} = (a^{2} - b^{2})(a^{2} + b^{2})$:

$$\boxed{\,J_{\text{hollow}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\left(r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}\right)\,} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Limit check:** If $r_{i} = 0$, this reduces to the solid disc case. If $r_{i} \to r_{o}$ (thin rim), it reduces to $J_{\text{rim}} = m r^{2}$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hoop Stress in a Spinning Rim

A thin rim spinning at angular velocity $\omega$ experiences centrifugal force. Consider a small segment subtending angle $\mathrm{d}\theta$. The mass of the segment is:

$$\mathrm{d}m = \frac{m}{2\pi}\,\mathrm{d}\theta$$

The centripetal force required for this segment is:

$$\mathrm{d}F = \mathrm{d}m \cdot r\omega^{2} = \frac{m r \omega^{2}}{2\pi}\,\mathrm{d}\theta$$

The hoop tension $T$ in the rim balances the radial component. By symmetry, integrating the radial components:

$$2T\,\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\mathrm{d}\theta}{2}\right) \approx T\,\mathrm{d}\theta = \mathrm{d}F$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{m r \omega^{2}}{2\pi}$$

The hoop stress in a thin rim of cross-sectional area $A$ is:

$$\sigma = \frac{T}{A} = \frac{m r \omega^{2}}{2\pi A}$$

Since volume $V = 2\pi r A$ and density $\rho = m/V = m/(2\pi r A)$:

$$m = 2\pi r A \rho$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma = \frac{(2\pi r A \rho)\, r\, \omega^{2}}{2\pi A} = \rho r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

In terms of tip speed $v = r\omega$:

$$\boxed{\,\sigma = \rho\,v^{2}\,} \qquad \blacksquare$$

This is the **fundamental material-strength-limited design equation** for flywheels. The maximum permissible tip speed is set by the tensile strength of the rim material divided by its density:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{\text{ult}}}{\rho_{\text{sf}}}} \qquad \text{where } \rho_{\text{sf}} = \rho \cdot (\text{safety factor})$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Energy Stored vs. Mass (Specific Energy Limit)

From the hoop-stress limit $v^{2} = \sigma/\rho$, the maximum specific energy of a thin-rim flywheel is derived as follows:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\rho}$$

Since $\rho = m/V$, this can also be written per unit mass:

$$\frac{E}{m} = \frac{\sigma}{2\rho}$$

In SI units, $\sigma$ is in $\text{Pa}$ and $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^{3}$, giving $E/m$ in $\text{J/kg}$. Converting to $\text{Wh/kg}$ (divide by 3600):

$$e_{\text{specific}} = \frac{\sigma}{2\rho \cdot 3600}\ \text{Wh/kg}$$

For example, **carbon-fiber composite** with $\sigma \approx 4000\ \text{MPa}$, $\rho \approx 1600\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$:

$$e_{\text{specific}} \approx \frac{4\times 10^{9}}{2 \times 1600 \times 3600} \approx 347\ \text{Wh/kg}\quad \text{(theoretical maximum)}$$

In practice, only 30–40\% of this value is achieved due to safety factors, hub mass, and containment housing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flywheel Accumulator Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VEHICLE_DC_BUS["Vehicle DC Bus 300 to 400 VDC"]
        DCBUS[DC Bus]
    end

    subgraph PRIMARY_STORAGE["Primary Energy Storage"]
        BATT[Li-ion Battery Pack]
    end

    subgraph FLYWHEEL_SYSTEM["Flywheel Accumulator Subsystem"]
        MACH[Flywheel BLDC Machine]
        ROTOR[Spinning Composite Rotor]
        BRG[Magnetic Bearings]
        HOUS[Vacuum Housing Containment]
        INV[Bidirectional Inverter]
    end

    subgraph TRACTION["Vehicle Drivetrain"]
        WHEEL[Traction Wheels]
    end

    BATT --- DCBUS
    DCBUS --- INV
    INV --- MACH
    MACH --- ROTOR
    ROTOR --- BRG
    BRG --- HOUS
    DCBUS --- WHEEL

    classDef storage fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef fwheel fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef drivetrain fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    class BATT storage
    class INV,MACH,ROTOR,BRG,HOUS fwheel
    class WHEEL,DCBUS drivetrain
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Flow State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Charging: Brake pedal pressed / Regen event
    Idle --> Discharging: Accelerator pedal pressed / Boost event
    Charging --> Holding: Target speed reached
    Holding --> Charging: Speed drops below threshold
    Holding --> Discharging: Demand signal received
    Discharging --> Holding: Speed drops to minimum
    Discharging --> Idle: Energy depleted
    Holding --> Idle: Vehicle parked / System off
    Idle --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of Flywheel Power Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT["Mechanical / Electrical Input Energy"]
    CONVERTER1["Bidirectional Power Converter AC to DC"]
    MACHINE["Integrated Motor Generator BLDC or PMSM"]
    SHAFT["High Speed Shaft Coupling"]
    FLYWHEEL["Composite Rim Flywheel Rotational KE Storage"]
    LOSSES["Parasitic Losses Friction Windage Eddy Current"]
    CONVERTER2["Bidirectional Inverter DC to AC"]
    OUTPUT["Boost Power to DC Bus or Direct Drive"]
    CONTROL["ECU Supervisory Controller Speed and Torque Demand"]

    INPUT --> CONVERTER1
    CONVERTER1 --> MACHINE
    MACHINE --> SHAFT
    SHAFT --> FLYWHEEL
    FLYWHEEL -.-> LOSSES
    FLYWHEEL --> SHAFT
    SHAFT --> MACHINE
    MACHINE --> CONVERTER2
    CONVERTER2 --> OUTPUT
    CONTROL --- CONVERTER1
    CONTROL --- CONVERTER2
    CONTROL --- MACHINE
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Material Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flywheel Rotor Material Selection]
    A --> B[Steel Alloy]
    A --> C[Carbon Fiber Composite]
    A --> D[Glass Fiber Composite]
    B --> B1[Specific Energy 5 to 30 Wh per kg]
    B --> B2[Max Tip Speed 300 to 500 m per s]
    B --> B3[Low Cost Proven Technology]
    C --> C1[Specific Energy 60 to 150 Wh per kg]
    C --> C2[Max Tip Speed 600 to 1000 m per s]
    C --> C3[High Cost Aerospace Grade]
    D --> D1[Specific Energy 30 to 80 Wh per kg]
    D --> D2[Max Tip Speed 400 to 700 m per s]
    D --> D3[Moderate Cost Automotive Grade]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a flywheel accumulator and write the equation for the kinetic energy stored in it.*

**Model Answer:**
A flywheel accumulator is a mechanical energy storage device that stores energy in the form of rotational kinetic energy of a spinning mass and releases it on demand through a coupled machine-generator system. The stored kinetic energy is given by:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,J\,\omega^{2}$$

where $J$ is the mass moment of inertia ($\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity ($\text{rad/s}$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three advantages of flywheel accumulators over electrochemical batteries in EV applications.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher specific power** (1000–5000 W/kg) compared to Li-ion batteries (~250–500 W/kg), enabling faster acceleration response.
2. **Virtually unlimited cycle life** — no chemical degradation; only mechanical wear governs lifetime.
3. **Rapid charge/discharge capability** — full energy exchange within seconds versus minutes for batteries.
4. **No thermal runaway risk** — eliminates fire hazard associated with battery failures.
5. **Wide operating temperature range** — performance is less temperature-sensitive than batteries.

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A flywheel rotor for a hybrid bus energy storage system has the shape of a solid uniform disc of mass **m = 12 kg** and outer radius **r = 0.18 m**, spinning at maximum angular velocity **ω_max = 12,000 rpm**. Compute:

(i) the moment of inertia $J$,
(ii) the maximum stored kinetic energy in Joules and in Watt-hours.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of flywheel accumulators in regenerative braking systems of electric vehicles, with the help of a suitable block diagram showing the energy flow between the battery, flywheel, and traction motor during a braking event.

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1 — Convert angular velocity to rad/s:**

$$\omega = 12{,}000\ \text{rpm} = 12{,}000 \times \frac{2\pi}{60}\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega = 12{,}000 \times 0.10472 \approx 1256.64\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute moment of inertia (solid disc):**

$$J = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,r^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 12 \times (0.18)^{2}$$

$$J = 6 \times 0.0324 = 0.1944\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute stored kinetic energy in Joules:**

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,J\,\omega^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.1944 \times (1256.64)^{2}$$

$$E = 0.0972 \times 1{,}579{,}144 \approx 1{,}534{,}489\ \text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Watt-hours:**

$$E = \frac{1{,}534{,}489}{3600} \approx 426.25\ \text{Wh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\max} \approx 0.426\ \text{kWh}}$$

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

During regenerative braking in an EV:

1. The traction motor (now acting as a generator) converts the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy at the DC bus.
2. The flywheel subsystem (motor + flywheel + inverter) operates in motoring mode (acting as a charging motor) and accelerates the flywheel rotor, storing the recovered energy as rotational kinetic energy.
3. The main Li-ion battery simultaneously accepts the remaining energy that the flywheel cannot absorb due to its current speed-saturation limits.
4. The supervisory ECU dynamically allocates braking energy between the flywheel and battery based on: (a) battery State of Charge (SOC), (b) flywheel current speed, and (c) instantaneous brake pedal rate.

The block diagram flow is:

**Wheels → Traction Motor (Gen Mode) → DC Bus → Bidirectional Inverter → Flywheel Machine → Flywheel Rotor (Spin-Up)** with a parallel path **DC Bus → Battery Pack (Charge)**.

**Advantage of this dual path:** The flywheel can absorb very high power pulses (hundreds of kW) in fractions of a second — much faster than a battery can accept charge — thereby capturing energy that would otherwise be wasted as heat through friction brakes.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the hoop stress in a thin-rim spinning flywheel. Hence explain why the specific energy of a flywheel is fundamentally limited by the material strength-to-density ratio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composite-rim flywheel operates at a maximum tip speed of **v_max = 800 m/s**. The rotor mass is **m = 4.5 kg** and the outer radius is **r = 0.12 m**. Determine:

(i) the maximum permissible angular velocity $\omega_{\max}$,
(ii) the maximum stored kinetic energy in Watt-hours,
(iii) the specific energy in Wh/kg.

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

The hoop stress derivation is shown in **Section 3.4** above. The final result is:

$$\sigma = \rho\,v^{2}$$

The maximum tip speed of the rim is constrained by the tensile strength of the material:

$$v_{\max}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{ult}}}{\text{SF} \cdot \rho}$$

where SF is the safety factor (typically 2 to 4 for composite rims).

The specific energy of a thin-rim flywheel is:

$$\frac{E}{m} = \frac{v^{2}}{2} \leq \frac{\sigma_{\text{ult}}}{2 \cdot \text{SF} \cdot \rho}$$

This shows that the **specific energy is fundamentally limited** by the ratio $\sigma / \rho$. Materials with higher strength-to-density ratios yield higher specific energy. Carbon-fiber composites ($\sigma/\rho \approx 2.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$) outperform steel ($\sigma/\rho \approx 2.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$) by roughly an order of magnitude in achievable specific energy.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{\max} = \frac{v_{\max}}{r} = \frac{800}{0.12} \approx 6666.67\ \text{rad/s}$$

In rpm:

$$\omega_{\max} = 6666.67 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 63{,}662\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia (thin rim approximation):**

$$J \approx m\,r^{2} = 4.5 \times (0.12)^{2} = 4.5 \times 0.0144 = 0.0648\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum stored energy:**

$$E_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,J\,\omega_{\max}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.0648 \times (6666.67)^{2}$$

$$E_{\max} = 0.0324 \times 4.444 \times 10^{7} \approx 1.44 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J}$$

Convert to Wh:

$$E_{\max} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{6}}{3600} \approx 400\ \text{Wh}$$

**Step 4 — Specific energy:**

$$e = \frac{E_{\max}}{m} = \frac{400}{4.5} \approx 88.89\ \text{Wh/kg}$$

$$\boxed{e \approx 88.9\ \text{Wh/kg}}$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes by KTU Students:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Penalty (−1 mark):** Writing $\omega$ in rpm directly into $\tfrac{1}{2}J\omega^{2}$ without converting to rad/s. *Always convert!*
> 2. **Wrong Geometric Formula (−2 marks):** Using $J = mr^{2}$ for a solid disc. *Use $\tfrac{1}{2}mr^{2}$ unless stated as thin rim.*
> 3. **Mixing Joules and Watt-hours:** Forgetting $1\ \text{Wh} = 3600\ \text{J}$.
> 4. **Skipping the Safety Factor Discussion** in hoop-stress derivations.
> 5. **In block diagrams, missing arrowheads or labels** — KTU examiners deduct for unlabeled flow paths.
> 6. **Stating that flywheels can REPLACE batteries in EVs** — they COMPLEMENT batteries, not replace them. Conceptual error worth up to **−2 marks**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Master Equation:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}J\omega^{2}$ — energy scales with the *square* of speed.
- **Disc:** $J = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^{2}$.
- **Hollow Cylinder:** $J = \tfrac{1}{2}m(r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2})$.
- **Thin Rim:** $J = mr^{2}$.
- **Hoop Stress Limit:** $\sigma = \rho v^{2}$ — sets max tip speed.
- **Specific Energy:** $e = \sigma/(2\rho)$ — material-limited, not design-limited.
- **Always convert rpm → rad/s:** multiply rpm by $2\pi/60$.
- **Always convert J → Wh:** divide by **3600**.
- **Composite materials** > steel for high specific energy.
- **Flywheels = high POWER, Batteries = high ENERGY** — they complement each other.
- **Safety considerations:** vacuum housing + magnetic bearings + containment ring are mandatory for high-speed rotors.
- **Cybersecurity / ECU:** The supervisory controller must manage speed limits, SOC limits, and fault detection in real time.
- **Applications in EHVs:** Launch assist, regen-braking buffer, stop-and-go energy recovery, torque smoothing.
- **Cycle life:** practically infinite (vs. ~1000–3000 for Li-ion).
- **Self-discharge:** parasitic — minimized by vacuum and magnetic levitation.
- **Round-trip efficiency:** typically 80–95%, dominated by bearing and aerodynamic losses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 4 · Hydraulic Accumulators

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 OEMUT721 Terminology)**
> A *Hydraulic Accumulator* is a **pressure vessel that stores potential energy** in the form of a **pressurized incompressible hydraulic fluid** (typically oil) and a **compressible gas (nitrogen) or mechanical spring**. It functions as a *rechargeable fluidic battery* within a hydraulic circuit — absorbing, storing, and subsequently releasing energy as demanded by the actuators of an electric or hybrid vehicle powertrain.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressurised Water Tower"

Imagine a **water tower** supplying a town:

| Water Tower | Hydraulic Accumulator |
|---|---|
| Water stored at height (gravitational PE) | Fluid stored at high pressure (pressure PE) |
| A pump fills it during off-peak hours | A hydraulic pump charges it during regenerative braking |
| The town draws water when demand is high | The vehicle draws fluid to drive motors during acceleration |
| The level (head) falls as water is drawn | The gas pre-charge pressure dictates the discharge profile |

In an Electric/Hybrid Vehicle, the accumulator plays the **same role as a battery in a BEV**, but the *currency of energy is pressurized fluid rather than electrons*. It is especially favoured in **hydraulic hybrid vehicles (HHVs)** used for heavy commercial transport (buses, refuse trucks, delivery vans) where very high power density and an almost unlimited cycle life are required.

### 1.2 Functional Roles in an EHV Powertrain

- **Energy Storage** — Captures kinetic energy during regenerative braking.
- **Pulse Power Source** — Delivers high transient power for vehicle launch/acceleration.
- **Pressure Stabiliser** — Dampens pressure ripples, shock loads, and pump pulsations.
- **Emergency / Backup Source** — Provides hydraulic power if the main pump fails.
- **Thermal Expansion Compensator** — Absorbs volume changes due to temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric — Round-Trip Efficiency (η_rt)**
> For a well-designed nitrogen pre-charge hydraulic accumulator system, **η_rt ≈ 70 %–90 %**, compared to **≈ 60 %–80 %** for electrochemical Li-ion cells under high-C regenerative braking. The accumulator's advantage is its **power density (kW/kg)** which can be 5–10× that of a battery.

### 1.3 Classification of Hydraulic Accumulators

The KTU 2024 OEMUT721 syllabus identifies three principal families:

1. **Spring-Loaded (Mechanical) Accumulators** — Uses a coiled compression spring.
2. **Weight-Loaded (Gravity) Accumulators** — Uses a dead-weight piston (rare in vehicles, common in stationary plants).
3. **Gas-Loaded (Pneumatic / Hydropneumatic) Accumulators** — The most widely used in EHVs:
   - **Bladder Type** — Rubber bladder separating N₂ gas and oil.
   - **Piston Type** — Floating piston with seal rings.
   - **Diaphragm Type** — A moulded metal-backed elastomeric diaphragm.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Nitrogen (N₂)?**
> Nitrogen is inert, non-flammable, inexpensive, has predictable polytropic behaviour (**γ ≈ 1.4**), and minimal solubility in mineral oils, making it the universal pre-charge gas for vehicular accumulators.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Gas-Loaded Accumulator

Consider a **bladder-type accumulator** of total geometric volume **V₀** (litres).

- **Step 1 — Pre-charge:** Before assembly, the gas side is pressurised to **P₀** (the *pre-charge pressure*) while the fluid port is open to atmosphere. The bladder is fully expanded, so the gas occupies the **entire** volume **V₀**.
- **Step 2 — Charging (Compression):** The system pump delivers fluid. When system pressure **P > P₀**, fluid forces the bladder to compress. At maximum system pressure **P₁**, the gas volume is reduced to **V₁**.
- **Step 3 — Holding (Storage):** The check valve closes. Energy is held as pressurised gas + slightly compressed fluid.
- **Step 4 — Discharging (Expansion):** When system pressure drops below **P₁**, fluid flows out to actuators. As gas expands from **V₁ → V₂**, pressure drops from **P₁ → P₂** (minimum working pressure).

## 2.2 Governing Thermodynamic Relations

The nitrogen gas is compressed and expanded following a **polytropic process**:

$$P \, V^{n} = \text{constant}$$

where the polytropic exponent **n** lies between:

- **n = 1.0** — *Isothermal* (very slow process, full heat exchange with surroundings).
- **n = 1.4** — *Adiabatic* (very fast process, no heat exchange; γ for diatomic N₂).
- **n = 1.0–1.4** — *Polytropic* (real vehicular operation, typically **n ≈ 1.0** for slow regenerative cycles, **n ≈ 1.2–1.4** for fast transients).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU ESE numerical is built from these six relations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gas law (polytropic) | $P_0 V_0^{n} = P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$ | Pa · m³ | Master compression equation |
| 2 | Compressed gas volume @ P₁ | $V_1 = V_0 \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$ | m³ | Minimum gas volume |
| 3 | Expanded gas volume @ P₂ | $V_2 = V_0 \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n}$ | m³ | Maximum gas volume |
| 4 | **Usable fluid volume (ΔV)** | $\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = V_0\!\left[\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{\!1/n} - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_1}\right)^{\!1/n}\!\right]$ | m³ | Volume discharged per cycle |
| 5 | **Energy stored (polytropic)** | $E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}$ | J | Valid for n ≠ 1 |
| 6 | **Energy stored (isothermal, n = 1)** | $E = P_0 V_0 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$ | J | Limit form of (5) |
| 7 | Mean discharge flow rate | $\dot{Q}_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta t}$ | m³/s | For pump/motor sizing |
| 8 | Gas temperature after compression (adiabatic) | $T_1 = T_0 \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_0}\right)^{(n-1)/n}$ | K | For thermal design |
| 9 | Pre-charge rule of thumb | $P_0 \leq 0.9 \, P_2$ | Pa | Avoids bladder fully collapsing |
| 10 | Max working pressure rule | $P_1 \leq 4 \, P_0$ | Pa | Safe operating envelope |
| 11 | Pump work input (charging) | $W_{in} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_0 V_0}{n - 1}$ | J | Mirror of (5) |
| 12 | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Usually 0.70–0.90 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use \vert or \mid in formulas above:** In KTU mark-sheets, evaluators prefer **LVM/HV** notation. Whenever the magnitude notation is required (e.g., for |ΔP|), write $\vert \Delta P \vert$ to avoid LaTeX/Markdown rendering issues.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the EHV Industry

| Application Sector | Use of Accumulator |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Hybrid Bus (e.g., Eaton, Bosch)** | Stores regenerative braking energy for next stop-start cycle |
| **Refuse-collection Trucks** | High-power recovery during bin-lifting actuation |
| **Fork-lift / Material Handling** | Boost during heavy load lifting |
| **Construction Equipment** | Active ride-control, vibration damping |
| **Aircraft Hydraulic Systems** | Emergency flap/gear actuation if APU fails |
| **Formula-Student Hydraulic KERS** | Race-recovered energy, compact 0.5–2 L units |

The accumulator is the **"energy currency vault"** of any hydraulic hybrid system — analogous to the **HVDC supercapacitor bank** in a battery-electric drivetrain.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** No step is omitted. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly so that a first-time KTU 2024 reader can reproduce the working in an exam.

## 3.1 Derivation — Usable Fluid Volume ΔV

**Given:** A piston-type accumulator with total volume **V₀ = 5 L**, pre-charge pressure **P₀ = 50 bar**, maximum working pressure **P₁ = 200 bar**, minimum working pressure **P₂ = 100 bar**, isothermal compression (n = 1).

**To find:** (a) Gas volume at P₁, (b) Gas volume at P₂, (c) Usable fluid volume ΔV per cycle, (d) Energy stored, (e) Mean flow rate if discharge takes 4 s.

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI units (mandatory for KTU marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_0 &= 5\ \text{L} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3} \\
P_0 &= 50\ \text{bar} = 50 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa} = 5.0 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \\
P_1 &= 200\ \text{bar} = 20 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \\
P_2 &= 100\ \text{bar} = 10 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \\
n &= 1.0 \quad \text{(isothermal assumption)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boyle's Law (isothermal form, n = 1)**

Since $P_0 V_0 = P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$, we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= \dfrac{P_0 \, V_0}{P_1} = \dfrac{(5.0 \times 10^{6})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{20 \times 10^{6}} \\
    &= \dfrac{25\,000}{20 \times 10^{6}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3} \\
    &= 1.25\ \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute V₂**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= \dfrac{P_0 \, V_0}{P_2} = \dfrac{(5.0 \times 10^{6})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{10 \times 10^{6}} \\
    &= \dfrac{25\,000}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3} \\
    &= 2.5\ \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Usable fluid volume ΔV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= V_2 - V_1 = 2.5\ \text{L} - 1.25\ \text{L} = 1.25\ \text{L} \\
         &= 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The usable volume (1.25 L) is **25 % of V₀** — a typical utilisation factor for an isothermal cycle with a pre-charge ratio **P₀/P₂ = 0.5**.

**Step 5 — Energy stored (isothermal, using formula 6)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= P_0 V_0 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) \\
  &= (5.0 \times 10^{6})(5 \times 10^{-3})\ln\!\left(\dfrac{200}{100}\right) \\
  &= 25\,000 \times \ln(2) \\
  &= 25\,000 \times 0.6931 \\
  &= 17\,328.7\ \text{J} \approx 17.33\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Mean discharge flow rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{avg} &= \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3}}{4\ \text{s}} \\
              &= 3.125 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}\text{s}^{-1} \\
              &= 312.5\ \text{mL/s} = 18.75\ \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Pre-charge Ratio on Performance

> [!TIP]
> **The pre-charge ratio $r = P_0 / P_2$ is the most powerful design lever.** The table below (computed for V₀ = 5 L, P₁ = 200 bar, n = 1) shows the trade-off clearly. This is a *favourite KTU examiner's question* — commit it to memory.

| Pre-charge ratio $r = P_0/P_2$ | P₀ (bar) | V₁ (L) | V₂ (L) | ΔV (L) | E (kJ) | Gas utilisation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.10 | 10 | 0.25 | 2.50 | 2.25 | 28.5 | High ΔV, but gas underused |
| 0.25 | 25 | 0.625 | 2.50 | 1.875 | 24.0 | Good balance |
| **0.50** | **50** | **1.25** | **2.50** | **1.25** | **17.3** | **Standard KTU choice** |
| 0.75 | 75 | 1.875 | 2.50 | 0.625 | 9.6 | Low ΔV, gas over-compressed |
| 0.90 | 90 | 2.25 | 2.50 | 0.25 | 3.9 | Near-collapse — forbidden |

**Engineering Conclusion from the matrix:**
- A **lower pre-charge** → larger ΔV (more usable fluid) **but** lower final pressure and lower energy.
- A **higher pre-charge** → smaller ΔV (gas cushion too stiff) but faster response.
- The **KTU-recommended sweet spot is r ≈ 0.5 to 0.6** for vehicular hydraulic hybrid systems.

## 3.3 Derivation — Round-Trip Efficiency (Theoretical Maximum)

For a **reversible** (lossless) gas cycle, the **charging** work (pump compressing gas from P₀ to P₁) is:

$$
W_{in} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_0 V_0}{n - 1}
$$

The **discharge** work (gas expanding from P₁ to P₂ while delivering fluid) is:

$$
W_{out} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}
$$

For a **closed cycle** where the gas returns to its initial state, $P_2 V_2 = P_0 V_0$ only if the same ΔV is recharged — in practice the system is **not** perfectly closed, so:

$$
\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{P_1 V_1 - P_0 V_0} \times 100\ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Real-world η_rt is reduced by (a) gas hysteresis ≈ 5 %, (b) fluid friction in valves/lines ≈ 5–10 %, (c) heat transfer irreversibility ≈ 3–5 %, giving a practical ceiling near **90 %**.
