| F1 racing | Li-ion | Supercapacitor + Flywheel | Extreme peak power in milliseconds |

### 3.4 Derivation: Energy Management — Power Split Logic

Let the load demand power be $P_{load}(t)$. The control objective is:

$$P_{load}(t) = P_{bat}(t) + P_{uc}(t)$$

The control law for a simple **frequency-based split**:

$$P_{bat}(t) = \mathcal{LPF}\left\{P_{load}(t)\right\}$$

$$P_{uc}(t) = P_{load}(t) - P_{bat}(t) = \mathcal{HPF}\left\{P_{load}(t)\right\}$$

where $\mathcal{LPF}$ denotes a low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c$ (typically 0.01 – 0.1 Hz for EVs), and $\mathcal{HPF} = 1 - \mathcal{LPF}$ is the complementary high-pass filter.

**Interpretation:** Slow, sustained power fluctuations are absorbed by the battery; fast transients are routed to the ultracapacitor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the LPF/HPF frequency with electrical frequency (Hz of AC mains). Here $f_c$ is in **drive-cycle-time frequency**, not grid frequency.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HESD Topology Family — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Energy Storage Device A - High Energy] --> B{Topology Type}
    C[Energy Storage Device B - High Power] --> B
    B --> B1[Passive Parallel]
    B --> B2[Semi-Active Bat-Side]
    B --> B3[Semi-Active UC-Side]
    B --> B4[Fully Active Cascaded]
    B1 --> D[DC Bus]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    D --> E[Traction Inverter]
    E --> F[Electric Motor]
    F --> G[Wheels - Mechanical Output]
    B2 -.uses.-> H1[Unidirectional DC-DC]
    B3 -.uses.-> H2[Bidirectional DC-DC]
    B4 -.uses.-> H3[Two Bidirectional DC-DC]
```

### 4.2 Power Flow Diagram — Battery + Ultracapacitor Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Li-ion Battery Pack] -- Controlled Current --> DC1[Bidirectional DC-DC Converter 1]
    UC[Ultracapacitor Bank] -- Controlled Current --> DC2[Bidirectional DC-DC Converter 2]
    DC1 --> BUS[Common DC Bus]
    DC2 --> BUS
    BUS --> INV[Three-Phase Inverter]
    INV --> M[BLDC or PMSM Traction Motor]
    M --> W[Vehicle Wheels]
    BUS --> AUX[Auxiliary Loads: 12V, HVAC, ECU]
    ECU[Vehicle ECU / BMS] -.PWM Control.-> DC1
    ECU -.PWM Control.-> DC2
    ECU -.Gate Signals.-> INV
```

### 4.3 Energy Management Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Driver Presses Accelerator] --> S1{Compute P_load from Pedal Position}
    S1 --> S2{P_load less than P_bat_max}
    S2 -- YES --> S3[Battery supplies full load]
    S2 -- NO --> S4{SoC of UC above threshold?}
    S4 -- YES --> S5[UC supplies peak delta Power]
    S4 -- NO --> S6[Limit power, alert driver]
    S3 --> S7[Update SoC of Battery]
    S5 --> S8[Update SoC of UC]
    S6 --> S9[Activate limp mode]
    S7 --> END[End Cycle]
    S8 --> END
    S9 --> END
```

### 4.4 Ragone Plot — Visual Justification of Hybridization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RP[Ragone Plot Conceptual]
        direction LR
        A1[Capacitor: High Power / Low Energy] -->|Hybridization Vector| A2[Battery + UC Hybrid Region]
        B1[Fuel Cell: Medium Power / High Energy] -->|Hybridization Vector| A2
        C1[Battery: Medium Power / Medium Energy] -->|Hybridization Vector| A2
        A2 -->|Ideal Target| A3[Top Right Corner: High Power AND High Energy]
    end
```

### 4.5 Fully Active HESS Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ESS[Energy Storage Cluster]
        BAT1[Li-ion Battery Module]
        UC1[Ultracapacitor Module]
    end
    subgraph CONV[Power Electronics Stage]
        DCDC1[DC-DC Bidirectional Converter 1]
        DCDC2[DC-DC Bidirectional Converter 2]
        FILTER[LC Filter Network]
    end
    subgraph INVT[Inverter Stage]
        SVPWM[SVPWM Modulator]
        IGBT[Three-Phase IGBT Bridge]
    end
    subgraph MTR[Motor and Drivetrain]
        PMSM[Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor]
        GEAR[Reduction Gearbox]
        WHL[Drive Wheels]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Control Layer]
        VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
        BMS[Battery Management System]
        UCM[Ultracapacitor Management System]
    end
    BAT1 --> DCDC1
    UC1 --> DCDC2
    DCDC1 --> FILTER
    DCDC2 --> FILTER
    FILTER --> IGBT
    SVPWM --> IGBT
    IGBT --> PMSM
    PMSM --> GEAR
    GEAR --> WHL
    VCU --> SVPWM
    VCU --> DCDC1
    VCU --> DCDC2
    BMS --> BAT1
    UCM --> UC1
    BMS --> VCU
    UCM --> VCU
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the term "Hybridization Factor" in the context of Electric Vehicles and state its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The hybridization factor $H_f$ is defined as the ratio of the peak power deliverable by the hybrid energy storage system to the peak power demanded by the drive cycle.

$$H_f = \dfrac{P_{\text{HESS, peak}}}{P_{\text{drive, peak}}}$$

**Significance:**
- $H_f = 1$: Just sufficient hybridization.
- $H_f > 1$: Over-hybridized (with redundancy and improved battery life).
- $H_f < 1$: Under-hybridized (battery gets stressed, life reduces).
- It guides the designer in sizing the secondary power source (UC, flywheel, etc.) optimally.
- A high $H_f$ reduces stress on the primary energy source and extends cycle life.

**(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Differentiate between "specific energy" and "specific power" with one example device for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Unit | Example Device |
|-----------|------------|------|----------------|
| Specific Energy | Energy stored per unit mass | Wh/kg | Li-ion battery (~200 Wh/kg) |
| Specific Power | Maximum power deliverable per unit mass | W/kg | Ultracapacitor (~8000 W/kg) |

- **Specific energy** determines how long a vehicle can run on a single charge.
- **Specific power** determines how fast the vehicle can accelerate or absorb regen braking energy.
- An ideal EV storage device would have BOTH high specific energy and high specific power — no such single device exists, hence **hybridization**.

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four major hybridization topologies for combining a battery and an ultracapacitor in an EV. Compare them in terms of control flexibility, converter losses, and DC bus voltage stability.

**Model Answer:**

**Topology 1: Passive Parallel**
Both battery and ultracapacitor are directly connected across the DC bus without any DC-DC converter.
- Pros: Lowest cost, no converter losses, simplest layout.
- Cons: Load sharing is uncontrolled; DC bus voltage is dictated by the SoC of both devices; cannot independently regulate current through each device.

**Topology 2: Semi-Active (Battery-Side)**
The ultracapacitor is directly connected to the DC bus, while the battery is interfaced through a (typically unidirectional buck or bidirectional) DC-DC converter.
- Pros: Battery current is regulated → prolongs battery cycle life.
- Cons: DC bus voltage drops as UC discharges; UC voltage must match inverter input range.

**Topology 3: Semi-Active (UC-Side)**
The battery is connected directly to the DC bus, while the ultracapacitor is interfaced through a bidirectional DC-DC converter.
- Pros: Stable DC bus voltage (anchored by battery); independent UC power control.
- Cons: All UC power flows through converter → higher losses during regen.

**Topology 4: Fully Active (Cascaded)**
Both battery and ultracapacitor are connected to the DC bus via independent bidirectional DC-DC converters.
- Pros: Maximum control granularity; optimal energy management; both sources can operate at different voltages; supports regenerative braking fully.
- Cons: Highest cost, most complex, two converters contribute switching losses.

**Comparison Table:**

| Topology | Control Flexibility | Converter Loss | DC Bus Stability |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|------------------|
| Passive Parallel | Very Low | None | Poor |
| Semi-Active (Bat-side) | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Semi-Active (UC-side) | Medium-High | Medium | High |
| Fully Active | Very High | High | Very High |

**Conclusion:** The fully active topology is preferred for performance EVs and heavy-duty applications; the semi-active topology is the cost-effective compromise for mass-market EVs.

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hybrid energy storage system for an EV uses a Li-ion battery pack (rated 30 kW continuous, 60 kW peak) and an ultracapacitor bank (rated 40 kW peak). The drive cycle demands a peak power of 80 kW for 10 seconds. Calculate (i) the hybridization factor, (ii) the energy that the ultracapacitor must deliver during the peak, and (iii) comment on whether the system is appropriately sized.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Hybridization Factor:**

$$P_{\text{HESS, peak}} = P_{bat,peak} + P_{uc,peak} = 60 + 40 = 100 \text{ kW}$$

$$H_f = \dfrac{P_{\text{HESS, peak}}}{P_{\text{drive, peak}}} = \dfrac{100}{80} = 1.25$$

**(ii) Energy from Ultracapacitor:**

Power the battery alone can deliver during the peak = 60 kW.
Shortfall = $80 - 60 = 20$ kW (assuming UC supplements the battery's 60 kW).

$$E_{uc} = P_{uc} \times \Delta t = 20{,}000 \times 10 = 200{,}000 \text{ J} = 200 \text{ kJ}$$

$$E_{uc} = \dfrac{200{,}000}{3600} \approx 55.56 \text{ Wh}$$

**(iii) Commentary:**

Since $H_f = 1.25 > 1$, the system is **over-hybridized** by 25%, providing a comfortable safety margin. The battery is not forced to operate at its absolute peak (60 kW) for the full 10 s, which:
- Reduces battery heating.
- Slows down battery degradation.
- Allows the UC to absorb part of the regenerative braking energy at the end of the acceleration phase.

**Verdict:** The system is appropriately (and conservatively) sized for this drive cycle.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, describe the fully active hybridization topology for combining a fuel cell and an ultracapacitor in a fuel cell hybrid electric vehicle (FCHEV). Explain why this combination is preferred over a fuel cell + battery topology.

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic Description:**
The fully active topology consists of:
- A **PEM fuel cell stack** connected via a **unidirectional boost DC-DC converter** (since fuel cells cannot accept reverse current).
- An **ultracapacitor bank** connected via a **bidirectional DC-DC converter** to the same DC bus.
- The DC bus feeds a **three-phase inverter** driving a **PMSM motor**.

**Schematic:**

```
[Fuel Cell Stack] --> [Uni-Directional Boost DC-DC] --+
                                                       |--> [DC Bus] --> [Inverter] --> [PMSM] --> [Wheels]
[Ultracapacitor Bank] --> [Bidirectional DC-DC] ------+
                            ^
                            |
                         [VCU control]
```

**Operating Principle:**
- During steady cruising: Fuel cell alone supplies the average power demand.
- During acceleration: Fuel cell supplies base load; UC supplies the transient peak.
- During regenerative braking: UC absorbs the regen energy (fuel cell cannot accept reverse current, so it is bypassed).
- During idle/low load: UC can assist in maintaining bus voltage; fuel cell can be throttled down to its efficient operating point.

**Why Fuel Cell + Ultracapacitor is Preferred over Fuel Cell + Battery:**

| Parameter | Fuel Cell + Battery | Fuel Cell + Ultracapacitor |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| Regen braking acceptance | Limited by battery C-rate | Excellent (UC accepts high C-rate) |
| Cold start / Low temp | Battery degraded | UC performs better |
| Cycle life of secondary | ~2000 cycles | > 1,000,000 cycles |
| Response time | Battery ~ 100 ms | UC ~ 1 ms |
| Weight for same power | Heavier | Lighter |
| Cost over lifetime | Higher (battery replacement) | Lower |

**Conclusion:** The UC's high power density and near-instantaneous response perfectly complement the fuel cell's slow dynamic response and inability to absorb regen energy.

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the usable energy stored in an ultracapacitor bank operating between $V_{max}$ and $V_{min}$. If a UC bank has $C = 100$ F, $V_{max} = 48$ V, and $V_{min} = 24$ V, compute (i) the total energy stored at full charge, (ii) the usable energy, and (iii) the energy wasted in the unusable voltage band.

**Model Answer:**

**(Derivation):**
The instantaneous energy stored in a capacitor at voltage $V$ is:

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$$

The energy at full charge ($V = V_{max}$):

$$E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_{max}^{2}$$

The residual energy at minimum operating voltage ($V = V_{min}$):

$$E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_{min}^{2}$$

**Usable energy** (the difference, since the inverter cannot operate below $V_{min}$):

$$E_{usable} = E_{max} - E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} C \left(V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2}\right)$$

**Energy wasted** (locked below $V_{min}$):

$$E_{wasted} = E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_{min}^{2}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $C = 100$ F, $V_{max} = 48$ V, $V_{min} = 24$ V.

**(i) Total energy at full charge:**

$$E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times (48)^{2} = 50 \times 2304 = 115{,}200 \text{ J} = 32 \text{ Wh}$$

**(ii) Usable energy:**

$$E_{usable} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times \left((48)^{2} - (24)^{2}\right) = 50 \times (2304 - 576) = 50 \times 1728 = 86{,}400 \text{ J} = 24 \text{ Wh}$$

**(iii) Wasted energy:**

$$E_{wasted} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times (24)^{2} = 50 \times 576 = 28{,}800 \text{ J} = 8 \text{ Wh}$$

**Verification:** $E_{usable} + E_{wasted} = 24 + 8 = 32$ Wh = $E_{max}$ ✓

**Useful voltage utilization factor:**

$$\eta_V = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{E_{max}} = \dfrac{24}{32} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly compute $E_{usable}$ as $\dfrac{1}{2} C (V_{max} - V_{min})^2$. This is **WRONG** — the formula is $(V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$, NOT $(V_{max} - V_{min})^2$. The quadratic nature of energy in a capacitor is non-negotiable. Losing 2 marks here is the most common error observed in KTU valuation reports for this module.

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices**

- **Hybridization** = combining two or more storage devices to leverage complementary strengths.
- **High-energy device:** Battery / Fuel Cell → supplies steady power.
- **High-power device:** Ultracapacitor / Flywheel → handles transients and regen.
- **Four topologies:** Passive Parallel, Semi-Active (Bat-side), Semi-Active (UC-side), Fully Active.
- **Hybridization Factor:** $H_f = P_{\text{HESS,peak}} / P_{\text{drive,peak}}$; $H_f = 1$ is ideal.
- **Ragone Plot:** plots specific power vs specific energy; ideal devices sit in top-right corner.
- **Usable UC energy:** $E_{usable} = \frac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$ — note the **square** of voltages, NOT the squared difference.
- **Fuel cell cannot accept reverse current** → always pair with a buffer (UC or battery) that absorbs regen energy.
- **LPF/HPF split rule:** Battery handles low-frequency load demand; UC handles high-frequency transients.
- **C-rate** is the discharge/charge rate relative to rated capacity (1C = full discharge in 1 hour).
- **Sizing methodology:** Compute peak power shortfall → multiply by peak duration → divide by UC energy formula → determine cell count.
- **Fully active topology** offers best control but highest cost and complexity.
- **State of Charge (SoC)** of both devices must be continuously monitored by the BMS and UCMS.
- **Practical examples:** Toyota Prius (NiMH + small UC), Tesla (Li-ion + regen-only UC support), Honda Clarity (Fuel Cell + Li-ion).
- **Common pitfalls to avoid:** confusing LPF frequency with grid frequency; using $(V_{max} - V_{min})^2$ instead of $(V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$; assuming passive parallel offers control flexibility; stating that fuel cells can absorb regen energy (they cannot).
- **Key trade-off:** Energy density ↔ Power density — no single device is on both extremes → hybridization bridges the gap.
- **Industrial relevance:** Hybridization extends battery life by 30–50%, improves regen energy capture by up to 70%, and reduces vehicle-level cost over lifecycle.
